anchor: Preface
text:
This book is not a traditional grammar guide or a collection of lesson plans, but a foundation for understanding language and a catalyst for informed teaching. It doesn't merely complement existing knowledge - it lays the groundwork for novices to understand the landscape of language, while offering seasoned educators new vantage points from which to view familiar territory.

Each chapter equips you with tools for a different facet of English, moving from concepts to use. We begin by building a clear and solid understanding of familiar labels and categories and by asking what counts as standard and grammatical. We then map categories (noun, verb, phrase, clause) onto functions (head, modifier, subject, dependent), and revisit lexical categories—especially adverbs and prepositions—to show how a leaner system accounts for the data (including stubborn items like on). From there we introduce syntax trees as a professional lens for teachers rather than a student exercise. Subsequent chapters take up the sound system—suprasegmentals and connected-speech processes—followed by vocabulary (frequency, definition, deliberate practice, idiomaticity) and the writing system (phonics and spelling patterns). We then survey key constructions (questions, negation, relatives) to expose shared structures, shift to fluency and flow across skills and their trade-offs with accuracy, and end with discourse organization (coherence, information packaging) and the dynamics of conversation (turn-taking, pragmatics, cultural norms).

This order is intentional: it lays conceptual ground first (what counts as a category, a standard, a judgment), stabilizes the category–function distinction before representation (trees), fills in the interfaces that shape form and processing (sound, lexicon, orthography), and only then generalizes across patterns (constructions) before widening to performance and interaction (fluency, discourse, conversation). In short, we move from foundations to form, from form to patterns, and from patterns to use.

The chapters are more or less modular. If your priority is classroom practice, begin with fluency and discourse, then consult constructions as needed. If you are preparing learners for pronunciation-intensive work, start with the sound system and writing system, then loop back to categories and functions. For grammar-focused courses, take the default path through categories, functions, lexical classes, and trees before dipping into constructions. Cross-references can be used to find light prerequisites; otherwise, feel free to reorder to fit your cohort, time, and aims.

Throughout the book, you'll find that it often prioritizes areas that are typically underrepresented in TESL training. Although the examples draw primarily on ESL and EFL contexts, the book is equally relevant for English language arts teachers at any level and for college-level writing and communication instructors, anyone who needs to understand how English works in order to help others use it well. I'll point out common pitfalls and misunderstandings, not to criticize, but to help you avoid the ruts that many language courses get stuck in.

As you explore this linguistic landscape, remember that language is an ever-changing ecosystem. My goal is not always to provide definitive answers, but to equip you with the fundamental knowledge and critical thinking skills to navigate the terrain of English as you encounter it in your teaching journey.

anchor: Preface
text:
This book maps the terrain; it's up to you to explore it.

The global market for English grammar textbooks is vast, from learner series like Azar-Hagen and Murphy's Grammar in Use to teacher references like Larsen-Freeman et al. (2015) and Parrott (2010), with locally produced counterparts worldwide (see also (Berry, 2021); (Park-Johnson et al., 2020); (Valenzuela, 2020)). What nearly all share is a traditional body of inherited analysis that has served generations of teachers and learners. Much of it is useful, and much of it is right. But much of it has also been passed down largely untested, and some of it doesn't hold up under scrutiny ((Pullum, 2024) offers a bracing critique). This book starts from a different foundation: the framework of The Cambridge Grammar of the English Language, which subjects traditional claims to systematic testing and replaces those that don't hold up. It also reaches well beyond grammar to the sound system, the writing system, vocabulary, fluency, discourse, and conversation.

anchor: Preface > A note about the grammatical framework
text: The framework provided here follows that of The Cambridge grammar of the English language (CGEL (Huddleston et al., 2002)) very closely, but has been simplified for accessibility. For instance, I use a two-level noun and noun phrase analysis instead of CGEL's three-level approach, employ a smaller set of functions, and avoid the issue of function fusion. This book focuses on providing the essential elements and ideas that teachers need to embark on their English-teaching journey, rather than attempting to cover all constructions dealt with in CGEL. For a more in-depth treatment, consider A student's introduction to English grammar (Huddleston et al., 2022) or CGEL itself.
subsection_title: A note about the grammatical framework

anchor: Preface > A note about Canadian English
text: While most of the book applies to any English-language teaching situation, the phonology section presents vowels as spoken in southern Ontario, Canada. Many of these will align with General American English, but I include features such as Canadian raising (e.g., /hʌʊs/, (Audio recordings are available on English Wiktionary: https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/File:En-ca-raising-house.ogg) as opposed to the British or General American /haʊs/ (https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/File:en-us-house-noun.ogg)), or indeed any other pronunciation. This choice reflects my own dialect and provides a specific reference point for phonological discussions.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Box: Learning objectives
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
- Understand why necessary and sufficient conditions fail for linguistic definitions

- Distinguish everyday from technical meanings of grammatical terms

- Apply critical thinking strategies including looking for counter-examples

- Begin to identify the basic lexical categories of English: nouns, verbs, adjectives, and determinatives

- Recognize how words can belong to different categories in different contexts

- Understand the concept of lexical categories as prototype-based rather than rule-based

- Apply systematic criteria to identify lexical categories despite boundary cases

- Appreciate the complexity underlying seemingly simple grammatical concepts

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
If the idea of teaching English grammar makes you nervous, you're not alone. Many aspiring English-language teachers feel intimidated by grammar, unsure about the difference between an adverb and an adjective, or worried they don't know enough about the intricacies of the language they hope to teach. But here's a secret: understanding grammar isn't about memorizing a set of rigid rules. There will, of course, be memorization, but that won't be of much use unless we develop new ways of thinking about language, ways that will help us make sense of English and, eventually, help your future students do the same.

This chapter is designed to ease you into this new way of thinking. I'm not going to start by bombarding you with entirely unfamiliar terms like clitic, deixis, or ergative, and I won't be setting out any complex rules. Instead, we'll begin by questioning some basic assumptions about language and exploring how we define the things we think we understand.

We'll start with some fundamental concepts and gradually build up to more specific grammatical ideas. Along the way, we'll develop critical thinking strategies that are crucial for anyone planning to teach language. You'll learn to look for counter-examples, to consider different perspectives, and to recognize the difference between everyday and technical uses of terms.

If you're skeptical that there's any value at all in knowing a lot about English, again, you're in good company. But research on Teacher Language Awareness (TLA) suggests that teachers who understand how language works as a system are better equipped to diagnose learner errors, anticipate difficulty, and adapt their explanations on the fly (Andrews, 2007). As Stevick puts it, the teacher needs to know the pathways and obstacles of the language not only to run back and forth in them for herself, but also to see them as they look to a newcomer (1980: 251, cited in (Andrews, 2007): 7). Don't get me wrong: this book is not about becoming able to teach linguistics to your students. It's about what you, as a teacher, need to know in order to anticipate their problems, respond to them, and put into words what fluent speakers do without thinking.

But for something to count as knowledge, it has to be true, and much of what passes for knowledge about language fails this test. Nouns are people, places, and things; verbs are action words - these are beliefs about language, not knowledge. Bartels (2009) cautions that academic knowledge about language doesn't automatically transfer to classroom practice. I'd say that's partly because it doesn't qualify as knowledge. True knowledge about language does become useful when teachers connect it to the demands of their classrooms, and this book includes many exercises that help you make those connections.

Don't worry if you feel unsure about your grammar knowledge right now. By the end of this chapter, you'll have a basic toolkit for thinking about language that will help you approach grammar with more confidence. And we'll start with a question that might seem simple at first, but will challenge us to think more deeply: What makes soup soup?

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
It seems like a silly, irrelevant, and trivially easy question, at least until you try to answer it. What's the difference between tea and soup, sauce and soup, porridge and soup, stew and soup, juice and soup? Don't forget that there's gazpacho, salmorejo, cucumber soup, and other cold soups. (Source: BBC Scotland (https://youtu.be/Y1HVTNxwt7w).)

This isn't special to soup. Most things, when you start looking at examples and counter-examples, turn out to be really hard to pin down. The problem is that we try to define things by picking out necessary and sufficient conditions.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > Necessary and sufficient conditions
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
In both philosophical and scientific circles we find the concept of necessary and sufficient conditions. A necessary condition must be present for acceptance; a sufficient condition, if present, guarantees acceptance. In other words, when we say a condition is necessary and sufficient for a word to be categorized as a noun, we mean that the word can't be a noun unless it possesses characteristic A, and that if characteristic A is present, the word is definitely a noun.

A clear and straightforward example of this is the concept of even numbers in mathematics. An integer is an even number if dividing it by two results in another integer. This is both a necessary and sufficient condition for an integer to be even. No even number exists that is not divisible by two in this way (making it a necessary condition), and any such number is always even (making it a sufficient condition). (Even something as seemingly definitive as the difference between even and odd numbers may not be as clear-cut as we think (Heubner et al., 2018).)

So, from philosophical abstractions to mathematical certainties, necessary and sufficient conditions often frame our understanding of concepts.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > The trouble with (linguistic) definitions
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
But when we come to language and its components, definitions that use necessary and sufficient conditions tend to fail. Let's say you defined a noun as a word that denotes a person, place, or thing and you defined a verb as an action word. Take a moment to think about what kind of word could be more of an action word than action. And then think about whether action is a verb or a noun (hint: check a dictionary (https://www.ldoceonline.com/)).

To be clear, action is usually a noun, but it's also a verb (e.g., We need to action these tasks before the end of the day.). Or perhaps it would be better to say that there are two actions, the more common one being a noun and the relatively rare one being a verb.

But it's not just action. Consider the definition of violation. (https://www.ldoceonline.com/dictionary/violation) It's a noun, and it's an action. The same is true of many nouns, from blow to cue and signal to sin. They are defined using the phrase the action of, so this pretty much destroys the very common idea that a noun is a person, place, or thing and a verb is an action word. Nouns can be action words too.

We define words to understand language, but we need to understand language to define words.

A thinking trap
You see four cards on a table. Each card has a number on one side and a color on the other side. The sides you can see have a 3, an 8, and the colors blue and red. To check the rule if a card has an even number on one side, then the other side is blue, which card(s) should you flip over?

figures/wason.png
figureImage by User:Life of Riley and User:Domdomegg, CC BY-SA 4.0 via Wikimedia Commons.

License: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0

Many people would say the 8 and the blue. And indeed, you need to check the 8. If the other side is not blue, the rule is violated. On the other hand, flipping the blue card doesn't test the rule. The rule says nothing about odd numbers at all, only about even numbers, which is presumably why almost nobody checks the 3 card. So, whether the other side of the blue card is an even or odd number, it doesn't affect the rule. The red card holds the key. Turn it over, and if you find an even number, the rule would be violated.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > The trouble with (linguistic) definitions
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
Our goal is not only to affirm the rule but also to look for counter-examples, situations where the rule fails. This is just as important with the definitions of linguistic concepts. A definition that either includes non-members or excludes members is not a good definition. If we're assessing an action word as a definition for a verb, we need to consider both affirming and contradicting evidence. For example, we can check whether the word run is an action word (it is) and whether it is a verb (it is). On the other hand, we also need to look for contradicting evidence such as words that are considered action words but are not verbs, like action or run in the sentence I went for a run. And words like exist and sense, which are not necessarily perceived as action words, can be verbs.

But maybe we can save something here. Can you think of any words denoting physical objects that are verbs? Spend a few moments.

It's safe to say that nouns and verbs can both denote actions, but verbs never denote physical objects, or people or places, for that matter. We need a noun for that. This leads us to the following conclusion:

It's true that persons, places, and things are only denoted by nouns, but

it's false that nouns only denote persons, places, and things.

That's a subtle difference, but an important one. Nouns can denote a wider variety of things, while verbs are more limited, and the sets of things that nouns and verbs can denote overlap.

So far, we've identified three issues when it comes to defining nouns and verbs. When it comes to adjectives and other lexical categories, similar points apply, but let's take stock right now.

- A simple definition with necessary and sufficient conditions will include things that don't belong (gravy is made from stock but it's not soup) and exclude things that do belong (gazpacho is served cold but it's still soup).

- We can't just make our definition fit the examples we have in mind. We need to consciously think of counter-examples.

- Sometimes we need to restate our ideas to accurately express them.

As language teachers, we use categories like noun and verb a lot. But categorizing words is a tool, not the end goal. The purpose of categorizing is to be able to make general statements about the world or, in our case, about language.

Here's why this matters: the wrong categories lead to more rules and more exceptions than you need. Having the right categories means you can say more with less, and this makes life easier for you and for your students.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > The trouble with (linguistic) definitions > Box: Questions
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
- What's the main goal of this course?

- What's wrong with the idea that an English noun is the name of a person, place, or thing?

- Which statement is a necessary and sufficient definition of English verbs: A) verbs denote actions. B) actions are denoted by verbs. C) neither of the above.

- How is the soup video relevant to defining word categories?

- What's the thinking trap we should try to avoid?

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > The trouble with (linguistic) definitions
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
Answers

- We are gaining facility with the analysis of language so that we can provide our students with the tools that they can use to decide how to speak English.

- In English, nouns denote a far wider range of concepts than this, including actions and characteristics.

- C) is correct. The category of verbs can't be reliably defined based on denotations.

- It illustrates that categories in general don't have necessary and sufficient properties.

- We look only for confirmatory evidence to support our hypotheses, and we fail to look for evidence that might disconfirm them.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > What do we mean by phrase?
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
Just as we found with nouns and verbs, some of our most basic assumptions about language can trip us up. Take the word phrase, for instance. You probably think you know what it means (after all, we use phrases every day). In everyday language, a phrase is just a group of words that go together to express an idea, like on the table or be going to. And that's a perfectly good sense of phrase. It's the most common one, and the one most people associate with the word.

But there is also a technical sense, and that's the one I'll be using throughout. A phrase in English grammar is a specific type of structure with two key properties:

- It has a head, a core word that determines the basic properties of the whole phrase

- It may have dependents, optional words or other phrases that modify or complete the meaning of the head

This is quite different from the everyday notion of words that go together. For example, coffee and tea might seem like a perfectly good phrase in everyday speech, but technically it's not a single phrase: it's a coordination of two noun phrases (coffee and tea) joined by and (see Section for more about coordinations). None of these words is the head of the whole expression.

Examples of phrases, in the everyday sense, that aren't phrases in the technical sense include:

2

can't help but

let alone

want to try again

pick up the pencil

good or bad

salt and pepper

Examples in ( and b) are missing their required dependents: you can't say this isn't working, let alone. Examples ( and d) show bracketed portions that could form complete phrases (want to try again, pick up the pencil), but we've artificially extracted them from their full contexts.

The coordination examples in ( and f) present a different issue entirely. Unlike the headed structures we've been discussing, coordinations join elements of equal status with no single head. When we say salt and pepper, neither noun licenses the other; they're simply equals. This makes coordinations fundamentally different from phrases in our technical sense, which require a head.

For now, there's no need to think too hard about what a phrase is. I just want to alert you to the fact that I'm going to be using phrase in a way that might seem familiar but is actually subtly different.

This distinction between the technical meaning of phrase and the everyday meaning is not so unusual. Academics often adopt common words but give them a technical meaning. This doesn't mean that the everyday sense is wrong or that the technical one is; it's common for words to have various meanings. But it's useful for English teachers to be aware that many linguistic terms, like phrase, have a technical sense which is similar to but slightly different from the everyday meaning.
subsection_title: What do we mean by phrase?

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Introduction > What do we mean by phrase?
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
This technical definition of phrases will be important throughout the following chapters. When we talk about noun phrases, verb phrases, and other types, we'll always be referring to these headed structures, whether or not they have dependents, not just any group of connected words. To be explicit, this allows for one-word phrases such as those italicized in ().

Stop!

Would you like pepper with that?

With this clearer understanding of what we mean by phrases, we can now turn to the specific types of phrases we find in English, starting with noun phrases and the nouns that head them.
subsection_title: What do we mean by phrase?

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
There's no perfect definition of noun, (This book is about English, and so, here and throughout, I mean English nouns, verbs, tenses, etc. When I want to talk about nouns in languages in general, I'll make that clear.) just as there's no perfect definition of soup, but we can get very close, as long as we're willing to hedge with words like can, almost, and typically.

I will use words here that have not yet been explained. This may be frustrating, but it is inevitable. Syntax is a system, and its elements can only be defined in relation to each other. The meaning of the other words will become clear soon, and the Glossary collects all the technical terms in one place.

- Nouns can denote almost anything.

- A word that denotes a person, place, or thing is a noun
(but not a noun is a word that denotes person, place, or thing).

- A word that is countable is a noun
(but not all nouns are countable).

- Almost any word that is plural is a noun (but not all nouns can be plural).

- Nouns often follow the or a (but not words following the or a are nouns).

- Nouns head noun phrases (NPs).

- Noun phrases can typically include adjective phrases as modifiers
(and other types of phrases typically can't).

- Noun phrases can't typically include adverb phrases as modifiers
(but other types of phrases typically can).

- Noun phrases can perform a variety of functions, including subject and object (but not subjects and objects are noun phrases). (Some examples follow, and I'll say more about what subjects and objects are later.)

- Noun phrases can typically be possessive. (The Cambridge grammar of the English language uses the term genitive instead of possessive because the relationship is often not one of possession. The term possessive, though, is much more common, so I will use it here, as long as you promise to remember that your pet's vet is not a vet possessed by your pet.)

Importantly, each of these characteristics can be odd or unfamiliar to English-language learners. In the other languages they speak, there may be no plural nouns, for example. So the elements of the definition point to facts about English nouns that may be useful for English-language learners to learn. It's also important to note that many of the statements are not commutative (multiplication is commutative but division isn't; but ). In other words, the way you say something can be really important.

Unfortunately, people often won't notice (or will later disregard) the difference between nouns can typically follow the or a and a word that follows the or a is typically a noun. In fact, the is followed immediately by a noun about 34,500 times per million words, while it's followed by a non-noun about 48,400 times per million words. (Author's calculations from the Corpus of Contemporary American English (Davies, 2024).) So, while the next word after the is often a noun, anyone relying on this to be true will be wrong more often than they're right.

text: The following is meant to make the characteristics listed above more concrete by providing examples. After considering each set of examples, try to think of nouns that do NOT have one or more of the properties listed. In other words, look for counter-examples.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > What nouns denote
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text: We've already seen how nouns can denote actions. We also have characteristics (e.g., beauty, happiness, size, aptitude), states (e.g., absence), relations (e.g., togetherness, superiority), and on and on.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Countability
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text: Here are some examples of count nouns: apple, book, chair, pen, tree, giraffe, telescope, flute, cathedral, quasar, enzyme. And here are some non-count nouns: water, information, rice, music, furniture, advice, syrup, flour, machinery, courage, syntax, kinship.

chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text: Consider the following examples of plural nouns: we, women, children, students, things, eyes, police, fish, words, thanks, feet, and problems.

chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text: These examples are very simple noun phrases with the and a plus a head noun: the world, a lot, the way, the end, the people, a man, the day, a couple, and a year.

subsubsection_title: Noun phrases with adjective-phrase modifiers and head nouns

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Noun phrases functioning as subjects
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text: In each of the following sentences, the italicized noun phrase is the subject: you win; time is on my side; a little bit goes a long way; other people can help; the supreme court released its decision; often, the twelfth one is perfect; a very good morning is a rare pleasure; on Monday, the shop will be closed; and what do human rights mean.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Possessive noun phrases
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text: The following noun phrases are possessive: my, mine, its, whose, Brett's, time's, other people's, the supreme court's, and the lady who lives here's.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Good examples of nouns
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text: A word like school, for instance, has all the properties listed above. It's a prototypical noun, a good example. Here are some other generally good examples of nouns, though they might not each exhibit every property given above: person, way, day, life, man, school, student, thing, house, family, work, part, night, country, government, place, money, water, and room. Which nouns lack which properties?

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Less good examples of nouns
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
A word like umbrage, for instance, is missing a number of the properties listed above. For instance, it has no plural, nor do you find possessive NPs with umbrage as the head. It doesn't denote a physical object, a person, or a place. It is not countable and therefore it doesn't follow an. It has other oddities too; can you think of any?

Other not-so-good examples include: contemplation, crockery, furniture, sake (as in for heaven's sake), shrift, what, and behalf. What properties does each of them lack? Practice thinking of nouns that are missing one or more of the properties. Use a good dictionary to check if you're not sure the word is a noun. (I would suggest the Simple English Wiktionary. (https://simple.wiktionary.org/wiki/Main_Page) I'm an editor there, and I'll stand behind the category system there. If you don't find the word you're looking for there, then try the Longman Dictionary of Contemporary English. (https://www.ldoceonline.com/))

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Less good examples of nouns > Box: Questions
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
From each group of statements, choose the one that is true and relevant. (180[t]0.91. a; 2. a; 3. c; 4. b; 5. a; 6. c
)

-

- Nouns denote almost anything: objects, actions, characteristics.

- A noun is a word that denotes only people, places, and things.

-

- Any word that is countable is a noun.

- You can count nouns.

- All nouns are countable.

-

- A plural noun always ends in an -s.

- Any noun can be plural.

- Almost any word that is plural is a noun.

-

- A noun is any word that follows the or a.

- Nouns can typically follow the or a.

- Any word that typically follows the or a is a noun.

-

- A noun phrase can typically include an adjective phrase as a modifier.

- A noun phrase can typically include an adverb phrase as its modifier.

- Any phrase with an adjective phrase as its modifier is a noun phrase.

-

- Nouns themselves can perform a variety of functions, including subject and object.

- Noun phrases function only as subjects or objects.

- Noun phrases can perform a variety of functions, including subject and object.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Some examples > Less good examples of nouns > Box: Find the nouns
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
In reality, from the top of the ladder, standing erect on the last rung, you could just touch the Moon if you held your arms up. We had taken the measurements carefully (we didn't yet suspect that she was moving away from us); the only thing you had to be very careful about was where you put your hands. I always chose a scale that seemed fast (we climbed up in groups of five or six at a time), then I would cling first with one hand, then with both, and immediately I would feel ladder and boat drifting away from below me, and the motion of the Moon would tear me from the Earth's attraction. Yes, the Moon was so strong that she pulled you up; you realized this the moment you passed from one to the other: you had to swing up abruptly, with a kind of somersault, grabbing the scales, throwing your legs over your head, until your feet were on the Moon's surface. (180[t]0.9
reality, top, ladder, rung, you, Moon, you, your, arms. We, measurements, we, she, us; thing, you, you, your, hands. I, scale, we, groups, time, I, ladder, boat, me, motion, Moon, me, Earth's, attraction. Moon, she, you; you, moment, you, other; you, kind, somersault, scales, your, legs, your, head, your, feet, Moon's, surface.
)
- Italoauthorcalvino2018, The distance of the moon

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Countability
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
Countability is a remarkably easy concept. If you can say two, where is the noun, then is a count noun. For example, two pencils is fine so pencil is a count noun. But you can't say *There are two furnitures (* means it's ungrammatical), so furniture is a non-count noun. Sometimes, words like jeans seem like they might be count nouns, but it turns out that we count pairs of jeans, not jeans. Another perhaps-surprising example of a non-count noun is police: we don't say *two police were standing on the corner. Instead, we say two (police) officers, where officer is a count noun. Meat is an example of a noun that is not usually countable but can be if we mean 'kinds of meat'. This way of creating count nouns from non-count nouns in a 'kinds-of' meaning is quite common.

The concept of countability may be easy to understand, but the trick is in knowing for which nouns one can or can't say two. It may seem quite obvious to you what is countable and what is not, and you might think that because it's so obvious, it will be very easy to explain to students. You might even have some deep down feeling that somebody who failed to get the concept must be a little slow. But if you have that feeling, it's only because you already know. In fact, even experts can have a very muddled concept of countability. Take, for example, the Longman Dictionary of Contemporary English.

I check this excellent dictionary regularly because it has very informative entries, but if you look up uncountable, (https://www.ldoceonline.com/dictionary/uncountable/) you'll find the following definition:

An uncountable noun has no plural form and refers to something which can't be counted or regarded as either singular or plural, for example 'money' or 'happiness'.

Sadly, Longman has fallen into the very thinking trap we considered earlier: They thought up a few matching examples and failed to consider counterexamples. Let's look, on their behalf, for the counterexamples. Can you think of a noun that does have a plural form but which can't be counted, in other words, a plural noun for which you can't say two? We've already considered at least one example in Section. See the footnote after you've given it some thought. (180[t]0.9
We've already seen police. Pants is another example. We say two pairs of pants, not *two pants. Still others include groceries, remains, clothes, scissors, genitals.
) The funny thing is that the dictionary applies these labels correctly to individual words. The entry for glasses, (https://www.ldoceonline.com/dictionary/glasses/) for instance, is correctly labeled as uncountable. The editors know that glasses is plural and that it's a non-count noun, but they haven't noticed that their definition is in conflict with their practice.

Why does this matter?
Consider the following:

[]I had (a) tea.

[]I had a cookie.

[*]I had cookie.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Countability
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
I hope you'll agree with me that () is perfectly fine with or without the a, but that in () the a is required and that () is ungrammatical. What could explain this difference? It turns out that singular count nouns require a determiner (a term I'll explain in a later chapter). The words a and the can function as determiners. So can words like one, his, Brett's, every, etc. So, if you have a concept of count vs non-count, and you've got a sense of singular vs plural, you can use that to explain why () is fine either way, but () isn't. This is what I mean when I say that we're working with a system: as you learn about some parts of it, you can use those parts to solve mysteries in other areas that are not obviously connected.

So, we see that the definition of uncountable in the Longman dictionary is wrong, even though it's generally a very good dictionary and even though its own entry for glasses (among many others) contradicts its definition of uncountable. Maybe this determination isn't so easy. But even if creating a good definition turns out to be a little harder than we thought, it may still feel like it should be quite obvious what is countable and uncountable based on certain properties. If that were the case, though, then we would find that singular nouns always translated as singulars and plural nouns always as plurals (at least, when a language has plurals). But in the realm of countability, what counts in one language may not count in another.

If you're multilingual, perhaps you've already run into this. Consider the French:

[]J' ai coupé mes cheveux.

I have cut my-pl hairs-pl.

'I cut my hair.'

In, the French word cheveux is a plural count noun (though it would be rare to count hairs, even in French), while in English it's usually a singular non-count noun. As the French shows, there's no need to enumerate hairs; you could just say I cut my hairs and leave it at that. English treats hair as a mass; French treats it as discrete items. Neither is more logical.

It's easy to think that ideas that we take for granted, such as whether something is countable or not, are objective facts. In many cases like this, they are actually inter-subjective judgments, many entirely unpredictable to those outside the community of speakers of a given language or dialect. Part of the trouble is that countable pulls double duty: it describes a grammatical property of the noun (takes numerals, pluralizes) and a conceptual property of what the noun refers to (individual items vs. undifferentiated stuff). These mostly line up, but when they don't, as with furniture or news, confusion follows. We'll see this kind of double duty again.

And yet, many language-teaching materials treat countability as if it were obvious from mere introspection. Far from it! Consider the following explanation from Allen (2008), a textbook for children. It's perfectly simple if you speak English and almost entirely unhelpful if you don't.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Nouns and noun phrases > Countability
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
You can see from the list that some nouns can be counted and others can not. For example, if you want to tell your friend about the fruit on the tree, you could tell them your tree has plenty of plums on it. The noun 'plum' is countable so you have to make it plural by adding an '-s-' (sic). Or you could tell them your tree has plenty of fruit on it. In this case, the noun 'fruit' is uncountable, so you can not count an '-s'. Allen (2008)

The book assumes that plums are countable because you can count them, and you can count them because they're countable. What could be easier? What about rice and seed? There is no way for a learner of English to guess that you can say two seeds but not *two rices if you mean 'two grains of rice'. Two rices can only mean 'two kinds of rice', not 'two grains'.

E.g., The two fish escaped. countable, plural

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
So, what can we say about English verbs? Here's a list of verbs, nice verby verbs, really typical ones:

jump, walk, ask, turn, work, move, play, pull, reach, laugh

Are they action words? Of course they are. Did you notice that this could be a list of nouns though?

She missed the jump.

I went for a walk.

That's a pretty big ask.

Give it a turn and see.

I went to work.

Each of the italicized words above is a noun. (If you doubt me, consider the characteristics we studied earlier.) But in the following cases, they're verbs.

She jumped the gap.

I walk the dog each morning.

He's asking for a 10,000 raise.

It's hard to turn the knob.

I work in Toronto.

So, how can we tell the difference between nouns and verbs? Sometimes we can't until they're in context. In form, they may be the same. In function, they are worlds apart. The list in is perfectly ambiguous. Still, verbs generally have the following properties (examples below):

- Verbs typically denote actions and states, and they don't denote physical objects.

- A word that has a past-tense form is a verb (but not all verbs have past-tense forms).

- Almost every verb has an -s form (but not all).

- Almost every verb has an -ing form (but not all).

- Almost every verb can follow the infinitive marker to.

- A verb can't be modified by an adjective phrase.

- A verb can typically be modified by an adverb phrase.

- A verb phrase must be headed by a verb (always).

- A clause must be headed by a verb phrase (always).

- A verb is often in an agreement relationship with a phrase functioning as the subject of the clause.

Let's look at some examples.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > What verbs denote
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
These are just everyday classifications, not technical ones, and other types could be added to the list.

Verbs:

actions: come, go, wave, run, swim, play, hit

mental activities: consider, wonder, decide, recall

states: be, seem, own, need, taste, matter, appear, exist

mental states: believe, know, agree, mind, like

accomplishments: win, sleep, become, remain, relax, stop, fail

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Past-tense forms
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
Verbs almost always have a past-tense form, while nouns never do. (Can you think of any verbs that lack a past-tense form? (180[t]0.9
Must, beware, and ought are the only verbs that don't have a past-tense form, as far as I know. Verbs that lack one or more forms are said to be defective.
))

Verbs:

Nouns:

There are a lot of nouns and verbs that share a history, spelling, and pronunciation, such as those in. They belong to the same [sec:family-lemma-shape-token]word family, but they are distinct words, in the same way that my brothers and I belong to the same Reynolds family but are, nevertheless, distinct people.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Other verb forms
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
Almost all verbs also have an -s form and an -ing form, along with their plain form.

run runs running

go goes going

try tries trying

make makes making

do does doing

have has having

contemplate contemplates contemplating

recondition reconditions reconditioning

Can you think of any verbs that lack these forms? (180[t]0.9
Can, may, will, shall, must, and ought are the most common verbs that lack these forms.
)

chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text: A verb phrase typically allows an adverb phrase as a modifier but not an adjective phrase. Here are some verb phrases modified by adverb phrases: run quickly, think deeply, try consistently, always be good, easily do it, and happily help. And for comparison, here are some matching noun phrases modified by adjective phrases: a quick run, deep thoughts, consistent effort, an easy goal, and the happy helper.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Agreement with subject
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
In the case of present-tense verbs, if the subject is third-person singular, then the verb typically agrees with the subject by appearing in the -s form. A third-person noun phrase is any one that is not first-person (I, me, myself, we, ours, etc.) or second person (you, yours, yourself).

She studies English.
(She is 3rd person singular, and the -s at the end of the present-tense verb studies marks the agreement.)

She studied English.
(She is 3rd person singular, but the verb is in the past tense.)

They study English.
(They is not singular.)

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Verbs and verb phrases > Auxiliary verbs
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
There is a small sub-category of verbs called the auxiliary verbs, shown in Table. They have the NICER properties (Sag et al., 2020).

Table: The NICER properties

These include the modal auxiliary verbs:

will/won't; would/wouldn't

shall/shan't; should/shouldn't

may; might/mightn't

can/can't; could/couldn't

must/mustn't

And a few others:

- be, do (for [ch:questions]interrogatives), and have (for the perfect)

- need, dare, and ought (as modal auxiliaries, though not typically in North America)

Again, these explanations use terms that I haven't yet defined. I understand that it may be frustrating, but it is inevitable. Syntax is a system, and its elements can only be defined in relation to each other. The meanings of the other words will become clear soon. When you meet them, you should cycle back and have another look at this material.

Language learners face this kind of frustration all the time. It's not helpful to say it builds character, but it is true that those who can accept this kind of temporary ambiguity tend to be more successful in the end.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Summary
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
In this chapter, along with introducing some key grammatical terms and concepts, I've tried to suggest some mental habits and dispositional attitudes that, I believe, are extremely useful for English-language teachers. These approaches to thinking about English include:

- Seeking counter-examples: This habit pushes us beyond simplistic rules and explanations and helps us to avoid telling our students things that are common knowledge and also wrong.

- Scrutinizing definitions: By examining how we define grammatical concepts, we can better explain them to learners and anticipate their questions.

- Recognizing non-commutativity in language descriptions: Understanding that A implies B doesn't mean B implies A can help us avoid common pitfalls in explanation.

- Challenging our linguistic intuitions: What seems obvious to us as expert English speakers may be opaque to learners. This awareness can help us empathize with our students' struggles.

- Distinguishing between technical and everyday meanings of terms: Such distinctions can help prevent the familiar meanings from confusing our understanding.

An important overarching concept is that language is a system. Each part relies on others, and none can be fully understood in isolation. The more we weave ideas together, the more of the whole tapestry will appear both for us as teachers and for our students.

Short answer quiz

Instructions: Answer the following questions in 2-3 sentences each.

- Why can't we simply define verbs as action words? Use examples to support your answer.

- Explain how (e.g., walk) can be either a noun or a verb. What determines its category?

- What are the forms that most lexical verbs can take? What are some exceptions?

- Explain the difference between how adjective phrases and adverb phrases function as modifiers in relation to verbs.

- What is meant by agreement with subject for verbs? When does it occur?

- What distinguishes auxiliary verbs from other verbs? Give examples of two properties.

- How do verbs differ from nouns in terms of what they can denote? Why is this distinction important?

- What is the relationship between verbs, verb phrases, and clauses?

- What are defective verbs, and which forms do the examples in the chapter lack?

Answer key

- Many nouns denote actions (e.g., action, run, walk), while some verbs don't denote actions (e.g., exist, seem). The relationship between meaning and grammatical category is more complex than simple action/non-action.

- Words like walk (N) and walk (V) belong to the same word family but are different words. The verb takes verbal inflections and heads verb phrases; the noun takes determiners and inflects for number.

- Most lexical verbs have a plain form, an -s form, and an -ing form. In fact, there are a few other forms, but we'll cover those later.

- Adverb phrases commonly function as modifiers in VPs (run quickly), but adjective phrases don't. The situation is reversed in NPs, where we have (a quick run) but not *a quickly run.

- Agreement selects between the plain and third-person singular present-tense verb forms. It also selects between were and was in the past tense.

anchor: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar > Summary
chapter_title: Some key concepts, terms, and ways of thinking about English grammar
text:
- Auxiliary verbs have the NICER properties, including the ability to form negations directly (will not) and participate in subject-auxiliary inversion (Has Lee eaten?). Regular verbs lack these properties.

- Verbs are chiefly limited to denoting actions and states, though they also denote time through tense. Nouns are not limited in this way.

- Verbs head verb phrases, which in turn head clauses. This hierarchical relationship is fundamental to English sentence structure.

- Defective verbs are those that lack one or more of the typical verb forms. The chapter gives examples like must, beware, and ought, which lack past-tense forms, and modal auxiliaries like can, may, will, shall, and must, which lack -s and -ing forms.

Discussion questions

- Discuss how formal properties (inflection, modification patterns) and meaning interact in the identification of nouns versus verbs in English. How does this understanding help us avoid oversimplified definitions like action words or person, place, or thing? Provide specific examples from the text.

- Explain how seemingly straightforward concepts like countability and plurality reveal the complex and often arbitrary nature of linguistic categories. Use examples from different languages to illustrate why these categories are inter-subjective judgments rather than objective facts.

- Analyze the relationship between verbs and auxiliary verbs in English. How do the NICER properties distinguish auxiliaries, and what implications does this have for English language teaching?

- Examine the hierarchical relationships among lexical and phrasal categories (for example, between nouns and noun phrases, or verbs, verb phrases, and clauses). How does understanding these technical relationships help teachers better analyze and explain grammatical structures?

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Introduction
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
In [ch:categories]the next chapter, I'll return to describing the lexical categories, but here, we should spend some time considering a few issues that are all connected to perceptions of what is right and wrong when it comes to English. This chapter should help you think about the concepts of standards, Standard English(es), grammaticality, and normativity, all of which are crucial for English language teachers to understand.

Why are these concepts important? Well, as teachers, we're often asked to be the arbiters of what's correct in English. But as you'll see, it's not always as straightforward as it might seem. What's standard? What's grammatical? And perhaps most importantly, what should we actually teach?

We'll start by unpacking the concept of standard and how it applies to language. Then we'll explore the notion of Standard English - or rather, Standard Englishes - and why this distinction matters. We'll dive deep into grammaticality, examining what makes something grammatical or ungrammatical, and why our intuitions about this can sometimes be misleading. Finally, we'll consider the normative aspects of language and how they impact our decisions as teachers.

By the end of this chapter, you should have a more nuanced understanding of these concepts and be better equipped to navigate the complex landscape of English language teaching. So, let's begin by considering what we mean when we say something is standard.

For most people, standard has positive connotations. Consider the words that we use together with standard, pairs like gold standard and quality standard, or standard of living and standard of care. If you have high standards or exceed them, that's a good thing. We set educational standards, environmental standards, and technology standards, all of which are supposed to make our education, environment, and technology better.

Standards, in short, are things we aspire to and Standard English (note the capital S) may sound like something to aim for. You might think it's equivalent to good English, grammatical English, or literary English. But it's not that simple, not that clear, not that straightforward to define or aim for.

You're likely old enough to know what VHS is. What you may be too young to know, though, is what Betamax is. For a time, they were competing video tape standards. By most criteria, Betamax was a higher quality product. It had a better picture and better sound. But it died so long ago that you may never have heard of it, and VHS became the standard for video cassette tapes and players (at least while people still used VCRs).

Standard doesn't mean good, and good doesn't mean standard.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Introduction
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
There are a lot of different rail gauges (Fig. ), but the standard rail gauge is 1.435 m wide. That's what they're using for a new light rail line in Mississauga, where I live. Toronto gauge, the one traditionally used by the Toronto Transit Commission (TTC), one city to the east, though, is 1.495 m wide. To be sure, each size has benefits and drawbacks, but the main benefit of standard rail gauge is that it is standard. It is the most commonly used, and so if you want new rail cars, or even used rail cars, it's much cheaper and faster to get them if you use standard gauge. That's why Mississauga chose it, despite meaning that rolling stock will not be able to interchange directly with Toronto's system. The choice was not about quality or superiority; it was about cost and availability.

Standard English is not the best form of English. Standard English is not inherently superior to other dialects. Standard English is simply the most widely accepted, the most institutionalized, the most common ground for diverse communication.

In linguistics, the word standard often serves more as a sociopolitical marker than a qualitative one. It is a product of historical, cultural, and often colonial forces that have elevated one dialect (usually that of a socially or economically dominant group) over others.

So, when we talk about Standard English, what we're really discussing is a set of conventions that have been codified and are taught in schools, used in official documents, and expected in formal settings. It is not a measure of intelligence, morality, or worth; it is simply what is most commonly and broadly expected and accepted. And it's what most folks have in mind when they say they want to learn to speak English. The word standard in Standard English is less about quality and more about ubiquity and institutional backing. It's the VHS, not necessarily the Betamax, of the English language.

Figure: Track gauge

Standards underpin our modern world, but standards are often arbitrary. A4 paper is the standard in most of the world, so A4 is better there. US Letter is the standard in North America, so US Letter is better there. Neither is inherently superior; their superiority comes from being standard.

Standard English follows this pattern. It's not inherently better; it's better because it's standard. Many Englishes could be standard, if only they were.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Introduction
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
But there are two important differences between technology standards and language standards. First, a technology standard is stipulated. Somebody planned it out and promulgated it. Language isn't like that. Second, if you don't follow a technology standard, your technology often won't work. A TTC streetcar, for instance, simply will not run on standard gauge rails. It's useless as a means of transportation. Similarly, a VHS cassette simply will not play on a Betamax player. In contrast, much of the time - perhaps most of the time - non-standard English is a perfectly workable means of communication, even with speakers of Standard English. There are dialects and varieties that you or I can't understand - or can't understand easily - but there are so many more that we can. If communication is the main function of language, then, why do we have Standard English?

One answer is that another function of language is to regulate group membership. In other words, language helps us to identify people who are like us, who belong to our group. Young people often create a sense of identity and belonging within their peer groups by developing, innovating, and using slang. For example, fire as an adjective meaning excellent gained traction among some younger speakers for a period. Those who used it that way were also saying, I'm part of this group that knows this newer meaning of fire, and because I use it with you, it means I think you are too. Of course, adults were rarely, if ever, part of the group, and they rarely, if ever, used fire that way.

Or consider clothing. Jeans are standard at most colleges and universities in Canada. Some people wear suits or dresses, which are more formal but still standard ways of dressing. Arriving topless to class might be perfectly functional if the classroom is warm, but it's not standard, and people will react to such a choice of outfit with surprise and discomfort. A snowsuit is also non-standard, as is a sari, a chef's uniform, or a man's kilt. Some people on campus will react positively and some negatively. Many will suppress any outward reaction, but everyone will recognize that these ways of dressing are non-standard.

That's kind of the way it is with non-Standard Englishes. If you use one of them, people will notice. Standard English is a social standard, and it has social implications. Usually those are negative for those who don't speak Standard English, and as language teachers, we should recognize that our students' goals are often as social as they are instrumental. They want to learn English so that they can function in society, but they want to learn Standard English so that they will be a part of it.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Standard English(es)
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
George Eliot presents the following conversation in her novel Middlemarch.

All choice of words is slang. It marks a class.

There is correct English: that is not slang.

I beg your pardon: correct English is the slang of prigs who write history and essays. And the strongest slang of all is the slang of poets.

Eliot knew the difference between correct English and Standard English. But many people don't. Unfortunately, there is no brief definition of Standard English that I can use to clarify it (more difficulties with definitions). In fact, one goal of this book is to outline a partial definition of the syntax, vocabulary, and phonology of Standard English. But I can at least make a few remarks about its dominant status.

To some degree any dialect of English is a standard among some group, but most are not called Standard English. If you try to sell a Standard gauge train to the TTC for its subway, you will not make a sale; Standard gauge is not the standard used in Toronto.

If you grew up speaking First-Nations English, then First-Nations English is likely the standard in your home community; anyone who goes to your community and speaks Standard English will mark themselves as outsiders and will be treated as such, for good or ill.

And to be clear, there isn't a single Standard English. There's Standard Canadian English, Standard British English, Standard Singapore English, etc. For the most part, these are almost indistinguishable, at least when written down, but there are small differences; the unique aspects of any of the varieties will often jump out at speakers of another. Still, they are all recognized as Standard English, or more recently as Standard Englishes.

But let me reiterate that this is not because Standard English is better, more formal, or even the most common (though it is the most common in particular communities). (For more about what it isn't, see Trudgill (1999).) Standard is not synonymous with grammatical or correct. Nor does it imply truth, logic, beauty, purity or clarity. It is not how English ought to be spoken. It is just a cluster of dialects, albeit a powerful one, and a norm that is enforced by the dominant social group of English speakers. In much of the world, that means educated speakers of English from Britain and the former British colonies where the Indigenous peoples were largely displaced (e.g., Canada), but not those where the Indigenous peoples were not (e.g., Bangladesh).

What's a shibboleth?
There is a story about the gate-keeping function of dialects in Judges, the 7th
book of the Hebrew bible, which goes like this.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Standard English(es)
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
Jephthah then called together the men of Gilead and fought against Ephraim. The Gileadites struck them down because the Ephraimites had said, You Gileadites are renegades from Ephraim and Manasseh. The Gileadites captured the fords of the Jordan leading to Ephraim, and whenever a survivor of Ephraim said, Let me cross over, the men of Gilead asked him, Are you an Ephraimite? If he replied, No, they said, All right, say Shibboleth. If he said, Sibboleth, because he could not pronounce the word correctly, they seized him and killed him at the fords of the Jordan. Forty-two thousand Ephraimites were killed at that time. -Judges 12:4 (NIV)

From this story, we get the English word shibboleth, a manner of speech distinguishing a particular group of people, often used as a means of identification or a test of belonging. The term shibboleth now signifies any type of linguistic or cultural indicator that separates insiders from outsiders. In modern usage, a shibboleth might not be a matter of life and death, but it can still significantly influence how we perceive and interact with others.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Questioning grammaticality
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
When I was first hired as an English teacher, it wasn't because of my qualifications. I had none. Nor was it because of my experience. As I said, it was my first teaching job. Even so, when I arrived in Japan, I was hired within two weeks. I assume this was because I am a native speaker of Standard English. Being white probably helped, too.

As someone who grew up speaking Standard English, I was obviously a competent user of the language, but I had little explicit grammatical knowledge. Most schools in Canada teach essentially nothing at all about English grammar. That was true when I was in school, and it remains true today. I was lucky in that I was among an early wave of French immersion students, and so I knew something about (French) grammar at least. But this was only the tiniest help to me when I tried to address my students' questions.

Why do you use the present continuous here, but the present simple there? When do we need the? Why do we ask who does she know but not usually who does know her? To all of these questions, I could say little more than, it sounds right. I had strong intuitions about what was grammatical and what was not, but I had no way of conveying those intuitions to my students in any systematic and generalizable way. I'd never even thought about what it means for something to be grammatical.

At this point, you - yes, you - already have much more explicit knowledge of English grammar than I did. In fact, you probably have more explicit knowledge about English grammar than the average college professor. If a student asked you about the determiners in I had coffee and a piece of toast for breakfast, you could tell them that coffee is a non-count noun, so it doesn't require a determiner, but, as a singular count noun, piece can't go without (see Section ). You know something about English grammar. So you're way ahead of where I was when I started out.

But you still probably haven't thought about what it means for something to be grammatical. This is what I'd like to explore here. (A fuller treatment is given in Chapter.)

The fallacy of monosemy
Before we dive in, though, just a few words about the fallacy of monosemy. If a word is monosemic, it has a single (mono-) meaning (semantics). This is the idea at play when a child asks, can I go to the toilet, and the teacher responds cruelly, I don't know, can you? The teacher acts as though can really has only one meaning, 'ability', when in fact can has multiple meanings. In Chapter 1, I mentioned technical meanings and everyday meanings. When somebody says, technically the word means, they could simply be saying that there is a technical sense of the word, but more often they're falling into the fallacy of monosemy.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Questioning grammaticality
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
We don't need to pretend that grammatical has only one true meaning, but nor does it mean just anything. How do we establish the meaning of grammatical? As the comic in Figure explains, what a word means depends on what we mean when we use it.

So, spend some time thinking about what grammatical and ungrammatical mean to you. Recall cases in which you've used them. What were you referring to? If not, consider creating some examples for each. Either way, try to think of commonalities that might generalize to other examples, especially if some borderline cases occur to you.

Figure: Is a Hotdog a Sandwich? A Definitive Study, from Existential Comics by Corey Mohler.

The concise Oxford dictionary of linguistics (Matthews, 2003) defines grammatical like this.

- Having to do with grammar [].

- Specifically of sentences, etc. which conform to the rules of a given language: e.g. I like tea is grammatical in English, while I tea like is ungrammatical. Hence grammaticality or grammaticalness is variously a property either assigned to sentences by a specific grammar, or of sentences judged to be acceptable to speakers who hypothetically 'know' its grammar.

Are we reduced to Wittgenstein's notion that language is mere use? That whatever we say is grammatical? That our speech defines our grammar? Well, yes, but that's of no help to you or your students, so I would like to consider some examples.

Let's start with an easy one.

[]Me key there happy mine the.

I hope you agree that () seems unequivocally ungrammatical. But why? No, really, why? If you feel that it is ungrammatical, what can you point to to support that intuition? If somebody claimed that this is actually grammatical, what counter-arguments could we make?

Well, we could start with key and say that, as a singular countable noun, it needs a determiner and it doesn't seem to have one (see Section ). An NP like my could function as a determiner, but me doesn't function that way, at least not in Standard English, though perhaps this is the me of a different dialect wherein me can mean my. You could look at key and ask yourself whether it's a noun or a verb. If it's a noun, then there is no verb, and if this is supposed to be a clause, it needs a VP as its head (see Section ). So, let's assume key is a verb. That makes me the subject, but we would expect I here, not me. And if key is a verb, then you expect to key something. Unfortunately, there and happy aren't somethings that can be keyed. You could say, please key mine, but mine seems too detached from key for that to work. And the is just left at the end there, in a position where it never occurs.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Questioning grammaticality
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
I could go on, but perhaps it's simpler than that. Maybe () is ungrammatical because it doesn't make sense. Could it be that grammatical language always makes sense? Could it be that something that makes sense is grammatical? (These are not the same claims.) As critical thinkers, we need to try to disprove our ideas. We could do this by creating a grammatical utterance that makes no sense and comparing it to an ungrammatical utterance. Fortunately, the hugely influential linguist Noam Chomsky has done that for us already with the famous (at least in linguistics) examples in ().

[]Colorless green ideas sleep furiously.

[*]Furiously ideas sleep green colorless.

Figure: Colorless green ideas sleep furiously.

Chomsky (2002) claims that () is grammatical and that () is ungrammatical, which matches my intuition (and yours too, I expect). It seems you don't have to make sense to be grammatical.

But Chomsky also claims that neither the sentences nor their subparts would occur in a corpus of English, and they would be equally 'remote' from English [] in any statistical model of English. He wants to say that frequency has nothing to do with it. Here, I think he errs. Fernando Pereira (2000) has shown that () is actually much more statistically likely than (). And the success of large language models like ChatGPT shows that language truly is built on statistical regularities (Kallini et al., 2024).

Next, we'll consider the converse, that all meaningful language is grammatical. Before we do, try to think of something that is ungrammatical but has a clear meaning.

Now consider ().

[]Jean Paul, very very good.

This example was heard by my friend, Geoff Pullum, in an interview on NPR. The speaker was Turkish. Her meaning, in its context, is clearly that Pope John Paul was a very, very good pope or person. But for this to be grammatical in English, we need a clause, with a subject and a head. That head should be a VP, which means we need a verb, presumably be, but another verb that licenses predicative complements, such as seem or become, would also be possible.

It seems, then, that something can be grammatical but not meaningful and meaningful but not grammatical. So meaning is neither a necessary nor sufficient condition for grammaticality.

So, here's another thought: perhaps we just go by the rules in grammar books.

[]He was a beautiful young man, a beautiful beautiful boy.

[*]The boy was beautiful beautiful.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Questioning grammaticality
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
As you can tell by the star, I think () is grammatical, but not (). I assume you agree. The rule that explains this appears in CGEL (p. 561) and was first noted, I believe, by Watt (1968). But it is not widely known, and I assume that you've never encountered it before. I also assume that the grammaticality of () and () didn't change between the time Watt wrote his article and the publication of CGEL. Given that, I guess we can set aside the idea that rules in grammar books dictate what is grammatical. Many rules are not mentioned in grammar books. In fact, many languages exist without any linguist having written a grammar for them at all. We wouldn't want to say that anything goes in those languages, just because no books have been written about them.

Now consider ().

[*]What movies do you have friends who acted in?

You'll probably agree that this is a very strange sentence, almost like an optical illusion, feeling grammatical and ungrammatical at the same time. The reason is thought to be the result of something called island constraints, a kind of rule or limitation that many linguists think might apply to all languages, though there is some debate about this. This could be the result of some processing constraint in our brains, or it could be something about how information can be structured. Fortunately, if this is true, then you'll never have to explain it to your students, because nobody would naively try to use a sentence like this.

So it seems that our processing ability may help us decide what is grammatical and what is not. With this in mind, consider which image in Figure is described by ().

[]After the trip, my suitcase sat unpacked on the floor for days.

Figure: Which suitcase is unpacked?

Many people say this is the first suitcase (the one on the left with the clothes in it) but that is incorrect. The first suitcase is packed. You put clothes in when you pack a suitcase. The second one has been unpacked; all the clothes have been removed.

Why would anybody get this wrong? The people who have thought about this question often feel that there's something tricky in the negation, the un- at the beginning of unpacked. After all, if you replaced it with packed, the meaning would be perfectly clear. So, is there some kind of glitch in the way we process language that messes with our comprehension sometimes? Maybe we can't rely on instinct to judge what is grammatical at all. In fact, this is exactly what () suggests.

[]The horse raced past the barn fell.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Questioning grammaticality
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
I expect that (), another sentence famous in linguistics, from Bever et al. (1971), seems ungrammatical to you, but you may have noticed that there is no star. What if we change raced to ridden? Now it is clearly grammatical (if it's not yet clear, it means 'the horse that was raced past the barn fell'). The problem you had with () was that you interpreted raced as the past-tense form (like rode) rather than the past-participle form (like ridden). Again, maybe our instinct isn't 100% reliable. Sometimes, we need to think things through carefully, step-by-step.

When it comes to (), it seems to me that this is perfectly grammatical (compare: I have 30 dollars). In fact, this is how ages are discussed in many languages. But even though it's grammatical, it's still the wrong way to say I'm 30 years old, at least in English. So even being grammatical isn't the same as being acceptable.

[?]I have 30 years.

North American speakers of English generally judge () to be ungrammatical, but it is the normal way to express I'll see you on the weekend in British English.

[%]I'll see you at the weekend.

This is one of the small differences among Standard Englishes I mentioned in Section, and the '!' marks it as differing between varieties of Standard English. The reason for this difference is not a grammatical one, but rather that we are operating under different cultural assumptions about the relevant metaphor for weekends: it has been claimed that North Americans see a weekend as a platform or stage on which events play out, while the British see it as a point at the end of the week. (See Section for more about metaphors and prepositions.)

[!]I ain't no quitter.

Like (), () is not wrong but rather an example of a dialectal difference. It may not be grammatical in Standard English, but it is in other varieties.

[]Me and Mia are taking the bus.

In contrast, () is fully grammatical in all dialects, but it belongs to an informal register. The more formal version would use Mia and I. This is a shibboleth for some people.

Finally, try to fix (), which I find ungrammatical.

[*]Jim and me's teacher was Geoff.

There's no good solution here. If we change it to Jim and my, then it's like 'my teacher and Jim', which is not what I mean, and a similar problem applies to me and Jim's. But *Jim and my's sounds just as ungrammatical as *Jim and me's. Could it be that we need to be ungrammatical sometimes in order to express certain simple concepts? I think that might be the case.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Questioning grammaticality > Summary
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text: Generally, things that are grammatical are meaningful and vice versa, but being meaningful is neither necessary nor sufficient for grammaticality. Many of the rules are described in grammar books, but many aren't (and often the descriptions in grammars are just wrong). Expert users of English can generally rely on our intuition to know what is grammatical, but our intuition can be fooled. Sometimes, an explanation helps us to see something we didn't before. Some grammatical things are just not what we'd say, and some things just can't be said grammatically. So, what makes grammatical grammatical?

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > A model of grammaticality
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
Okay, so we've seen that grammaticality isn't as straightforward as we might have thought. But that doesn't mean we can't come up with a useful way of thinking about it. Here's a model that might help.

At its core, grammaticality is about form-meaning pairings that work for a particular group of speakers in a particular situation. It's not universal. Figure shows just a few of those groups. To see what I mean, consider that a teen saying mommy sock to mean 'that's your sock, mom' feels very ungrammatical (and very odd).
