The same utterance by an infant doesn't evoke either of those feelings, though, because it's a good form-meaning pairing that works for a particular group of speakers in a particular situation.

Or consider the following conversation from Nishimura (2006). The text in italics is Japanese, and the translation is in single quotes.

B.C. ni iku toki ('When I went to B.C.'), hikōki de yomoo to omotte kara ('thinking I would read it on the plane'), I bought it, eh? So, it's not finished yet. (Laughs) And it's hard, 'cause me-nanka ('a person like me'), mō ('really'), hon-nanka yomu to ('when I read a book'), cover-to-cover yomanakattara ('unless I read it cover-to-cover'), if I stop dokka de ('at some point'), I forget the story.

This kind of code-switching is perfectly grammatical for any English-Japanese bilingual, but much of it seems ungrammatical for other English speakers.

Figure: Relationship among groups of English speakers (not to scale).

When it comes to the meaning, there can often be layers and layers, any of which need to match the construction used to convey them. Try it and you'll regret it has literal meaning: an instruction to try something followed by a predication that the experience will lead to regret. In everyday speech, though, it has a conditional meaning: 'if you try it, you'll regret it'. On top of those, it also may carry an implied threat: 'if you do that, I will punish you'. Here, the literal meaning of imperative try it is turned on its head and comes to mean 'don't try it'.

Sometimes old constructions pair with new meanings. That's how we ended up with the going to future-time idiom: it began literally meaning going somewhere to do something (e.g., Why am I going there? I'm going to shop.), but the fact that the activity could only happen after some period of travel time provided the opportunity for it to take on a futurate meaning. Other times grammatical constructions become less grammatical or fall out of use. Take the way we make questions and negatives in English, for instance. Shakespeare would have asked know you not? instead of don't you know?, and obviously, that's not how you and I say it.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > A model of grammaticality
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
So when do we say something's ungrammatical? A few situations come to mind. First, when the form doesn't pair with or even suggest any meaning at all, like Can the have running? Second, when there's a mismatch between what you mean and what people expect you to mean. I have 12 years is a perfectly good way of replying to the question How long do you have until retirement? but it's ungrammatical in response to the question How old are you?

Third, when you've got contradictory form-meaning pairs in the same utterance. I have two job tells us both that that number of jobs is plural and that it's singular. And finally, when there's a strongly preferred alternative form, and there's no good reason not to use it.

Take a common phrase like It's very good. Most English speakers would call this grammatical. Say It very good instead, and you'll still get your meaning across; after all, ungrammatical doesn't mean meaningless. But listeners might pause, wondering why you skipped the verb. Maybe you're quoting something? Referencing a meme? Without a clear reason for the unusual form, they're likely to judge it ungrammatical.

These judgments aren't always reliable guides. Our brains sometimes flag perfectly grammatical sentences as wrong just because they're tricky to process. Other times, we breeze right past actually ungrammatical phrases without noticing, especially in casual conversation when we're focused on meaning rather than form.

This model frames grammaticality not as following rules from grammar books but as being about using form-meaning pairs that work for your linguistic community in a given context. Grammar isn't hardwired into our brains, despite what many linguists since the 1950s have claimed. (For example, we don't have innate principles and parameters the way Chomsky (1981) claimed.) Language is fluid, shaped by its speakers. What counts as grammatical shifts across time, space, and social groups.

Most learners aim their speech at the Standard English-speaking world (Rezaei et al., 2019). Not everyone needs or wants to match these standards exactly. Many just want to communicate effectively, maybe to do business in a country like Vietnam. When students pursue Standard English, we guide them toward that goal. Fortunately, unlike rigid technical standards, language standards bend without breaking. You can diverge quite a bit while still making perfect sense.

Grammar alone doesn't determine how language works. Social expectations and values cast long shadows, quietly shaping what people consider correct. And this brings us to normativity, how social rules ripple through language, shaping both our words and our place in the world.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Normativity
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
A normative statement is one that deals not with how things are but how they ought to be, or how it is appropriate for them to be given some set of values.

Table: Normative and positive domains

Geoff Pullum (2019) makes the following normative observations:

- It is not polite to lick your knife offers a reason for not licking your knife;

- Torturing children is wrong implies a reason for not torturing children;

- Bach's music is beautiful suggests a reason for planning to attend a Bach concert;

- Inflation is too low signals to central bankers that they should lower interest rates;

- From the fact that torturing a child is immoral, it follows that we ought not to torture a child.

Are the rules of English grammar normative? And, if so, what can be learned from them about how folks ought to speak English? Pullum observes:

- Since nobody is under any obligation to speak English, in general, nothing at all follows concerning what anyone ought to do or not do.

- If we want to be taken to be speaking a particular variety of English, then we ought to follow the rules of that variety.

On the other hand, if we merely want to communicate with an English speaker, then what we ought to do is less clear.

- Perhaps we ought to aim to be comprehensible or to be able to comprehend. Grammatical accuracy does not guarantee comprehensibility and ungrammatical language can be comprehensible, as we've seen, but most comprehensible English is also grammatical.

- Similarly, perhaps we ought to be timely and fluent, but concern about being grammatical may reduce timeliness and fluency.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Normativity > Audience
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
I would like to make one last point about normativity, which is that it often presumes a population within which the norm applies (recall Figure ). If grammaticality is a combination of meaning, expectedness, and processability, for a particular group of speakers in a particular situation, then we really need to take account of not only who the speaker is but who the audience is. That audience is a key piece here, and, when it comes to learning English, the audience is often the social group of Standard English speakers.

This may not always be the case though. Some people learning English may wish to communicate with other non-native speakers, or with a specific cultural group, or perhaps even just within their own personal networks, and these groups may not be speakers of Standard English. For these folks, the norms of Standard English may be less relevant, and they may instead need to master a different set of linguistic norms, those that are relevant and meaningful within their specific target audience. But to the extent that our students wish to become speakers of Standard English, it seems clear to me that we, as language teachers, should facilitate that. And yet, even for those students, time should most likely not be spent grasping for finer and finer levels of Standard English fidelity instead of [ch:vocabulary]learning more vocabulary or [ch:fluency]increasing their processing fluency.

As noted above, unlike technology standards, which often require high fidelity, language standards are quite forgiving within a language, as long as we are. And so English-language learners can often communicate highly successfully while diverging significantly from the standards of their audience. As Vivian Cook observed, people can't be expected to conform to the norm of a group to which they do not belong, whether groups are defined by race, class, sex, or any other feature. People who speak differently from some arbitrary group are not speaking better or worse, just differently (Cook, 1999). The difficulty will be in how differently they speak, how much they wish to belong, and how much we wish to accept them.

Finally, facilitation can go two ways. The standard way is to put the burden on students to conform. That's how most English teachers see our jobs, and it's probably our most central role. But there is another way, and that is to help Standard-English speakers to accommodate, include, accept, and welcome users of other varieties of English. Needless to say, language teachers typically do much less of this - many of us none at all. But, as English-language teachers, we should at least be aware of the possibility.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Conclusion
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
Standard English has social and economic power, and your students need access to it. But is this correct? is rarely a yes-or-no question. The answer depends on the community, the register, and the purpose - and sometimes the most useful thing you can say is why a form is standard, not just that it is. That's a harder conversation than red-penning errors, but it's the conversation this chapter equips you to have.

Note: Terms are cross-referenced to relevant sections where they are discussed in detail.

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Conclusion > Box: Exercises
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
- Explain the concept of standard in the context of language. How is it different from the idea of good or correct?

- Why is the distinction between Standard English and Standard Englishes important?

- Provide an example of a shibboleth and explain how it functions as a social marker.

- What are some limitations of relying solely on intuition to determine grammaticality?

- The text argues that meaning is neither a necessary nor sufficient condition for grammaticality. Explain this claim with an example.

- What factors contribute to a form-meaning pairing being considered grammatical for a specific group of speakers in a particular situation?

- Describe a situation where an utterance might be considered ungrammatical even if it successfully conveys meaning.

- How can the concept of audience inform our understanding of grammaticality?

- What is meant by the term normative in the context of language?

- What is the role of a TESL teacher in navigating the complexities of Standard English and grammaticality for their students?

anchor: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach > Conclusion
chapter_title: Standards, Standard Englishes, and what to teach
text:
Answer key

- Standard language is a set of widely accepted and institutionalized conventions, not quality or correctness. It's more about ubiquity and institutional backing than about being good or correct.

- The distinction recognizes that Standard English is not one but a cluster of dialects with subtle variations across regions and cultures.

- A shibboleth is a linguistic feature that distinguishes a particular group of people. An example is the pronunciation of car in various English dialects, where some pronounce it with a strong r sound, while others do not. It serves as a social marker, signalling belonging to a specific group.

- Intuition can be subjective and influenced by personal dialectal background. Also, complex grammatical structures or unfamiliar expressions can lead to incorrect judgments about grammaticality.

- Colorless green ideas sleep furiously is grammatically well-formed, but it lacks meaningful content. Conversely, the utterance Go store conveys meaning but is ungrammatical in Standard English.

- A form-meaning pairing is considered grammatical when it aligns with the shared linguistic conventions, expectations, and processing capabilities of a particular group in a given situation.

- Him and me seen it already. would be considered ungrammatical in many settings, even though its meaning is clear. The context and the audience's expectations influence perceptions of grammaticality.

- Different audiences have different expectations regarding language use. An utterance that might be acceptable among close friends may be considered ungrammatical in a formal presentation.

- Normative statements are those about how language ought to be used based on established conventions or standards, rather than describing how it is actually used.

- TESL teachers need to guide students towards achieving communicative competence in Standard English while acknowledging the diversity of English dialects and fostering an understanding of language as a dynamic social phenomenon.

Discussion questions

- Discuss the historical and social factors that have contributed to the emergence and dominance of Standard English. How has its role evolved over time?

- Critically evaluate the arguments for and against teaching Standard English to learners. Consider the potential benefits and drawbacks for students in different social and professional contexts.

- Analyze the relationship between grammaticality, meaning, and comprehensibility. Provide examples to illustrate how these concepts can sometimes align and sometimes diverge.

- Explore the concept of linguistic prejudice and discuss how it can manifest in attitudes towards non-standard dialects. How can language teachers promote respect for linguistic diversity?

- Consider the future of Standard English in a globalized world. How might factors such as technology, migration, and the rise of new Englishes influence its evolution and international role?

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview
text:
the domestic labour

that purchased

the silence

that wrote every poem

that you've ever readAbby Paige, Selected Hoems

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Explain the many-to-many relationship between grammatical categories and syntactic functions

- Distinguish between lexical categories (nouns, verbs, adjectives, etc.) and phrasal categories (NPs, VPs, AdjPs, etc.)

- Identify the main syntactic functions: head, subject, modifier, determiner, complement, and object

- Apply diagnostic tests to distinguish adjectives from other lexical categories

- Recognize and analyze adjective phrases in various syntactic contexts

- Differentiate between pronouns, determinatives, and adjectives based on their grammatical properties

- Understand how definiteness works in English noun phrases

- Use category and function terminology accurately in grammatical analysis

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Introduction
text: With some understanding of the concepts of standards, Standard English(es), grammaticality, and normativity under our belts, I turn back to the ideas we started to develop in Chapter. For the reasons laid out in Chapter 2, as well as for practical reasons, the focus will be on Standard English in Chapter 3 and most of the book. Here, I'll explain how categories and syntactic functions interact (Section ) in Standard English. I'll then provide explanations of the following categories: adjectives and adjective phrases in Section, pronouns in Section, and determinatives and determinative phrases in Section.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview
text:
I'm in my front garden in late May. The garden is lush, and the peonies are particularly notable as they begin to set their buds. Soon, they'll bloom into large flowers. But a closer examination of their stems reveals that they aren't very thick or strong, despite being fairly long. In fact, these peonies grow to about three feet in height. Given the size of the blooms, which can rival that of a newborn's head, their slender stems often struggle to support the weight, and the flowers droop.

Stems play a crucial role in plants. They provide structural rigidity, ensuring the plant remains upright. In the case of these peonies, the stems might not be up to the task. But in the same garden, there's a rosebush. Its stems are sturdy and can easily support the weight of its flowers. While roses and peonies are different types (or categories) of plants, their stems serve a similar function: transporting nutrients and providing support.

But stems aren't the only plant parts with important roles. Consider the roots. While they anchor the plant into the ground, offering some structural support, their primary function is nutrient absorption. So, while roots prioritize nutrient absorption and offer some support, stems focus more on support while also aiding in nutrient transport.

In my garden, there's also a challenge with weeds, especially on the pathways. To combat this, I have spread mulch. Interestingly, it is made of wood, which was a tree's stem (or trunk and branches). In its original role, the wood provided structural support to the tree and aided in nutrient transport. Now, repurposed as mulch, its function has shifted to weed suppression and moisture retention in the garden. Different organisms might see other uses for this wood: insects might view it as a food source, while a salamander might see it as a potential home.

The garden serves as a microcosm, illustrating the multifunctionality of natural elements. We categorize plant parts into stems, roots, flowers, and leaves, and each of these categories can serve multiple functions, from structural support to nutrient transport. Similarly, in language, words belong to lexical categories: nouns, verbs, adjectives, etc. These categories serve various linguistic functions, from modification to heading a phrase or acting as a subject or determiner.

The key takeaway is the flexibility and multifunctionality inherent in both nature and language. There isn't always a one-to-one relationship between categories and functions: a single function can be realized by multiple categories, just as a single category can fulfill various roles.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Box: Check your understanding
text:
Before moving on, make sure you can answer these questions:

- In the garden metaphor, what do stems represent in language?

- Can you think of a word that serves different functions in different sentences?

- Why might the same function be performed by different categories?

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Many-to-many relationships
text:
There's no one-to-one relationship between categories and functions, and the belief that there is is akin to the [sec:fallacy-of-monosemy]fallacy of monosemy. If there were, then we would have a much easier time defining the categories. For instance, if nouns and only nouns functioned as subjects, then we could simply define a noun as any word that functions as a subject, and we wouldn't have to bother with the stuff about coming after the and a, adjective phrases as modifiers, plurals, and the rest of it (Section ). But that's not how language works.

This echoes the point that was being made in the soup sketch: no single criterion will define a category. And yet, assuming one-to-one relationships is more or less what traditional English school grammars have always tried to do. They say that an adjective is any word that modifies a noun. Here's an example from the 11th edition of the very popular The big blue book of grammar and punctuation (Straus et al., 2014).

A noun is a word or set of words for a person, place, thing, or idea (p. 1).

An adjective is a word or set of words that modifies (i.e., describes) a noun or pronoun. Adjectives may come before the word they modify (p. 15).

Examples:

That is a cute puppy.

She likes a high school senior.

Of course, this can't be right. A high school is a place (or a thing?), which should make high school a noun under the definition above, but in the example sentence, it's supposed to be an adjective. In other cases, such as the Trudeau government, Trudeau is a person but the word is, in the phrasing of The blue book of grammar and punctuation, modifying the noun government. So is Trudeau a noun or an adjective? Or is it in some kind of quantum superposition of being both at the same time? In fact, both high school and Trudeau are just nouns, full stop (check the properties in Section yourself if you doubt this).

This illustrates how we can better understand language by recognizing that the relationships between categories (like noun and adjective) and functions (like subject or modifier) are many-to-many. Traditional grammars often try to resolve this complexity with explanations like "it's a noun functioning as an adjective," but that's like saying that the woody stem of a rhododendron is "functioning as a rose stem," or that poppies' long, slender stems "function as peony stems." These stems all function as (better or worse) supports, but the only stem that "functions as a rose stem" is a rose stem. Claiming that a noun "functions as an adjective" is a category mistake: adjective is not a function.

So to be perfectly clear, almost all categories perform multiple functions, and almost all functions can be performed by more than one category. Noun phrases, being no exception, have multiple functions, and not every subject is a noun phrase. In fact, it's quite common for nouns to function as modifiers in NPs as Trudeau does in the Trudeau government.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Many-to-many relationships
text:
Make a list of other noun–noun combinations (e.g., soccer ball, faculty office), where the first is a modifier and the second is the head, and then check the following examples.

- health care, world war, law enforcement, climate change, air force, family member, video clip, credit card, breast cancer, interest rate, justice department, tea party, blood pressure, Saturday night, security council, heart attack, task force, press conference, stock market, school district, death penalty

- cell phone, phone call, call centre, centre stage, stage manager, manager position, position paper, paper towel, towel rack

- community college, college student, student loan, loan guarantee, guarantee program, program director, director level, level change

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Categories
text:
We have two fundamental types of categories: lexical categories and phrasal categories. These categories can often be identified out of context. For instance, if you see the word happy, you can categorize it as an adjective. You can look it up in a dictionary, and it will say "adjective". There is no need for any more context. In some cases, the category will be ambiguous, as we saw with verbs/nouns like run, jump, and walk in Section. It's not the case that each of these words belongs to two categories; the verb walk and the noun walk are different words, belonging to different lexical categories. It's simply the case that we may not have enough information to say which of the two words we're looking at in a particular case. It is clear, however, that walk is not an adjective, adverb, preposition, or anything other than a noun or a verb.

Functions, on the other hand, are relational. We can't ever look up a word in a dictionary and find out whether it's a modifier, a subject, a head, or a dependent. These functions can only be determined in a context by seeing how the phrases relate to those around them. A dictionary can tell you what a word is, but not what it is up to.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Categories > Box: Quick self-check
text:
Categories vs. functions: Can you give an example of:

- One word that belongs to the same category but serves different functions?

- Two different categories that can serve the same function?

(If you're stuck, think about the word run or how both nouns and pronouns can be subjects.)

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Categories > Lexical categories
text: Lexical categories contain individual words. Usually, in English, these are set off by spaces in writing, although there are some words that contain an internal space (e.g., each other). So far, we have considered nouns and verbs. The other lexical categories we will look at in this book are adjectives, pronouns (a kind of noun), determinatives, prepositions, adverbs, coordinators, and subordinators. The only lexical category we won't eventually look at is interjections. (If you'd like to learn about them, I suggest starting with the Wikipedia entry for English interjections, for which, as of this writing, I am the primary author: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/English_interjections.)

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Categories > Phrasal categories
text:
Most of the lexical categories mentioned so far have a related phrasal category, as shown in Figure. For nouns, there are noun phrases (or NPs); for verbs there are VPs; for adjectives, AdjPs, etc. There's one additional phrasal category, clause, which is headed by a VP as we saw in Section.

Figure: Some lexical and phrasal categories

As we saw in chapter 1, the everyday meaning of phrase happens to be a group of words that have a particular meaning when used together, but for our purposes, all we need is a head word. Often, this comes with dependents, but one-word phrases are also possible. This is another example of the difference between the everyday meaning and the technical definition. So, here, go to school is a VP, but so is go in please, go. We can have a long NP such as all the very interesting people who I met yesterday, but we can also have single-word NPs such as they or water.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions
text:
Syntactic functions (or just functions) are broadly split into Head and Dependent. There is only one kind of head, but we'll divide dependents into Subject, Modifier, Determiner, and Complement. We'll also include a special kind of complement called an Object. There are some other functions that I'll introduce later, but we'll stick with these for now.

Once again, the following explanations may include terms that you're not yet familiar with. As I've said, because this is a system, this is difficult to avoid, but the terms will be explained soon, at which point, you may wish to review these definitions.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Head
text:
The head is the function of the most important element in a phrase. The phrase is almost always named after the lexical category of its head (e.g., an AdjP has an adjective as its head), and every phrase has a head. Heads are often the only element in a phrase. For instance, in rain fell, the subject NP contains just the head noun rain and the head VP contains just the head verb fell.

I will often shorten Head to H.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > What counts as a dependent
text:
A dependent is any element in the structure of a phrase or clause other than the head: the best kind (NP headed by kind with two dependents); won an award (VP headed by won with one dependent); more important (AdjP headed by important with one dependent). Subjects, complements, modifiers, and determiners are all kinds of dependents.

I will often shorten Dependent to Dep.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Subject
text:
(a type of Dependent)

- The subject typically functions as the topic of the clause and is syntactically a dependent of the VP in a clause.

- It most often precedes the VP in declarative clauses (e.g., A sunny day [should be savoured]).

- In questions with subject-auxiliary inversion, the subject follows the auxiliary (e.g., Could they be right?), a structure further explored in Section.

- Semantically, the subject often denotes the agent in active sentences, but this is not universally the case, setting the stage for exceptions discussed in Section.

- Agreement with the verb in person and number is a key characteristic of the subject, particularly visible in present tense verbs (e.g., I like it vs She likes it).

- While typically an NP, subjects can also be clauses, VPs, or even preposition phrases (PPs), highlighting the flexibility of this grammatical function.

I will often shorten Subject to Subj.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Determiner
text:
(a type of Dependent)

- Determiners are found only in NPs.

- A Head noun will never have more than one determiner
(e.g., *my the class).

- Determiners are usually determinative phrases (DPs) or possessive NPs
(e.g., the, almost all, some, each, my, Brett's).

- A singular countable noun needs a determiner to form an NP
(e.g., I need a/the/each/every pen, but not *I need pen).

- Determiners come before modifiers in the NP
(e.g., the Trudeau government).

I will often shorten Determiner to Det. Determiner and modifier are syntactic functions. The -er tells you it's a function. In contrast, Determinative and adjective are lexical categories.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Modifier
text:
(a type of Dependent)

- Most heads allow one or more modifiers.

- Typical modifiers are AdjPs and AdvPs, but almost any phrase can be a modifier.

- Modifiers can often change position
(e.g., he quickly jumped the fence, he jumped the fence quickly).

- Modifiers can often be removed without drastic or unexpected changes of meaning (e.g., a nice, furry dog was sitting there a dog was sitting there).

- Similarly, modifiers can almost always be added or removed without affecting grammaticality.

- In theory, the number of modifiers is unlimited.

- Unfortunately, the boundary between complements and modifiers can be very fuzzy.

I will often shorten Modifier to Mod.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Complement
text:
(a type of Dependent)

- Complements "complete" a phrase. This meaning is reflected in the spelling complement. (Compliments, in contrast, are nice things you say about somebody.)

- Almost any kind of phrase can be a complement, though AdvP and DP complements are very rare.

- Complements usually follow their head. In contrast, modifiers can often move around (e.g., the modifier quickly can go at the beginning, middle, and end of she changed direction).

- Complements are selected by a head
(e.g., listen to the music not *listen about the music; listen selects a to PP.).

- If a phrase is indifferent about a head, it's not a complement. For example, on Monday can go with almost any verb
(e.g., eat/play/work/think/sit on Monday).

- There are zero, one, two, or sometimes three complements, but never four. (If you think of subjects as a kind of complement, then four is possible.)

- Unfortunately, the boundary between complements and modifiers can be very fuzzy.

I will often shorten Complement to Comp.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Object
text:
(a type of Complement)

- Objects are a kind of complement.

- Objects are always dependents in VPs or in PPs
(e.g., grab the bag or in the bag).

- Objects are almost always NPs.

- If there's an action, the object is usually what is acted upon
(e.g., I threw the ball).

- The object of an active sentence is usually the subject of the related passive sentence (e.g., A friend made the cake the cake was made by a friend).

- Verbs like be, become, seem, appear, feel (e.g., I feel good), look (e.g., it looks easy), etc. take complements, but not the special kind called objects.

I will often shorten Object to Obj.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Object > Box: Exercises
text:
Underline the whole subject in each quotation. If there is more than one subject, underline the first two. Ignore any further subjects.

- Undressing her was an act of recklessness, a kind of vandalism, like releasing a zoo full of animals, or blowing up a dam. xxxxxxxxxxxxxx Chabon

- The war in Zagreb began over a pack of cigarettes.Nović

- Jack put his arm out the window, waving his hat like a visiting dignitary, backed into the street, and floated away, gentling the gleaming dirigible through the shadows of arching elm trees, light dropping on it through their leaves like confetti as it made its ceremonious passage. xxxxxxxx Robinson

- A sudden warm rainstorm washes down in sweet hyphens.xxxxxxxx M. Ledger

- And as the ax bites into the wood, be comforted in the fact that the ache in your heart and the confusion in your soul means that you are still alive, still human, and still open to the beauty of the world, even though you have done nothing to deserve it.Harding

- Within seconds of that thought, the train entered Washington, where she was to come to her end more than sixty-eight years later, a mother to seven living and two dead, a grandmother to twenty-one living and three dead, a great-grandmother to twelve, a great-great grandmother to twins.P. Jones

- We were all a little drunk with spring, like the fat bees reeling from flower to flower, and a strange insurrectionary current ran among us.Wolff

- When he was dry, he believed it was alcohol he needed, but when he had a few drinks in him, he knew it was something else, possibly a woman; and when he had it all - cash, booze, and a wife - he couldn't be distracted from the great emptiness that was always falling through him and never hit the ground.Johnson

- Lizards skit like quick beige sticks.Beard

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Categories and functions: an overview > Syntactic functions > Object
text:
Answer key

- Undressing her

- The war

- Jack it

- A sudden warm rainstorm

- the ax the ache in your heart and the confusion in your soul

- the train she

- We a strange insurrectionary current

- he he

- Lizards

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases
text:
Now that you have a better understanding of categories and verbs, it's time to look at the characteristics of adjectives and adjective phrases.

Before we start, I think it's useful to restate that these characteristics are often related to teaching points. For example, the issue of nouns being countable is actually an issue that causes a great deal of confusion for students. So, with that said,

- Adjectives typically denote properties or characteristics, but not objects or actions.

- Adjectives always head adjective phrases.

- Adjective phrases typically function as modifiers in NPs (a nice day) and complements in VPs (was nice). Sometimes they also function as modifiers in AdjPs, though not in VPs or most other phrase types, and complements in PPs (for free).

- Any phrase that can function as complement to all three of be, and become, and seem is an adjective phrase.

- Any word that can be modified by all three of very, and too, and so is an adjective (or an adverb).

- Many adjectives can take -er and -est endings, but so can a number of commonly used adverbs.

- Many adjectives can be graded with more and less, and most and least, but so can many adverbs.

- Many adjectives can take an -ly ending and become adverbs.

Here are some examples.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Denotation
text:
As in Section, the terms used here are just everyday descriptions. They're not technical terms, nor are they meant to be exhaustive.

- evaluative properties: good, great, helpful, interesting

- scalar properties: big, heavy, high, old, thick

- relationship properties: Canadian, different, mutual, ninth, other

- material properties: ceramic, metallic, plastic, wooden, woolen

- state properties: amorphous, aqueous, crystalline, gaseous, viscous

- etc.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Heading adjective phrases
text:
- happy, very happy, very happy with the outcome

- nice, not nice, no nicer than the other one

- interesting, easily more interesting than mine

- slightly too big

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Adjective phrases as modifiers in noun phrases
text: - civil rights, some quite good news, national security, the whole thing, recent years, our mental health, her very best friend, a really big deal, a supremely important responsibility
subsection_title: Adjective phrases as modifiers in noun phrases

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Adjective phrases as complements to be, become, and seem
text: - is/became/seems clear, aware, available, pregnant, familiar to me, popular with the ladies, and very interested in the difference
subsection_title: Adjective phrases as complements to be, become, and seem

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Adjective phrases with very, too, and so as modifiers
text:
- very/too/so good, important, late, different, difficult, and nice

- (Many adverbs work here too: well, importantly, differently, nicely)
subsection_title: Adjective phrases with very, too, and so as modifiers

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Taking -er and -est suffixes
text:
- bigger, biggest; greater, greatest; older, oldest; lower, lowest; younger, youngest

- (A number of commonly used adverbs work here too: stay longer, leave earlier, run faster, work harder, jump higher)

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Graded with more and less, and most and least
text:
- more/less/most/least likely, expensive, favourite, important, restrictive, effective, able, possible

- (Again, adverbs like frequently, environmentally, politically, often, etc. work here too)
subsection_title: Graded with more and less, and most and least

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Take -ly to form adverbs
text: - quick quickly; real really; actual actually; probable probably; final finally; simple simply; exact exactly; certain certainly

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise
text:
Apply the properties to identify the adjectives in this excerpt from A very old man with enormous wings by Gabriel García Márquez, translated by Gregory Rabassa. If you're not sure how to do this, have a look at the worked answers that follow.

On the following day, everyone knew that a flesh-and-blood angel was held captive in Pelayo's house. Against the judgment of the wise neighbor woman, for whom angels in those times were the fugitive survivors of a celestial conspiracy, they did not have the heart to club him to death. Pelayo watched over him all afternoon from the kitchen, armed with his bailiff's club, and before going to bed he dragged him out of the mud and locked him up with the hens in the wire chicken coop. In the middle of the night, when the rain stopped, Pelayo and Elisenda were still killing crabs. A short time afterward, the child woke up without a fever and with a desire to eat. Then they felt magnanimous and decided to put the angel on a raft with fresh water and provisions for three days and leave him to his fate on the high seas. But when they went out into the courtyard with the first light of dawn, they found the whole neighborhood in front of the chicken coop having fun with the angel, without the slightest reverence, tossing him things to eat through the openings in the wire as if he weren't a supernatural creature but a circus animal.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Following
text:
On the following day everyone knew that a flesh-and-blood angel was held captive in Pelayo's house.

- Following is a modifier in the noun phrase the following day, but it is not modifiable by very, so, or too.

- There is no more/most/less/least following.

- We can't say something is the followingest.

- There is no adverb followingly.

- It can't function as the complement of become or seem
(e.g., the day became following).

Therefore, following is probably not an adjective, or at best it is a very bad example of an adjective. Since it ends in -ing, consider whether it might be a verb.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Those
text:
Against the judgement of the wise neighbor woman, for whom angels in those times were the fugitive survivors of a celestial conspiracy, they did not have the heart to club him to death.

- Those comes before the noun times, but it doesn't denote a property. Instead, it has a kind of pointing meaning.

- It is not gradable with -er, more, or less.

- It can't be modified by very.

- It can't function as complement of become or seem.

- There is no word *thosely.

So, those is probably not an adjective either.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Those > Box: Pause and reflect
text: Pattern recognition: Notice how we've now seen two words (following and those) that seem like they might be adjectives but fail the diagnostic tests. What does this tell us about the importance of using multiple tests rather than relying on just one criterion?

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Wire
text:
Pelayo watched over him all afternoon from the kitchen, armed with his bailiff's club, and before going to bed he dragged him out of the mud and locked him up with the hens in the wire chicken coop.

- Wire functions as a modifier in the noun phrase the wire chicken coop, but it denotes a physical object, not a property.

- We can say that seems like wire, but not *that seems wire.

- It has none of the other characteristics of an adjective.

- It can modify a verb (e.g., a wire wrapped guitar string).

Again, wire is probably not an adjective. Given that it denotes a physical object, consider whether it might be a noun.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Magnanimous
text:
Then they felt magnanimous and decided to put the angel on a raft with fresh water and provisions for three days and leave him to his fate on the high seas.

- Magnanimous doesn't modify a noun, but it functions as a complement in a VP headed by felt.

- It denotes a characteristic of the people (they).

- There isn't a word *magnanimouser, but we do have more or less magnanimous.

- It can be modified by very.

- There is an adverb magnanimously.

- There's nothing wrong with it seems magnanimous.

So, magnanimous seems to be an adjective.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Adjectives and their phrases > Exercise > Fresh
text:
Then they felt magnanimous and decided to put the angel on a raft with fresh water and provisions for three days and leave him to his fate on the high seas.

- Fresh is a modifier in the noun phrase fresh water.

- It denotes a scalar property.

- It can be graded (e.g., fresher, freshest, least fresh).

- Something can be very fresh.

- There is an adverb freshly.

- Something can seem fresh. And while it's kind of unusual for something to become fresh, just because things tend to go from fresh to stale and not the other way around, there's no grammatical problem with become fresh.

Clearly, fresh is an adjective.

Now, have another look at the passage with these examples in mind.

Summary and review
Fill in the gaps with noun(s), verb(s), or adjective(s).

a

zzzA word that denotes a person, place, or thing is a/an [gap].
[gap] It can typically follow the or a. A word that is countable is a/an [gap].
Almost every [gap] can follow the infinitive marker to.
A noun phrase is typically headed by a/an [gap].
An adjective phrase is always headed by a/an [gap].
[gap] phrases can perform a variety of functions, including subject and object. Any phrase that can function as a complement to be, become, and seem is
a/an [gap] phrase. Almost every [gap] has an -ing form.
[gap] commonly denote actions and states, and they do not denote physical objects.

zzzMany [gap] can take -er and -est endings, but so can many adverbs.
[gap] typically denote properties or characteristics, but not objects or actions.
[gap] phrases can typically be possessive. Typically,
[gap] can be modified by adjective phrases. Many [gap] can take an -ly ending and become adverbs. Almost every [gap] has a third-person singular -s form. A/an [gap] is often in an agreement relationship with a phrase functioning as its subject. A/an [gap] can't be modified by an adjective phrase. The head of a clause is typically a/an [gap] phrase.
[gap] can denote almost anything from objects to actions to characteristics.

zzzAny word that can be modified by very, too, and so is a/an [gap] (or an adverb).
[gap] can't typically be modified by adverb phrases. A word that has a past-tense form is a/an [gap]. Many [gap] can be gradable with less and least, and so can many adverbs.
[gap] phrases modify nouns and [gap], but not verbs. Almost any word that is plural is a/an [gap].
A [gap] phrase must be headed by a verb.

a

zzzKeep working on recalling the properties of nouns and verbs, and add adjectives to your practice.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Pronouns
text:
- Did you ever notice that we sometimes say things like, look at the baby. It's so cute. But we'd never say, look at her boyfriend. *It's so cute.

- Did you ever notice that plural they is used to refer to anything from teachers to trees but singular they is only for persons? If you doubt me, give it a try.

- Did you ever notice that in some uses, whose is used to refer to anything from children to chambers (e.g., The chasm ran through a chamber whose ceiling was lost in the darkness above), but who is only for persons?

- Did you ever notice that in some cases which can't be used for persons (e.g., *that's the guy which sold me the car)?

- Of course, everyone's aware of the masculine/feminine gender pronouns, but did you ever think about personal/non-personal gender pronouns?

Pronouns are a special kind of noun. They typically refer to another noun phrase that has come before or that comes later (e.g., in Xi saw the egg fall but caught it before it smashed, the pronoun it refers to the egg). There are only a small number of pronouns. The main ones are set out in Table.

The main pronouns in Modern English

A dummy pronoun is one which does not refer, as in There are two dummy pronouns or It's windy today. There is no semantic reason for there and it to appear in those sentences. Their role is purely grammatical, because English demands a subject in this context.

For discussion of interrogative and relative pronouns, see Sections and.

When it comes to the possessive pronouns, gender agreement can be an issue. In some languages, they agree in gender with the head noun, while in English, his, hers, and its agree with the possessor. So, for the grandmother of a man, English would use the masculine his because he is a man, while French would use the feminine sa because the grandmother is a woman.

[]It's his grandmother. [masculine] C'est sa grand-mère.[feminine]

[]It's his grandfather...[masculine] C'est son grand-père.[masculine]

[]It's her grandmother...[feminine] C'est sa grand-mère.[feminine]

[]It's her grandfather.....[feminine] C'est son grand-père.[masculine]

This can be a challenging concept to adjust to if the language you grew up speaking (L1) makes the opposite choice. Even for speakers whose L1 doesn't make any such grammatical distinction, it may be worth pointing out how English gender agreement works in such cases.

One interesting property of pronouns is that only a pronoun (but not all pronouns) can appear in a tag question, such as that's very interesting, isn't it? or there's more, isn't there?

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Determinatives and determinative phrases
text:
There is a good deal of confusion about the terms determiner and determinative. The former is a function and the latter a category. Unfortunately, some books use them the other way around. (Those using them flipped around include A comprehensive grammar of English, English and Simple English Wiktionary, and Wikipedia.) I will use determiner as a function (like modifier) and determinative as a category (like adjective). In general, a reliable dictionary for checking whether a word is a determinative is the Simple English Wiktionary, (https://simple.wiktionary.org/wiki/Category:Determiners) although, confusingly, it calls them "determiners". It will help if you can keep the following terminological reminder in mind.

- Determiner and modifier are syntactic functions. The -er tells you it's a function.

- Determinative and adjective are lexical categories.

For the most part, determinative phrases (DPs) (The term DP here refers simply to phrases headed by determinatives (e.g., some, many more, less than five). This differs from how some linguists use "DP" to refer to what we call noun phrases.) tend to be single-word phrases. Occasionally, though, a DP includes a modifier. In the following examples, the modifiers are italicized.

- more people, many days, these sandwiches, every time, least two times

The function that DPs typically perform is that of determiner. Only NPs have determiners, and a head noun may only have one determiner (the ball) or none (balls), never two or more (*the my ball). A singular countable noun typically requires a determiner to be a full NP, and without one, it is usually ungrammatical (e.g., *she passed me ball).

Determinatives also commonly function as modifiers as in I feel better, enough, good, etc.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Determinatives and determinative phrases > Determinatives vs. adjectives
text:
In traditional English grammar, there is no concept of determiner or determinative. There was a special category for the and a called articles, but all the other determinatives were just called adjectives when they preceded a noun. For instance, the word some in some people is categorized as an adjective in the Merriam-Webster dictionary, even though it has none of the other characteristics of adjectives. There are two reasonably good ways to distinguish whether a word is a determinative or an adjective.

- Most adjectives are happy to follow the, but it's quite rare for determinatives to do so.

- Most determinatives can function in the frame of the Noun (e.g., some of the people, all of the time, each of the tests, many of the changes), but adjectives never do.

And, of course, you can simply consult the list of determinatives in the Simple English Wiktionary (https://simple.wiktionary.org/wiki/Category:Determiners) or the appendix because there aren't very many. A sample is presented in Table.

Table: A sample of determinatives in Modern English

Only when used without a head noun (e.g., What person is this? but not What is this? for a person.) The demonstratives are not always impersonal (e.g., This is my friend. or Those who try win.)

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Determinatives and determinative phrases > Determinatives vs. pronouns
text: Like pronouns, determinatives can often refer to NPs that appear elsewhere in the discourse (e.g., I bought a bag of chocolate chip cookies, and I ate some on the way home). For this reason, they are often categorized as pronouns. For example, this use of the word some is categorized as a pronoun in the Merriam-Webster dictionary, even though it has none of the other characteristics of pronouns. For one thing, determinatives don't have the various forms that most pronouns have and, like adjectives, pronouns can't appear in the frame of the Noun, shown above (e.g., *she of the students or *us of the teachers).

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Definite vs. indefinite
text:
A given NP is either definite or indefinite. In English, this is usually marked by the choice of determiner (the function). Certain determinatives, such as the and this along with possessive (genitive) NPs, such as my and Brett's, mark the NP as definite. Other determinatives such as a, any, and some mark the NP as indefinite, and most NPs without a determiner are also indefinite. The main exceptions here are proper names and most pronouns, which are definite.

But there's a subtlety here. Determiner choice is a grammatical fact about the NP. Whether the speaker expects the listener to identify the referent is a semantic one. The two usually align, but not always: generic the (the lion is a noble beast) is grammatically definite but doesn't pick out a specific lion, while specific a (a certain student kept disrupting class) is grammatically indefinite but does.

Definiteness is one of those concepts that seems obvious at first blush but which, on second thought, turns out to be less clear. A useful if somewhat simplified way to think about it is found in CGEL, which says definiteness can be understood as pre-empting a question with which?

Compare, for example:

-

- Where did you park the car?

- The father of one of my students rang me up last night.

- The first person to run the mile in under four minutes was Roger Bannister.

Example (i) illustrates the frequent case where the addressee can be assumed to be familiar with the referent of the definite NP: you have been driving the car and presumably know a good deal more about it than that it is a car -what colour and make of car it is, and so on. You thus don't need to ask Which car?: you know which one I'm referring to.
(Huddleston et al., 2002)

(If you did respond to my utterance of [2i] by asking Which one?, it would indicate that there had been a breakdown in communication, that I had been mistaken in assuming that you'd be able to identify which car I was referring to.)

Definiteness can be a trouble point for learners of English because definiteness in English is always marked (by a definite determiner or by virtue of being a pronoun or a proper name), but only optionally marked in many languages. Determiner choice is also only partially conditioned by definiteness. Countability, quantity, number, proximity, and possession all factor in.

Figure: The determinatives and their semantics

From "The love of a good woman" by Alice Munro

Each NP is bracketed (there are some smaller NPs inside these, but we'll ignore them for now). If there is a phrase that functions as Det in an NP, underline it.

a

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Pronouns, determinatives, and determinative phrases > Definite vs. indefinite
text:
[These women] aren't so much older than [Kath] and [Sonje]. But [they]'ve reached [a stage in [life] that [Kath] and [Sonje] dread]. [They] turn [the whole beach] into [a platform]. [Their burdens], [their strung-out progeny and maternal poundage], [their authority], can annihilate [the bright water], [the perfect small cove with [the red-limbed arbutus trees]], [the cedars growing crookedly out of [the high rocks]]. [Kath] feels [their threat] particularly, since [she]'s [a mother] now [herself]. When [she] nurses [her baby] [she] often reads [a book], sometimes smokes [a cigarette], so as not to sink into [a sludge of [animal function]]. And [she]'s nursing so that [she] can shrink [her uterus] and flatten [her stomach], not just provide [the baby] - [Noelle] - with [precious maternal antibodies].

xxx[Kath] and [Sonje] have [their own Thermos of [coffee]] and [their extra towels, rigged up as [a shelter] for [Noelle]]. [They] have [their cigarettes] and [their books]. [Sonje] has [a book by [Howard Fast]]. [Her husband] has told [her] that, if [she] has to read [fiction], [she] should read [Fast]. [Kath] is reading [the short stories of [Katherine Mansfield]] and [the short stories of [D. H. Lawrence]]. [Sonje] has got into [the habit of putting down [[her] own book] and picking up [whichever book of [Kath's] that [Kath] is not reading at [the moment]]]. [She] limits [herself] to [one story] and then goes back to [Howard Fast].

When one label names two things

In Chapter 1, you saw that furniture refers to individual items but is grammatically non-count. There's a syntactic property called countability - takes numerals, pluralizes, requires a determiner with singulars - and a semantic property also called countability, whether the referent consists of distinct individuals. They usually agree; when they don't, confusion follows.

This pattern (one label, two overlapping categories) recurs throughout linguistics. We've just seen it with definiteness: syntactic marking by determiners and semantic identifiability usually align but can diverge. We'll meet it again with tense and time in Chapter 5, where a past-tense form can carry present meaning (I wish I knew). It shows up with aspect, gender, and more. The large overlap makes the two look like one; the mismatches then look like exceptions.

When a reference book's rule seems to have too many exceptions, check whether it's conflating a syntactic property with a semantic one. The exceptions are usually just cases where the two don't align. Seeing this is one of the most practical things analytical training gives you: you stop blaming the language for being irregular and start noticing that the description was muddled.

tableSyntax-semantics mismatches

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Noun phrases as determiners
text:
If you look back at the Munro passage, you'll notice that most of the determiners you italicized aren't determinatives. They're possessive NPs: their, her, his, Kath's. This is the other major category that fills the determiner slot.

The dependent genitive pronouns in Table - my, your, his, her, its, our, their - are all NPs. They're nouns (specifically pronouns), and they function as determiners, just as the and some do. The difference is purely categorical: the is a determinative; my is a pronoun.

Longer possessive NPs work the same way. In my mother's sister, the NP my mother's functions as a determiner in the larger NP. And my mother's has its own internal structure: the pronoun my functions as a determiner inside it. So we have an NP nested inside an NP, each with its own determiner:

- mother'ssister - my is Det in my mother's; my mother's is Det in my mother's sister

- dog'sowner - the is Det in the dog's; the dog's is Det in the dog's owner

- morning'sheadlines - this is Det in this morning's; this morning's is Det in this morning's headlines

This nesting can go further. In my uncle's dog's vet, the possessive NP my uncle's is a determiner in the NP my uncle's dog's, which is itself a determiner in my uncle's dog's vet. In principle, there's no limit, though in practice we rarely go beyond two levels. Tree diagrams for these structures appear in Figure.

Notice how this connects to the many-to-many theme from the start of the chapter. The determiner function can be filled by a DP (the, some, every) or by a possessive NP (my, Brett's, the dog's). Two different categories, one function.

anchor: Categories and functions: An overview > Summary
text: Most of this analytical detail stays behind the scenes in a language classroom. But it informs every professional judgment you make: whether a textbook's explanation will cause confusion down the road, what examples you choose, and what you say when a student asks you what an adjective is. You don't need to cover all the exceptions and edge cases from the beginning, but you shouldn't be setting traps for yourself to fall into later.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions
text:
they are the people

who live

where two rivers come together

where the people fish

where the humpback salmon spawnRita Bouvier, deeper than bone

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Identify adverbs and adverb phrases (AdvPs) and their characteristics

- Identify the various semantic types of adverbs (manner, time, degree, etc.)

- Recognize prepositions and preposition phrases (PPs) and their functions

- Apply diagnostic tests to distinguish between AdvPs and PPs

- Understand the flexible-complement view of prepositions vs. traditional analysis

- Analyze how prepositions can take various types of complements (NPs, clauses, PPs, or none)

- Recognize and understand phrasal verbs and particle movement

- Appreciate the challenges that preposition selection poses for language learners

- Apply fronting and stranding concepts in questions and relative clauses

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Introduction
text:
While traditional grammars drew seemingly clear lines between prepositions and adverbs, this apparent simplicity masked significant complexity. The rigid classifications often led to contradictions and failed to capture important distinctions in how these elements actually function in English. This can be quite confusing.

Many dictionaries and traditional grammars adhere to what we might call the "NP-complement view" of prepositions, defining them primarily as words that must precede and govern a noun phrase. This often leads to categorizing words like before or down as adverbs when they appear without a following noun phrase (I've seen him before, He fell down) or as "conjunctions" when followed by a clause (before you arrived). But this approach just creates unnecessary complexity and often contradicts the dictionaries' own examples (Reynolds, 2025).

This chapter aims to untangle this confusion, to present a clearer framework, and to establish a more consistent "flexible-complement view." This view recognizes prepositions as heads of preposition phrases (PPs) that can take various types of complements (including Object:NPs, other PPs, clauses, or even no complement at all). I argue that adopting this perspective aligns with evidence, simplifies grammar, enables robustness, and enhances pedagogy for teachers. We'll explore the characteristics and functions of adverbs and prepositions, examine the evidence for the flexible-complement view, address common misclassifications, and discuss specific challenges these categories present for language learners. My goal is to equip you with the tools to navigate this complex landscape with confidence.

Tests used in this chapter
This chapter introduces several diagnostic tests for distinguishing adverbs from prepositions:

- Test 1: modification patterns - PPs accept modifiers like right, just, straight; AdvPs accept very, too, so

- Test 2: predicative complement function - PPs can follow be, seem, remain; AdvPs typically can't

- Test 3: coordination patterns - PPs coordinate with PPs; AdvPs coordinate with AdvPs; mixed coordination is sometimes possible but often ungrammatical

- Test 4: modification within NPs - PPs can modify nouns; AdvPs generally can't (with rare exceptions)

- Particle movement test - For phrasal verbs, particles can move around NP objects but not pronoun objects

These tests provide systematic evidence for the flexible-complement view of prepositions.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Adverbs and their phrases
text: The most typical adverbs are those like happily where you begin with an adjective and end with -ly. But there are many others. These include: again, best, due, even, far, hard, just, long, maybe, never, often, pretty, quite, right, seldom, thus, and very.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Adverbs and their phrases > The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases > What they denote
text:
The meanings of adverbs as a group are quite diverse.

- manner: easily, quickly, silently

- time and frequency: already, always, often, rarely, temporarily

- degree: almost, thoroughly, very

- order: initially, last, next

- certainty: definitely, likely, probably

- domain: politically, scientifically, technically

- evaluation: fortunately, happily, well

- speech act: confidentially, frankly, honestly

- connective: furthermore, however, thus.

- etc.
subsection_title: The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Adverbs and their phrases > The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases > Morphological characteristics
text:
Many adverbs share morphological properties with adjectives:

- adjective + -ly: The following are quite common examples: exactly, recently, usually, quickly, clearly, particularly, completely.

- grade: Like adjectives, adverbs are often gradable: well, better, best; fast, faster, fastest; more skillfully, least helpfully, etc.

- modifiable by very: Also like adjectives, adverbs can often be modified by very: very well, very quickly, very often, very hard, very long, very soon, etc.
subsection_title: The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Adverbs and their phrases > The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases > Adverb phrases
text:
- function as modifier of almost any constituent, including NP, but not noun

- rarely function as complements

- rarely take complements

- are headed by an adverb

The italicized adverbs are the heads of the following adverb phrases: clearly, very, very clearly, not so well, etc.
subsection_title: The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Adverbs and their phrases > The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases > Adverb phrases > Box: Check your understanding
text:
Before moving on to functions, make sure you can answer these questions:

- What are the main semantic types of adverbs? Give one example of each.

- How can you identify the head of an adverb phrase?

- What morphological features do many adverbs share with adjectives?
subsection_title: The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Adverbs and their phrases > The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases > Adverb phrases as modifiers
text:
Most typically, AdvPs function as modifiers in a wide range of contexts:

- in VPs: She sings beautifully. He ran quickly to the store.

- in AdjPs: The movie was incredibly boring. She is almost ready.

- in other AdvPs: He runs very quickly. She sings quite beautifully.

- in clauses: Hopefully, she will arrive soon. Surprisingly, he didn't enjoy the concert.

- in PPs: He lives right in the city center. She sat just behind the curtain

- before NPs: Even the thought of it is frightening. Everyone's presence was comforting, particularly yours.

- but not in NPs: *The quickly runner won. *She bought the beautifully dress. (Payne et al. (2010) document rare exceptions such as the demand internationally for lithium.)
subsection_title: The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Adverbs and their phrases > The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases > Adverb phrases and complements
text:
It's quite unusual for AdvPs to function as complements, but they do occur. She [treated it carefully]. He [worded his response very precisely]. Wait [until later]. Before long, they were ready

Similarly, AdvPs rarely allow complements of any kind, but there are some adverbs that do: [happily for them] it had worked; [independently of his parents]; [appropriately for the situation]. In some cases, the complements are indirect: more important than that, as good as you, so bad that it's scary, too late to change.
subsection_title: The characteristics of adverbs and adverb phrases

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Adverbs and their phrases > Words that are not actually adverbs
text: Unfortunately, almost all published dictionaries and grammar books are quite confused about the distinction between adverbs and prepositions. They commonly categorize words like after, away, before, here, home, now, then, there, when, where as adverbs when they're really prepositions. The main discussion will be in Section, but for now, consider that in Standard English, typical adverbs don't allow modification by right, though some other dialects do, so that she came right quickly is easily identified as a non-Standard dialect (see Section ). In contrast, right commonly functions as a modifier in preposition phrases such as right into the hole. Notably, it also does so in phrases headed by the list above. That's just one piece of evidence that these are prepositions, not adverbs.
subsection_title: Words that are not actually adverbs

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Adverbs and their phrases > Words that are not actually adverbs > Box: Try this: progressive practice
text:
Step 1 (easy): Try modifying typical -ly adverbs with right: *right quickly, *right carefully. Notice how these sound wrong.

Step 2 (medium): Now try modifying words like before, here, down with right: right before, right here, right down. These sound natural.

Step 3 (challenging): What does this difference suggest about the categories these words belong to?
subsection_title: Words that are not actually adverbs

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases
text:
So, what are prepositions then? Here's a sample (those marked with o have related words in other lexical categories):
about, before, concerningo, during, excepto, foro, given, homeo, ino, likeo, minuso, nearo, of, pluso, qua, regardingo, since, there, under, versus, with, and yonder. For a more thorough list, see Section.

Prepositions are some of the most common words in English. Behind only the and and, of occurs roughly once every 42 words (Davies, 2024). Other extremely common prepositions are in, to, for, with, on, at, from, and by, all of which are among the 30 most frequently used words in English.

Part of the reason for this is that along with their basic meanings, they tend to have grammatical functions. For example, by indicates the agent in passive constructions, like The book was written by her. Similarly, of can denote possession, origin, or indeed any relationship, as in The sound of the waves. If we can say I gave her the note or I gave the note to her, how much meaning can to really be adding?

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > The characteristics of prepositions
text:
English prepositions have the following characteristics.

- Prepositions typically denote relations in space, relations in time, and relations in logic.

- They function as the head of a preposition phrase (PP). (Remember, our technical meaning of phrase allows for single-word phrases.)

- PPs often function as modifiers in NPs and VPs.

- PPs often function as complements in a range of phrases.

- Prepositions usually have no suffixes or prefixes.

- And they usually have only one form.

- Preposition phrases may include a Mod:AdvP, especially even, just, and almost; and distinctively right, straight, clear, and way.

- They are almost never modified by degree modifiers like very, too, and pretty.

- They most typically take Obj:NP complements.

- But they also take a variety of other complements, including PP (out of the box), Clause (before I arrived), and AdjP (for free), or no complement at all.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > The characteristics of prepositions > Denotation of prepositions
text:
Prepositions primarily denote relations. These relations can be:

- spatial: in, on, under, between

- temporal: before, after, during, until

- directional: to, from, towards, away

- causal: because, due, since,

- etc.

Often prepositions can denote different types of relations (e.g., before 6:00; before the mirror).

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > The characteristics of prepositions > Functioning as the head of a preposition phrase
text:
Every preposition phrase has a preposition at its head:

- In the park, during the movie, under the table
subsubsection_title: Functioning as the head of a preposition phrase

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > The characteristics of prepositions > Preposition phrases as modifiers and complements
text:
Preposition phrases function as modifiers in NPs and VPs:

- Modifying NPs: the book on the shelf, the man with the hat

- Modifying VPs: slept beside a tree, studies during the day

They can also function as complements:

- In VPs: She is in the garden. He talked to us. I received it from them.

- In NPs: knowledge of finance, interest in movies, desire for love.

- In AdjPs: eager for work, happy about the change, proud of you.

- In PPs: down from the cottage, due to the time, except for that.
subsubsection_title: Preposition phrases as modifiers and complements

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > The characteristics of prepositions > Form and structure of prepositions
text:
Most prepositions, like those in (), have only one form and lack prefixes or suffixes, but there are exceptions.

at, by, from, home, in, now, on, of, over, through, to, under, where, with

apart, beside, ceilingward, depending

in front, on board, so as

close, far, near

Prepositions like those in () have internal structure. For example apart is, historically a- + part. Sometimes, single prepositions are written with a space, like those in (). Despite the space, there's good reason (which I won't go into here; see (Huddleston et al., 2002)) to think of these as single words. Finally, the three prepositions in () are a little like adjectives and adverbs in that they have -er and -est forms. Again, there are good reasons to think they are prepositions, but for those, you should consult Huddleston et al. (2002).

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > The characteristics of prepositions > Modifiers in preposition phrases
text:
Preposition phrases can include modifiers, especially adverbs even, just, and almost; and distinctively right, straight, clear, and way. They also include measure phrases like two metres as modifiers, but rarely degree modifiers.

- almost to the door, even without it, just before noon.

- right in front of the house, straight up the tree, clear to the horizon, way out west.

- two hours before he arrived, a kilometre down the road.

- *very in the room, *so down the well, *too on the table.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > The characteristics of prepositions > Complements of prepositions
text:
While Obj:NP is the most common complement in PPs, they can take various complements.

- Obj:NP: on the table, with John

- Comp:VP before going to bed

- Comp:PP: out of the box, from behind the clock

- Comp:Clause: before I arrive, of whether it works or not

- Comp:AdjP: for free, as possible

- (no complement): above, home, here, now then, where

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Distinguishing preposition phrases from adverb phrases: key syntactic differences
text: The flexible-complement view analyzes words traditionally called "adverbs" (like before, down, in when used alone) or "conjunctions" (like before, after, since when followed by a clause) as prepositions. A key justification for this comes from syntax: these words, and the phrases they head, consistently pattern with undisputed prepositions/PPs, and contrast sharply with undisputed adverbs/AdvPs. Here are four diagnostic tests that illustrate this distinction:
subsection_title: Distinguishing preposition phrases from adverb phrases: key syntactic differences

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Distinguishing preposition phrases from adverb phrases: key syntactic differences > Test 1: modification patterns
text:
As noted above, PPs accept modification by a specific set of AdvPs headed by words like right, just, straight, clear, and way. Typical adverbs and AdvPs do not accept these modifiers. Contrast the acceptable PPs in () with the unacceptable AdvPs in ().

right before lunch

just inside the house

straight down the road

way up the tree

*right quickly

*just carefully

*straight locally

*way often

Conversely, typical adverbs and AdvPs readily accept degree modifiers like very, too, and so, whereas PPs generally do not.

very quickly

too carefully

so locally

*very before lunch

*too inside the house

*so down the road

Words like before, inside, down, etc. (even when used without an overt complement) pattern with prepositions in accepting modifiers like right and rejecting modifiers like very.

Learning strategybulbon
Building complexity gradually: Start by mastering the modification test (Test 1) before moving to the other tests. Each test provides a different kind of evidence, but the modification test is often the most accessible starting point for distinguishing AdvPs from PPs.
subsection_title: Distinguishing preposition phrases from adverb phrases: key syntactic differences

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Distinguishing preposition phrases from adverb phrases: key syntactic differences > Test 2: function as predicative complement
text:
PPs frequently function as predicative complements, especially after verbs like be, seem, remain. AdvPs typically can't function this way. Crucially, PPs headed by words often misclassified as adverbs pattern with other PPs here.

The meeting is at noon.

Your keys are on the table.

The power remained off.

Is he in?

*The meeting is easily.

*Your keys are quickly.

*The power remained locally.

*Is he carefully?
subsection_title: Distinguishing preposition phrases from adverb phrases: key syntactic differences

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Distinguishing preposition phrases from adverb phrases: key syntactic differences > Test 3: coordination patterns
text:
Constituents generally coordinate only with constituents of the same category performing the same function. PPs coordinate readily with other PPs, but typically not with AdvPs. Again, words like up, before, in pattern as PPs.

He looked down the street and all around. (PP + PP)
