Come before lunch or after dinner. (PP + PP)

Is she in or at lunch? (PP + PP)

She works quickly and efficiently. (AdvP + AdvP)

Speak too loudly or too softly. (AdvP + AdvP)

*He looked up and efficiently. (PP + AdvP)

*Come before lunch or quickly. (PP + AdvP)
subsection_title: Distinguishing preposition phrases from adverb phrases: key syntactic differences

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Distinguishing preposition phrases from adverb phrases: key syntactic differences > Test 4: modification within noun phrases
text:
PPs commonly function as modifiers within NPs, appearing after the head noun (postmodifiers) or occasionally before (premodifiers). AdvPs can't function as modifiers in NPs except in very special circumstances. (One example is the changes globally. See Payne et al. (2010).) Words like in, down, abroad pattern with PPs in this regard.

the door in the wall (Postmodifier)

the down escalator (Premodifier)

his trip abroad (Postmodifier)

the people inside (Postmodifier)

*the door quickly

*the carefully escalator

*his trip beautifully

*the people locally
subsection_title: Distinguishing preposition phrases from adverb phrases: key syntactic differences

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Preposition phrases and their complements
text:
A major confusion in almost all discussions of prepositions is the mistaken idea that they obligatorily take Obj:NP complements. This oversimplified view, while pedagogically convenient, is at odds with the empirical data of the language.

Consult any standard dictionary and you will likely find inconsistency in the Obj:NP dogma (Reynolds, 2025). While their definitions of preposition might suggest that prepositions exclusively take noun phrases as their complements, the very same dictionaries often provide counter-examples that defy this convention. Even the venerable Oxford English dictionary falls into this trap. It defines preposition as An indeclinable word or particle governing (and usually preceding) a noun, pronoun, etc., But under the entry for for (categorized as a preposition), you can find VI.18.a.ii. With an adjective as complement. Now chiefly in set expressions, as in to give a person up for lost, to leave a person for dead, to take for granted, etc.

But the problem goes well beyond this edge case. Remember the counterexample habit from Chapter? When faced with a rule that seems suspiciously tidy (like prepositions always take NP complements) that's precisely when you should start hunting for exceptions. Try it yourself: pick any common preposition like after, since, or until. Can you find authentic examples where they take something other than an NP? What about cases where they take nothing at all? The dictionaries themselves are littered with such counterexamples, often awkwardly explained away as the same word mysteriously belonging to three different categories. Once you start looking, the exceptions pile up so quickly that the original rule begins to look more like wishful thinking than linguistic reality.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Preposition phrases and their complements > Box: Apply your knowledge
text:
Test the rule: Before reading further, try this exercise:

- Find three examples where since takes different types of complements

- Find two examples where before appears with no complement

- How would traditional grammar classify these words?

(If you're having trouble, think about: since yesterday, since you left, I haven't seen her since.)

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Preposition phrases and their complements > The problem of words like before
text:
The word before serves as a prime example of the challenges in categorizing prepositions. Traditional grammar sometimes labels before as an adverb in contexts like I've seen it before with no complement, as a preposition in before dinner, and as a so-called "subordinating conjunction" in before he arrives, where there is a clausal complement. This tripartite classification is not only cumbersome but also conceptually problematic.

Words like before
There aren't actually that many words like before, those allowing three types of complement: Obj:NP, clause, or none. Apart from before, we have after and since. But the list of prepositions that take clausal complements is much larger. It includes: after, although, as, assuming,
because, before,
considering,
despite + that,
except + that,
for,
given,
if, in + that, in case, in order + that,
lest, notwithstanding + that, now + that, on condition, once,
provided, seeing, since, so, supposing,
though, till,
unless, until,
when, whenever, where, whereas, and while.

The main prepositions that allow no complement (at least some of the time) include:
a priori, aboard, about, above, ago, back, below, beneath, down, downstairs, downtown, east, here, home, in, indoors, inside, leftwards, next, overseas, then, there, when, where, and within.

If we accept that prepositions can take various complements, including clauses, then there's no need to posit a long list of subordinating conjunctions. We also keep the category of adverbs from becoming too unruly. Recall from Section that AdvPs rarely allow or function as complements. What then are we to make of before now if both before and now are adverbs?

The core adverbs are the -ly words like really, probably, actually, and simply. Such words almost never function as complements in be VPs. For example, you can't say *that was probably or *the concert is really. But there's no problem with that was before or the concert is now. This requires some kind of extraordinary explanation if you think that before and now are adverbs in these examples. In contrast, if you understand that they're prepositions, then everything simply follows from that and no exceptional explanation is needed.

Most English teachers subscribe to the widespread view of prepositions being words that always take Obj:NP complements, and when they're exposed to the view of prepositions I'm explaining here, they typically reject it as "difficult". If you already have one way of looking at things, it is clearly difficult to change your mind. There's no question about that. But for English-language learners, this is the simpler system. It dramatically reduces the number of "exceptions" and it's obviously easier for students to learn that after is always a preposition than it is to try and understand when it's an adverb, when it's a preposition, and when it's a "subordinating conjunction". The choice then becomes: a system easier for teachers, or a system easier for students? (It's also hardly an innovation, being at least a quarter of a millennium old (Hunter, 1784).) Teachers, of course, already know the older system, which can make our convenience feel suspiciously like principle.
subsubsection_title: The problem of words like before

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Preposition phrases and their complements > The problem of words like before > Box: Pause and reflect
text: Pedagogical implications: Consider the teaching challenge here. Which approach would be easier for your students: learning that before has three different categories depending on context, or learning that before is always of the same category and - like verbs - can take different types of complements (or none)? What are the pros and cons of each approach?
subsubsection_title: The problem of words like before

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Why prepositions challenge learners
text:
Even if grammarians seem to have had a lot of difficulty analyzing prepositions,
learning to use them might not seem so hard. Unfortunately, it turns out to be
another aspect of English that is actually quite a minefield for learners.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Why prepositions challenge learners > Selecting the right preposition in a complement
text:
One problem is the selection of heads for PP complements. For example, it may
seem obvious to you that one graduates from a university, but the meaning and
structure associated with the verb graduate has evolved over time. Originally,
graduate meant 'admit to a university degree' and took an Obj:NP denoting the
student(s) (The university graduated a large class this year). Later, graduate came
to mean 'complete a course of study at high school or college and obtain the qualification', and it licensed a from PP complement denoting the school (I graduated
from high school). In its most recent usage, graduate means 'successfully
exit an educational institution upon completion of the course of study' and takes
an Obj:NP denoting the school (I graduated high school).

For earlier speakers of English, the newer patterns would likely have sounded ungrammatical;
learners find them arbitrary; you just find them natural.

The verb listen is another that has seen changes in its complementation patterns over time. In its earlier usage, listen could take either a direct object () or
a to PP complement ().

At which I ceas't, and listen'd them a while. (Milton, 1634)

The holy man lestned well to all hir confession. (c. 1450)

Both examples are from the OED entry for listen. (https://www.oed.com/dictionary/listen_v)

In contemporary usage, a direct object is no longer possible, and a to PP complement has become essentially obligatory. But how did it change from a direct
object to a to PP? The metaphorical reasoning behind the choice of to can be seen
in the phrase give ear to. It's obvious when you know it, but otherwise, it would
be very hard to guess.

And a to PP is only the most common complement. The following either were previously possible (marked with ) or still are.

- listen of: 'hear tell of'

- listen on: equivalent to 'listen to'

- listen out for, listen after: 'be eager or make an effort to catch the sound of', 'endeavour to hear or hear of'

- listen in: 'listen to a broadcast program'

- listen up: 'listen carefully', 'pay attention'

Even with very similar meanings, there is sometimes a choice between two or more valid options: while there may be very slight nuanced differences among
them, each of the following is possible: speak about/of/on/to prepositions. But
other choices are more questionable or downright ungrammatical (e.g., *speak
above/at/in/towards/over prepositions).

I hope it's becoming clear that it's not a simple thing to guess whether a Comp:PP is possible or which preposition might head it and that it's important
that you keep this in mind when teaching complementation (See Section ). Some folks will argue that the choice of preposition is "motivated". They're not
wrong, and understanding the motivation may (or may not) lead to better recall,
but it's wrongheaded to expect students to be able to work out the motivations.

At the same time, it's useful to consider that attempts like I'm happy on my grades, while at best non-Standard, are still likely to convey the intended sense.
subsubsection_title: Selecting the right preposition in a complement

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Why prepositions challenge learners > Meanings
text:
To understand how the choice of prepositions could be motivated, we need to
understand how their meanings expand through analogy. (For a deeper dive into this idea, see Lakoff et al. (1980) and Tyler et al. (2003).) The basic meanings
are well covered by good learner dictionaries, like the Longman dictionary of
contemporary English, and teachers should refer to these regularly. For extended
meanings, consider the following chain of analogy for up.

The basic meaning of up is 'away from the ground', as in The balloon went up.
This is extended to 'a higher/larger amount or value', as in The price has gone up.
From there, we get the idea of 'conscious/functioning', presumably from amount
of function, as in The system's back up. If something is functioning, then it is
'prepared/able', as in Are you feeling up to it. And from being able to complete
something, we get the meaning of 'completion/totality', as in They ate it up.

This chain of development may not be entirely historical, but when it's laid out
that way we can see how (possibly) one might get from 'away from the ground'
to 'completion/totality'. But the motivation is not one that a typical learner or
teacher of English could be expected to work out on their own. Prepositions can
be complex and confusing.

For to, Tyler et al. (2003) give the following meanings and examples:

location: In this picture, Diana is standing to my left.

contact: Apply the soap directly to the stain for best results.

attachment: He added a fence to the garden.

event: The captain went to the boaters' rescue.

comparison: The design of this sweater is inferior to that one.

Cross-linguistic awarenessbulbon
Think about another language: How does a non-English language you know handle spatial and temporal relationships? Does it use the same words/particles for both, or different ones? Understanding these differences can help explain why preposition learning is challenging: the conceptual mappings vary across languages.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Why prepositions challenge learners > Uses
text:
Even when the meaning of a preposition is the most basic one, the use of that meaning depends on culturally-determined perspectives.

In using the prepositions, we are concerned not so much with objective measurements, i.e., with the actual dimensions of the things to which we are referring, as with how we imagine them to be at the time of speaking. (Close, 1992)

Consider at meaning 'something or someone's exact point'. We use this at for
the following:

- clock times: at 12:01 but not at January 8

- weekends in the UK: at the weekend (with the stress on end), but not in
North America, where we say on the weekend (with the stress on week)

- nights, usually without a determiner: at night, but not *at morning

- meals, also without a determiner: at dinner, but not *at rice

- ages: at the age of 27, but not *at 2023

While these patterns appear idiomatic in modern English (with preposition selection often depending on syntactic factors like determiner presence rather than clear conceptual mapping) their historical development likely reflects cognitive metaphors of time as space. The original metaphorical extension of spatial at to temporal domains suggests early English speakers conceptualized certain temporal references (clock times, nights, meals) as points. This cognitive foundation helps explain why these particular temporal expressions crystalized with at, even though modern speakers may no longer feel this metaphorical mapping.

The UK/US weekend distinction (at/on) particularly illustrates this process: while we might reconstruct the UK usage as reflecting a conceptualization of the weekend as the endpoint of a linear week, and the US usage as treating the weekend as a platform for activities, modern speakers likely use these prepositions conventionally rather than through active conceptual mapping.

Other things that are (at least sometimes) based on the metaphor of surfaces include: days (on Monday, on the 12th, on your birthday), specified weeks (on the
week of January 5th), and mornings, afternoons, and evenings of specified days
(on Monday morning, on the evening of January 5th). In contrast, we conceptualize
the following things as containers and use in for them: mornings, afternoons, and
evenings generally (in the morning), months and seasons (in January, in (the) summer), and years (in 2023). There is little rhyme or reason to these choices.
They are just what we, the community of English speakers or some part of it,
have chosen.

The same confusion applies to physical places. We can arrive at destination
cities for airline flights, transit stations and stops, places where we engage in
regular activities such as school or work, street addresses, beginnings and ends,
tops and bottoms, and levels and stages. In contrast, on gets used for streets and
transit lines, sides and borders, tops (without a determiner; contrast on top vs at
the top), trips, journeys, and vacations. Where's the logic?

We think of provinces, states, and countries as containers, and so we use in.
The same holds for destination cities for airline flights (even though we sometimes think of them as points), for individual buildings and rooms, and for doors,
windows, and mirrors.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Why prepositions challenge learners > Uses
text:
Media also gets pulled into this unsystematic system. Shows are on TV, on
the radio, and on the internet. We hear interesting ideas on podcasts. Text appears
on pages and screens, and your photos are saved on your computer or your phone.
But we see moving performances in movies, programs, and series. We learn about
the world in books, magazines, newspapers, and emails, and our images appear in
photographs and paintings.

We contact people at their phone number or email address, follow them at a
distance, and look at them at an angle. And we do things at certain rates, frequencies, and prices. Are these all clearly points? When we are under medical
care, we may need to be on medication. When we drive, our cars run on gasoline, diesel, or electricity. When we're relevant, we're on topic, and when we're
organized, we're on schedule. Are these all clearly surfaces?

Anyone learning English would struggle to find systematicity in this hodgepodge. And the list goes on and on.

Teaching tipbulbon
Managing student expectations: When students ask Why do we say on YouTube but in a movie? it's important to acknowledge that many preposition choices are simply conventional. Rather than seeking logical explanations for every usage, focus on helping students notice patterns and develop strategies for remembering common collocations.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Why prepositions challenge learners > In in English and Italian
text:
Even the core meanings of a central preposition may differ across languages.
The preposition in in English and its Italian counterpart (also in) are used extensively in each language and share a core 'container' meaning, 'enclosed or
surrounded by something'.

Physical location: in the house/in casa

Temporal context: in the morning/in mattinata

Abstract space: in linguistics/in linguistica

But English in has senses that are not shared with Italian. For instance, larger locations like cities are usually in in English but a ('at') in Italian. English also uses in for smaller vehicles like cars, where Italian would use su ('on'), as in buses or boats for English. Conversely, Italian prefers in for large vehicles that would normally attract on in English. These are issues of metaphor selection.

A more fundamental difference appears in (), showing the Italian use of in
for movement over a path to a point.

Movement towards a place: I'm going to the bank./Vado in banca.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Why prepositions challenge learners > Salience
text:
To make matters worse, prepositions aren't very salient: they tend to be small words, often pronounced without stress. As a result, in both print and speech, they get overlooked.

- Even if I spend 10 hours, I can't fix it. Even I spend 10 hours, I can't fix it. (Although this conditional if is a preposition, there is also a subordinator if used in certain interrogative clauses such as I wonder if she lives here. See Chapter.)

- That depends on your ability. That depends your ability.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Phrasal verbs
text:
One area that illustrates the difficulties of preposition choice and meaning is so-called phrasal verbs.

Phrasal verbs include examples like break up, run into, give away, take off,
put on, look up, come across, and turn down. (CGEL (Huddleston et al., 2002) eschews the term phrasal verbs in favour of prepositional verbs.) These expressions consist of a verb
combined with one or more prepositions, and together they often express a distinct meaning.

To be clear, they're often not phrases in our technical sense. Still,
they're vocabulary items, the kinds of things you can look up in a dictionary,
and the term phrasal verb is so widely used in language teaching, that I'll use
it here too.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Phrasal verbs > Characteristics of phrasal verbs
text:
- The meaning of the phrasal verb often can't be deduced from the meanings
of its individual parts.

- The preposition can often be separated from the verb, especially when the
object is a pronoun: take them off vs take off your shoes.

- Phrasal verbs can be transitive (requiring an object) or intransitive (not
requiring an object).

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Phrasal verbs > Idiomatic meanings
text:
Some phrasal verbs have meanings that are generally clear to anyone who knows
the basic meanings of the verb and the associated preposition. Consider look at
something: there's a thing, it's at some point, and you have your gaze trained
on that point. There's no clear reason why you shouldn't just say look it, but if
someone says look at it, the meaning is not hard to perceive.

On the other hand, if a plane takes off, it leaves the ground, which has very
little to do with taking. If you come across something, you find it by chance,
and you probably didn't move towards or across it. Carrying out a plan typically
involves no carrying, and, while looking up a word often involves looking, it's
hard to see how look + up becomes 'check in a reference source'.

That said, many of these phrasal verbs need not take up much of your attention
because most are actually not all that frequent. They may feel quite common, but
if you check how often they appear in a relevant collection of text, you'll see that
very few individual phrasal verbs are particularly common. For more on word
frequency generally, see Section.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Phrasal verbs > Movable particles
text:
One aspect of objects in VP is that nothing can typically come between the head verb and the object, as shown in ().

[]Quickly, they ate the cookies.

[]They quickly ate the cookies.

[*]They ate quickly the cookies.

[]They ate the cookies quickly.

This is true when the intervening constituent is a PP, as in (), where () is, if anything, worse than ().

[]In the morning, they ate the cookies.

[*]They ate in the morning the cookies.

[]They ate the cookies in the morning.

But there are quite a few phrasal verbs that either allow or require that a PP occur after the head but before the object. These PPs are usually among the most common prepositions like in, on, up, etc. and they usually appear without any complement, as in (). These PPs function as particles in the VP. The examples with the particle after the object are in (), and the examples with the particle before the object are in ().

2

[]Pick the pen up.

[]Put the pen down.

[]Get the pen out.

[]Push the pen in.

[]Pick up the pen.

[]Put down the pen.

[]Get out the pen.

[]Push in the pen.

Interestingly, when the object is a pronoun, it can't typically undergo this alternation, as shown in (). (This is another categorial test for pronouns.)

2

[]Pick it up.

[]Put it down.

[]Get it out.

[]Push it in.

[*]Pick up it.

[*]Put down it.

[*]Get out it.

[*]Push in it.

The examples in () are superficially similar to those in (,, ), but off functions as the head of the Mod:PP off your chair, in this case, and not as a particle. That's why the preposition and the NP can't be reversed, as shown in ().

2

[]Take off your jacket.

[]Take your jacket off.

[]Fall [off your chair].

[*]Fall your chair off.

Notice that you can only fall off your chair because you're on it, but you're not on your jacket, so off your jacket is not a phrase in (i).

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Phrasal verbs > Movable particles > Box: Practice consolidation
text:
Try analyzing these examples using the particle test:

- turn off the light vs. turn off the highway

- pick up the phone vs. walk up the stairs

- Can you reverse the order in each case? What does this tell you about the structure?

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Prepositions and preposition phrases > Fronting and stranding
text:
In Chapter, we'll consider interrogative clauses, and relative clauses are covered in Chapter. It's hard to clearly address preposition fronting and stranding until you have at least some idea about these two structures, but fronting and stranding are too important to be left out of this chapter entirely.

Fronting refers to the process by which elements, often PPs or their objects, are moved to the beginning of a clause, while stranding is where part of the phrase is left behind. This is commonly seen in questions and relative clauses. For example, consider ().

[]You disagree [about what]?[in situ]

[]What do you disagree [about [gap]]?[fronting with stranding]

[][About what] do you disagree [gap]?[fronting]

() is how you would ask this if you hadn't heard clearly, were asking a clarifying question, or were expressing surprise: the full about what PP appears in the VP after the head verb. In (), which is the normal way to ask the question, the NP what has been fronted and the preposition about is stranded. In (), which is an awkwardly formal way of asking the question, the whole PP is fronted.

The structure of these phrases can be difficult to grasp when just written out in text, as in this chapter. That's why it's often useful to show them in a graphic format that linguists call syntax trees or just trees. Such trees are the focus of the next chapter.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Summary
text:
The challenges described in this chapter (arbitrary collocations, metaphorical extensions, culturally determined usage) are genuine. No analytical framework makes depend on predictable or explains why we say at night but in the morning. When students ask why, the honest answer is usually convention.

But the flexible-complement view keeps your analytical framework from adding unnecessary confusion on top of genuine difficulty. Under the traditional analysis, a student looking up before in a learner dictionary encounters separate entries for preposition, conjunction, and adverb, with overlapping definitions and examples (Reynolds, 2025). Under the flexible-complement view, there's one entry: before is always a preposition that takes different complements, just like a verb. The same simplification applies to after, until, since, and dozens of other words. When you're choosing a dictionary or evaluating how a textbook handles prepositions, this is the kind of thing you can now see.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Exercises > Box: Adverbs prepositions
text:
Part A: recognition

Identify all adverbs and prepositions; label them adv / prep.

- She spoke frankly to the committee.

- The train arrived right before noon.

- They stayed abroad for three weeks.

- I left the key on the desk just inside the door.

- Is he in yet, or still out?

Part B: diagnostics

For each word, apply the named test and decide adv or prep.

- since (very-modification)

- outside (predicative-complement)

- downstairs (coordination)

- likely (modifier right)

Part C: production

- Clause with a PP modified by just.

- Sentence where before has a clausal complement.

- List of three adverbs that can't be modified by very.

- Explain the source of the error *She arrived for the station late.

anchor: Adverbs and prepositions > Exercises
text:
Answer key
2
Part A

- frankly adv; to prep

- right adv; before prep

- abroad prep; for prep

- on prep; just adv; inside prep

- in prep; out prep

Part B

- prep (∗very since)

- prep (It's outside)

- prep (downstairs and along the hall)

Sample Part C

- We waited just until the bell.

- Finish your work before you leave.

- here, now, upstairs.

- It should be She arrived at the station late. The PP functions as a complement in the VP. The verb arrive selects a PP headed by at, not by for.

sheets of soil and in the gloaming

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Distinguish between lexical and auxiliary verbs and their roles in English

- Identify and apply the NICER properties to test for auxiliary verbs

- Recognize the various forms of verbs (plain, tensed, participles)

- Understand the difference between grammatical tense and semantic time

- Analyze the English aspect system (simple, progressive, perfect, perfect-progressive)

- Explain how modal auxiliaries express modality (possibility, necessity, obligation)

- Recognize and analyze different verb complementation patterns

- Appreciate the challenges that auxiliary verbs pose for English language learners

- Apply diagnostic tests to distinguish auxiliary verbs from lexical verbs

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Introduction
text:
If you've ever tried to explain to someone why we say I'm going to the store instead of I go to the store, or why I've lived here for five years means something different from I lived here for five years, you've grappled with the complexities of verbs, tense, and aspect.

In this chapter, we'll turn to the world of English verbs, exploring their syntax and semantics, along with the systems of tense and aspect that are so associated with them. We'll start by looking at the two basic verb types: lexical and auxiliary verbs. We'll set out the special characteristics of the auxiliary verbs (the NICER properties) and then consider the morphology, the different forms verbs take.

We'll also tackle the issue of tense (distinguishing between grammatical tense and semantic time), aspect, and modality.

Finally, we'll deal with the issue of verb-phrase complementation, the various ways verbs combine with other elements to form complete VPs.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Basic verb types
text:
Figure: Syntactic subclasses of verb

When we talk about verbs in English, we're dealing with two main types: lexical verbs and auxiliary verbs. Understanding the difference between them is helpful for grasping how English constructs clauses and conveys certain types of meaning, especially since this distinction is far more significant in English than in many other languages, such as Spanish or Japanese, and because auxiliary verbs are involved in a large number of grammatical constructions (e.g., question formation negation).

Lexical verbs make up the vast majority of different English verbs. With a conservative estimate of around 10,000 verbs in English, over 99% are lexical. These words express actions (run, eat, write), states (exist, seem, like), changes (become, grow, turn), and many other situations. In the sentence The cat sleeps on the couch, sleeps is the lexical verb, even though not much action is going on.

While lexical verbs carry the weight of meaning, auxiliary verbs often bear the burden of grammar. They help form negatives. They help express aspect. They help construct questions and passive voice. No wonder they are often called helping verbs. There are very few, but they are in heavy rotation, making up about 36% of all verbs in a typical English text.

The auxiliary verbs can be further subdivided into: modal and non-modal auxiliaries. Modal auxiliaries express modality (notions like possibility, necessity, or obligation). The primary modal auxiliaries in English are will, would, can, could, may, might, shall, should, and must. (See Table for their tensed forms.) For example, in I should study more, should is the modal auxiliary expressing obligation, while study is the lexical verb.

Each individual modal auxiliary verb typically has a variety of modal meanings: can, for instance, is often associated with ability, but it also expresses other meanings, as shown in ().

She can speak three languages. (ability)

You can leave early today. (permission)

It can be quite cold here in winter. (possibility)

That can't be true! (logical conclusion)

And though should usually expresses obligation, it occasionally substitutes for conditional if as in Should I be late, please go on without me.

The primary non-modal auxiliaries are be, have, and do.

- Be is used to form the progressive aspect (I am studying) and passive voice (The book was written).

- Have forms the perfect aspect (I have studied).

- Do is used for emphasis (I do like it) and in questions (Do you like it?) and negatives (No, I don't like it).

Few languages rely on auxiliary verbs to the extent that English does. This means that learners from many backgrounds may need extra support to grasp the importance of auxiliaries in forming questions, negatives, and expressing modal meaning in English.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Basic verb types > Box: Check your understanding
text:
Before moving on to the diagnostic tests, make sure you can answer these questions:

- What's the fundamental difference between lexical and auxiliary verbs in terms of meaning vs. grammar?

- Can you give examples of modal vs. non-modal auxiliary verbs?

- Why might auxiliary verbs be particularly challenging for English language learners?

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Basic verb types
text: The difference between auxiliaries and lexical verbs becomes clearer when we examine the NICER properties that set auxiliary verbs apart.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > The NICER properties of auxiliary verbs
section_title: The NICER properties of auxiliary verbs
text:
As mentioned in Section, auxiliary verbs in English have some special properties that set them apart from lexical verbs. These properties are sometimes referred to as the NICER properties, an acronym that stands for Negation, Inversion, Contraction, Ellipsis, and Rebuttal. Let's look at each of these in turn.

Table: The NICER properties of auxiliary verbs.

Negation in English is typically formed by adding not after an auxiliary verb. Lexical verbs, on the other hand, require the auxiliary do to form negatives. This is called do-support:

Lee will not eat apples.

*Lee eats not apples. (This would have been fine even as recently as Early Modern English (the language of Shakespeare), but not today.)

Lee does not eat apples.

Inversion describes constructions where the auxiliary verb comes before the subject, such as certain types of questions. Again, lexical verbs need do for this:

Has Lee eaten apples?

*Eats Lee apples?

Does Lee eat apples?

Inversion for question formation is not a feature shared by a wide range of languages. English learners commonly omit do in negation and inversion, likely because it contributes only syntactically, not semantically. For example, they might say I not like apples or He eat apples? without do-support.

"Contraction" allows not to be shortened to -n't and suffixed to most auxiliary verbs. (The scare quotes are because there's a difference between a true contraction, like 's for is, which can go basically anywhere is can go and -n't, which is a true suffix (see Chapter )). This isn't possible with lexical verbs:

didn't, shouldn't, isn't

*eatn't, *gon't, *maken't

Ellipsis permits a verb phrase to be understood from the context instead of appearing overtly:

Lee was eating apples, and Kim was eating them too.

*Lee kept eating apples, and Kim kept eating them too.

Rebuttal uses auxiliary verbs along with so, too, or indeed, to contradict a previous negative statement:

A: We shouldn't eat apples.

B: We should SO.

A: We didn't try to eat apples.

B: *We tried SO.

The NICER properties are not just helpful diagnostics, they actually identify many of the things about auxiliary verbs that make them challenging for our students. For example, they explain why we say Don't you like it? instead of *You no like it?, or why you might utter the assertion I do like it.

In the next section, we'll look at the various forms that verbs can take in English, which will set us up to discuss tense and aspect.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > The NICER properties of auxiliary verbs > Box: Exercise: basic verb types
section_title: The NICER properties of auxiliary verbs
text:
Identify whether the italicized verbs in the following sentences are lexical or auxiliary. If auxiliary, specify whether they are modal or non-modal. Remember that some auxiliary verbs have a matching lexical verb.

- The cat sleeps on the couch.

- I will attend the meeting tomorrow.

- She has a test tomorrow.

- She has finished her homework.

- Did she do her homework?

- They are planning a surprise party.

- It's at my house.

- It is not.

- You must complete the assignment by Friday.

- I don't think I can.

For each of the following sentences, apply one of the NICER properties (Negation, Inversion, Contraction, Ellipsis, or Rebuttal) to create a new, grammatically correct sentence. Identify which property you used.

- She can swim very well.

- They have finished the project.

- We are having lunch.

- You should try it.

- Do you study grammar?

Example: He's Cambodian. He isn't Cambodian. (Contraction)

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Verb forms
text:
English verbs, whether lexical or auxiliary, have several different forms. As a proficient English speaker, you already know their syntax, morphology, and spelling, but, as an English teacher, being able to identify them is useful for understanding and explaining clause structure and the English tense and aspect system.

The plain form is the simplest form of the verb. It's the "dictionary form".

arrive, be, come, do, eat, fall, go, have, interrupt

Table: Verb Forms: Plain, Tensed, and Participles

The plain present form is identical to the plain form for all verbs (see Table ) except be, which has an unusual number of present forms: am, is, and are.

arrive, come, do, eat, fall, go, have, interrupt

The -s form, or the third-person singular present form, adds -es to the plain form, with a few irregular forms, as in ().

arrives, is, comes, does, eats, falls, goes, has, interrupts

The past form is used for the simple past tense. Regular verbs add -ed, but many common verbs have irregular past forms:

arrived, was/were, came, did, ate, fell, went, had, interrupted

The -ing form, also known as the present participle, (CGEL uses the term gerund-participle for this form.) is used to form the progressive aspect, among other things:

arriving, being, coming, doing, eating, falling, going, having, interrupting

What about gerunds?

You may have heard about gerunds (verbs in the -ing form that function as nouns) as in the italicized phrases in ( and ).

Merely looking can make a difference.

I actually enjoy making dinner after a long day,

The problem is that the idea of a verb functioning as a noun is as odd as that of a goldfish functioning as a dog; while both species may function as pets, their fundamental natures remain distinct. Similarly, subject and complement are functions, but noun is what a word intrinsically is. So, are looking and making fundamentally nouns or verbs?

In considering this question, you should return to the characteristics of nouns and verbs you encountered in Sections and. If the italicized phrases in ( and ) were NPs, then we might expect them to allow plural forms and AdjP modifiers. They don't. Instead, they allow AdvP modifiers as in merely looking and license objects as in making dinner. These are the characteristics of VPs.

That's not to say that there aren't -ing nouns, but they're not so common. They show up in examples like ( and ).

The opening of the new store attracted a large crowd.

The city funded the building of the bridge.

In such cases, the -ing words are true nouns:

- They can be pluralized:
The openings of the new stores were scheduled for next month.

- They can be modified by adjectives:
The grand opening of the new store was a success.

- They require a preposition phrase with of to introduce their complement: The building of the bridge, not *The building the bridge.

These characteristics align with what we expect of nouns.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Verb forms
text:
So what to do with the term gerund? Personally, I don't find it useful for discussing the English of today. Instead, I would simply say that the subject of () is a VP, as is the complement of enjoy in (). (CGEL would call these clauses, not VPs.)

The past participle has various grammatical roles, including forming the perfect aspect and passive constructions. For regular verbs, it's identical to the past form (sometimes called the preterite), but many irregular verbs have distinct past participles:

arrived, been, come, done, eaten, fallen, gone, had, interrupted

Most lexical verbs have all six of these forms, but modal auxiliaries are defective: they lack the plain form and participles.

can/could, will/would, shall/should, may/might, must/-

The auxiliary verbs, modal or not, also have negative and contracted forms.

Table: Tensed auxiliary verb forms

The non-modal auxiliaries (do, have, and be) also have plain, present-participle, and past participle forms. It's also worth noting that these three verbs also have lexical-verb counterparts. In each case, we know they're not modal because the NICER properties don't apply.

Don't be late.

I have a cat.

I do my homework every day.

In (), we can't have *Be not late or even *ben't. In (), we have the verb of belonging, not the one that forms the perfect aspect. We don't ask Have you a cat? or say I haven't a cat (at least not in most present-day North American dialects). And if we want to ask about doing your homework, we need an auxiliary do (italicized) to go with the lexical do: Do you do your homework?

The verb be is highly irregular, with more forms than any other English verb:

be, am, is, are, was, were, being, been

isn't, aren't, wasn't, weren't

'm, 's, 're

The various verb forms combine with auxiliaries to create the tense and aspect system. For instance, the present perfect uses have + the past-participle form: (In (Huddleston et al., 2002), the perfect is a secondary past tense, but I don't find that analysis helpful here, and so I don't follow it.)

I have eaten.

She has gone.

The past progressive combines the past of be with the -ing form:

I was reading.

They were sleeping.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Tense across languages
text:
Imagine for a moment that you're learning a language where verbs don't change to show when something happened. No -ed endings for past tense, no will. How would you talk about time? It might seem impossible, but for speakers of many languages, this is perfectly normal.

In Mandarin Chinese, for instance, the verb form stays the same regardless of when the action occurs:

Wǒ chī píngguǒ. (I eat apple)

Example () could mean 'I eat apples', 'I ate an apple', 'I'm eating an apple', or 'I will eat an apple', depending on the context. Time is often indicated by other words like the equivalent of tomorrow or before, or it's left to context.

As English speakers, we might find this mind-boggling. But before we get too comfortable with our own tense system, consider the Yimas language of Papua New Guinea. It has not two, not three, but seven distinct tenses: (Some Yimas forms encode both tense and aspect, making them better analyzed as tense-aspect amalgams rather than pure tenses.)

wa-ntut more than a few days ago'

wa-kiantut a few days ago'

wa-nan yesterday'

wa-t today'

wa-n now'

wa-wat go'

wa-kiak go tomorrow'

wa-kt go after tomorrow'

These examples highlight a crucial point: the way we conceptualize and express time is not universal. It's just the way each speech community has come to express time, and speakers of other languages have often arrived at different conventions.

So, given this diversity, how many tenses would you guess English has? Three? Sixteen? In fact, English has

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Tense across languages > Box: Exercise: comparing tense and aspect across languages
text:
Often the elements that seem very important in one language are simply left out in another. English speakers learning French often want to translate the form directly, and French has a form for that: I'm doing it can be translated as Je suis en train de le faire. But even though you can translate it like that, usually you wouldn't. You'd just say je le fais. French speakers generally just don't care about the distinction between I do it and I'm doing it. They don't find it relevant.

Conversely, some languages regularly mark distinctions that English speakers might not consider. For instance, in Quechua, speakers must indicate how they know what they're saying: whether they witnessed it themselves, heard it from someone else, or inferred it from evidence. This evidential marking can feel unusual for English speakers, who may not typically specify their information source unless it's directly relevant. In Quechua, however, saying something like the llama escaped without indicating the knowledge source (e.g., sight, hearsay, or inference) would be incomplete or even misleading.

Reflect on the following:

- If you are proficient in another language, consider how it handles time and duration. Does it distinguish I do from I'm doing, for instance? And if it can, does it do so as much as English?
box_title: Exercise: comparing tense and aspect across languages

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Tense and time
text: But, first, we need to make a crucial distinction: tense is not the same as time. Tense is a grammatical category, typically marked on the verb. ("Typically" because Japanese, for example, has a class of adjectives that conjugate for tense.) Time, on the other hand, is a semantic concept: it's about when things happen. It refers to something that physicists are concerned with, something that can be measured. We made this same move with definiteness in Chapter 3 (see the box on p. ): separating the grammatical category from the semantic concept it usually, but not always, tracks.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Tense and time > Box: Quick self-check
text:
Tense vs. time: Before continuing, make sure you understand this crucial distinction:

- Can you explain the difference between grammatical tense and semantic time?

- Why might this distinction be important for English language teaching?

- What would you expect: more tenses or more ways to express time?

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Tense and time
text:
So, if tense is simply a grammatical category, albeit one that generally has a time-based semantics, how many tenses does English actually have?

Only two: past and non-past (which we usually call "present tense"). Don't believe me? Let's look at some examples:

I walked to school yesterday. (past tense)

I would walk to school yesterday. (past tense)

I walk to school every day. (present tense)

I'm walking to school right now. (present tense)

I'm going to walk to school tomorrow. (present tense)

I'll walk to school tomorrow. (present tense)

Notice anything strange? In (c-f) the verbs are all present tense walk, am, and will, even though they're talking about different times. The only past-tense forms are in (a b), where we use walked and would.

"But wait," I hear you say, "Isn't I'll walk" in (f) the future tense? Not quite. Will is actually a modal auxiliary verb in the present tense. It expresses a high degree of certainty about an event, but it's not a tense in itself. It's not even necessarily about future time.

Consider the situation where your friend is coming to visit you. You're busy right now and haven't checked your phone, but the plane was supposed to arrive an hour ago. You have no reason to believe anything different, so, with a high degree of certainty you can say she'll have landed an hour ago. Notice that you use will, but an hour ago is not the future.

This might seem like a technicality, but it has real implications for how we teach and learn English. For one thing, it explains why English has so many ways of talking about the future:

I'll see you tomorrow. (will + infinitival)

I'm seeing the doctor next week. (present progressive)

My flight leaves at 6pm. (simple present)

I'm going to visit my parents this weekend. (be going to + infinitival)

Each of (-) is grammatically present tense, but, semantically, they're all talking about future time. There's no single English "future tense" to learn; instead, there's a whole array of present-tense constructions that can refer to future time.

But it's actually not limited to the present tense. Remember how we said tense is about grammar, not meaning? Well, sometimes English uses past tense forms to talk about present or future time:

I wish I knew the answer.(present time, past tense form)

If I won the lottery, I'd buy a house. (future possibility, past tense form)

In ( and ), the past tense isn't about time at all - it's expressing something about the speaker's perception of likelihood or reality.

As teachers, we often find ourselves grappling with how to explain these subtleties to our students. Do we stick with the familiar but inaccurate past, present, future tense model because it seems logical? Or do we present the simpler yet somewhat less intuitive facts of tense in English?

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Tense and time
text:
The two-tense analysis isn't something you teach instead of the familiar model - it's the framework that makes the familiar model legible. Textbooks that list twelve or sixteen tenses are mixing up grammatical tense with semantic time and aspect, and the result is a system that contradicts itself at every turn. With the two-tense analysis, you can see exactly where those contradictions arise and evaluate whether a textbook's presentation will help or confuse.

Whether you teach it directly depends on your professional context, not your students' level. If you're in a program where everyone understands tense, teach it correctly. If the only way to teach it correctly is to contradict your fellow faculty, that's no longer a question about pedagogy.

A general difficulty that this brings up is that we want - even expect - there to be a one-to-one form-meaning relationship. But we've already seen that the situation is often quite the opposite: it's not only adjective phrases that function as modifiers in NPs; prepositions take a wide range of complements, not just NP objects; and the modal auxiliary verb can isn't just for ability. So too do we find that neither tense is limited to a single meaning. In fact, as we will see later, the time meaning of tense has been almost entirely replaced in the modal auxiliary verbs with other meanings.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Tense and time > Box: Exercise: tense and time
text:
For each italicized string, identify the grammatical tense (past or present) and the semantic time (past, present, or future) being expressed. Explain any discrepancies between grammatical tense and semantic time.

- I wish I knew the answer.

- The train leaves at 5 PM tomorrow.

- If I won the lottery, I would buy a house.

- She has lived here for ten years.

- We are meeting the client next week.

E.g., I'm going to visit my dad this weekend. present tense, future time

(Am is present tense, but the sentence refers to a future visit. It's also about a present plan, though.)

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Aspect
text: Tense locates a situation relative to the present. Aspect is about something else: the temporal perspective we take on that situation. But what does that really mean?

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Aspect > The progressive aspect
text:
Let's start with a seemingly simple question: what's the difference between these two sentences?

I live in Mississauga. (simple aspect)

I'm living in Mississauga. (progressive aspect)

At first glance, these sentences might appear identical: both state my city of residence and both are factually correct. But there's a subtle distinction: () suggests an indifference to the duration of the situation, while () asserts the relevance of its temporariness. This difference in perspective is illustrated in Figure.

Figure: Comparison of the simple and progressive aspects for I live in Mississauga on a timeline.

Anyone might say () in the simple aspect, but only someone like an international student attending University of Toronto would choose the progressive aspect of (). While all situations are ultimately ephemeral, the progressive aspect, formed with expresses relevance not just to what continues but to its conclusion. The simple aspect, in contrast, draws no special attention to endings.

To illustrate this shift in viewpoint, consider the difference between paddling a river that flows into the sea vs paddling one that comes to a dam. It's the same body of water either way, but in the first, you're aware only of the water, while in the second, the dam requires your attention.

Unlike these paddlers, a speaker typically has a choice of how to portray the situation. The selection of one aspect or the other invites the audience to consider the situation from a particular vantage point, with neither being grammatically better.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Aspect > The progressive aspect > States often resist the progressive aspect
text:
Sometimes, though, one perspective really does seem wrong to certain groups of speakers. Consider, for instance, ().

I know that feeling.

*I'm knowing that feeling.

The progressive in (b) implies that an endpoint to this knowledge is relevant, a perspective that feels unnatural. For many speakers of English, drawing attention to the potential cessation of knowing is odd, not because knowledge can't end - people do forget - but because it's so clearly not relevant. The simple aspect, by contrast, allows us to focus on the current state of knowing without unnecessary implications about its duration. (b is, nevertheless, quite common in South Asian English, for example.)

The same applies to other verbs. Consider the stative examples in (). The (a) sentences with the simple aspect are fine, but the (b) examples in the progressive are ungrammatical.

i. Birds have feathers.
ii. Earth is a planet.
iii. Honey tastes sweet.

i. *Birds are having feathers.
ii. *Earth is being a planet.
iii. *Honey's tasting sweet.

The end of birds having feathers, of Earth being a planet, or of honey tasting sweet might be relevant in some peculiar context, but it's scarcely conceivable what that context might be. By asking us to take an irrelevant perspective with these stative verbs, the progressive aspect creates a linguistic mismatch and registers as ungrammatical. If Earth is only being a planet for the time being, we have probably wandered into a different textbook.

And yet, sometimes it works, as in ().

We're having dinner.

He's being grumpy.

It's tasting sweeter.

In these cases, the progressive aspect fits because the impending end of the state matters. I can call you back when the meal is over, it would be better to talk to him when he's no longer grumpy, and presumably, the honey will soon return to its usual taste once the effect of whatever changed it has worn off. These examples demonstrate that the progressive aspect isn't inherently ungrammatical with stative verbs. It's just not usually a good fit.
subsubsection_title: States often resist the progressive aspect

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Aspect > The progressive aspect > Progressive aspect for limited repetition
text:
While most actions take time, others are instantaneous: the light flashed, she sneezed, it popped, he blinked, the show ended. In cases like these, the progressive aspect can still be used. But rather than inviting us to consider the end of a single situation, it asserts the relevance of the end of a series of events. I'm falling may be a long, drawn-out descent, but She's sneezing doesn't imply a super slow-mo spit-spraying expulsion of air. Instead it presents a set of sternutations for which an end is in sight.

This interpretation of the progressive aspect as indicating a limited series isn't restricted to momentary events. I'm going to the gym regularly is the claim of a person who's not sure how long they can keep up the commitment or has a limited-term membership, as in Figure.

Figure: Timeline for the progressive aspect in I'm going to the gym regularly.

Another illustrative example of this phenomenon is I'm eating at that new restaurant. While eating a meal is not an instantaneous action, the use of the progressive aspect here doesn't necessarily imply that the speaker is currently in the middle of a meal at the restaurant. Instead, it suggests a series of visits until something new comes along.

In the gym example, the use of regularly makes the repeated-temporary-action interpretation the only possible meaning of the progressive. The restaurant example, though, is ambiguous. If said outside the restaurant, then it implies serial patronage. If inside, perhaps it refers to a meal that will end before some other relevant event.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Aspect > The progressive aspect > The progressive for plans
text:
The progressive aspect has yet more uses. It can also express future plans, as it does in ().

The Smiths are having dinner with us this week.

This doesn't imply an ongoing, extended feast. Rather, it encapsulates the planning and anticipation of the event. This usage presupposes that the dinner has been arranged; the progressive aspect wouldn't be appropriate if the idea had just occurred and the Smiths hadn't been invited yet. The progressive here spans from the moment of agreement (the start) to the anticipated conclusion of the dinner (the end).

Figure: Representation of the "planned future" use of the progressive aspect for We're having dinner with the Smiths next week.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Aspect > The perfect aspect
text:
While the progressive aspect focuses on the ongoing nature of a situation, English has another aspect that expresses a different temporal perspective: the perfect. The perfect aspect, formed with a form of have + past participle, asserts the relevance of a previous situation (or action, etc) to a later time.

The reference time can be in the present, past, or even in the future, but let's start with the present perfect. This uses the present tense and has "now" as a reference time. Consider ().

I've lived in Mississauga for five years.

In its most salient sense, this sentence conveys a situation that began in the past and continues to be true in the present. It stretches back for five years and remains relevant now. To appreciate the nuance of the perfect aspect, let's compare it to the simple past:

I lived in Mississauga for five years.

In (), I'm talking about a situation fully in the past. I no longer live there. The cases of the present-perfect () and the simple past () are contrasted in Figure. Non now reference times are shown in Figures -. In each case, the perfect aspect connects two time points: the time of the situation itself and the subsequent reference time to which it's relevant. This allows speakers to express complex temporal relationships and emphasize the significance of past (or completed future) situations to other points in time.

Figure: Comparison of the present perfect and simple past for I lived in Mississauga for five years. The reference point is "now".

Figure: Representation of the past perfect: I'd lived there for five years. The reference point is before "now", and the situation is fully before that point.

Figure: Representation of the futurate perfect: I'll have lived there for five years. The reference point is after "now" and the event is before that point. Note that now could be anywhere before the end of the blue line.

Figure: Representation of the timeless perfect: To have lived there for five years is quite an experience. The reference point is any time.

The perfect aspect can sometimes lead to ambiguity. The situation must begin prior to the reference time, but whether or not it is complete at the reference time is open to interpretation. As a result, the red line in Figure representing the simple past and the blue line immediately above it may refer to exactly the same period of time, just from different perspectives. The ambiguity can usually be resolved from the context, or from some modifier such as already (completed).

Figure: Comparison of the present perfect and simple past for two periods of residence. The reference point is "now".

With these aspectual distinctions in hand, we can now put the whole system together: two tenses, three aspects, and the combinations they produce.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Aspect > The perfect aspect > Box: Exercise: aspect
text:
Identify the aspect (simple, progressive, or perfect) in each sentence and explain the temporal perspective it conveys.

- I've worked here for ten years.

- She was reading when the phone rang.

- We play tennis every Saturday.

- They will have completed the course by June.

- The sun is setting earlier these days.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > The English tense/aspect system
text:
If you've ever tried to create a chart of all the possible English verb forms, you might have ended up with something that looks more like a complex subway map than a neat grammatical table. But it can actually be made quite simple if you are willing to let go of the traditional analysis of tenses.

First, let's recap: we have the past and non-past (present) tenses, along with the simple, perfect, and progressive aspects. If we include the non-tensed infinitival forms, the result is Table.

Table: The tense/aspect system

Take the present perfect progressive example above, for instance. This construction conveys that:

- My reference point is now. (present)

- My efforts started before this point. (perfect)

- I think of them as being relevant to this point. (perfect)

- I think of them as being time-limited. (progressive)

That's a lot of information packed into one construction!

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > The English tense/aspect system > Box: Practice consolidation
text:
Aspect analysis: Try breaking down these examples using the framework above:

- She had been studying What does each element contribute?

- They will have finished How do tense and aspect combine here?

- I am working What aspect information is conveyed?

Reflection: How does understanding the layered meanings in aspect help explain why these constructions are challenging for learners?

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > The English tense/aspect system
text:
No progressive perfect

The perfect progressive (e.g., I've been trying) is possible, but the progressive perfect (e.g., *I'm having tried) isn't. This is because have in the perfect doesn't describe a temporary situation. Instead, it marks the relevance of a past situation to the present. Using it with the progressive would oddly suggest an end to this relevance, rather than to the situation itself (see Section ).

Like the lexical verbs, the modals also have present and past-tense forms. (See Table ). In their case, though, tense has become almost entirely unmoored from time semantics. Instead, they typically express different degrees of likelihood, politeness, or hypotheticality. If we were to tie these things together, we might say that the past tense modals express remoteness.

Table: Present and past-tense forms of modal auxiliary verbs

Cross-Linguistic Awarenessbulbon
Think about another language you speak: How does this other language express modality (possibility, necessity, obligation)? Does it use auxiliary verbs like English, or different grammatical strategies? Understanding these differences can help explain why modal auxiliaries are often challenging for English language learners to master.

For instance, consider the difference between the two tenses in (). Here, past tense doesn't mean past time but rather a more remote chance of success.

It'll work. (high likelihood)

It should work. (lower likelihood)

The same concept applies to conditionality. In (), the shift from will to would doesn't necessarily indicate a shift in time, but rather a shift in the speaker's level of commitment to the project.

I'll help you. (unconditional)

I would help you. (conditional)

Similarly, () isn't about now and then but about expressing the informality of a family member or friend vs the more formal kind of request you'd make of someone else.

Will you get that for me? (intimate)

Would you get that for me? (public)

While the past tense forms of modal verbs offer different ways to express remoteness in various contexts, they're limited in other ways. The modals are highly defective (see Section ). In particular, they lack present participle and past participle forms. Yet, these are exactly the forms needed to appear in the progressive or perfect aspect constructions. As a result, the modal auxiliary verbs occur only in the simple aspect.

Despite this limitation in their own forms, modal verbs have a way to express different aspects. They can license progressive or perfect VP complements, as illustrated with could in Table, attaching modal meanings to these two aspects. In I could try, could has an infinitival complement in the simple aspect; in I could be trying, could has an infinitival complement in the progressive aspect; and in They could have tried, the complement is in the perfect aspect.

Table: The aspect system in conjunction with modals

All in all, the modal auxiliary verbs don't multiply the number of tenses. Rather they have present and past-tense forms and interact with aspects by combining with non-tensed verb forms to express different facets of time and modality.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > The English tense/aspect system > No tense
text:
While we've focused on tensed verb forms, English also has several non-tensed (or non-finite) verb forms. These include plain forms and participles.

Infinitivals use the plain form of the verb, often preceded by to:

I want to sleep.

She likes to dance.

Some verbs (e.g., modals) license bare infinitival complements without to:

You should stay.

I can swim well.

How do I make it make sense?

Semantically, infinitival VPs often have a timelessness or a futurate sense. They do so in Hamlet's famous predicament to be or not to be and Tennyson'sTennyson, Alfred, Lord reflection that It is better to have loved and lost than never to have loved at all. Occasionally, an infinitival VP functions as a subject, as in Alexander Pope'sPope, Alexander To err is human; to forgive, divine, where it again suggests an eternal truth.

As we've seen, participles come in two forms: present participles (ending in -ing) and past participles (often ending in -ed for regular verbs).

Apart from their role in the progressive aspect, present participial VPs also commonly function as subjects and complements.

Swimming is good exercise. (VP as subject)

I enjoy reading. (VP as complement)

Semantically, present participles often convey a limited ongoing sense, while past participles typically indicate completion or a resultant state. This is reflected in their use in expressing aspects () as well as in modifier function ().

I am reading a book. (until the end)

I have read the book. (complete)

The book was read. (resultant/state)

The sleeping baby looks peaceful. (for now)

The broken vase lay on the floor. (resultant/state)

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > The English tense/aspect system > No tense > Box: Exercise: the English tense/aspect system
text:

- Identify the non-tensed verb forms (plain form or participles) in the following sentences:

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Verb complementation
text:
Tense and aspect describe what a verb does with time. Complementation describes what a verb does with everything else: what kinds of phrases it requires, permits, or refuses. Knowing a verb means knowing both.

The idea of complementation comes from completing a phrase. If you say, I make, there's a sense in which we're left hanging: what is it that you make? This isn't a bad first approximation of what a complement is, but there are also optional complements, as in (). (a) doesn't have a complement, but nor does it leave us hanging. We understand from it that she is literate and that she engages in the process of reading from time to time. (b) does, in some way, complete the idea, so there's that. But, as we've seen before, labels aren't definitions, and simple semantic ideas about what things are rarely cover all cases. So we shouldn't expect this idea of completing to reliably identify complements.

She reads.

She reads blogs.

Another characteristic of complements is that they are selected by the head, here the head verb. Read can select NP objects, but, generally speaking, sleep can't. Read doesn't select AdjP complements, but seem does.

A verb may select a wide variety of complements, but it usually does so one at a time. In (), we see that know selects an NP object (a) or a that clause (b), but not both (c). So if you're wondering whether a PP, for instance, is a modifier or a complement, try adding another of the same type. If you can, then it's probably a modifier.

I know your name.(NP)

I know that you study grammar.(that clause)

*I know your name that you study grammar.(NP that clause)

But VPs do occasionally allow two complements, as make does in ().

He made them dinner.(two NPs)

He made them happy.(NP + AdjP)

And, as () shows, verbs will even very occasionally license three complements. But this is quite unusual, and, as far as I know, three plus a subject is the limit.

I bet him 3 that it wouldn't work.(three complements)

Although they tend to license complements one at a time, verbs can be quite indiscriminate in what that complement may be. () shows a selection of the complement types that be licenses. (The complements in (d, e) are predicative complements. For more on predicative complements, see Huddleston et al. (2002).)

I'm going. (present participial VP)

It was broken by the sound of a truck. (past participial VP)

It's to go back. (to infinitival VP)

It's a book. (NP)

It's nice. (AdjP)

It's on the table. (PP)

The important thing is that you're here. (that clause)

Unfortunately, guessing what complements a verb will license is often very difficult. The choices are motivated but fundamentally arbitrary at the same time. They can even change over time, as graduate has done over the last hundred years or so ().

Humber graduated 100 students.(old)

I was graduated from Humber. (older use)

I graduated from Humber. (later use)

I graduated Humber. (newer use)

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Verb complementation
text:
A good learner's dictionary, such as the Longman Dictionary of Contemporary English (https://www.ldoceonline.com/) is very useful if you want to know what kind of complements are licensed by a given verb. Here are some common verbs and their complements.

Verbs that often appear without a complement include arrive, appear, begin, belong, collapse, come, cry, emerge, exist, and fade. Such verbs are intransitive (as introduced in Section ).

Those that take NP objects are transitive. Examples of transitive verbs with their objects include: build a fort, buy groceries, carry an infant, eat dinner, find a new idea, give hope, hear your name, love someone, make a great catch, open a book.

Verbs with two objects are called ditransitive. Here, the first object is typically a recipient, while the second is the thing that is given/sent/transmitted/etc.

give someone a gift

tell your friend a story

send them a letter

show the class a video

offer us advice

Some verbs license that-clause complements. These verbs tend to be related to speech or thought.

I hope that it works.

I heard that it works.

I know that it works.

I said that it works.

I like that it works.

I expect that it works.

Those that take AdjP complements are often stative and resist the progressive aspect as a result. (See Section.)

I feel happy.

They seem happy.

They looked happy.

They appear happy.

They became happy.

Some verbs mix it up with an object + an AdjP complement.

I made it bigger.

I painted the room blue.

I left the door open.

She kept the audience happy.

They declared the area safe.

The great majority of verbs taking a PP complement limit the permissible prepositions, often to no more than one.

I headed to school.

They stayed in school.

They listened to their teachers.

They spoke about school.

They apologized for my mistakes.

She laughed at the joke.

Like I said, it's arbitrary, and you just have to know.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Verb complementation > Auxiliary verbs as heads
text:
The idea that auxiliary verbs function as the heads of verb phrases might seem counter-intuitive at first. After all, we often call them "helping verbs", which makes them sound like dependents rather than heads. But let's look at this more closely:

She sings beautifully.

She can sing beautifully.

She's singing beautifully.

She has sung beautifully.

In (a), there's no question: sings is the head of the verb phrase; there's no other verb there to head it. In this example, sing is intransitive and takes no complement. Beautifully functions as a modifier.

But what about (b-d)? Traditionally, we might have said that sing/singing/sung are still the heads, with the auxiliary verbs just "helping out". But the head selects particular types of complements. Modal auxiliary verbs like can in (b) select bare infinitival VP complements ("bare" because they've been stripped of the to you'd find in infinitivals like the complements in she wants/ought to sing beautifully). That's why we get the plain-form sing in (b). In (c), be (realized as 's) selects a present participial VP as its complement, and so we find the present-participle form singing. And in (d), have (as has) selects a past participial VP, which is headed here by the past-participle form sung.

More importantly, auxiliary verbs don't always select VP complements. As we've seen in (), be also selects a wide range of other phrase types. In fact, they don't need any complement at all. Can you dig it? Yes, I can.

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Verb complementation > Auxiliary verbs as heads > Box: Exercise: verb complementation patterns
text:
- Explain why the following sentences are ungrammatical:

- *I rely of my phone.

- *She went school already.

- *I must to leave.

- Rewrite the following sentences to use a different complementation pattern:

- I heard that the concert was cancelled.

- She painted the room blue.

- They want to visit Paris.

- Create sentences for each complementation type:

- A verb with two NP complements

- A verb with an NP complement and an AdjP complement

- A verb with a PP complement

- A verb with a that-clause complement

anchor: Verbs, Tense, and Aspect > Summary
text: When a textbook presents the "present perfect continuous," you can now decompose it: present tense, perfect aspect, progressive aspect, lexical verb. Each piece contributes something specific, and that decomposition tells you where a student's difficulty actually lies. The same analytical habit applies to complementation: knowing what complements a verb selects is part of knowing that verb, and it's the kind of knowledge that lets you evaluate whether a dictionary entry or a textbook explanation is helping or hindering.

anchor: Syntax trees
text:
half living,

half leaving,

half outward,

half injured
[0.5em]
quartered

section

of parcelled

landBertrand Bickersteth, Soil

anchor: Syntax trees > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Read and interpret syntax trees to understand sentence structure

- Identify heads, dependents, and their functional relationships in tree diagrams

- Construct syntax trees for simple and complex phrases using top-down and bottom-up approaches

- Apply constituency tests (coordination, pro-form replacement, ellipsis, preposing) to identify phrase boundaries

- Use syntax trees to disambiguate structurally ambiguous sentences

- Recognize the pedagogical value of visual representation in grammar analysis

anchor: Syntax trees > Introduction
text:
In previous chapters, we've explored the building blocks of English grammar: words, phrases, and their functions. Now, we'll introduce a useful tool for visualizing how these elements come together to form sentences: syntax trees.

Figure: A syntax tree

Syntax trees are diagrams that represent the hierarchical structure of sentences, showing how words combine into phrases, and phrases into larger units. While you likely won't use these diagrams directly in your ESL classroom, understanding them will deepen your grasp of English syntax and sharpen your ability to analyze language structures.

This chapter guides you through the basic principles of syntax trees. It will show you how to read and construct these diagrams, along with ways that trees can clarify ambiguous sentences. And it will lay out the pedagogical value of syntax trees for language teachers.

By the end of this chapter, you'll have a new perspective on sentence structure, one that will enhance your understanding of English grammar and, in turn, your effectiveness as an ESL teacher. Let's begin by exploring what these trees look like and why they're useful.

anchor: Syntax trees > A first tree
text:
Figure is a syntax tree. Starting at the bottom right, it tells you that there is a word tree. Follow that branch up and you'll see Head:N, which means that this is a noun functioning as the head of something. If you follow that next branch up, you can see that it's the head of a noun phrase (NP). Back down at the bottom, the second-to-last word is a, which is labeled Head:D. D is short for determinative (the category). Following its branch upwards, we see it's the head of a determinative phrase (DP). This determinative phrase functions as the determiner (Det) for the NP with the head noun tree.

Moving to the left, we encounter the verb is, labeled as a verb with the subscript aux, indicating its special categorization as an auxiliary verb (one of the small group that has the NICER properties; see Section ). This verb functions as the head of the verb phrase (VP). The VP also contains a complement (Comp), which is the noun phrase we discussed earlier (with the head noun tree).

Further to the left, we have the pronoun it. This word is labeled as a noun with the subscript pro, indicating it's a pronoun. Following its branch up, we find it's labeled as the head of an NP, and this NP functions as the subject (Subj) of the clause.

The entire structure is labeled as a clause, which is the highest level of this tree. This clause contains both a subject (the NP with the pronoun it) and a predicate (the VP with the auxiliary verb is and its complement).

anchor: Syntax trees > A first tree > Box: Check your understanding
text: Practice reading the tree: Starting from the word tree, trace upward and identify: (1) its category, (2) its function, (3) the phrase it heads, and (4) that phrase's function in the larger structure. Then do the same for is and it.

anchor: Syntax trees > Syntax trees, a solar-system metaphor
text:
