Consider the solar system as a metaphor to understand categories and functions in language, particularly the functions of head and dependent. In the solar system, the central entity is the Sun, which serves as the head of the system. Everything else, from planets to dust, depends on the Sun, making them dependents. We can draw this as the tree in Figure.

Figure: A partial tree of the solar system

Consider the categories and functions in the solar system:

- The Sun belongs to the star category. This star functions as the head of the solar system.

- Jupiter, Earth, Venus, etc. belong to the planet category. Each of these planets functions as the head of a planetary system (PlanSys), and those planetary systems are dependents (Dep) in the solar system.

- Within each planetary system:

- The planet itself, such as Earth or Jupiter, is the head.

- Satellites around the planets are categorized as Moon or Ring. These function as dependents in their respective planetary systems.

Figure: Another syntax tree

This hierarchical structure of heads and dependents isn't limited to the solar system. For instance, our solar system is a dependent within the larger galaxy, which has its own head: a supermassive black hole.

Applying this metaphor to language, consider a verb phrase. Just as the Sun is the head of the solar system, the verb (e.g., give) is the head of a verb phrase. Without the verb give, the connection between the dog and his dinner is lost. Both the dog and his dinner are dependents in the VP. Specifically, they are objects, a sub-type of complement, which, in turn, is a kind of dependent. The tree is shown in Figure.

Breaking it down further:

- Give is the head of the VP.

- The dog is an NP functioning as an object.

- Within this NP, the noun dog is the head, and the determinative the is the determiner.

- His dinner is another noun phrase object. Here, the noun dinner is the head, and the pronoun his is the determiner.

This structure showcases how different phrases in language have heads and dependents, similar to the entities in our solar system.

anchor: Syntax trees > Syntax trees, a solar-system metaphor > Box: Practice: applying the metaphor
text: Using the solar system metaphor, analyze this sentence: The students read difficult books. Identify: (1) What is the sun (head) of the whole sentence? (2) What are the planets (major dependents)? (3) Within difficult books, what is the head and what is the dependent?

anchor: Syntax trees > Why syntax trees?
text:
Syntax trees are visual models of the relations between words and phrases in larger constructions. They are common in linguistics, but they are rarely used in language teaching. To be clear, I'm not employing trees in this book so that you can use them in your teaching. While a particularly academically oriented student may find trees useful in understanding the structure of sentences, the level of detail involved is generally not a level that students need.

So, why employ trees?

- Better learning through dual-encoding: Syntax trees provide a visual representation of the relations between words and phrases in larger constructions. There is good evidence that multi-modal learning is more effective than learning that takes place in a single modality (Ginns, 2005). And so, it's likely that by employing syntax trees alongside other methods, you will be able to recall the ideas of phrase structures, categories, and functions.

- Brevity and clarity: As you can see above, it takes a paragraph or more to describe the information set out in a simple syntax tree, and the explanation is often much harder to understand.

- Explicitness: When you draw a tree, you take an explicit position on the categories of words and phrases, along with their functional relationships. This is much more difficult in prose, which means that prose descriptions tend to be vague. As a result, it's much easier in prose to fool yourself into thinking that you've understood when you haven't. With trees, it is obvious when the analysis is correct.

- Practice with explicit feedback: There is very strong evidence that mastery is achieved through deliberate practice (Ericsson et al., 1993). Simply put, deliberate learning involves the following cycle: attempt explicit feedback reflection attempt. Employing trees allows you to easily get quick, accurate feedback by comparing trees you draw to the answer key.

In short, we are employing trees because they are an extremely effective tool for learning syntax and for demonstrating that learning.

anchor: Syntax trees > Composing your trees > Marking particular dependents
text:
Figure: Tree for Who helped you?

The tree diagram presented in Figure can be interpreted as a single tree or as a combination of three smaller trees, distinguishable by their colors: one black, one orange, and another black.

Starting at the bottom of the rightmost black tree, there's the word you. You, a pronoun, is a specific type of noun, so it's labeled with an N. This pronoun, you, functions as the head of an NP.

Jumping to the leftmost black tree, who is another pronoun and is similarly labeled. It functions as the head of its NP.

The orange tree is centered around the word helped, a verb. Its verb status is evident from its past-tense form, a feature exclusive to verbs. This verb is the head of a VP, analogous to how the noun is the head of an NP. The VP, in turn, is the head of the whole clause.

Each of the NPs functions as a dependent; the who NP is a dependent in the clause, while the you NP is a dependent in the VP. But we can consult Section to see more precisely what kind of dependent each is. You is an NP in a VP, and it's you who is being helped, which is to say, being acted upon. And if we make the sentence passive (you were helped), you becomes the subject. All of which is to say, it has all the characteristics of an object.

We can go through the same process with who. It's an NP at the start of a clause, and it denotes the person doing the helping. It's not really the topic, and we can't check agreement because the verb is not present tense, but it seems like who must be the subject.

To ensure the accuracy of the tree diagram, I'll apply the checks from Section. The topmost label should be a phrasal category like Clause, NP, or VP without any function 51. Each branch should culminate in a single word at its base 51. As we trace up a branch, we should encounter a label indicating head X, where X is a lexical category 51. Continuing upwards, the next label should indicate a function and then a phrasal category 51. This pattern should persist until reaching the topmost label.

anchor: Syntax trees > Composing your trees > Marking particular dependents > A note on tree styles
text: Linguists have many different ways to draw syntax trees. You can find trees of the style I present here in CGEL or in A student's introduction to English grammar (Huddleston et al., 2022). If you want to search online for more examples, be sure to include CGEL in your search query. Otherwise, you could end up with something rather different.

anchor: Syntax trees > Composing your trees > A top-down example
text:
Let's try creating a syntax tree for the clause I had my breakfast. We'll start at the top and work down and left to right. So, since this is a clause, we put that at the top:

Clause

Now, let's work left to right. I is a subject NP, so we add a branch sloping down and to the left from the Clause label. The function goes above the category, so Subj: goes on the first line and NP below it.

This NP needs a head, so we add a branch going straight down and label it Head:Npro.

And finally, we add I.

Next, we'll add the Head:VP on a rightwards sloping branch from the Clause.

This VP has a head:V, which terminates in had. There's also the object NP my breakfast, so the branch from the VP should slope leftwards.

Finally, we'll add the object:NP my breakfast, with its Det:DP my and Head:N breakfast.

Some people prefer to start with the sentence at the bottom of the page and work up through the lexical category head, the phrasal category and its function, etc. Play with both ways and see what works for you.

anchor: Syntax trees > Composing your trees > A top-down example > Box: Try it yourself
text: Practice the top-down approach with this sentence: She likes chocolate. Start with Clause at the top, then work left to right. Remember: (1) Subject NP on the left, (2) Head VP on the right, (3) Each phrase needs its own head, (4) Every word sits at the bottom of a branch.

anchor: Syntax trees > Composing your trees > Some trees
text:
Let's start with a very simple tree (Figure ). We'll start with a single-branching AdjP composed only of a Head.

- As always, the top node is a phrasal category, in this case AdjP. There's no function because functions are relational, and this AdjP is not in a relation.

- The very bottom of our tree is a word node, in this case happy.

- Immediately above happy is a node with a function labeled Head. This is the function of happy in the AdjP. It also has the category label Adj, which tells us that happy is an adjective.

Figure: Tree for happy

Let's try another one (Figure ). This time we have a single-branching AdvP.

- As always, the top node is a phrasal category, in this case AdvP. There's no function because functions are relational, and this AdvP is not in a relation.

- The very bottom of our tree is a word node, in this case very.

- Immediately above very is a node with a function labeled Head. This is the function of very in the AdvP. It also has the category label Adv, which tells us that very is an adverb.

Figure: Tree for very

Let's put these together in Figure. This time we have a double-branching AdjP.

- As always, the top node is a phrasal category, in this case AdjP. There's no function because functions are relational, and this AdjP is not in a relation.

- If you start at the top and follow the right-hand branch, you see we have simply reproduced the AdjP from Figure.

- On the left, though, we have the AdvP from Figure, but this time it's in a relation in the AdjP, so it has a function. Its function in the AdjP is Modifier.

Figure: Tree for very happy

This is the basis of all trees. Below the picture, we'll repeat this with the PP about it. Before scrolling down, try drawing trees like Figures - for about, it, and about it.

We'll try that again in Figure. We start with a single-branching PP.

- As always, the top node is a phrasal category, in this case PP. There's no function because functions are relational, and this PP is not in a relation.

- The very bottom of our tree is a word node, in this case about.

- Immediately above about is a node with a function labeled Head. This is the function of about in the PP. It also has the category label P, which tells us that about is a preposition.

Figure: Tree for about

This time we have a single-branching NP (Figure ). Remember that pronouns are a special kind of noun.

- As always - and by now you can probably finish this sentence for me - the top node is a phrasal category, in this case NP. There's no function because functions are relational, and this NP is not in a relation.

- The very bottom of our tree is a word node, in this case it.

anchor: Syntax trees > Composing your trees > Some trees
text:
- Immediately above it is a node with a function labeled Head. This is the function of it in the NP. It also has the category label N, which tells us that it is a noun (specifically a pronoun).

Figure: Tree for it

This time we have a double-branching PP.

- As always, the top node in Figure is a phrasal category, in this case PP. There's no function because functions are relational, and this PP is not in a relation.

- If you start at the top and follow the left-hand branch, you see we have simply reproduced the PP from Figure. Note that in Figure the Head was on the right and here it's on the left. There's no rule about which side the Head goes on. It depends on which dependents we find.

- On the right, though, we have the NP from Figure, but this time it's in a relation in the PP, so it has a function. Its function in the PP is Complement. (More specifically, it is an object.)

Figure: Tree for about it

Now we'll try putting Figures and together in the AdjP very happy about it (Figure ). Try drawing this before scrolling down to check. Here's our final tree, the AdjP very happy about it.

- As always, the top node is a phrasal category, in this case AdjP. There's no function because functions are relational, and this AdjP is not in a relation.

- If you start at the top and follow the middle branch, you see we have simply reproduced the AdjP from Figure, and the leftmost two branches are just the AdjP from Figure.

- The right-hand branch is the PP from Figure. But this time it's in a relation in the AdjP, so it has a function. Its function in the AdjP is Complement.

- Notice that there is a complement inside a complement. This property of being able to put one thing inside a thing of the same kind is called recursion (or "nesting"). It is the reason you can say the dog that chased the cat that ate the rat that lived in the house that Jack built, and also the reason you probably shouldn't.

Figure: Tree for very happy about it

anchor: Syntax trees > Composing your trees > Identifying constituents
text:
When drawing more complex syntax trees, it can be challenging to identify the constituents of your sentence. Constituents are groups of words that function as a single unit within the phrase structure. Determining what constitutes a constituent is crucial because it informs how you will structure your tree.

To identify constituents, you can use several constituency tests. Here are the four primary tests you can apply:

- Coordination test

- Explanation: If a group of words can be coordinated (joined with a conjunction like and or or) with a similar group, it's likely a constituent.

- Example:

- Original: My well-meaning aunt gave all her loyalty cards to the kind man who helped you.

- Coordination: My well-meaning aunt gave all her loyalty cards to the kind man who helped you and to the woman at the counter.

- Analysis: The ability to coordinate to the kind man who helped you with to the woman at the counter suggests that it's a constituent.

- Pro-form replacement test

- Explanation: If a group of words can be replaced by a single pro-form (like he, it, there), or do so, it's likely a constituent.

- Example:

- Original: My well-meaning aunt gave all her loyalty cards to the kind man who helped you.

- Replacement: My well-meaning aunt gave them to the kind man who helped you.

- Analysis: Replacing all her loyalty cards with them indicates that it's a constituent.

- Ellipsis test

- Explanation: If a group of words can be omitted without making the sentence ungrammatical (and the meaning remains clear), it's likely a constituent.

- Example:

- Original: My well-meaning aunt gave all her loyalty cards to the kind man who helped you, and her friend did too.

- Ellipsis: The omitted portion corresponds to gave all her loyalty cards to the kind man who helped you.

- Analysis: The omission of the repeated phrase suggests that it functions as a constituent.

- Dislocation (preposing) test

- Explanation: If a group of words can be moved to the beginning of a sentence without rendering it ungrammatical, it's likely a constituent.

- Example:

- Original: My well-meaning aunt gave all her loyalty cards to the kind man who helped you.

- Dislocation: To the kind man who helped you, my well-meaning aunt gave all her loyalty cards.

- Analysis: Moving to the kind man who helped you to the front of the sentence suggests it's a constituent.

By applying these tests, you can determine how to break down the sentence into its constituent parts before drawing your syntax tree.

anchor: Syntax trees > Composing your trees > Identifying constituents > Box: Practice: constituency testing
text: Apply the constituency tests to this sentence: The teacher explained the difficult concept clearly. Test whether the difficult concept is a constituent by trying: (1) Coordination:...explained the difficult concept and the easy one, (2) Pro-form replacement:...explained it, (3) Can it be moved to the front?, (4) Can it be omitted in ellipsis?

anchor: Syntax trees > Composing your trees > Checking tree structure
text:
After you draw a tree, you should complete the following seven checks.

- The very top label is a phrasal category (e.g., Clause, VP, NP, etc.) with no function.

- No matter how many branches go down from that label, one and only one is labeled Head.

- At the very bottom of each branch, there is a single word (e.g., jump, ball, the, etc.).

- If you trace that branch up from that word, you run into a label that says Head:X.

- That X is a lexical category (e.g., N, V, Adj, D, etc.), not a phrasal category.

- If you follow that branch up, the next label is Function:XP (e.g., Subj:NP, Mod:AdvP, Head:VP, etc.).

- This is true all the way up until the very top label, which doesn't include a function (see 1.).

Try these checks on Figures and. It's possible, though unlikely, that I introduced an error there.

anchor: Syntax trees > Composing your trees > Checking tree structure > Box: Self-assessment checklist
text: Before continuing, ensure you can confidently: Read a syntax tree from bottom to top; Identify heads and dependents in any phrase; Explain why we use function labels; Apply at least two constituency tests; Use the seven-point checklist to verify tree accuracy. If you can't confidently do all of these, review the relevant sections.

anchor: Syntax trees > More trees
text:
We'll now try some slightly more complex trees, using very happy about it. First, we'll look at a simple clause: We won (Figure ).

- At the top, as always, we have a phrasal category label, in this case, Clause. Despite not calling it a Clause Phrase, it is still a phrasal unit, consisting of a Head and any dependents.

- The Head of the clause is the VP, which, in turn, has the verb won as its Head.

- The dependent in the Clause is the Subject, on the left. It's an NP headed by the (pro)noun we.

Figure: Tree for We won

Next, we will change won to the auxiliary verb are, and add the complement very happy about it.

- This tree takes the basic form of Figure. The verb has changed from won to are, and we have added a complement.

- The complement is simply the AdjP from the previous section very happy about it. The only difference is that there it wasn't in a relation, so it had no function label. Now it is the Complement of the VP, and its label reflects that.

Figure: Tree for We are very happy about it

Sometimes, linguists will use a triangle (or a coat hanger) when they don't want to show the internal detail, usually because they don't have room on the page or because they want to focus on something else in the tree. We could, for example, replace the AdjP tree from Figure with a triangle, as in Figure.

Figure: Tree for We are very happy about it with triangle

anchor: Syntax trees > Disambiguating with trees
text:
A clause can be ambiguous, but a tree will usually disambiguate it by showing the structure. Consider Sherlock saw the man with the binoculars in Figure. Out of context, this is ambiguous between a sighting via the binoculars and a sighting of a man equipped with the binoculars.

Figure: Sherlock using binoculars to look at a man and Sherlock looking at a man who has binoculars

We can show the two meanings with trees. In Figure, the PP with the binoculars is a modifier in the VP; it explains how the seeing was accomplished. In Figure, the PP with the binoculars is a modifier in the NP; it tells us about the man.

Figure: VP modifier reading

anchor: Syntax trees > Disambiguating with trees > Box: Practice: disambiguating with trees
text: Try this ambiguous sentence: I saw the student with the telescope. Draw two different trees to show: (1) I used a telescope to see the student, (2) The student had a telescope. Where does the PP with the telescope attach in each interpretation?

anchor: Syntax trees > Do we need the function labels?
section_title: Do we need the function labels?
text:
Many linguists label only the categories, not the functions. This certainly produces less cluttered trees, but for language teachers, it's useful to be able to state the function of each constituent. It's also less clear if we omit the functions. Consider the difference between Brett and Michael, try harder and Brett and Michael try harder. The first one is an instruction to Brett and Michael, who have, it seems, been slacking off. The second is a positive statement about Brett and Michael's work ethic. If you avoid the function labels, the two sentences would have the same tree (Figure ).

Figure: Tree for Brett and Michael try harder without function labels

But when we add function labels, we can identify whether Brett and Michael is a subject (Brett and Michael try harder. Figure ) or an adjunct (Brett and Michael, try harder! Figure ). (Adjunct is a function we haven't talked about yet, but it means roughly something that is stuck on. In this case it's calling out to Brett and Michael, like saying "hey".)

Figure: Subject reading

anchor: Syntax trees > Some terminological issues
text:
You will often see expressions like adverbial, adverb phrase, or adverb clause used when there isn't a single adverb to be found. This is based on an entirely semantic idea of what it means to be adverbial. Specifically, if something answers the questions when, where, why, or how, then many grammarians use the term adverbial.

They're not wrong.

Terminology is arbitrary. There's no inherently correct term. We could use a different term for what we're calling determiners. We could call them specifiers instead. Or we could call them articles or markers or spoons or category 6. In principle, any label should do.

We could even call them Steve. (Let's call it "Steve". https://www.youtube.com/embed/CiGFRLCC-Ao)

BUT.

While the label does not matter, the way we constitute the category does. And a category of things that answer the question 'when', 'where', 'why', or 'how', based entirely on semantics, is not a useful syntactic category. By this definition, my birthday is adverbial. So are Toronto, reasons, and with binoculars. But these things just don't go together. They don't make a coherent set, and you can't teach anybody anything useful about grammar by lumping them together.

Terms are just labels, and any label will work in principle. (In practice, if you use a highly eccentric label, obviously you will not be understood by many people. Recall the discussion of standards in Section.) But categories matter. They tell us about the world. And trying to make sense of badly constructed categories just makes life harder for everyone.

anchor: Syntax trees > Summary
text: If you can draw a tree, you can't hide from your own analysis. Every node forces a commitment - this word belongs to this category, this phrase serves this function - and the result is either right or visibly wrong. The next time a student's sentence puzzles you, sketch the tree. The structure will show you where the difficulty is, often before you've finished drawing.

anchor: Speech and the sound system
text: Words vibrate worlds if you listen.Selina Boan, "Minimal Pairs Are Words Holding Hands"

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Explain the complexity of speech production and identify major articulatory organs

- Distinguish between phonemes and allophones, and understand their significance for pronunciation teaching

- Describe English consonants and vowels using articulatory features (place, manner, voicing, tongue position)

- Set realistic pronunciation goals emphasizing intelligibility over native-like accuracy

- Analyze syllable structure and apply phonotactic principles to understand learner difficulties

- Recognize suprasegmental features (stress, intonation, rhythm) and their role in English pronunciation

- Identify connected speech processes (linking, assimilation, reduction) in natural speech

- Apply phonetic knowledge to diagnose pronunciation challenges and provide targeted feedback

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Introduction
text:
As English language teachers, we often focus on grammar and vocabulary, along with reading, writing, listening, and speaking. Underpinning all of these, though, is a fundamental aspect of language: pronunciation. This chapter introduces English speech and its sound system, providing you with the knowledge and tools to effectively teach reading, listening, and pronunciation to your students.

We'll start by setting pronunciation goals, then explore the complexity of speech production, from the precise control of breathing to the intricate coordination of articulators. From there, we'll examine the sound system of English, considering phonemes, allophones, and the challenges they present for learners. We'll also discuss:

- Setting realistic pronunciation goals for learners

- The distinction between phonemes and allophones

- Vowel length and quality

- Syllable structure and phonotactics

- Suprasegmental features like stress, intonation, and rhythm

- Connected speech processes

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Setting pronunciation goals
text:
When learning a new language, setting realistic and achievable pronunciation goals is crucial for both learners and educators. But determining what constitutes an appropriate pronunciation goal can be a complex and sometimes controversial issue, particularly in the context of English language learning.

One perspective on pronunciation goals is the idea of "native-like" pronunciation. From this viewpoint, the ultimate aim of pronunciation instruction is to help learners achieve a level of proficiency that is indistinguishable from that of a native speaker. This goal is often motivated by the belief that native-like pronunciation is necessary for effective communication and social acceptance within the target language community. It's also the goal that many students have, at least implicitly.

But the concept of native-like pronunciation has been challenged on several grounds. First, the very notion of a "native speaker" is very hard to pin down, given the wide variety of accents and dialects that exist within any language community, especially in a global language like English. Second, research has shown that even highly proficient English speakers who did not grow up speaking English typically retain some degree of foreign accent, and that this accent does not necessarily impede communication or social integration (Derwing et al., 2009). Third, the pursuit of native-like pronunciation can be demotivating for learners, as it sets an unrealistic standard that may be difficult or impossible to achieve.

An alternative perspective on pronunciation goals emphasizes intelligibility and comprehensibility over native-likeness. Intelligibility refers to the extent to which a listener can accurately decode the words and utterances produced by a speaker, while comprehensibility refers to the ease with which a listener can understand the meaning of those utterances (Derwing et al., 2015). From this viewpoint, the primary aim of pronunciation instruction is to help learners develop the skills and strategies needed to communicate effectively and efficiently in the target language, even if their pronunciation retains some non-native characteristics.

Research has provided support for the intelligibility and comprehensibility perspective on pronunciation goals. Studies have shown that even speech with noticeable accents (typically we don't notice our own accents or those like them) can be highly intelligible and comprehensible, and that factors such as speech rate, fluency, and lexical and grammatical accuracy often play a more significant role in communication success than accent (Munro et al., 1995). This focus can also be more motivating for learners, as it emphasizes the communicative value of their language skills rather than their ability to conform to some idealized native-speaker norm.

Another factor to consider when setting pronunciation goals is the role of identity and individual differences. For some learners, developing a native-like accent may be an important personal or professional goal, reflecting their desire to fully integrate into the target language community or to project a particular social or cultural identity. For others, retaining some degree of foreign accent may be a way of asserting their linguistic and cultural heritage or of expressing their unique personality and style. Ultimately, the choice of pronunciation goals should be informed by a learner's individual needs, motivations, and contexts of language use.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Setting pronunciation goals
text:
In setting pronunciation goals, it is also important to consider the potential for accent discrimination and bias. Everyday experience shows that non-local accents can sometimes trigger negative stereotypes or prejudices, and this can lead to social, educational, or professional barriers for speakers (Lippi-Green, 2012). While the ultimate solution to this problem is greater linguistic diversity and inclusion, language teachers can both help learners surmount accent bias and urge a greater acceptance of non-native speech.

Ultimately, the key to setting effective pronunciation goals is to strike a balance between aspirational and realistic expectations. While the goal of native-like pronunciation may be appropriate for some learners in some contexts, a more broadly applicable and achievable goal is the development of intelligible, comprehensible, and confident speech that allows learners to communicate successfully in a variety of social and professional settings. By prioritizing these communicative outcomes over a narrow focus on accent reduction, language educators can help learners develop the skills and strategies needed to become effective and empowered users of the English language.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Speech production
text:
Speaking is one of the most complicated things we do. Even when we put aside all the linguistic processing that's completely invisible, the physical act of speech itself remains a feat of remarkable intricacy.

Start with the breath. As we speak, we push an exquisitely controlled stream of air from our lungs, up our windpipe, past our vocal cords, and then out, through the mouth, the nose, or both. This is surprising. While breathing typically occurs automatically, speaking requires voluntary control over the breathing apparatus. Speakers often inhale quickly and deeply, and exhale more slowly to prolong phonation (making speech sounds). This pattern is crucial for long sentences or when emphasis is needed on particular words or phrases.

The muscles of the trunk, which include the abdominal and back muscles, play a supporting role in breathing and speech. These muscles help regulate the pressure within the abdominal and thoracic cavities, enhancing control over the air expelled during speech. There's a feedback loop between trunk stability and breath control.

But what of the mouth, the tongue, the lips, the jaw, the soft palate? The level of precision they achieve rivals that of a fine orchestra, the placement of each articulator shaping the fundamental frequencies and various overtones that characterize the various vowels and consonants. Unlike an orchestra, though, nobody rehearses. You've been doing this flawlessly since before you could tie your shoes, and you've never once thought about it. Until now. Sorry about that.

The brain has to send signals to each of these structures in a precise sequence and timing. Due to the varying distances between the brain and the different articulators, the neural impulses need careful orchestration. Speech production isn't a simple sequence of brain signals matching the order of sounds. The brain issues motor commands in complex patterns that may not follow the phonological order of words, constantly adjusting for varying articulator positions in different sound contexts (Tatham et al., 2006).

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Speech production > Articulatory organs and their roles
text:
Speech production involves a coordinated effort of several organs, each contributing to the formation of sounds. Figure illustrates various places of articulation, which we'll explore in this section.

Figure: Passive and active places of articulation: (1 2) Labial; (3) Dental; (4) Alveolar; (7) Palatal; (8) Velar; (9) Uvular; (11) Glottal. By User:ish shwar (original.png deleted),.svg by Rohieb, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons.

A place of articulation is a location in the vocal tract where the tongue, lips, or other organ can form a constriction or closure to produce a speech sound. We'll focus on those most relevant to English.

The lungs provide the airstream for most speech sounds. During speech, we control this airflow, using it to generate different sounds as it passes through the vocal tract.

The larynx (11) houses the vocal folds. When these folds vibrate as air passes through, they produce voicing, forming vowels and voiced consonants like /b/, /d/, /g/, /n/, /m/, /r/, and /l/. Holding the folds apart results in voiceless sounds like /p/, /t/, and /k/.

The glottis (11) is the space between the vocal folds. When the vocal folds are brought together abruptly, they produce a glottal stop, heard in expressions like uh-oh and other words in various dialects.

The velum (8), or soft palate, controls nasal airflow. A lowered velum creates nasal sounds like /m/, /n/, and /ŋ/ (the ng in sing), while a raised velum produces oral sounds. (Try saying murmur or nanny while firmly holding your nose.) The velum also serves as a place of articulation for velar consonants such as /k/, /g/, and /ŋ/.

The uvula (9) is used as a place of articulation in some languages. This is uncommon in English, though it occurs in some dialects. An example of a uvular sound is the French /ʁ/ in rouge.

The teeth (3) and alveolar ridge (4) serve as points of contact for the tongue for many sounds, including /t/, /d/, /s/, and /z/.

The tongue's versatility allows for a wide range of sound productions:

- Tip (17) and blade (16): For alveolar sounds like /t/, /d/, /s/, and /z/.

- Front (15): For palatal sounds like /ʃ/ (the sh in ship).

- Back (14): For velar sounds like /k/ and /g/.

The lips (2) shape sounds in various ways:

- Rounded: As in /u/ (the vowel sound in boot)

- Spread: As in /i/ (the vowel sound in beet)

- Brought together: For bilabial sounds like /p/ and /b/

anchor: Speech and the sound system > The sound system
text:
English pronunciation varies from place to place and time to time. There is no universal standard, so I will describe the English that I speak, a somewhat conservative variety found in and around Toronto. When I say conservative, I mean that I don't have a number of the sound changes that many folks I grew up with have. When I say which, for instance, it doesn't sound like witch: it starts /hw/, not /w/.

I can't emphasize enough that there is no single correct pronunciation. There is no way to adjudicate between those who say /ˈɑftən/ and those who say /ˈɑfən/, those who say /ˈnu.kli.ɚ/ and those who say /ˈnju.kli.ɚ/ or /ˈnu.kjə.lɚ/, those who apologize with /ˈsɑɹ.i/ and those who use /ˈsɔɹ.i/, etc. Even if you've never learned the international phonetic alphabet (IPA), I expect you can work out that those words are often, nuclear, and sorry. People will nevertheless continue to announce one, because certainty is cheaper than analysis.

But it's time to go through the symbols of the International Phonetic Alphabet (IPA) one by one. Most of these are just what you'd expect: /m/ as in mom, /d/ as in dad, etc. Only a few are unfamiliar (/ð/, /ʒ/, /d͡ʒ/, /ŋ/, /θ/, /ʃ/, /t͡ʃ/) or confusing (/j/ is the y sound in yellow).

anchor: Speech and the sound system > The sound system > Consonants
text: I have roughly 24 consonant phonemes in my dialect (depending on whether you count things like the glottal stop in uh-oh), each characterized by its place and manner of articulation, and voicing. Let's explore these features:

anchor: Speech and the sound system > The sound system > Consonants > Place of articulation
text:
The place of articulation refers to where in the vocal tract the airflow is obstructed. English uses these main places:

- Bilabial (2): Both lips come together. Examples: /p/, /b/, /m/, /w/

- Labiodental (2 3): Lower lip touches upper teeth. Examples: /f/ /v/

- Dental (3): Tongue tip behind or between teeth. Examples: /θ/ (as in thin), /ð/ (as in this)

- Alveolar (4): Tongue tip or blade on the alveolar ridge. Examples: /t/, /d/, /s/, /z/, /n/, /l/

- Post-alveolar (5): Tongue blade behind the alveolar ridge. Examples: /r/, /ʃ/ (as in ship), /ʒ/ (as in measure), /t͡ʃ/ (as in chip), /d͡ʒ/ (as in judge). Here, /t͡ʃ/ and /d͡ʒ/ are single phonemes, even though they're represented in the IPA with two symbols each.

- Palatal (7): Tongue body against the hard palate. Example: /j/ (as in yes)

- Velar (8): Tongue body against the soft palate. Examples: /k/, /g/, /ŋ/ (as in sing)

- Glottal (11): Constriction at the vocal folds. Example: /h/

anchor: Speech and the sound system > The sound system > Consonants > Manner of articulation
text:
The manner of articulation describes how the airflow is obstructed. English uses these manners:

- Plosives (also called stops): Complete closure followed by sudden release. Examples: /p/, /b/, /t/, /d/, /k/, /g/

- Nasals: Velum lowered, air escapes through nose. Examples: /m/, /n/, /ŋ/

- Fricatives: Close approximation of articulators, turbulent airflow. Examples: /f/, /v/, /θ/, /ð/, /s/, /z/, /ʃ/, /ʒ/, /h/

- Affricates: A plosive beginning and a fricative end. Examples: /t͡ʃ/ /d͡ʒ/

- Approximants: Articulators approach each other but don't cause turbulence. Examples: /r/, /j/, /w/

- Lateral: Air escapes around sides of tongue. Example: /l/

anchor: Speech and the sound system > The sound system > Consonants > Voicing
text:
Voicing refers to whether the vocal folds vibrate during the production of a sound. English consonants come in voiced-voiceless pairs for many places and manners of articulation:

- Voiced: /b/, /d/, /g/, /v/, /ð/, /z/, /ʒ/, /d͡ʒ/, /m/, /n/, /ŋ/, /l/, /r/, /j/, /w/

- Voiceless: /p/, /t/, /k/, /f/, /θ/, /s/, /ʃ/, /t͡ʃ/, /h/

Understanding these features helps in identifying why certain sounds might be challenging for learners. For instance, a native speaker of a language without the /θ/ sound might substitute it with /s/ or /t/, as these are the closest sounds in their phonetic inventory.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > The sound system > Consonants > Voicing > Box: Check your understanding
text: Test your consonant analysis skills: Describe /f/ using the three-feature system (place, manner, voicing). Then predict why a Spanish speaker might confuse /b/ and /v/ - what features do these sounds share and how do they differ?

anchor: Speech and the sound system > The sound system > Vowels
text:
My dialect has about nine main vowel phonemes and seven diphthongs. Vowels are characterized by the position of the tongue, the shape of the lips, and their length. The vowels in my dialect are shown in Figure.

Figure: Vowel chart of Canadian English. This diagram represents the position of the tongue in the mouth for selected vowels. The horizontal axis shows tongue position from front (left) to back (right), while the vertical axis represents tongue height from close/high (top) to open/low (bottom).

anchor: Speech and the sound system > The sound system > Vowels > Tongue position
text:
The tongue position is described in terms of height (high, mid, low) and frontness (front, central, back).

Figure: Cross-section of the mouth showing tongue positions for selected vowels. The diagram illustrates how the tongue's height and frontness in the mouth correspond to different vowel sounds.
By Ishwar CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons.

- High front: /i/ (as in beet), /ɪ/ (as in bit)

- High back: /u/ (as in boot), /ʊ/ (as in book)

- Mid front: /ɛ/ (as in bet)

- Mid central: /ə/ (as in the first syllable of about)

- Mid back: /ʌ/ (as in but)

- Low front: /æ/ (as in bat)

- Low back: /ɑ/ (as in bot)

anchor: Speech and the sound system > The sound system > Vowels > Lip rounding
text:
Vowels can be rounded or unrounded:

- Rounded: /u/, /ʊ/, /o/

- Unrounded: All others

anchor: Speech and the sound system > The sound system > Vowels > Diphthongs
text:
Diphthongs are vowel sounds that involve a change in quality during their production (from Greek diphthongos 'having two sounds'). For example, ride starts at /a/ and "glides" to /ɪ/. My dialect includes the following diphthongs.

- /aɪ/ (as in ride before voiced consonants)

- /ʌɪ/ (as in write before voiceless consonants)

- /aʊ/ (as in loud before voiced consonants)

- /ʌʊ/ (as in out before voiceless consonants)

- /ɔɪ/ (as in boy)

- /eɪ/ (as in bait)

- /oʊ/ (as in boat)

Many languages have fewer vowel distinctions than English, making vowel production and discrimination a common challenge for learners. For instance, speakers of languages with a five-vowel system (/a/, /e/, /i/, /o/, /u/) may struggle to differentiate between pairs like /i/ /ɪ/ or /u/ /ʊ/.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Phonemes and allophones
text:
The human capacity for language is remarkable.

Not only can we produce and understand complex sounds, but our brains can also categorize these sounds in ways that create meaning. Those sound categories, the smallest ones, the building blocks of spoken English, are phonemes and allophones.

Now, consider, for a moment, the strawberries in Figure. At first glance, they likely appear red to you, a colour universally associated with ripeness in this fruit. But there are no red wavelengths in the image at all. The pixels are actually greys and cyans. In most contexts, they would look grey with a tinge of cyancyan. Instead, redred is a process in your brain that guesses at what you must be perceiving, given the cyan background, and then serves that up to your conscious experience. It's an optical illusion, nothing more.

Figure: Are the strawberries red? Despite containing no red pixels, the strawberries are usually perceived as red. Based on a technique by Akiyoshi Kitaoka.

Phonemes function in a similar way. They are useful illusions that our brains serve up to our conscious experience, mental categories used to group similar sounding speech units in particular contexts. They're how we tell cat from cot, fan from van, ducks from Doug's, and ripe from wipe. They're the smallest sounds that make a difference in a language, the colours of words, the psychological experience of speech atoms.

But, just as the redness of the strawberries in Figure depends on the background colour (cyan), the specific sounds that make up a phoneme can vary depending on the language background of the speaker and the surrounding sounds in a word. Red is a mental category, not a physical property of the world. Similarly, the /m/ phoneme (the one at both ends of mom) is a mental category, not a physical property of the world.

As /m/ illustrates, we write phonemes in between slashes. For instance, the phoneme that starts the word top is /t/, and so is the one that comes after /s/ in stop.

Phonemes are the smallest speech sounds that make a difference in a language. The /t/ phonemes in top and stop each have even sonic aspects that distinguish them from one another, but not in a way that makes a difference to English speakers. These different versions of the same phoneme are called allophones. Allophones are simply different ways to pronounce the same sound, without changing the meaning of a word.

For example, the first /t/ in top is aspirated: it's accompanied by a puff of air, which we show like this [tʰ]. The second /t/ in stop is not; it's just plain old [t]. [tʰ] and [t] are allophones of the /t/ phoneme.

If you're sure that those two sounds are, in fact, identical - as I did the first time I was told that they were different - then you should hold a tissue or a flame in front of your mouth and say the two words. Because the /t/ in top is aspirated, the tissue or flame will jump (see Figure ), something it won't do for the /t/ in stop. Give it a try.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Phonemes and allophones
text:
Figure: The flame reacts to the aspirated /t/ in top, but not to the unaspirated /t/ in stop.

If you speak a language such as Hindi/Urdu, then you don't need a flame to perceive this difference. It's obvious to you, because /tʰ/ and /t/ are different phonemes. They seem as different as /t/ and /d/ do to English speakers or as red and cyan.

So, to review, a phoneme is the smallest language sound that makes a difference in a particular language. Phonemes are written in slashes, like /ðɪs/. By contrast, phones are the smallest language sounds that make a difference in human language, and they're written in brackets, like ðɪs. While we're at it, spellings are written like this.

Vowels and consonants
We often say that the English vowels are a, e, i, o, u, and sometimes y. But linguists prefer vowel for phonemes, not for graphemes (individual letters or groups thereof represent a sound like t = /t/ and th = /θ/ are called graphemes.) When I use vowel, here, that's what I mean: a phoneme, not a letter/grapheme. Same thing for consonant. So English doesn't have five or six vowels. It's got more than 20, depending on the dialect.

This is, incidentally, a useful diagnostic. If someone confidently tells you English has five vowels, you can be fairly certain they're talking about letters. If they say six, they've remembered y. If they say twenty-odd, they may have taken a linguistics course and would like to tell you about it.

Two different phones may make no phonemic difference in one language (like [t] and [tʰ] in English) while being distinct in another. If you said stop with an aspirated [tʰ], it would still be /stɒp/. On the other hand, if you changed a [t] in the Hindi word /tɑl/ 'rhythm' to [tʰ], it would become /tʰɑl/ 'plate'.

Here, English has one phoneme where Hindi has two. But cases where English has two phonemes where another language has one are also common. Japanese speakers, for instance, somewhat famously perceive both English /r/ and /l/ as one phoneme.

In fact, there are even differences among English dialects. In my Ontario English, the vowels in cot and caught are the same, but many people perceive cot as /ɑ/ and caught as /ɔ/. For them, these words are as perceptually distinct as pin and pen or ban and bun.

So why does this phoneme/allophone business matter for teachers? For one thing, it helps us understand the nature of pronunciation errors. If a Spanish speaker says berry instead of very, they're not messing up the phonemes - they're applying Spanish allophonic rules to English. In Spanish, [b] and [β] are allophones of the same phoneme, with [β] occurring between vowels and sounding to English speakers half-way between a /b/ and a /v/. But in English, /b/ and /v/ can change meaning. Recognizing this can help us give more targeted, meaningful feedback.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Phonemes and allophones
text:
It also matters because if students sometimes perceive us as saying the clear [l] in love and other times the dark [ɫ] in ball, but we think they're always hearing only the English /l/, then there's a good deal of possibility for miscommunication.

So as ESL teachers, we need a solid grasp of the phoneme/allophone distinction. We need to know which sound differences are phonemic in English (and thus essential for meaning) and which are allophonic (and thus more flexible). We need this knowledge to prioritize our teaching, to troubleshoot learner difficulties, and to make informed choices about pronunciation models and targets.

Luckily, you don't need to be a trained linguist to develop this kind of phonemic awareness. A good place to start is simply by studying a phonemic chart of English, like the ones provided by the International Phonetic Association. Get familiar with the symbols, the articulatory descriptions, and the example words. Train your ear to hear the contrasts through minimal pair activities and transcription practice.

And crucially, start listening to English with phonetic ears. When you hear a learner's pronunciation error, ask yourself: are they mixing up phonemes, or just using non-native allophones? When you listen to regional accents, try to consider precisely how they vary. When you teach new vocabulary, think about how to highlight the phonemic contrasts and relationships.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Vowel length and quality
text:
One area of confusion for ESL learners and teachers alike is the concept of long and short vowels in English. This terminology, while widely used, can be misleading, as it conflates two distinct aspects of vowel sounds: duration and quality.

In everyday language, we often describe the vowel in words like mate /meɪt/ as being long and the vowel in words like mat /mæt/ as short. But from a phonological perspective, the primary difference between these vowels is not one of length, but of quality. The two vowels are simply distinct sounds, /eɪ/ vs /æ/. But, generally speaking, they have very similar durations.

That said, vowel duration does play a role in English pronunciation, but in a different way. In many varieties of English, vowels tend to be phonetically longer before voiced consonants than before voiceless consonants.

Consonant voicing
Consonants in English can be either voiced or voiceless. Voiced consonants involve vibration of the vocal cords during production, while voiceless consonants do not. For example, /b/ is the voiced counterpart to voiceless /p/, /d/ to /t/, and /g/ to /k/. If that's not clear, try putting your fingertips to your throat and alternating between /p/ and /b/. You'll feel the vibrations with /b/.

For example, the /æ/ vowel in mad /mæd/ is typically slightly longer than the /æ/ in mat /mæt/, something we can mark with ː as mæt vs mæːd. This is because the vibration of the vocal cords during the production of /d/ allows the vowel to be sustained for a bit longer than before the voiceless /t/.

This durational difference is a phonetic detail, though, not a phonological contrast, at least not in English. It doesn't change the identity of the vowel in the same way that the quality difference between /æ/ and /eɪ/ does. In other words, /æ/ in mad and /æ/ in mat are allophones of the same phoneme, while /æ/ and /eɪ/ are separate phonemes.

For students who have difficulty producing the voiced vs unvoiced distinction after vowels (bag vs back, hid vs hit, etc.), it is sometimes helpful to try "holding" the preceding vowels a bit longer, especially if they speak a language in which vowel length is phonemic, that is where /ti/ and /tiː/ could be different words.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Vowel length and quality > The pronunciations of -es and -ed
text:
Two of the most common inflectional endings in English are -es, used for plural nouns and third-person singular present tense verbs, and -ed, used for past tense and past participle forms of regular verbs. Despite their simple spellings, these endings can be pronounced in multiple ways depending on the phonological context in which they appear.

The -es ending has three possible pronunciations:

- /s/ after voiceless consonants (except /s/, /ʃ/, /t͡ʃ/):
cats /kæts/, books /bʊks/, laughs /læfs/

- /z/ after vowels and voiced consonants (except /z/, /ʒ/, /d͡ʒ/):
dogs /dɒɡz/, rooms /rumz/, cars /kɑrz/

- /ɪz/ after sibilants (hissing or buzzing sounds) (/s/, /z/, /ʃ/, /ʒ/, /t͡ʃ/, /d͡ʒ/): buses /bʌsɪz/, garages /ɡærɑʒɪz/, watches /wɒt͡ʃɪz/

Similarly, the -ed ending has three possible pronunciations:

- /t/ after voiceless consonants (except /t/):
walked /wɔkt/, helped /helpt/, laughed /læft/

- /d/ after vowels and voiced consonants (except /d/):
played /pleɪd/, showed /ʃəʊd/, loved /lʌvd/

- /ɪd/ after /t/ and /d/: wanted /wɒntɪd/, headed /hedɪd/, guarded /ɡɑ:dɪd/

These pronunciation patterns are not random but rather reflect a process called progressive assimilation, where the voicing of the final consonant of the base word influences the voicing of the inflectional ending. In other words, voiceless consonants trigger voiceless pronunciations (/s/ and /t/), while voiced consonants and vowels trigger voiced pronunciations (/z/ and /d/). The /ɪz/ and /ɪd/ pronunciations occur when the base word ends in a sound that is phonetically similar to the inflectional ending, in order to maintain the contrast between the two sounds.

The past tense/participle -ed ending often causes problems for students who speak particular languages. Such students default to the /ɪd/ ending, even where /t/ or /d/ would typically be preferred. This usually has to do with limits on what and where consonant clusters are allowed to occur in the first language. These are called phonotactic limits and are the topic of the next section.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Syllable structure and phonotactics
text:
If phonemes are the fundamental sound units of English pronunciation, syllables are the fundamental rhythmic units. Every word is composed of one or more syllables, each with its own internal structure and rules for which sounds can go where. This structure underpins everything from word stress to connected speech processes to pronunciation teaching strategies.

At its most basic, a syllable consists of a vowel sound (the nucleus), optionally preceded by one or more consonants (the onset) and optionally followed by one or more consonants (the coda). The nucleus is the peak of sonority, the loudest and most prominent part of the syllable. The onset and coda are the margins, the consonantal bookends that shape the syllable's identity.

Another useful unit of analysis in the syllable is the rime, the nucleus the coda, which is the part that matters for rhyming. The rime in a word like teach is /it͡ʃ/ spelled each. Another example is /eɪt/ in plate, spelled ate.

To put these syllable parts all together, consider the word cats /kæts/. It's a straightforward monosyllabic word, consisting of a single syllable. The /æ/ vowel is the nucleus, the /k/ is the onset, and the /ts/ cluster is the coda. All together, they form a tidy syllabic package: onset + nucleus + coda.

But of course, English doesn't always opt for tidy. One of the delightful (and devilish) things about English syllable structure is its sheer variety. English allows for all sorts of syllable types: from the simple V (vowel) of a word like eye /aɪ/, to the complex CCCVCCCC of a word like strengths /stɹɛŋkθs/. These long consonant clusters are actually quite uncommon across languages, and they can be hard to produce if you're not used to them. In fact, many languages allow only V and CV, with nary a coda in sight. At times, English seems to regard the syllable as a dare.

Depending on the first language, sometimes this difficulty is somewhat circumvented by inserting extra vowels in between consonants. For example, a Japanese speaker saying McDonalds will produce /makudonarudo/, and an English speaker might produce Russian /vzgljad/ 'look' as /vəzgljad/. In other cases, some of the consonants simply disappear. Vietnamese speakers will often produce problem /prɒbləm/ as /pɒbəm/, not because they can't say /r/ or /l/, but because they can't say /pr/ and /bl/.

Both coping mechanisms can cause confusion for listeners, but it seems that deletion is generally more confusing than insertion.

And yet, even in this wild variety, there are patterns and constraints. Enter phonotactics: the permissible and impermissible combinations of sounds in a language. Phonotactics determines both which sound sequences are allowed and which positions they can occupy within syllables (onset, nucleus, coda).

For instance, English is happy to have /sl/ as an onset cluster, as in slide, slap, slip. But it categorically rejects /sl/ as a coda. There are no English words that end with /sl/. Similarly, English allows /nd/ as a coda cluster in words like hand and pond, but it doesn't allow it as an onset: /nda/ and /ndi/ are not well-formed English syllables.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Syllable structure and phonotactics
text:
These aren't just random quirks, but deeply ingrained patterns in the language. And they can vary across varieties of English. Many British and American speakers, for example, happily produce syllabic /l/ in words like bottle /bɒtl̩/, with the /l/ functioning as a nucleus. But for some New Zealand speakers, this syllable type is illicit, and bottle becomes /bɒtʌl/.

The /ŋ/ sound (as in sing) is the one found at the beginning of the Vietnamese name Nguyen. But English doesn't allow syllable-initial /ŋ/, which is why most English speakers mangle that name so badly. To be clear, we can all say /ŋ/ perfectly well. But our brains balk when asked to do it at the beginning of a syllable.

For teachers, phonotactic awareness is just as important as phonemic awareness. It helps us understand why certain pronunciations are difficult for learners (they might be trying to map L1 phonotactics onto English), and it gives us a basis for providing targeted, principled feedback. If a Spanish speaker consistently adds a vowel before initial /st/ clusters, saying "estop" for stop, we can recognize this as a phonotactic issue (/st/, /sp/, /sk/ etc. are not possible onsets in Spanish) and deal with it accordingly.

Phonotactics also has implications for syllabus design and materials development. When creating word lists or pronunciation activities, we need to consider syllable structure and consonant cluster sequencing in a way that builds complexity gradually. Starting with simple CV and CVC syllables, then moving on to more complex onsets and codas, can help learners build phonotactic competence in a systematic way.

And of course, phonotactics interacts with other aspects of pronunciation in crucial ways. Syllable structure is the foundation for word stress patterns, with stressed syllables attracting more complex onsets and codas. Phonotactics also plays a key role in connected speech processes like linking and elision, as sounds adjust to maintain well-formedness across word boundaries.

As ESL teachers, we need to have a mental map of English syllable structure and phonotactics. We need to know not just what's possible, but what's probable, which syllable types and sound sequences are most common and productive in the language. This knowledge allows us to prioritize our teaching, to anticipate learner challenges, and to provide authentic, relevant input and practice.

So how can we develop this mental map? Again, it starts with self-study and reflection. Analyze the syllable structure of words you encounter, breaking them down into onset, nucleus, and coda. Notice patterns in the distribution of consonant clusters. Consult phonotactic descriptions in pronunciation resources and linguistics textbooks.

But also, crucially, start listening to English with syllabic ears. Tune into the rhythmic ebb and flow of the language, the way sounds group and divide themselves into syllabic units. Pay attention to how syllable structure shapes stress, intonation, and connected speech. And when you hear learner errors, consider whether they might have a phonotactic basis.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Syllable structure and phonotactics
text: Above all, remember that syllables and phonotactics are not just abstract linguistic concepts, but fundamental organizational principles of English pronunciation. By grasping these principles, we gain a powerful framework for understanding and teaching the sound system holistically. We can help our learners not just to pronounce individual sounds, but to internalize the rhythmic architecture of the language.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Suprasegmentals
text: Suprasegmental features extend over more than one sound segment in an utterance. They play a crucial role in the rhythm, melody, and overall sound of a language.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Suprasegmentals > Stress > Word stress
text:
Word stress refers to the emphasis placed on a particular syllable within a word. In English, stress can distinguish meaning:

- REcord (noun) vs reCORD (verb)

- DEsert (arid land) vs deSERT (to abandon)

English word stress patterns are complex and often unpredictable, though some general tendencies exist:

- Two-syllable nouns often stress the first syllable

- Two-syllable verbs often stress the second syllable

- Words ending in -tion or -sion typically stress the penultimate syllable

Stress also distinguishes compounds from ordinary phrases. A black BIRD (phrase: any bird that's black) is not necessarily a BLACKbird (compound: a species). A green HOUSE needs paint; a GREENhouse grows plants. These contrasts cause real confusion for learners, and they resist explicit rule-giving because compound stress depends partly on how established the compound is. Ice cream is typically stressed like a compound (ICE cream), but for many speakers, ice water is not (*ICE water).

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Suprasegmentals > Stress > Sentence stress
text:
In connected speech, not all words receive equal stress. Content words (nouns, lexical verbs, adjectives, adverbs) are typically stressed, while function words (articles, prepositions, auxiliary verbs) are often unstressed:

The ˈCAT sat on the ˈMAT.

This pattern of emphasizing content words contributes significantly to the rhythm of English speech.

But speakers can override the default pattern to mark contrastive focus, shifting stress onto the word that carries the new or corrective information:

I didn't say he ˈSTOLE the money - I said he ˈBORROWED it.

Here, the stress on borrowed signals a correction. Without it, the sentence loses its point. Contrastive stress can land on any word in a sentence, including function words that are normally unstressed: I gave it TO her, not FOR her. This flexibility makes English stress a powerful information-packaging tool (see Chapter ), but a subtle one. Misplaced stress rarely causes misunderstanding, but it does make the speaker harder to follow.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Suprasegmentals > Intonation
text:
Intonation refers to the rise and fall of voice pitch over the course of an utterance. It serves several functions in English:

- Distinguishing between statements and questions:

You're going. (falling intonation)

You're going? (rising intonation)

- Expressing emotions or attitudes:

Really. (falling intonation: agreement or boredom)

Really? (rising intonation: surprise or disbelief)

- Indicating whether a speaker has finished or is continuing:

I bought apples, pears, and bananas.
(falling intonation on bananas indicates the list is complete)

I bought apples, pears, bananas...
(level or slightly rising intonation suggests the list continues)

Tag questions are a particularly clear case. The same words carry different meanings depending on the intonation:

You're coming, aren't you? (falling: I expect so)

You're coming, aren't you? (rising: I'm genuinely asking)

With falling intonation, the tag is a confirmation: the speaker already believes the answer is yes. With rising intonation, the speaker is uncertain and wants a real answer. This is the kind of distinction that grammar alone can't capture: the syntax is identical, but the intonation changes the speech act from an assertion to a question.

Intonation can be hard to notice consciously; we tend to hear what someone says before we hear how they say it. But intonation errors can cause real pragmatic misfires: a flat or falling intonation on what's meant as a question can sound like a statement or even a command.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Suprasegmentals > Rhythm > Stress-timed vs syllable-timed languages
text:
Languages are often categorized as stress-timed or syllable-timed, though this distinction is more of a continuum than a binary classification.

In stress-timed languages like English, the time between stressed syllables tends to be roughly equal, regardless of the number of unstressed syllables in between. This leads to the compression or reduction of unstressed syllables in rapid speech:

The ˈcat has ˈateen the ˈrat. (5 syllables)

The ˈcat has ˈeaten the ˈrat. (7 syllables)

Both sentences take approximately the same time to say, despite the different number of syllables. The unstressed syllables in the longer version get compressed to fit.

This compression is what drives vowel reduction. In unstressed syllables, most vowels collapse toward /ə/, the schwa, the most common vowel in English. The word photograph /ˈfoʊtəgræf/ has full vowels in its first and last syllables, but when we shift to photographer /fəˈtɒgrəfər/, the stress moves and the formerly full vowels reduce. This is not sloppy speech; it's the system working as designed. Learners who give every syllable equal weight sound effortful and choppy, not because their vowels are wrong but because their rhythm is.

Syllable-timed languages, like Malayalam or Spanish, tend to give each syllable roughly equal duration and stress. In these languages, the duration of an utterance is more directly related to the number of syllables it contains. Learners from syllable-timed backgrounds often resist vowel reduction because their L1 has trained them to give each syllable its due. The result is intelligible but effortful English: every syllable gets full articulation, and the characteristic bounce of English stress-timing disappears.

The stress-timed/syllable-timed contrast is an oversimplification; as the chapter noted, it's a continuum, and even stress-timed English varies by dialect and register. But as a teaching heuristic, it explains a real and pervasive source of difficulty. Rhythm is rarely taught explicitly in pronunciation classes, yet it may matter more for intelligibility than any individual consonant or vowel.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Connected speech
text:
In the previous sections, we've looked at English pronunciation as a system of discrete parts: phonemes, allophones, syllables, stress, intonation. But in real-life language use, these parts are rarely produced in isolation. Instead, they flow and blend together in the stream of connected speech, undergoing all sorts of modifications and transformations in the process.

These connected speech processes aren't just linguistic trivia, but fundamental features of natural, fluent English. When learners can comprehend and produce connected speech, they can engage more effectively in real-time communication, with all its speed, variability, and spontaneity.

So what exactly happens in connected speech? At a broad level, we can identify three main types of processes: linking, assimilation, and reduction.

Figure: Illustration of phonetic reduction in the phrase going to. The figure humorously shows the progression from the fully articulated form to an extremely reduced form in rapid speech. By Randall Munroe. Source: https://xkcd.com/2942/.

Linking refers to the way sounds are joined together across word boundaries. In English, as in all languages, there's a tendency to "glue" words together, to create smooth, continuous transitions rather than choppy, staccato breaks. This can involve linking consonants to vowels (as in an apple /ən æpəl/), vowels to vowels (as in go in /gəʊ ɪn/), or even consonants to consonants (as in top piece /tɒp piːs/).

Assimilation is when a sound takes on characteristics of a neighboring sound, becoming more similar to it in place or manner of articulation. This can happen within words (like the /n/ becoming /m/ before /p/ in input), but it's especially common across word boundaries. The phrase ten books might be pronounced [ˈtʰɛmˈbʊks] because the /n/ assimilates to the bilabial place of articulation of /b/.

Reduction, as illustrated in Figure, involves the simplification of sounds, often resulting in the omission or weakening of sounds. This process is particularly prominent in rapid, casual speech. For example, the phrase going to might be pronounced as gonna /ɡʌnə/, or even further reduced to something approximating /gə/ (where the tilde indicates a nasalized vowel). This reduction process helps make speech more fluid and less effortful for the speaker, but it can pose significant challenges for language learners who are accustomed to more deliberate, articulated forms of speech.

These processes are not random but follow predictable patterns that can be taught and practiced. Understanding the principles behind linking, assimilation, and reduction helps not only in producing more natural-sounding speech but also in better understanding native speakers, who naturally employ these processes in everyday conversation.

anchor: Speech and the sound system > Conclusion
text:
The sound system is where many teachers feel least prepared, and for good reason: the gap between what we hear and what's actually happening in the mouth is wide. Phonemes vs. allophones, place and manner of articulation, stress and intonation - these tools let you hear a pronunciation difficulty and trace it to a specific articulatory habit, or spot a systematic L1 transfer behind what looks like a random error. That diagnostic ear is worth more than any pronunciation drill.

Note: These definitions are simplified for accessibility and may not capture all nuances or exceptions. In practice, many of these concepts have complex interactions and variations across different contexts and varieties of English.

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary
text:
Now words are the agony

of a girlhood populated by star anise and fermented beans,

ground together roughly until the decadence of love and

brine simmered through the night.Helen Han Wei Luo, Consider the Peony

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Distinguish between semantic and syntactic notions of words and their implications for language analysis

- Analyze word structure for bases, affixes, derivation, and inflection

- Differentiate between word families, lemmas, shapes, and tokens for vocabulary counting purposes

- Explain word frequency distribution patterns and their relationship to text coverage

- Calculate vocabulary size requirements for different levels of text comprehension

- Identify polysemy, collocations, and formulaic language

- Understand how complementation patterns and selectional restrictions link vocabulary to syntax

- Recognize common vocabulary teaching pitfalls and apply frequency-based principles

- Apply morphological knowledge to decode unfamiliar words and understand word relationships

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > Introduction
text:
For many people, language is all about words. It's not that they discount grammar, but words are just so salient, and there are so very many of them. Grammar provides the architecture; vocabulary furnishes the rooms.

This chapter examines vocabulary as a linguistic phenomenon:

- How words are constructed (morphology)

- What constitutes a word and different ways to count vocabulary

- The frequency distribution of words in English

- How vocabulary size relates to text coverage

- The relationship between vocabulary and other aspects of language

- Common misconceptions that can derail vocabulary instruction

We begin by examining the building blocks of vocabulary: morphemes and word formation. This foundation will inform our subsequent discussions on vocabulary size, frequency, and the various ways linguists conceptualize and count words.

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > What's a word
text:
Defining exactly what constitutes a word is trickier than it might seem at first glance. Like soup, it becomes much less obvious the moment you try to define it. The complexity arises from multiple perspectives on what counts as a word and how words relate to each other through morphological processes.

In this section, we'll consider:

- Semantic vs syntactic notions of a word

- How words are formed and change (morphology)

- Different ways of conceptualizing words for various purposes

- The relationship between word forms and word families

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > What's a word > Semantic and syntactic notions of words
text:
When discussing words, it's useful to distinguish between the semantic and syntactic perspectives. They don't always coincide, and sometimes misunderstandings are simply due to a lack of a shared perspective.

From a semantic standpoint, a word is a unit of meaning. This view focuses on the conceptual content rather than grammatical behaviour. Dictionaries typically reflect this perspective, treating semantically unified strings such as at the moment, (https://www.ldoceonline.com/dictionary/at-the-moment) meaning 'now' as single entries even when they consist of multiple syntactic parts. A prime example of this is the treatment of so-called "phrasal verbs" (See ). (Phrasal verbs like put up with are often only phrases in the traditional sense of 'a group of words' and not in the technical sense of 'a syntactic head and its dependents'.) Consider the case of give up:

I decided to give up smoking.

Semantically and lexicographically, give up functions as a single unit of meaning 'stop doing or trying something'. You'd find it as one entry in a dictionary, despite its two-part composition.

Syntactically, though, we often need to treat the components of such expressions as separate words. This is the view we've been employing in our discussions of lexical categories in earlier chapters. From this angle, give and up are distinct syntactic units, a verb and a preposition, as evidenced by their ability to be separated in certain constructions:

I gave up smoking last year.

I gave it up last year.

This syntactic flexibility demonstrates why, grammatically, we often need to consider give and up as separate words, even when it takes both to convey the intended meaning.

Both perspectives are valid: semantics asks what words mean, syntax asks how meanings work as words. Pay attention to which perspective is in play.
subsection_title: Semantic and syntactic notions of words

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > What's a word > Semantic and syntactic notions of words > Box: Check your understanding
text: Consider the expression pick up: In I'll pick up the book vs I'll pick the book up, how do semantic and syntactic perspectives differ? Why might a dictionary list pick up as one entry while syntax treats it as two separate words?
subsection_title: Semantic and syntactic notions of words

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > What's a word > Semantic and syntactic notions of words
text: While these different notions of words help us understand their behaviour in phrases and sentences, there's another crucial aspect to consider: how words themselves are formed and how they change. This brings us to the study of morphology.
subsection_title: Semantic and syntactic notions of words

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > What's a word > Morphology
text:
Morphology is the study of how words change their form or category, how they morph. For example, the fact that you can add -ly to the adjective happy and get the adverb happily is a morphological fact. As is the fact that you can get the noun run from the verb run without changing anything at all. Some languages are rich in morphology, but English is relatively impoverished in this regard.

Words can be formed of bases and affixes. All words contain at least one base, and most bases are words in their own right. For example, the base of happiness is happy, which is obviously a word. In contrast, -ness is an affix, which depends on a base. This is very much like the syntactic idea of heads (Section ) and dependents (Section ). But just as some syntactic heads, such as the verb helped, can't form a sentence on their own, some bases can't stand alone as words. One example is dur, as in durable or duration. Dur is never a word on its own, but nor does it combine with any free bases.

There are also special bases called combining forms. These include examples such as bio and ology, which combine to form words such as biology. Combining forms usually combine with each other, but sometimes they can combine with a free base, as in bio-mechanical. The distinction between combining forms and affixes isn't always clear-cut. Elements like tele- (as in television) or mini- (as in miniskirt) started as combining forms but now function more like prefixes. The key difference is that true combining forms typically derive from Greek or Latin roots and often combine with other bound forms, while affixes attach to existing words. (For a detailed discussion of the combining form/affix boundary, see Bauer (1983).)

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > What's a word > Derivation and inflection
text: English employs two types of morphological processes: derivation and inflection. Derivational morphology creates new words or changes a word's lexical category. Inflectional morphology marks grammatical distinctions without creating new words.

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > What's a word > Derivation and inflection > Derivational affixes
text:
According to The Longman grammar of spoken and written English (Biber et al., 1999), certain affixes stand head and shoulders above others in terms of their frequency in a 40-million-word corpus.

Verb-forming suffixes change words into verbs. For example, to describe somebody's character is to characterize them. The verb characterize is formed by adding the verb-forming suffix -ize to the noun character. The most common verb-forming suffixes, occurring in roughly 20-170 verbs each, are: -ize/-ise, -en, -ate, and -(i)fy.

Verb-changing prefixes change the meaning of the verb without changing its lexical category. For example, if you overdo something, you do it too much. But both do and overdo are verbs. The most common of these prefixes are: re-, over-, un-, mis-, and out- (Biber et al., 1999).

Noun-forming suffixes change adjectives and verbs into nouns. For example, openness is the adjective open + the suffix -ness. The most productive are: -tion, -ism, -ity, -ness, -ment, -ant, -ship, -age, and -ery.

There are about 2,000 nouns ending in -tion in the Longman grammar's corpus, and only about 50 ending in -ery. This dramatic difference in frequency between the most frequent and even slightly less frequent elements is a fundamental pattern we'll see repeated across various aspects of vocabulary (e.g., in Section ).

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > What's a word > Derivation and inflection > Derivational affixes > Box: Practice: morphological analysis
text: Try your morphological skills: (1) Identify the base and affixes in uncomfortable, (2) Explain why singer and singing belong to different lemmas but the same word family, (3) Create three new words using the productive suffix -ize.

anchor: Analyzing vocabulary > What's a word > Derivation and inflection > Inflectional suffixes
text: English has a limited set of inflectional suffixes that mark grammatical systems: tense, number, and comparison.
