This is just to avoid confusion, not because certain uses of terms are correct or incorrect. So, whenever I use the term question, for example, I mean it semantically, and whenever I use interrogative, I mean it syntactically. (For more on how a single label can name two overlapping categories, see the box on p..)

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Main-clause interrogatives
text: Main-clause interrogatives (As I mentioned, subordinate interrogative clauses, like I wonder who it could be, will be dealt with in a later chapter.) can be broadly divided into those with an interrogative word (e.g., What is it?) and those without (Could it be a platypus?). I'll use the terms focused for those that include an interrogative word and basic for the others that start with an auxiliary verb. (CGEL uses open and closed for these interrogative types, but since those terms describe semantic question types (see Section ), applying them to syntactic forms can be misleading. This chapter therefore uses basic and focused to describe the form of the clauses.)

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Basic interrogatives
text:
Basic interrogatives invert the typical subject-verb order: The declarative Today is a lovely day becomes the basic interrogative Is today a lovely day?

Figure: The subject and the auxiliary verb is switch places in the basic interrogative.

Not just any verb can participate in this inversion; only auxiliary verbs. In contemporary English, you can ask Are they going? with the auxiliary verb are in front of the subject they but not *Go they? with the lexical verb go in front. (Go they was possible until the end of the eighteenth century (Lass1999, 1999).)

I introduced the auxiliary verbs in Section as those that have the NICER properties, and this kind of inversion is the I in NICER (see Table ).

Table: The NICER properties

The other part of the inversion is the subject, which is introduced in Section. There, I mentioned that Subjects typically precede the Head VP, but that in a basic interrogative (Could they be right?), the subject comes immediately after an auxiliary verb. Subject-auxiliary inversion is a key aspect of basic interrogatives, and that's actually kinda weird. Most languages don't make questions this way, and many language learners struggle with it. Instead of asking Is this right? they'll ask This is right? And of course, that's perfectly clear in most contexts, though, by default, it has the pragmatic force of requesting a clarification or expressing a doubt, rather than that of questioning.

Identifying the subject

1.5em

Sometimes, it's hard to find the subject. When you're stuck, alternating between a declarative and a basic interrogative can be helpful. Consider ( and ).

Tuesday could be a good day to fly to Paris.

Could Tuesday be a good day to fly to Paris?

Tuesday, if things work out, you could fly to Paris.

Tuesday, if things work out, could you fly to Paris?

In (a), the first verb is the past-tense modal auxiliary could. Modal auxiliaries don't have third-person singular forms, even in the present, and this is the past. As a result, agreement provides no clues about the subject. But in the basic interrogative in (b), could has moved in front of Tuesday, which almost certainly means that Tuesday is the subject.

(a) also lacks any agreement signals about the subject. But in the basic interrogative in (b), could is in front of you, which means that Tuesday can't be the subject; it must be you.

Here are some more examples with the auxiliary in small caps and the subject in italics. Those in () show different forms of auxiliary be, those in () show different modal auxiliary verbs, and those in () show two forms of auxiliary have (as opposed to the lexical verb have).

Is rain ever warm?

Are the evenings sometimes too bright?

Am I functioning normally?

Last night, was the moon, perhaps, broken?

Will mornings ever surprise you?

Could silence be too loud?

In your experience, have dreams lost their way?

Had the silent night revealed its secrets before?

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Basic interrogatives
text:
You can see from these examples that, even with inversion, the auxiliary verb still agrees with the person and number of the subject, when possible: is is the right form to agree with singular rain, are for plural the evenings, and am for first-person singular I.

I included (d) to show that the auxiliary verb doesn't necessarily appear as the first element of the clause; other elements - the NP last night, in this case - may come before it. The auxiliary verb appears before the subject, but not always before everything. The same is true in (a): the auxiliary precedes the subject, but it still follows the PP in your experience.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Basic interrogatives > Do support
text:
In all the examples so far, both the interrogative clauses and their corresponding declaratives would have an auxiliary verb. For example, the declarative counterpart of (a) is Mornings will sometimes surprise you, with the modal auxiliary verb will. But lots of declarative clauses - perhaps most - don't have an auxiliary verb available for subject-auxiliary inversion. In such cases, we recruit do, in its appropriate form. This is called do support.

If inverting the subject and the auxiliary to form a question is a bit odd, magically pulling some form of do out of thin air and having it appear in front of the subject seems even stranger. It's no wonder language learners have problems with this. Still, it is the way we do it in Standard English, as you can see in ().

Do coconuts migrate?

Does the Holy Grail actually exist?

Did Sir Robin bravely run away?

Do knights prefer saying Ni to Hello?

Does the Norwegian Blue pine for the fjords?

There's an extra step to watch out for here. The declarative counterpart of (b) would be The Holy Grail actually exists with that third-person singular -s on the present-tense verb. But in the basic interrogative, it's present-tense do that agrees with the third-person singular, so do does and exists exist. Figure shows this change under do support with third-person, singular, present tense.

Figure: Do support with third-person, singular, present-tense.

Figure shows do support with past tense. Again, the tense is marked on do and not on the lexical verb.

Figure: Do support with past tense.

A verb-form puzzle

1.5em

In Does the Holy Grail actually exist, isn't exist also present tense? So, shouldn't it also agree? In fact, exist is the plain form of the verb: there is only one tensed verb in this clause and that's do (es). This is surprisingly difficult to show, and I can't think of a way to do it with a basic interrogative, but I'll return to the issue after discussing focused interrogatives (Section ). In the meantime, try to think it through.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Basic interrogatives > Semantics
text:
Basic interrogatives are usually used to ask closed questions. (Again, CGEL uses closed interrogative for the syntactic construction I'm calling the basic interrogative.) One kind of closed question is the yes/no question. If I ask you, Is rain ever warm? then I basically expect you to either confirm or deny that rain is at least sometimes warm. You could say, Yes, it is, or Sometimes, or I think so, but Aardvark is not an answer. I don't know may be a valid response, but it too doesn't qualify as a answer. So, such a question has a closed set of answers that, specific wording aside, boil down to 'yes' or 'no'.

Another kind of closed question offers a set of alternatives. For example, Would you like to eat at 5:30, 7:00, or 7:30? closes the set of answers to one of those three times. Pragmatically, you could respond with indifference, express a love for food, comment on other obligations you have, suggest another time, or decline the offer of dinner altogether. But strictly within the semantic framework, none of those responses qualify as direct answers. The only ones that count as answers are those that state or imply a preference for one of the three times.

Figure: Two types of closed questions.

And on the topic of semantics, I would remind you that, syntactically, basic interrogative clauses are not always semantically questions. They can also be exclamations (Isn't this the best cake you've ever tasted?), expressions of surprise (Are you serious?), insults (Did an infant dress you?), advice (Wouldn't it be better to read the instructions?), and so on. So, while the structure may be interrogative, the intended meaning can diverge from straightforward questioning.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Basic interrogatives > Intonation
text:
In most dialects, yes/no questions typically have a rising intonation, while alternative questions fall on the final alternative after rising on each previous one, as shown in Figure.

Figure: Typical intonation in yes/no and alternative questions.

This can vary quite a bit though, depending on the attitude behind the question (mere curiosity, doubt, anger, etc.), whether it is said in isolation or as one in a series of questions, and various other factors (Bartels, 1999).

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses
text:
Focused interrogatives typically start with an interrogative word: who (including whose and with decreasing frequency whom), what, which, when, where, why, or how. (There are also some older or archaic words that students don't need to learn, including whence, whither, and some compound prepositions such as whereby and wherein.) This is an important group of words, a group that has a central syntactic role in forming interrogative phrases and clauses and a central semantic role in asking questions. But interrogative word isn't a lexical category like pronoun or auxiliary verb; interrogative words belong to many different lexical categories, as shown in Table. (Did you notice which category/ies is/are not represented?)

Table: Lexical categories of interrogative words (based on CGEL)

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Interrogative phrases
text:
The interrogative words appear in interrogative phrases, such as those italicized in (). Sometimes they're the Head and the only word in the phrase, as in (), where who is the Head of an NP. Sometimes they're the Head along with one or more Dependents, as in (). And sometimes, they are a Dependent somewhere in a larger phrase, as in () and (). In both, what functions as the Determiner in the NP what time, but in (), the interrogative phrase is the whole at PP, which includes the NP, which, in turn, includes what.

Who's the kid?

Precisely who called you?

What time did they arrive?

At what time did they arrive?

Common interrogative phrases, apart from the individual words already listed include how many, how often, how long, what day, and since when. There are also a number with how that are semantically interrogative but function syntactically unlike the others. These include How come you did that?, How about if we take a break?, What about this one?

Interrogative phrases work very well as questions all on their own, given the right context. If somebody says, they're arriving tomorrow, then What time? is a perfectly reasonable way to ask what time they are arriving.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Interrogative phrase subjects
text:
Cases like () are the easiest. Who is a pronoun that is the Head of an NP, and that NP functions as the subject of the focused interrogative clause. Starting with He's the kid, we just replace he with who, and we're done.

He's the kid. Who's the kid?

And, as long as the interrogative phrase is the subject, then focused interrogative clauses work like this. () shows various such examples.

Notice that in (), the question is asked in the plural.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Subject-auxiliary inversion and fronting
text:
But it's not always the case that the interrogative phrase is the subject. When it's not, we're back to subject-auxiliary inversion and sometimes do support, as with the basic interrogatives. And again, this can cause difficulties for students, who often produce uninverted clauses. The inversion is shown in ().

Wh is it It is a pen.

Wh is it It is what?

What is it is [gap]?[fronted]

There are a few things to observe here. First, it functions as the subject in all three examples, even when it's the last word in the clause. Second, a pen functions as a complement in the VP in the declarative (), as does what in (). And then there's the fronted interrogative phrase in (). (I mentioned fronting and stranding back in Section.) That phrase is now in what we'll call Pre function (see Section ), the auxiliary verb is before the subject, and there's just an empty space where the complement in the VP used to be. I'm going to claim that there really is a gap there, and its function really is the same as that of a pen in (a): it's still a complement. See () for a few more examples.

As exemplified in (), when using singular you or I, the declarative and the interrogative typically have contrasting pronouns: I ask about you and you answer I; I ask about myself and you answer you. For this reason, unless you're making this specific point, I avoid you/I examples when explaining interrogatives. That said, as () shows, sometimes we speak to ourselves.

() is also another example of PP fronting. Notice that all the other examples in () have interrogative phrases that start with (and are limited to) an interrogative word. Only the interrogative PP about what starts with a non-interrogative word.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Subject-auxiliary inversion and fronting > Box: Practice
text:
1.5em

Identify an interrogative phrase that would result in the given answer, and state its function in the interrogative clause. See the example.

x

Example: Brett wrote it.

xxxInterrogative phrase: who, function: Subject

xxxExplanation: One likely question is Who wrote this book? (See Section )

- Answer: I'm from Toronto.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: I usually go to bed around 11 PM.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: Taylor did.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: I bought it last week.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: She went to the library.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: He's feeling much better now.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: I gave it to Jean.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: The tickets were 20.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: Don't worry, it's my phone.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: She's an architect.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: They visited Paris last summer.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

- Answer: I read about it in the newspaper.

Interrogative phrase: [gap], function: [gap]

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Subject-auxiliary inversion and fronting > Testing the inversion rule
text:
At this point, you might be forming a generalization: All English questions require subject-auxiliary inversion. This seems reasonable given basic interrogatives (Is it raining?) and most focused interrogatives (What did you eat?). But recall the counterexample habit from Chapter 1: when a rule seems too tidy, that's when to hunt for exceptions.

Look back at Section. The examples there - Who's the kid?, What smells?, Which of your friends can come? - all lack inversion. Why? Because when the interrogative phrase itself functions as the subject, there's nothing to invert with. The interrogative phrase is already in subject position, doing subject things like controlling agreement (How many people were there? not *How many people was there?).

This pattern extends beyond simple cases. Consider:

What happened next? (not *What did happen next?)

Who told you that? (not *Who did tell you that?)

Which solution works best? (not *Which solution does work best?)

Students who overgeneralize the inversion rule produce errors like *Who did come to the party? (when asking for new information, not emphasis). The revised rule? English questions require subject-auxiliary inversion except when the interrogative phrase is the subject. This exception isn't rare; subject-position questions are common in classroom discourse (Who knows the answer?, What comes next?, Which group wants to present first?).

Now search for more patterns: Can you find other systematic exceptions to inversion? What about echo questions (You said WHAT?!) or declarative questions with rising intonation (You're coming?)? Each exception refines our understanding of how English actually forms questions.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Do support inversion
text:
Just as with basic interrogatives, subject-auxiliary inversion works fine with focused interrogatives when there is an auxiliary in the corresponding declarative. But when there isn't, then we once again lean on do for support. See () for a few more examples.

And, just like before, the tense moves from the lexical verb to do, and the lexical verb appears in the plain form. For example, in (), past-tense smelled becomes plain-form smell, and past-tense did is used. Similarly, in (), present-tense get becomes plain-form get, only this time, the forms share a shape, so the change is surreptitious.

A verb-form puzzle

1.5em

Earlier (Section ), I brought up the question of whether or not exist is present tense in Does the Holy Grail actually exist? Now, I can use an interrogative to show you that it's clearly the plain form.

[]The cat is very loud.

[*]Does it ever just is quiet?

[]Does it ever just be quiet?

If we try to use the present-tense form of be, the result is the ungrammatical (b). It's the plain-form be in (c) that is correct. The reason this is so difficult to show is that be is the only verb that has a plain form that is distinct from the plain present-tense form.

Finally, there's the special case of asking about an activity, as in (). In such cases, since the speaker, who may or may not know what the activity is/was, assumes that the listener knows, we use lexical (not auxiliary) do as a stand-in for the activity. The structure, though, is the same. () exhibits do support (auxiliary did together with lexical do), () shows subject-auxiliary inversion with modal auxiliaries, and () does the same but with the progressive aspect, as does () with perfect aspect.

What do/did you do?

What might/can/will you do?

What are/were you doing?

What have/had you done?

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Summary
text:
So in sum, there are three different types of interrogatives. A decision tree is set out in Figure.

Figure: The choice between interrogative structures.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Semantics
text: It will be familiar by now that interrogatives can do more than ask questions, but, in general, the focused interrogatives are used to ask open questions. (CGEL uses the term open interrogatives for focused interrogatives, which typically ask open questions.) These are open in that, when you ask, who did you see? for example, the answer is limited only by the requirement that it be a person or persons. Otherwise, the possibilities are open. The main exception to this is which, which expects answers from a closed set, sometimes explicit (e.g., Which would you like, small, medium, or large?) and sometimes inferrable from the context (e.g., Which one looks best?).

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Focused interrogative clauses > Intonation
text: In general, open questions (and therefore most focused interrogative clauses) end with falling intonation.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Questions in the classroom and classroom questions
section_title: Questions in the classroom and classroom questions
text:
Even before students start learning the ins and outs of interrogative clauses, they're able to ask questions: simple interrogative phrases, like how long? for How long will it take? or declarative clauses with rising intonation, such as We have a test? for Do we have a test?

The first is perfectly natural and accurate, and should not be met with an injunction to use complete sentences. Usually, if the student has chosen an interrogative-phrase question, a complete sentence would contain awkward redundancy, making the asker appear stiff and foreign. In the second case though, the student's high-terminal declarative is inappropriate, unless the teacher has already said something about a test. That's because the speech act evoked from such a clause is not a straight-forward closed question. Rather, it's a request for confirmation, clarification, or restatement. The distinction is subtle, but consider Person A's second turn in ().

Person A: I saw a poster about that concert you mentioned last week.

Person B: Oh, the one at the park? Yeah, the whole team's going.

Person A: You're going?

Person B: Yeah, do you want to come?

You're going? is a request to clarify whether 'the team' includes Person B. The question Are you going? might suggest A wasn't paying attention because, from Person B's viewpoint, they've just told A they're going. By framing the question as a declarative, A acknowledges this possibility while asking for clarification. In other words, high-terminal declaratives have their place, but it's not a general question-asking form, and students should understand the difference.

There's one more common questioning strategy that students use. The clauses covered in this chapter are main clauses, but students sometimes employ subordinate interrogative clauses (See Section ) as questions. For example, a student may ask, pointing, how to say this word? This is a subordinate interrogative clause, and as such, it's perfectly fine inside another clause - Could you tell me how to say this word? or I wonder how to say this word. - but it's not Standard to use such clauses on their own to ask questions. Instead we'd use a focused interrogative with do support: How do you say this word?

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Questions in the classroom and classroom questions > Box: Classroom questions
section_title: Questions in the classroom and classroom questions
text:
1.5em

A good way to start learning how to ask questions is to memorize some, and a good set to memorize is the set of questions that come up in the language classroom regularly. Here are some ideas, but I'm sure you can think of others.

- What does [gap] mean?

- How do you spell [gap]?

- How do you say this/that word? (pointing)

- How do you say [first-language word] in English?

- Can you give me an example?

- Is this OK?

- Can I say [gap]?

- Do you have a [gap]?

- Can I borrow a [gap]?

- Do we have any homework?

- When is it due?

- Did I spell this right?

- Why do we use this tense here?

- What's the past tense of [gap]?

- What's your email address?

- When do we use [gap]?

- Could you repeat that, please?

- What's the difference between [gap] and [gap]?

- Where can I find out more about [gap]?

- What should we do next?

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Phrase structure > Basic interrogatives with subject-auxiliary inversion
text:
Trees may help you think about the structure of basic interrogatives. The tree for the basic interrogative clause Could they be right? is shown in Figure.

Figure: Could they be right: Subj-aux inversion.

The first thing to notice about the trees is the x subscripts after Vaux and GAP. Linguists call such a mark a co-index. It's called a co-index because it's shared between two (or more) elements, and it's called a co-index because, like an index in the back of the book, it points you to something you want to find: if you're wondering where could came from, you check the index, and it tells you it came from the position that now says GAP.

The next notable element is the GAP itself, which is a placeholder. The VP, like all phrases, needs a Head, but in this case, the Head is the GAP that's co-indexed to could, rather than being a verb, as we would usually expect for the Head of a VP. Such GAPs are more important for linguistic theory than for language teaching, but as we see more gaps in this chapter and others that follow, I expect you'll come to see them as important and useful tools for your own thinking about phrase structure.

Having looked at these two crucial elements, let's go through the tree on the left as a review. Starting at the top, we see that it has an auxiliary verb could in Prenucleus function, which is the key element of the basic interrogative clause type. Since prenucleus is an ugly, ponderous word used by virtually nobody outside the CGEL framework, I will simply call it Pre from now on (there will be a Post later). Be sure to understand that subject-auxiliary inversion does not mean that the subject somehow becomes the auxiliary and vice versa. As the tree shows, the subject remains the subject, and the auxiliary verb appears in the Pre function.

Next, there is the Head clause they - be right. At first glance, it may seem strange to have a clause as the Head of another clause. But this is an essential aspect of understanding basic interrogatives. In our example, the main clause functions as the Head of the overall interrogative structure. This arrangement is due to the unique positioning of could in Pre function. It has to be pre-something, and this shows it as preceding the central clause.

In the tree, the Head clause includes a subject NP (they) and a Head VP, represented by the GAP (-) where could would normally be. The clause be right acts as a Complement, and its internal structure is simplified as a triangle here. I encourage you to sketch this tree yourself to grasp its structure fully. Compare your version with Figure, and don't be discouraged if you miss some elements initially. Practice will help you capture all the components accurately.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Phrase structure > Basic interrogatives with do support
text: A tree with do support looks, structurally, just like one with subject-auxiliary inversion, such as the tree in Figure. The tree for the basic interrogative Do you like pizza? is shown in Figure. Again, the auxiliary verb do is in Pre function, again the rest of the clause follows as the Head, and again, there's a GAP in the VP.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Phrase structure > Focused interrogatives with subject focus
text:
Next is the focused interrogative. Again, we'll start with the easiest case, the one with the interrogative phrase as the subject. The example in Figure is Who plays the piano? There's no Pre and no gap, just who functioning as the subject. It couldn't be easier, and language learners rarely make mistakes with this clause structure.

Figure: Tree for who plays the piano.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Phrase structure > Focused interrogatives with non-subject focus
text:
When the interrogative phrase (i.e., the focus) is not the subject, then things get more complex and language learners are much more likely to form questions lacking inversion, like What you can play? In What can you play? in Figure, there are two constituents in Pre function, two gaps, and two different co-indices x and y. Each co-index connects the Pre to its relevant gap.

The functions of the gaps are clearer here. Play is a transitive verb, the kind that takes an Object, so there's an Object gap. While we might say informally that what is the Object of play, that would go against the idea that an Object is a Dependent in the VP, so we give what a different function (Pre) but connect it back to the Object through the x co-index.

anchor: Questions and interrogatives > Summary
text: Every student error in question formation traces to a specific point in the system: inversion applied where it shouldn't be, do-support omitted, subject focus mishandled. When a student says *Where you are going?, you can now locate the gap (inversion missing) and explain why the student's logic was sound even though the result wasn't..

anchor: Negation
text:
Since then wanting

turned into not

wanting to want but wanting

then wanting to want then

refusing to wantJason Purcell, Kris Knight, The Flying Monkey, 2014

anchor: Negation > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Explain how negation in English has evolved through Jespersen's cycle and predict future changes

- Distinguish between different types of negation: clausal (verbal and non-verbal), sub-clausal, affixal, and pragmatic

- Analyze scope relationships in negative sentences and understand how different modal verbs interact with negation

- Identify and use non-affirmative items (like any, ever, yet) and positive polarity items appropriately

- Apply diagnostic tests to determine whether a clause is syntactically positive or negative

- Understand cross-linguistic differences in responding to negative questions and their implications for communication

- Recognize asymmetries in negative meaning and how negation interacts with scalar contexts

- Analyze phrase structure trees for various types of negative constructions in English

anchor: Negation
text:
Tests used in this chapter
This chapter introduces several diagnostic techniques for analyzing negation:

- Non-affirmative item test - Use words like at all, ever, or yet to identify negative clauses: if they work, the clause is likely negative

- Scope ambiguity test - Examine sentences like I didn't finish homework because I was at the park to determine what falls within negation's scope

- Modal negation test - Compare can't vs mustn't to understand different scope relationships with modal verbs

- Polarity response test - Check how different languages handle responses to negative questions to understand cultural variation

- Positive polarity item test - Use words like already, some, a fair chance to identify contexts that resist negation

anchor: Negation > Introduction
text: Negation is a topic that seems extremely simple on the surface, but its underlying complexity makes it a great vehicle for understanding so many areas of the language landscape: scope, polarity, language change, grammaticalization, and exaptation.

anchor: Negation > Language change, grammaticalization, and exaptation
text:
I heard not of it before, says Bertram in Shakespeare's All's well that ends well. You and I would say I have not heard of it before.

Language - as you can clearly see - changes. But the changes are often surprisingly systematic.

Old English (450-1150 CE) used ne, simply placed before the verb, for negation. Take () for example.

Ne hæbbe ic hit ær gehīered.
no(t) have I it before heard of

'I have not heard of it before.'

If things had stayed this way, (Actually, there is a construction in which things more or less have: Never have I ) then the language-learner strategy of putting no before the relevant verb (I no put it there) would be basically the right one. It's simple, and many of the world's languages do essentially this.

But this is just one stage in what's known as Jespersen's cycle. (I derive most of the following stylized description from Chapter 1 of Jespersen, Otto(Jespersen, 2025).) Here's how it goes (see Figure ).

anchor: Negation > Language change, grammaticalization, and exaptation > Jespersen's cycle > Stage one
text:
There's a problem with no (or ne, or non, etc.) hanging out there on its own before the verb: that initial negator often gets swallowed up when your speech apparatus fails to engage quite in time. Think about saying Good morning! That good is often hardly voiced at all: G'morning!. Do people reliably say I'm afraid not with the subject I, am, and first syllable of the next word? 'fraid not. See you later is missing I and will in their entirety, and 'kay is short for OK, itself a slangy shortening of oll korrect ('all correct'), from a brief Bostonian fad of humorous initialisms in the early 1800s (Society, 2016). The start of a phrase is a perilous place for words to find themselves if they want to stick around.

Phrase-initial ne was at risk of erosion. And in due course, the stressed version in Old English gave way to unstressed, which, in turn, simply gave way. But not quite yet.

anchor: Negation > Language change, grammaticalization, and exaptation > Jespersen's cycle > Stage two
text:
The gradual erosion of phrase-initial elements creates little problem for cases like Morning! or Afraid not. But phrase initial ne carries a meaning fundamentally different from the same sentence without it. Without a clear, reliable marker of negation, the negative meaning is often lost entirely.

To compensate for a degraded ne, even before it had edged its way out of English entirely, we began adding extra elements to ensure the negative meaning wasn't missed. For example, the Old English nā in () directly negated nouns or adjectives, as in nā good meaning 'no(t) good'. And it was used together with ne.

Ne sceal nā man mid worde nē mid dǣde nān yfel dōn
no(t) schall no man with word nor with deed no evil do

'No man shall do any evil, neither in words nor in deeds.'

Today, no man shall not do any evil would mean that every man will do some evil. In Old English, though, that particular phrasing was emphatically negative. Of course, the same strategy in I don't do no evil is perfectly clear to Standard English speakers today, and claiming that those two negatives literally make a positive mistakes grammar for arithmetic. The contemporary No man shall do any evil, neither in words nor in deeds has three negatives, each propping up the next. So-called " are still with us in some form, even in Standard English.

This is the birth of today's not, which comes from noht (from nought/nowiht, meaning 'nothing'). Ic ne secge 'I no say' was strengthened by adding this word, as in ic ne secge nowiht 'I no say nothing.' But this strategy to reinforce the meaning made the word ne even more vulnerable to disappearance, and disappear it did.

anchor: Negation > Language change, grammaticalization, and exaptation > Jespersen's cycle > Stage three
text:
The decline and eventual disappearance of ne continued through Middle English (1150-1500 CE), making way for the rise of not. Consider the phrase I ne knowe not 'I no know not(hing)', a linguistic crossroads of sorts. In a process called grammaticalization, not was being bleached of its 'nothing' meaning and becoming simply a marker of negation, equivalent to ne. For a time, it could be understood as either 'I know nothing' or simply as 'I know not', but eventually, new generations of speakers forgot the 'nothing' meaning or never learned it in the first place.

Late in stage 2, this new not was even obligatorily paired with ne, as in the French Je ne sais pas, propelled by the evolving sound patterns of English, coupled with the desire for clear and unambiguous communication.

As Old English segued into Early Modern English (1500-1700 CE), with the devoicing of ne and the redundancy of two negators on opposite sides of the verb, ne completed its disappearing act, and double negatives lost favour. The early grammarians of Late Modern English (1700-Present) seem to have thought them obsolete by 1762 (Gilman1989, 1989).

But that's only half the story.

anchor: Negation > Language change, grammaticalization, and exaptation > Jespersen's cycle > Stage four
text:
Not arrived late in English and it was positioned late in the sentence, which caused a bit of a problem. In German, where a similar process occurred, the negator nicht can be delayed a lot, isolating it from the much earlier verb location,

the result being that the hearer or reader is sometimes bewildered at first and thinks that the sentence is to be understood in a positive sense, till suddenly he comes upon the nicht, which changes everything.
(Jespersen, 2025)

English was saved from this kind of confusion by another change that was going on around the same time, a proliferation of uses of do. (For a less stylized account of do, see Denison (1985).)

One thing that do did was to add emphasis: do come and see us, darling may not have been any more sincere than it is now, but at least it sounds emphatic. It was also stressed. And there were already other auxiliary verbs which were followed by not: I could not go, it might not rain, etc. This emphatic do, with its exaggerated stress, was a perfect candidate for hosting the negator. The end result was [sec:basic-do-support]do support, where the auxiliary do serves as a solid anchor for not, preventing any ambiguity about the presence of negation.

Do started out as a mere helper but quickly became an indispensable part of English negation. By the time of Early Modern English, constructions without do, like I know not, became increasingly rare and marked as formal or archaic. The rise of do in negation coincided with its spread in other constructions as well, such as interrogatives (Do you know?) and emphatics (I do want that). Do-support is unique to English among the Germanic languages.

anchor: Negation > Language change, grammaticalization, and exaptation > Jespersen's cycle > Stage five
text:
With the establishment of do as a crucial part of negation, not once more became dispensable, or at least reducible. A new variation emerged: contraction with do not naturally evolving into don't. Other forms followed suit: does not doesn't, did not didn't. And contracted forms of other auxiliary verbs cropped up: can't, won't, isn't, even amn't for a while.

These contractions (now negative inflections) have become so ingrained in the language that the uncontracted forms can sound overly formal, even pedantic, in everyday conversation.

Negative auxiliary verbs

You may have been told to write do not instead of don't, is not instead of isn't, will not instead of won't, etc. If so, you were misguided.

friPrior to the 1600s, not was obligatorily stressed, but in the early 1600s, it started to lose that requirement. In writing, the new unstressed form was sometimes spelled -n't, especially in comedic plays and when characters with country accents or nonstandard dialects spoke (Brainerd, 1989). This is the origin of the stigma and the subsequent avoidance.

friFast forward to the 19th and 20th centuries and you'll find -n't popping up everywhere in writing - not just in plays and novels, but also in personal letters, newspapers, and various descriptions. In the 21st century, -n't is ubiquitous. Proscribing its use in formal writing is a pedantic and outdated view. In reality, these forms are not only efficient but also reflect the natural rhythm of today's language. Their use should be embraced, not shunned.

anchor: Negation > Language change, grammaticalization, and exaptation > Jespersen's cycle > Stages six and seven
text:
Figure: Stylized illustration of Jespersen's cycle from Old English to a possible future English.

And still the contractions keep contracting. The /t/ on the end of can't is hardly audible most of the time, and the only way to distinguish between it and its positive form is the difference in their vowels (/kən/ vs /kæn/). Don't with wanna is a truncated /doʊ/, and, together with know, we get I dunno, the negator and verb fused into a single word, little more than a prepended d sound. (In fact, you can just sort of intone the standard pitches of I don't know without so much as moving your lips and you'll usually be understood.)

This brings up the possibility that we've cycled back to stage 2, where we're reaching for another element to shore up the negative. It could be anything, but perhaps, this time it will be anything. It's possible to imagine a future English in which don't is diminished to the point where people start using anything obligatorily, where it starts to have its central meaning bleached away, and where it becomes the new negator.

The stressed syllable in anything is /ˈɛ/, so perhaps, this new 'not' is reduced to en. Is it likely? I think en, but we'll have to wait and see.

anchor: Negation > Language change, grammaticalization, and exaptation > Jespersen's cycle > Stages six and seven > Box: Check your understanding: Jespersen's cycle
text:
Before moving on, test your grasp of language change:

- Can you explain why phrase-initial negators like ne are vulnerable to disappearance?

- What role did do-support play in stabilizing English negation?

- Do you see evidence of stage 5-6 contraction in your own speech (e.g., dunno, reduced can't)?

anchor: Negation > Language change, grammaticalization, and exaptation > Jespersen's cycle > Summary
text:
We all know that language changes. Some of us may have the pre-modern idea that we speak a fallen language, an idea rooted in the concept of a golden age or a primordial state of grace from which English has since degenerated, though no one is really sure what that was or when it prevailed. I hope this stylized history and weak attempt at prognostication suggest to you that language change too is rule-governed, natural, and eternal, not simply the accumulated rust of a once gleaming metal exposed to the elements. It is decidedly not the case that one can simply look at the way English is today and discern some kind of logical structure, but the structure is there, and the logic behind it can be uncovered.

I've seen many teachers say that English is crazy or illogical. It isn't. Like any language, it's a wonderful, complex, massively-multi-player game with many interacting logics. Calling English crazy or arbitrary when people are learning it (whether learning to speak it or learning to teach it) is counterproductive. Telling learners that English is illogical can foster unnecessary apprehension or even disillusionment. Students don't have to know the history of every word and grammatical system, but, for teachers, understanding something about the structure of language change and the forces driving it reveals that there are reasons and explanations if we're interested in looking for them. It helps us to approach teaching and learning with a sense of curiosity and discovery, rather than frustration and confusion.

The most important idea behind this potted history and projected future of negation is that forms come and go for reasons. Many of the errors that English-language learners make stem from those same reasons, and the mistakes often recapitulate plausible past or future forms of the language; they follow the logic that drives language change. Recall how pease became peas (Section )? It was a mistake, but it made so much sense to so many that it overwhelmed the original form. Likewise, students' mistakes are often insightful and motivated, not foolish or risible.

This is not an attempt to justify an anything-goes attitude. [ch:standards]Standard English is real, and students of English will mostly want to learn the Standard variety. Patterns like I no go should be recognized as the mistakes they are, but they should not be cause for frustration or distress on your part or your students'. When language learners make the correct choice, respect them for their insight into the peculiar way of looking at the world shared by English speakers. For your part, understanding how the language works often provides insight into where the confusion is coming from. It allows teachers to point out analogous aspects that are already familiar or that may be the next points of departure, and to suggest ways to improve.

anchor: Negation > Scope
text:
The scope of a problem is how big it is, how many things it impacts. A small-scope project might involve developing a single mobile application for a local business, while a large-scope project could be constructing a multi-functional smart city infrastructure. The scope of an investigation is the breadth and diversity of the questions asked, while a law's scope refers to the range of activities, individuals, or situations it covers. In, the multiplication operator has scope only over and, while in, it has scope over and. In the realm of programming, the scope of a variable dictates where the variable can be accessed or modified within the code.

Scope is like that in linguistics too. If one element has scope over some part of a clause or phrase, then it has a semantic effect on that part. Take the negative sentence I didn't finish my homework because I was at the park. What is within the scope of negation? In the most obvious reading, the homework was not completed, and the reason for my failure to do so was that I was at the park. Here the scope of negation includes only finish my homework. That's what didn't happen. But there is a second reading in which everything after didn't is in the scope of negation. Under this reading, I'm negating the whole claim that I finished my homework because I was at the park. In other words, it was not being at the park that resulted in my homework getting finished.

Or consider (). In (a) many is within the scope of negation: the number of friends who came is small. In (b) many is outside the scope of negation: the number of friends who did not come is large.

[]Not many friends came.

[]Many friends didn't come.

These may seem like the same thing, but if you have only a few friends, then (a) could be true while (b) would be false, a semantic difference.

anchor: Negation > Scope > Scope and modal auxiliary verbs
text:
Interestingly, modal auxiliary verbs have different implications for scope of negation. Consider ().

2

[]You can do it.'you're able to'

[]You can't do it.'you're unable to'

[]You must do it.'you're obliged to'

[]You mustn't do it.'you're obliged to not'

If you can do something, you're able to do it. There are two ways to negate this: 1) you are unable to do it, and 2) you're able not to do it, to avoid doing it. The only way to read can't in () is the first way, with ability negated. So here we say that negation has scope over the modal; can do it is negated.

In the rare case that you want to use can to say you're able to avoid doing it, then the normal way is to say you can not do it (e.g., It's OK, you can not go to school today.), with a deliberate pause before not.

When we go through the same analysis with must, we get a different result. If you must do something, you're obliged to do it. There are again two ways to negate this: 1) you are not obliged to do it, a lack of obligation, and 2) you're obliged not to do it, a prohibition. The only way to read mustn't in () is the second way, as a prohibition. So here we say that negation does not have scope over the modal; only do it is negated, not must.

Here, there is no simple way to negate must without resorting to completely different constructions or vocabulary, such as it is not the case that you must do that or more simply you're not obliged to.

Mustn't (or must not) is unusual here. Most of the negative modal auxiliary verbs are like can't, which is to say their modal meaning falls within the scope of negation.

anchor: Negation > Scope > Scope and modal auxiliary verbs > Box: Quick self-check: scope
text:
Test your understanding of scope with these examples:

- In Not many students came, what has scope over what?

- Why does You can't do it mean 'inability' rather than 'ability to not do it'?

- What's the scope difference in I didn't call because I was angry vs the reading where the reason itself is negated?

anchor: Negation > Types of negation
text:
There is an appealing, but ultimately incorrect sense that scope of negation is related to types of negation: clausal (including verbal non-verbal) negation, sub-clausal negation, and affixal negation. But it is not the case that clausal negation has scope over the whole clause or that affixal negation has scope only over a word. (See Chapter for more on affixation and morphology.) Still, it's useful to understand the different types of negation because of how it affects other parts of the sentence.

The seeming relationship is due to the intuition that, if a negative marker lacks scope over a verb, the verb, and therefore the clause, is not negative. And yet we've already seen that can't and mustn't scope differently. I assure you that both of the (i) examples in () are examples of verbal negation, which means they're examples of clausal negation even though their scope is quite different.

How can this be? Once again, it's because of the difference between syntax and semantics. Scope is a semantic concept, while the different types of negation are syntactic types. Semantically, can't and mustn't are different. Syntactically, they're the same.

Figure shows these syntactic types. (Pragmatic negation, discussed in Section, is a semantic phenomenon and doesn't belong in this taxonomy, another case of one label naming two things; see the box on p..)

Figure: Syntactic types of negation

anchor: Negation > Types of negation > Clausal negation
text:
[4]
Clausal negation makes a clause syntactically negative. It can be realized as verbal negation, as in the examples in (),

[]It isn't good.[negative form of auxiliary]

[]It doesn't look good.[negative form of auxiliary]

[]It is not good.[adverb]

[]It is never good.[adverb]

[]It is rarely good.[adverb]

or non-verbal negation, as in the examples in ().

[]No illness is good.[determinative]

[]I know neither manager.[determinative]

A key syntactic difference emerges when there is no auxiliary verb present. Verbal negation requires do support in such cases (b), while non-verbal negation does not (). In other words, the difference results in different syntax.

Returning to the idea of scope, the negative markers in () have scope only over the NP in which they function as a determiner: no illness and neither manager. They don't have scope over the verb, let alone the whole clause. And yet, the clauses are still syntactically negative. The reason isn't clear yet, but I'll explain in Section after dealing with the other types of negation.

anchor: Negation > Types of negation > Sub-clausal negation
text:
Sub-clausal negation refers to positive clauses that include negation, as in ().

[]I saw him not for the first time.

[]Not coincidentally, they won.

[]We ended up going nowhere.

In (a), this is quite clear: I did indeed see him, and it wasn't the first time. Similarly, in (b), they did indeed win, and it wasn't coincidental. (c), though, seems negative: In the end, we didn't go anywhere. But the main clause of (c) is not about going but about ending up, and we did indeed end up with some result, even if it was a non-peripatetic one. Though it might seem like clausal negation, it is sub-clausal.

Sub-clausal negation may be confused with non-verbal negation. After all, the negated elements in () are not VPs. But, in non-verbal negation, the clause is negative. In sub-clausal negation, it is positive. Again, I'll explain this claim in Section.

In case you were wondering, clausal and sub-clausal negation can be combined, as in ().

[]Not coincidentally, they didn't win.

Not coincidentally exhibits sub-clausal negation, and they didn't win is clausal negation of the verbal subtype.

anchor: Negation > Types of negation > Affixal negation
text: Unlike clausal or sub-clausal negation, which modify the meaning of clauses or phrases, affixal negation directly alters individual words. Affixal negation represents a morphological process (see Section ) where prefixes are attached to words, particularly adjectives and nouns, to negate their original meanings. Common prefixes used in affixal negation include un- (unfortunately, unidentified, unlike, unable, etc.), in- (independent, inevitable, invisible, insane, etc.), and non- (non-profit, nonfiction, non-stop, nonexistent, etc.). Negative suffixes are rare but include -less (homeless, endless, useless, etc.), -free (carefree, tax-free, fat-free, etc.), and -proof (waterproof, bulletproof, foolproof, etc.).

anchor: Negation > Types of negation > Pragmatic negation
text:
Lastly, pragmatic negation conveys its meaning indirectly, as seen in She finished the race, albeit slowly, where the slowness implies she was not fast. Other constructions like these are given in (). These are not typically the stuff of English-language classes. Perhaps they should be, but I would want to look at the frequencies of each form and consider the opportunity costs (Section ) before I invested my students' precious time in them. (For more on how expressions convey meaning beyond their literal content, see Chapter on pragmatics.)

Like hell we will.

Fat chance that will happen.

You'll take it over my dead body.

He'll clean his room when pigs fly.

In your dreams will I ever lend you the car again.

As if I would ever trust him.

Yeah, right, like you're going to start working out every day.

Good luck with getting that proposal approved by tomorrow.

anchor: Negation > Polarity items > Non-affirmative items
text:
We're now ready to answer the question raised in Section: how do we tell if a clause is positive or negative? One way is through syntactic tests using non-affirmative items. These items work in interrogative and negative environments, but they're often ungrammatical in affirmative environments. See ().

[]Does that make sense at all?[interrogative]

[]That doesn't make sense at all.[negative]

[*]That makes sense at all.[positive]

[]Does that ever make sense?[interrogative]

[]That doesn't ever make sense.[negative]

[*]That ever makes sense.[positive]

[]Does that make sense yet?[interrogative]

[]That doesn't make sense yet.[negative]

[*]That makes sense yet.[positive]

Overlap between interrogatives and negatives
If it hasn't already hit you, take a moment to think about how negation and interrogatives may be connected before going on.

Just as most interrogative clauses need do support without an auxiliary in the corresponding declarative (e.g., They speak English. Do they speak English?), a negative clause needs do support if there's no auxiliary in the corresponding affirmative clause (e.g., They speak English. They do not speak English?).

Now, we've seen another area of overlap: non-affirmative items are natural in both negative and interrogative clauses.

So, if you can add at all, ever, or yet, you are unlikely to have a positive clause. Let's apply these tests to the examples from Section:

[]Not many friends came yet.[negative]

[]Many friends didn't come at all.[negative]

As you can see, this applies both to verbal negation () and non-verbal negation ().

[]It isn't ever good.[negative]

[]It isn't any good at all.[negative]

[*]It is ever good.[positive]

[*]It is any good at all.[positive]

[]No illness is ever good.[negative]

[*]An illness is ever good.[positive]

[]I know neither manager yet.[negative]

[*]I know both managers yet.[positive]

Perhaps surprisingly, this holds even for approximate negators like the adverb rarely and the determinative few, as () shows.

[]I rarely ever go.[negative]

[*]I ever go.[positive]

[]Few problems ever persist.[negative]

[*]Many problems ever persist.[positive]

At all, even, and yet are by no means the only non-affirmative items. Others include the italicized words in ().

[]I haven't tried much.[negative]

[*]I have tried much.[positive]

[]I haven't had anything to drink.[negative]

[*]I have had anything to drink.[positive]

[]She didn't touch a drop.[negative]

[?]She touched a drop.[positive]

[]He wouldn't lift a finger to help.[negative]

[?]He would lift a finger to help.[positive]

To be clear, non-affirmative items are not ungrammatical in all positive environments, (Israel (2011) argues that sentences like ( and ) are ungrammatical. Me, I'm not so sure.) as () shows. It's just that there are limits, certain constructions, environments, and meanings for which they won't work.

[]I think literally anything would please her.[positive]

[]That's much better.[positive]

Non-affirmative items in other languages
If you find the concept of non-affirmative items intriguing, it's worth noting that such linguistic features are by no means limited to English. In fact, it's likely that they occur in most languages. Here's a range of examples.

Japanese

Hitokoto mo ittenai

one.word even say-neg.pst

'I didn't say a single word.'

Nishnaabemwin (Valentine, 2001)

anchor: Negation > Polarity items > Non-affirmative items
text:
Gaa wii gnabaj maamda.

maybe fut be.done at all

'Perhaps nothing at all can be done.'

22

Shilluk (p.c. Bert Remijsen Otto Gwado, 16 Dec. 2024)

Já pāa pwốt̪ ɪ̀ɪ ɛ́n kɪ̂nɪ̀ tɛ́k.

pr.1sg nge.pres beat:pass prp pr.3sg quot at all

'He is definitely not beating me.'

Persian

Yek kalame ham nagoft

one word even neg.say.pst.3sg

'He didn't say even one word.'

Amharic

t'ɨqit-ɨm al-särra-m

little-foc neg-work.pst-foc

'He didn't work at all.'

Czech

Nechtěl jsem promarnit sebemenší šanci.

neg.want.pst.1sg aux.1sg waste self.smallest chance

'I didn't want to waste even the slightest chance.'

Irish

Ní fhaca sí solas ná solas.

neg see.pst 3sg.f light or light

'She saw no light whatsoever.'

anchor: Negation > Polarity items > Positive polarity items
text:
Positive polarity items (PPIs) are the yin to the yang of non-affirmative items. While the latter flourish in negative and interrogative environments, PPIs naturally occur in positive, affirmative contexts. These items, such as some, already, and somewhat, sound odd or outright ungrammatical in negative sentences. Consider (), where a fair chance is intended as roughly 'good odds'.

[]I already know some of the answers.[positive]

[?]I don't already know some of the answers.[negative]

[]She has a fair chance of winning.[positive]

[?]She doesn't have a fair chance of winning.[negative]

In the positive examples, the positive polarity items are perfectly at home, but they are far less natural with negation. Yet, just like non-affirmative items, these items aren't entirely limited to one environment; they can sometimes appear in negative clauses (e.g., Before you know it, there won't be any left).

In fact, if anything, they're less polarity sensitive than the negative items. "While the licensing conditions for [non-affirmative items] are overt, those for [positive polarity items] are not: [non-affirmative items] must be triggered; [positive polarity items] need only avoid being anti-triggered" (Israel, 2011). To be sure, though, they are more likely in positive environments.

Other positive polarity items include: (From Israel (2011).)

a couple of, a little bit, a thing or two, a whole shitload, also, before you know it, by far, dribs and drabs, galore, scads, so, some, the whole shebang, too, utterly, etc.

anchor: Negation > Polarity items > Positive polarity items > Box: Pattern recognition: polarity items
text:
Notice the systematic pattern in polarity sensitivity:

- Non-affirmative items (any, ever, yet) are licensed by downward-entailing contexts (negation, questions, conditionals, few)

- Positive polarity items (some, already, a fair chance) resist these same contexts

- Both can appear in unexpected environments: any in I think anything would work and some in Before you know it, there won't be some left

Why might this pattern exist? Consider how information flows up and down in these contexts.

anchor: Negation > The meaning of negatives > Asymmetries
text: He's not third in line means just that, plain and simple: 'he is anything but number three'. He could be first or second; he could be 512th. In contrast, not ten meters from me means 'less than 10m from me' (Jespersen, 2025). There is an asymmetry there, that is bound up with the structure of the scale. He spends 4,000 a year and He lives on 4,000 a year have almost the same meaning, but He doesn't spend 4,000 a year suggests he's thrifty, while He doesn't live on 4,000 a year suggests that 4,000 wouldn't be enough.

anchor: Negation > The meaning of negatives > Polarity interpretation
text:
You've gone home from your job in Tokyo, and you realize you don't have your wallet with you. You call your Japanese co-worker, and explain your situation, but you're told nobody knows anything about the wallet. In desperation, you ask 机の上に無い？ (tsukue no ue ni nai?, 'Isn't it on my desk?'), to which you get a crisp はい (hai). Hai is affirmative, approximating 'yes', but what does 'yes' mean here? What is being affirmed?

In English, whether you ask the question in the positive Is it on my desk? or negative Isn't it on my desk? yes is normally interpreted the same way: as an affirmation that the wallet is there.

In Japanese, on the other hand, the negatively posed 机の上に無い？ and the positively posed 机の上にある？ (tsukue no ue ni aru?, 'Is it on my desk?') require opposite responses affirming or denying the polarity of the question. Responding with はい to the negative formulation 机の上に無い？ means agreeing with the negative polarity of the question: hai means, 'Yes, it is not there.' In contrast, if you ask without the negative 机の上にある？ hai means, 'Yes, it is there.'

This shows that the meaning of a response isn't predictable from language alone, but partly determined by cultural expectations. And this isn't a uniquely English-Japanese difference. There are many languages that follow the English approach (e.g., French and German) and many that follow the Japanese (e.g., Irish and Hungarian).

In other cases, an affirmative response could mean, 'I see why you're asking that,' 'that's an interesting observation,' 'let me think about it,' or 'that might be so.' Even within English, a drawn-out yes or yeah could just mean 'Good idea, I'll go and check your desk.'

For these reasons, it's often useful, in situations where at least one party is not reasonably familiar with these potential quagmires, to avoid negatively phrased questions and to respond unambiguously. An answer like I see it or it's not there isn't susceptible to misunderstanding the way a simple yes or no is.

anchor: Negation > The meaning of negatives > Beyond simple polarity
text:
At this point, you might have formed a tidy generalization: Non-affirmative items like any and ever only appear in negative and interrogative contexts. But remember the counterexample habit from Chapter 1. When faced with such a clean rule, start hunting for exceptions.

Look again at Section. We saw that I think literally anything would please her is perfectly grammatical despite being positive. Why? The verb think isn't negative, the clause isn't interrogative, yet anything is licensed. Search for more patterns:

- If you see anything suspicious, call me. (conditional)

- She's the smartest person I've ever met. (superlative)

- I doubt he'll ever understand. (inherently negative predicate)

- Few people have ever climbed that mountain. (approximate negator)

- That's the last thing I'd ever do. (extremity expression)

These environments - conditionals, superlatives, inherently negative predicates like doubt and deny, approximate negators, and extremity expressions - all license non-affirmative items despite not being syntactically negative or interrogative. What unites them? They're all downward entailing contexts: if few people have climbed Mount Everest, then even fewer have climbed it barefoot. If that's the smartest person you've met, then it's also the smartest blonde person you've met.

The revised generalization: Non-affirmative items prefer downward-entailing contexts, which include but aren't limited to negation and questions. This more sophisticated understanding explains both the core cases and the apparent exceptions.

Now test this with yes/no responses across languages. Ask speakers of French, Japanese, or Mandarin how they'd answer negative questions. You'll discover that the English pattern of ignoring question polarity (yes = 'it is there' regardless of how asked) is far from universal. Some languages have three-way systems, others have special words for agreeing with negative propositions. Each discovery refines our understanding of how polarity works across human language.

Appropriate polarity items

- Choose a CEFR or CLB level and use the ideas from Chapter to identify three negative and three positive polarity items that are worth your students' time and attention. Identify three of each which are not.

anchor: Negation > Phrase structure > Verbal negation
text:
The phrase structures for clausal negation are very similar to those for positive constructions. Let's start with verbal negation with a nice simple clause: I can't wait in Figure.

Figure: Syntax trees for I can't wait and I do not ski.

This is exactly the same phrase structure as the positive clause I can wait, or she won't fail, or you should try. That's because can't is simply the negative form of can, won't of will, etc. (In the CGEL framework, the complement of the VP is not just a VP but a whole clause, so that wait is a verb, which is the head of a VP, which is the head of a clause, and that clause is the complement. This is a better theoretical analysis for reasons I won't go into, but to keep things simple, I'm going to fudge and just call it a VP in this book.) In fact, any clause with a pronoun + auxiliary verb + lexical verb would have this structure.

Similarly, there's nothing at all special about do support for negation. A clause like I do not ski has exactly the same structure (Figure again) as she is always skiing or you should really come.

The only slightly odd structure is negative imperatives, such as don't worry, don't go away, don't tell me what to do, don't be afraid, etc. These all require do support. Most auxiliary verbs are impossible here, and be, while possible, still requires do support (Figure ).

Figure: Syntax tree for Don't be afraid.

anchor: Negation > Phrase structure > Non-verbal negation
text:
This is the other kind of clausal negation, but again, there's no special structure. No and neither are just determiners. The fact that they are negative has no bearing on the phrase structure. The clauses in Figure could just as easily be for positive determiners like some, many, or all. Nor would it change for approximate negators like few, a few, or hardly any.

Figure: Syntax trees for No injuries were reported and Neither side has all the answers.

That's not to say there are no interesting constructions at all. There is a slightly older construction (COHA suggests its use is fading, while the Google Ngram Viewer shows a strong resurgence starting around 2000), the never + auxiliary verb + subject construction (e.g., never did he try). Here never has been fronted (like an interrogative phrase, see ), and this fronting has triggered subject-auxiliary inversion. As a result, we can expect a structure similar to that in Figure. The actual tree structure is shown in Figure (Nor did he have any idea is a similar example but with a coordinator. See Section.)

Figure: Syntax tree for Never did he try with subject-auxiliary inversion.

anchor: Negation > Phrase structure > Sub-clausal and affixal negation
text:
I expect you're getting the idea by now that negation poses very few phrase-structure issues. That happy situation continues to hold with sub-clausal negation. Consider the example in Figure from (). Nothing notable comes of adding the adverb not.

Figure: Syntax tree for I saw him not for the first time.

And, of course, affixal negation only changes the internal structure of various words; phrase structure is not affected.

anchor: Negation > Phrase structure > Phrase structure summary
text: The purpose of the trees, as I've said, is to help you see the syntax at a glance. The trees in this section all recapitulate structures you've seen in previous chapters, providing a good opportunity for review. Deliberate practice with drawing trees will be invaluable to you in improving your ability to categorize words and phrases and to understand phrase structure.

anchor: Negation > Summary
text: Negation follows the same logic as the rest of English syntax - scope, polarity licensing, phrase structure - even when the surface forms look irregular. The practical dividend is that learner errors in negation almost always recapitulate historical patterns or follow productive analogies. A student who says *I no go is applying a logic that drove English through several stages of Jespersen's cycle. Knowing that won't make the error correct, but it will keep you from treating it as random.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions
text:
why a name is carried

by many at once,
[0.5em]
why we don't just have

our own to carry.Laurie D. Graham, Calling It Back to Me

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Analyze complex subject properties including position, categories, denotation, and licensing constraints

- Understand subject dropping patterns in English speech and distinguish grammatical from ungrammatical omissions

- Identify determiner functions and distinguish between determinatives (category) and determiners (function)

- Recognize different types of objects and apply passive alternation tests to identify them

- Distinguish complements from modifiers in NPs and AdjPs, understanding their selection and licensing

- Analyze modifier types, positions, stacking patterns, and integrated vs supplementary distinction

- Recognize major English constructions (resultative, light verb, conditional coordination, etc.) and their form-meaning relationships

- Apply diagnostic tests to identify syntactic functions and categorize complex grammatical relationships

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions
text:
Tests used in this chapter
This chapter introduces several diagnostic techniques for analyzing categories, functions, and constructions:

- Subject-verb agreement test: Change number/person of potential subjects to see which controls verb agreement

- Passive alternation test: Check if objects can become subjects in passive constructions: They reported a theft A theft was reported

- Pronoun case test: Verify subjects use nominative case (I, he, she) while objects use accusative (me, him, her)

- Determiner ordering test: Check if elements can appear in of the NOUN pattern (some of the people, many of the changes)

- Complement vs modifier test: Assess optionality, selection by head, and ability to stack multiple instances

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects
text:
In everyday discourse, the term subject refers to topics of discussion, areas of study, focal points in visual arts, and even roles in sociopolitical contexts. In syntax, though, subject has a specialized technical meaning denoting one kind of function of a phrase (remember that one-word phrases are common), a special sub-type of complement.

There are no perfect definitions (see Section ), but we can provide a set of characteristics.

While Section introduced the basic syntactic roles and behaviors of subjects, here we'll consider some exceptions and variations. The subject function in a clause typically exhibits the following characteristics:

- The subject is a phrase (often an NP, but can also be a clause, VP, or even PP) that occurs before the head verb in a standard declarative clause.

- It appears after an auxiliary verb in most interrogative clauses.

- Semantically, the subject often denotes the agent/actor in active clauses, but can also represent the patient/recipient in passive constructions or an instrument or other role. (A patient is the entity that undergoes an action or is affected by it, as opposed to the agent who performs the action. For example, in The dog chased the cat, the cat is the patient.)

- It's omitted or found in the by-phrase of any related passive clause.

- If the subject is a pronoun, it's typically in the nominative case (see Table ).

- The subject is licensed by the head verb, with different verbs allowing different types of subjects based on their semantic properties.

- It usually controls the person and number agreement of a present-tense verb, though features like collective vs plural interpretation can impact agreement patterns.

To illustrate these characteristics more concretely, consider the following examples.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > Position of subjects relative to verbs
text:
In each example, the subject is italicized and its verb is in small caps.

[]They greeted us.

[]The cat sleeps on the porch.

[]Quickly, I need to go to the store.

[]Did you meet him at noon?

[]Guys, can you help me?

[]If it works, I'll be pleased.

Consider, though, the following constructions (discussed in Section ).

[]Up popped the toast

[]In the pond live thousands of tadpoles.
subsubsection_title: Position of subjects relative to verbs

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > Categories of subjects
text:
In the examples above, all the subjects are regular NPs. Here are some others.

[]Whether it does is unclear.[Clause]

[]Here, to believe wholeheartedly is not always a virtue.[Infinitival VP]

[]Studying grammar seems useful.[Participial VP]

[]What you know can help.[Relative NP]

[]After 2:00 is too late.[PP]

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > Denotation
text:
Although the (syntactic) subject is typically the (semantic) topic, it needn't be. We can nominate other topics with phrases such as when it comes to, as for, as far as goes, speaking of, etc., in which cases, the topic of the sentence would not be its subject unless they happen to refer to the same thing (e.g., As for me, I hadn't thought about it).

Subjects denoting actors/agents:

[]As for the rest, I'll take them with me.

[]Who cooked dinner?

Subjects denoting patients/recipients:

[]The ball was hit over the fence, and the window got smashed.

[]We were given a choice.

Subjects denoting instruments and other roles:

[]The computer helped me speed up the analysis.

[]Studying grammar seems useful.

[]That the stars are visible surprises me.

[]The broom fell over.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > Active vs passive
text:
Not every active clause has a passive counterpart, but when one exists, the subject of the active clause is typically omitted in the passive, though it can appear in an optional by PP.

[]I dropped it. It was dropped.

[]We told them. They were told.

[]Anybody can use the room. The room can be used by anybody.

In (c), don't think that anybody remains the subject in the passive clause. Instead, the room becomes the subject, and anybody is the object in the by PP.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > Pronoun case
text:
Pronoun subjects are overwhelmingly in the nominative case: I, you, he, she, we, they. See the examples above. In more formal participial constructions, the possessive may be used; the accusative is now commoner, but may sound too informal:

[]Their/Them asking usis a huge honor.

[]His/Him hiding itwas a problem.

[]Your/You saying sodoes not make it true.

In a to infinitival VP, the subject appears in the accusative case after for. It's not for me to say. It's time for us to go. I've been waiting for them to ask. Is there a way for him to try again?

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > How subjects are licensed
text:
The subject is a kind of complement licensed by the head verb, but instead of following the verb inside the VP, as most such complements do, it typically appears inside the clause but outside of the VP: Subject Head VP.

Verbs almost universally license NP subjects. The other types of subjects are sensitive to selection by the licensing verbs, and only a few verbs license those subjects.

A whether clause is licensed as the subject by only a few lexical verbs such as affect, bother, concern, depend, influence, interest, matter, and worry. This construction is also mildly polarity sensitive, preferring negative contexts (see ). Compare

[]Whether it is true or not doesn't worry me much.[negative]

[?]Whether it is true or not worries me.[positive]

[*]Whether it rained last night explains the flood.[unlicensed]

Weather verbs, along with be in weather contexts, license dummy it (see Section ).

[]It snows here most of the time.

[]It's raining.

[]It should be sunny tomorrow.

Verbs of appearance and existence license dummy there:

[]There's a pen in the drawer.

[]There seem to be three options.

[]There appeared a gossamer web.

[*]There disappeared a gossamer web.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Some examples > subject-verb agreement for number and person
text:
Agreement is mainly an issue with present-tense verbs for third-person singular subjects. It also affects the various forms of be in both the present and past tenses.

[]I'm here.

[]We're here.

[]She's here.

[]You seem kind.

[]The changes work well.

[]Mia and Iman go to U of T.

If you're wondering whether a particular NP is the subject, try changing its number and person (see also Box ). For example, let's say you're wondering about the subject in of all the girls who I have loved,. You've decided it could be all the girls (third-person plural), who (third-person singular), or I (first-person singular). You can try the following: of the girl who I have loved (no change in agreement), of all the girls I have loved (no change in agreement), and of all the girls who he has loved (have changes to has). This is good evidence that the subject is I.

Unfortunately, it's not a perfect test. Other elements may control agreement. This can be some dependent in the subject, as in A number of people have arrived. Or with dummy there, it's the complement of the head verb: There was something on the floor. There were some things on the floor.
subsubsection_title: subject-verb agreement for number and person

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Dropping subjects
text:
In many languages it is common, and even sometimes obligatory, to omit the subject. For instance, Italian examples like () lack a subject, but the verb form tells you that it's first-person plural.

Abbiamo iniziato a studiare la grammatica.

have-1st-pl started to study the grammar

'We started studying grammar.'

Japanese often omits a subject, and without either number or person marking on the verb, the interpretation is entirely contextual.

食べた

tabeta

eat-past

'(I) ate.' (or somebody else)

行きたい?

ikitai

go-pres-desirative

'Do (you) want to go?' (or somebody else)

English subjects, though, are generally considered to be obligatory. Certainly, in formal writing, every main clause (apart from imperatives) has a subject. But in speech, even if the verb doesn't tell you who the subject is, it's often clear that it's the speaker or the addressee. In such cases, it's common to drop the subject I in English too, as in ().

[]Hope not.

[]Think so.

[]Dunno.

[]Can't find my keys.

[]Woke up late, ran out the door, and missed the bus.

For the addressee, it's also common to drop everything before the nucleus (Pre and subject together) in interrogatives, as in ().

[]Feeling OK?

[]Ever been to Paris?

[]Know what I mean?

[]Want some coffee?

In fact, it's possible to drop just the auxiliary and use you in each example in ().

Even third-person examples can work if the context is right, as in ().
