[]A: Where did Ying go? B: Went to the store.

[]A: Why isn't Patel playing today? B: Got injured during practice.

[]A: Did the kids finish their homework? B: Finished it ages ago.

Even in main clauses, third-person subjects are regularly dropped when the clause is the second or subsequent coordinate in a coordination, although these are arguably coordinated VPs. Whichever analysis you prefer, constructions like those in () are very common in English.

[]They turned around and they made their way back.

[]I opened it and I took a look.

[]A nurse came into the room and she asked Bill how he was doing

What this shows is that subject dropping is actually quite common when the subject is easily understood. In some languages, like Italian, it's easier because of verb endings. In others, like Japanese and Korean, it's just expected that the listener will follow the topic closely and work it out. There's more about this in Section.

It's common for English-language teachers to insist that their students produce "full sentences." This is misguided and can be confusing. It gives many students the idea that, to be polite, their English needs to sound stiff, unnatural, and disconnected. If they follow their teachers' admonishments, they may end up seeming more alien than if they used the drop forms.

That said, students commonly drop subjects where no expert English speaker would. This kind of subject dropping is ungrammatical and can be quite confusing to the audience. It should be discouraged when there is an appropriate opportunity for form-focused feedback.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Dropping subjects > Box: Check your understanding: subject properties
text:
Test your grasp of subject complexity:

- Can you explain why There were some books has some books controlling agreement, not there?

- In what contexts is subject dropping acceptable in English? When is it problematic?

- How would you help a student who says *Is very difficult instead of It's very difficult?

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Subjects > Conclusion
text: Subjects exhibit a range of characteristics, including their position relative to verbs, the categories that can function as subjects, their typical denotation, their behavior in active and passive constructions, and their role in subject-verb agreement. The topic of subject dropping highlights the differences between English and many other languages, while also showcasing the often-unacknowledged flexibility of subject omission in informal English speech. By examining these properties and considering counter-examples, we can better anticipate the kinds of problems our students will face.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function
text:
It's time to flip things around. For this section, we'll start with a bunch of examples, and then it will be up to you to draw out the characteristics.

Before we start, though, a quick reminder: determinative, like adjective, is a lexical category. Determiner, like modifier, is a syntactic function.

Hardly a day goes by without progress.

I saw him only a few days ago.

We've only got a little time.

All good people are welcome.

Do you want any fresh coffee?

The other two rooms had windows at both ends.

It happened each year.

Most people work about eight hours a day.

My new job takes far less than 40 hours per week.

How many times have I walked down this street where you lived?

Many more changes are coming.

Neither definition seems correct.

There is almost no water left.

Give me that book, not this one.

I'm going to take these sandwiches for my lunch.

It's different for us women.

Which ones do you like?

Even your critics say you're right this time.

Canada's population is growing quickly.

He's head of the World Health Organization's AIDS program.

- In which phrasal category/ies are determiners dependents?

- Where do determiners appear relative to the head?

- Where do determiners appear relative to modifiers?

- How does number and countability impact the need for a determiner?

- What is the role of determiners regarding definiteness?

- What kind of agreement do we find for determiners?

- What are the phrase types that most commonly function as determiners?

Determiners: a brief history

The very idea that determiners are something distinct from modifiers wasn't recognized until about 1933, at least, not as it applies to English. In his seminal 1933 work Language, Leonard BloomfieldBloomfield, Leonard introduced the term determiner to refer to a specific syntactic function performed by what he called "limiting adjectives."

Bloomfield observed that certain noun expressions, such as house or big house, are always accompanied by a determiner (Bloomfield, 1984). Modifiers, in contrast, are optional. This is sort of like an anthropologist realizing that some of these ancient arrowheads they'd known about for hundreds of years were actually spear points.

Despite almost 100 years having passed since Bloomfield's recognition of determiner as a distinct syntactic function, most pedagogical grammars remain confused on the point. If they use the term, they apply it indistinctly both to the category of determinatives and the function of determiners. It doesn't really matter what terms you use, but it seems very useful to me to maintain terminological hygiene, which is to say not to use one term for two distinct concepts.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function > Position and ordering within NP
text:
Determiners appear only in NPs, typically preceding all other dependents within the NP. For example:

- the big red ball

- some really interesting books

- every student in the class

There are cases where there is a predeterminer modifier, but these are rare and I've never been asked about them by ESL students. For completeness, it's worth mentioning that all functions as just such a predeterminer modifier in all the books, with the functioning as the determiner.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function > Categories that function as determiners
text:
The determiner function is a determinative phrase (DP) in the vast majority of cases. The remaining cases are almost all possessive NPs, either pronouns like my, their, whose, etc. or NPs headed by common or proper nouns, such as the last two examples in ().

A possessive NP often includes its own determiner, as in the two examples in Figure. My uncle's dog has my as the determiner in the NP my uncle's, which, in turn, functions as the determiner in the larger NP. In this morning's headlines, singular this is the determiner in the singular NP this morning's, not in the plural NP *this headlines.

Figure: Tree diagrams showing nested determiner structures in my uncle's dog and this morning's headlines.

Beyond these possessive structures, certain interrogative and relative words - the determinatives which and what, and the pronoun whose - can also function as determiners (see Sections and ).

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function > Agreement and concord
text: Determiners often exhibit agreement with the head noun in terms of number and, in some cases, gender. This phenomenon is known as concord.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function > Agreement and concord > Number agreement
text:
Determiners typically agree with the head noun in terms of number:

- Singular: the book, this car, my friend

- Plural: the books, these cars, my friends

Some determiners, such as some, any, and no, can be used with both singular and plural nouns without any change in form.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Determiners as a syntactic function > Agreement and concord > Gender agreement
text:
In English, gender agreement is not as prevalent as in many other languages. Still, there are a few cases where determiners agree with the natural gender of the referent:

- Masculine: his book

- Feminine: her book

- Neuter: its book

It's important to note that gender agreement in English is primarily limited to personal pronouns and possessive determiners. Most determiners do not exhibit gender distinctions.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Objects as complements
text:
Objects are a special kind of complement in VPs and PPs. They're overwhelmingly NPs, and they differ from other types of VP complements in that the same NP can usually function as the subject in the passive construction, though not always as () shows.

[]Somebody reported a theft.[object]

[]A theft was reported.[subject in passive]

[]I weigh seventy kilograms.[object]

[*]Seventy kilograms is weighed by me.[subject in passive]

[]It became a problem.[non-object complement]

[*]A problem was become.[subject in passive]

Most objects in PPs can't undergo this same alternation, though it is sometimes possible.

[]She went to Toronto.[object in PP]

[*]Toronto was gone to.[subject in passive]

[]Someone broke into the house.[object in PP]

[]The house was broken into.[subject in passive]

Verbs and prepositions that take objects are called transitive, while those that don't are intransitive. Some verbs are ditransitive: they take two objects, the first being the indirect object, and the second being the direct object.

[]I gave them indirect the books direct.[two objects]

[]They were given the books.[indir obj becomes subj in passive]

[]The books were given to them.[dir obj becomes subj in passive]

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements
text: Section deals with complements in VPs and Section with complements in PPs, but other phrases also allow complements. Here we'll examine complementation in noun phrases and adjective phrases, which were only briefly addressed in Chapter.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in noun phrases
text: Noun phrases can take several types of complements, which differ from modifiers in being more tightly integrated with the head noun and often corresponding to arguments of related verbs.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in noun phrases > Preposition phrase complements
text:
Many nouns take PP complements, often with specific prepositions. This is particularly common with deverbal nouns (nouns derived from verbs), where the PP complement often corresponds to the complement of the related verb:

[]her belief in ghosts[cf. believe in ghosts]

[]their reliance on fossil fuels[cf. rely on fossil fuels]

[]the attack on the fortress[cf. attack the fortress]

When the verb licenses a direct object, the related noun usually licenses an of PP.

[]management of the city[cf. manage the city]

[]the examination of evidence[cf. examine evidence]

[]their collection of artifacts[cf. collect artifacts]

Other nouns select PP complements unrelated to any verbal source:

[]a solution to the problem

[]access to healthcare

[]an addiction to sugar

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in noun phrases > Clausal and verb phrase complements
text:
Some nouns, particularly those related to speech and mental states, can take clausal complements:

[]the claim that Earth is flat

[]her suggestion that we leave early

Others, also related to speech and mental states, can take to-infinitival VP complements.

[]the desire to succeed

[]the decision to resign

Again, the type of complement often mirrors what's possible with the related verb:

[]She claimed that Earth is flat the claim that Earth is flat

[]They decided to resign the decision to resign

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in adjective phrases
text: Adjectives can also take various types of complements, which differ from modifiers in being selected by specific adjectives.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in adjective phrases > Preposition phrase complements
text:
Many adjectives select specific prepositions:

[]afraid of spiders

[]similar to his brother

[]proud of her achievements

[]dependent on fossil fuels

[]interested in linguistics

The choice of preposition is rarely predictable and needs to be learned with each adjective. Compare:

[]interested in grammar vs keen on grammar

[*]keen in grammar vs *interested on grammar

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Complementation in adjective phrases > Clausal and verb phrase complements
text:
Some adjectives can take clausal and VP complements. Again, these tend to be those related to speech or mental states:

[]happy that you came

[]certain that it would work

[]eager to help

[]busy preparing

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Other complements > Conclusion
text: While verb complementation may be more familiar to students, the complement system extends beyond VPs to other major phrasal categories. Both NPs and AdjPs employ a variety of complementation strategies to express relationships between heads and their arguments. The key distinction from modifiers lies in complements being selected by specific heads and often being obligatory for the phrase's grammaticality or complete meaning. Understanding these patterns helps explain both the syntactic structure of English phrases and the semantic relationships between their components.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers
text:
Modifiers are dependents that augment, qualify, or restrict the meaning of their head. Unlike complements, they aren't licensed by specific heads and are generally optional. Let's look at some examples with the modifiers italicized:

[]The tall woman in red smiled broadly.

[]The surprisingly difficult exam we took stressed everyone quite badly.

[]She works very efficiently in the morning.

[]The old book on the shelf that I mentioned belongs to Pat.

Several key characteristics define modifiers:

- They're typically optional; removing them may affect meaning but usually preserves grammaticality (but consider poorly in The results compare poorly to last year's.)

- Multiple modifiers can stack up with the same head

- They can appear in various positions relative to the head

- Different phrasal categories can function as modifiers

- Unlike complements, you can usually add more

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Position of modifiers
text:
The position of modifiers varies by the category of both the modifier and the phrase it's modifying. In NPs, we find both premodifiers (before the head) and postmodifiers (after the head):

[]two bright red cars[premodifiers]

[]the book on the table with the blue cover[postmodifiers]

[]the young student from Toronto[pre- and postmodifiers]

In clause structure, modifiers can generally appear in various positions:

[]Quickly, she answered the phone.[initial]

[]She quickly answered the phone.[pre-verbal]

[]She answered the phone quickly.[final]

Some modifiers have strong preferences for certain positions though. Compare:

[]The students merely wanted to help.

[?]The students wanted to help merely.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Categories functioning as modifiers
text:
Different phrasal categories can function as modifiers:

- AdjPs typically function as modifiers in NPs: the happy child

- AdvPs can modify any type of phrase except NPs: very quickly

- PPs can function as modifiers in most types of phrases: the book on the shelf

- Relative clauses function mostly as modifiers in NPs: the book that I bought

- Even NPs can sometimes function as modifiers: It snowed last week, I met her this morning, an old folks' home, the faculty office.

- DPs can also function as modifiers: It wasn't all that difficult, We drank almost all the water.

Table: Phrase Types as Modifiers

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Categories functioning as modifiers > Measure phrases
text:
A number of prepositions and adjectives take measure-phrase modifiers:

[]two years after the change

[]six months in

[]three kilometres down the road

[]two feet into the air

[]two meters tall

[]three years old

[]six feet deep

[]ten centimeters wide

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Stacking and ordering
text:
Multiple modifiers can stack up with the same head, but their order isn't random. In NPs, premodifying adjectives tend to follow patterns based on their semantic type:

[]The lovely old brass lamp[opinion-age-material]

[?]The brass old lovely lamp[non-standard order]

Similarly, different types of modifiers in clause structure tend to follow certain ordering principles:

[]She usually works efficiently in the morning at the office.

[?]She usually works at the office efficiently in the morning.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Integrated vs supplementary modifiers
text:
A key distinction in modification is between integrated and supplementary modifiers. Integrated modifiers are part of the larger phrase:

[]The student who scored highest won a prize.[integrated]

[]The books on the top shelf need dusting.[integrated]

Supplementary modifiers are simply added to the larger utterance with little regard for phrase structure. They're typically set off in speech with a slight pause, and in writing with commas, parentheses, or dashes:

[]Pat, who scored highest, won a prize.[supplementary relative]

[]The books, dusty and neglected, need sorting.[supplementary AdjP]

[]The meeting, surprisingly, went well.[supplementary AdvP]

The distinction between integrated and supplementary modification has important structural consequences. Integrated modifiers can only appear where the phrase structure allows them: inside the phrase they're modifying. Supplementary modifiers, by contrast, can appear in various interpolated positions:

[]The professor, as everyone knew, had cancelled class.

[]The professor had, rather unexpectedly, cancelled class.

[]The professor had cancelled class, as it turned out.

By contrast, while integrated modifiers can stack up inside a single phrase, supplementary modifiers tend to appear one at a time, each adding a separate comment:

[]The tall young energetic teacher...[integrated stack]

[]The teacher, quite young, surprisingly energetic, arrived.
[supplementary series]

In terms of meaning, integrated modifiers help identify or characterize the meaning of their head, while supplementary modifiers add parenthetical information or speaker commentary. This difference is particularly clear with relative clauses:

[]Students who finish early may leave.[integrated - defines subset]

[]The students, who finished early, left.[supplementary - adds info]

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Modifiers > Integrated vs supplementary modifiers > Box: Pattern recognition: modifiers vs complements
text:
Notice the systematic differences that help distinguish modifiers from complements:

- Optionality: Modifiers can usually be removed (the tall student the student), while complements often can't (proud of her *proud)

- Selection: Complements are selected by specific heads (afraid OF, not *afraid TO), while modifiers are more flexible

- Stacking: Multiple modifiers can pile up (the tall young energetic teacher), but complements are typically limited

Why might this distinction matter for teaching grammar effectively?

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions
text:
Language is not just a collection of individual words, and it's not just the words that hold meaning. Consider phrases like come here, think about it, or don't touch that. The form conveys something more than the sum of the word meanings. Try to take a step back and see it in a new light.

First of all, did you notice that the phrases address 'you'. If English had evolved a little differently, perhaps think about it could mean 'we think about it', 'she thinks about it', or 'they think about it'. In fact, in Japanese, for instance, the closest structural translation, dropping the subject, often means 'I'll think about it'. But not in English.

For us, the word you is absent and yet the directive is aimed at the person being spoken to. It is the form - the grammatical structure itself - that says 'you', and even a slightly different structure (to think about it or thinks about it) means something different.

Secondly, I called it a directive, and it absolutely is. This may seem obvious, but the phrase means both 'I want you to do this' and, at the same time, means 'I believe that our social and contextual relationship is such that I can reasonably expect you to comply'. It includes an element of compulsion (see Section ).

The form of think about it didn't have to convey a directive. It's close to a request, but not quite. It could, perhaps, have been a declaration, an exclamation, or a question. It could even have been a conditional = 'if you think about it'.

This may seem rather implausible to you. You probably wonder how think about it could possibly be conditional, and find the notion utterly preposterous. But not only could it convey conditionality, it does when part of a larger construction: think about it and you'll see what I mean (= 'if you think about it, you'll see what I mean').

When linguists speak about constructions, this is what they have in mind: not just some kind of grammatical structure but one that means something, a form-meaning pairing.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Conditional coordination
text:
One particularly striking example of a construction is the think about it example. I'll call this the conditional coordination construction.

[]go and you'll regret it

[]touch that and you'll burn yourself

What appears on the surface to be simple coordination with and actually expresses a conditional relationship: 'if you do X, then Y will happen.' The construction requires specific elements to work: the first coordinate must be an imperative and the second must express the predicted consequence. If we alter either requirement, the conditional meaning disappears:

[]Come closer and I'll tell you a secret.

[*]You came closer and I'll tell you a secret.

[]Come closer and tell me.[coordination only]

A closely related construction is what Peter Culicover (1970) called the one more construction, as in One more comment like that and I'm leaving. Again, the first coordinate can be interpreted conditionally, as 'if you make one more comment like that'.

The conditional-coordination construction is particularly useful for expressing warnings, threats, and promises. Like many constructions, it shows how English grammar can package complex meanings into conventional patterns that go well beyond the literal meanings of the words involved.

Another syntax-semantics mismatch

Culicover et al. (1997) made a beautiful observation about these constructions: while syntactically the structure appears to be coordination (and joining two equal clauses), semantically the first clause is actually subordinate to the second. This explains why go and you'll regret it means 'if you go, you'll regret it' rather than 'I direct you to go and subsequently you will regret it'. This syntax-semantics mismatch is yet one more example of how constructions can encode meaning patterns that diverge from what we might expect from their structure. It's also another reminder: believe in the fallacy of monosemy, and you'll regret it (see Section ).

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Resultative constructions
text:
In a resultative construction, an NP object and a following phrase express the result of the action denoted by the verb:

[]She wiped the table clean.

[]He hammered the metal flat.

[]It froze the lake solid.

The result phrase is usually an AdjP (as above) but can sometimes be a PP:

[]She tied her hair into braids.

[]They smoothed the dough to perfection.

[]He carved the wood down to size.

Importantly, the object NP must be affected by the action: we can't say *She drove the road flat or *He sang the baby hoarse. The result must also be a plausible outcome of the action: *She painted the wall tall is odd because painting doesn't cause height change.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Light verb constructions
text:
Light verb constructions combine a verb that is semantically near empty with an NP to express what could often be conveyed by a single verb:

[]take a walk ('walk')

[]have a look ('look')

[]give a try ('try')

[]make a decision ('decide')

When I say take, have, give, and make are semantically empty, I mean they appear more for their syntactic properties than for their meaning: Giving money and having money convey very different things, but give a try and have a try don't.

The most common light verbs are take, have, give, and make. While these constructions might seem redundant, they serve important discourse functions. Compare:

[]I walked in the park yesterday.

[]I took a nice long walk in the park yesterday.

The light verb construction allows some modification of the event (denoted by the noun walk here) more easily than does the simple verb. It also helps manage information structure, as the event noun can more easily serve as given information in subsequent discourse (See Chapter ).

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Serial verb constructions
text:
English has a limited but common set of serial-verb constructions where two verbs combine without any coordinator, subordinator, or preposition:

[]Go get some coffee.

[]Come see what I found.

[]Run grab your coat.

The first verb is typically a verb of motion (go, come, run) and the second describes the action to be performed after the motion. Both verbs must be in the same form: we can't mix forms like *went get or *go getting.

These constructions are more restricted in English than in many other languages. They're mainly limited to imperatives and infinitivals:

[]I'll go get some coffee.

[]She made me go get some coffee.

[*]I went got some coffee.

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Predicative complement constructions
text:
These constructions involve a verb taking both an object and a complement that predicates something of that object:

[]They elected him president.

[]The news made her happy.

[]We found the proposal acceptable.

The complement can be an NP or AdjP, but crucially it's not modifying the verb; it's predicating something of the object. Compare:

[]They painted the door red.[predicate complement]

[]They painted the red door.[attributive modifier]

In (a), red describes the result of the painting. In (b), it simply identifies which door was painted.

Different verbs allow different types of predicative complements:

- Make, get: mainly AdjPs (made her angry, got him ready)

- Consider, find: both NPs and AdjPs (consider him a friend/friendly)

- Elect, name: mainly 'role' NPs with or without a determiner (elected her treasurer, named him the captain)

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Spray/load alternations
text:
Some verbs allow their arguments to appear in two different arrangements, with a subtle difference in meaning:

[]Kim sprayed paint on the wall.

[]Kim sprayed the wall with paint.

[]Pat loaded boxes onto the truck.

[]Pat loaded the truck with boxes.

The (a) variant emphasizes the motion of the substance, while the (b) with variant emphasizes the effect on the location. This often correlates with whether the location is completely affected: load the truck with boxes suggests the truck becomes full, while load boxes onto the truck implies no such thing.

Other verbs showing this pattern include pack, stuff, stack, pile, splash, scatter, and smear. Not all semantically similar verbs allow both patterns:

[]pour water into the glass / *pour the glass with water

[*]fill water into the glass / fill the glass with water

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Constructions > Spray/load alternations > Box: Quick self-check: constructions
text:
Test your understanding of English constructions:

- What's the difference between Take a walk (light verb) and Walk (simple verb)?

- Why does Touch that and you'll regret it mean 'if you touch that'?

- Can you identify the result phrase in She hammered the metal flat?

anchor: Categories, functions, and constructions > Summary
text: Categories tell you what something is; functions tell you what it's doing; constructions tell you what the combination means. With those three lenses, you can pull apart any clause a student produces or any explanation a textbook offers. The subject diagnostics, the modifier-complement distinction, and the form-meaning pairings from this chapter give you the specific tests to back up your intuitions - or to discover that your intuitions were wrong.

anchor: Fluency and flow
text:
Cotton needs motion

to dry to softness.Frances Boyle, Pegging Out Washing

anchor: Fluency and flow > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Distinguish between fluency as flow and general language proficiency

- Explain the complexity-accuracy-fluency (CAF) triangle and how attention affects each dimension

- Identify the key characteristics of fluent vs. disfluent speech patterns

- Understand how vocabulary knowledge correlates with fluency across different language skills

- Recognize factors that promote or hinder fluency development in language learners

- Apply strategies for balancing fluency development with accuracy and complexity goals

- Appreciate the role of repairs and hesitations as natural parts of language learning

anchor: Fluency and flow
text:
Analytical approaches in this chapter
This chapter introduces several key concepts for understanding language fluency:

- CAF triangle analysis - Understanding how complexity, accuracy, and fluency compete for limited attentional resources

- Flow vs. proficiency - Distinguishing between smooth language processing and overall language knowledge

- Temporal measures - Using speech rate, pause patterns, and hesitation markers to assess fluency

- Cross-skill comparison - Examining how fluency manifests differently in speaking, listening, reading, and writing

- Repair analysis - Understanding how self-corrections and reformulations function in language production

These approaches help teachers recognize fluency as a multifaceted aspect of language proficiency that can be developed through targeted practice.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Introduction
text:
When folks speak about being fluent in a language, what they usually mean is proficient. In this chapter, I want to focus on one special aspect of proficiency: flow. I mean fluent in the way that a dancer is fluent, a flock of birds, a school of fish, Lionel Messi dribbling, Oscar Peterson soloing, or a skilled chef moving through the kitchen. Of course, these are also proficient, but fluency admits the possibility of error, and that's part of the distinction I want to draw between fluency and proficiency. For now, think of a fluent speaker as one whose speech flows, even if it may be ungrammatical at times. A fluent reader may not understand everything, but their reading is effortless. A fluent language user can still be learning the language, but they have automatized enough that they proceed with few stops or hesitations. They can get it out, and they can take it in. (The ideas in this section are largely due to Robinson (2011), Segalowitz (2010), and Skehan (2003).)

At the same time, what they're saying may not be all that profound, and the language could be quite simple. It's less about the content, and all about the flow.

anchor: Fluency and flow > What is fluency?
text:
Technical terms are often drawn from common words but have more or less differing meanings. For us, technically, fluency can be described as the ability to process language receptively and productively at a reasonable speed (Nation, 2014). Let's be careful about ability though. Linguistic proficiency is purely internal, but our ability to apply it runs smack into the world around us. Knowing more vocabulary, for example, is an internal, linguistic factor that will typically increase your fluency via your proficiency, but being in a noisy, distracting environment is an external factor that will typically decrease your ability to process language. In such an environment, you may become slow or halting, which is to say disfluent.

Almost everything in this book so far has been about accuracy and to a lesser extent complexity, but knowing the landscape of a language isn't of much use unless one can traverse it with ease, with fluency.

To better crystallize this perhaps abstract concept, consider Figure, which shows that these three aspects of proficiency - complexity, accuracy, and fluency - are interrelated through our limited attention span. Just as a piece of string can form different triangles but its length remains constant, our attention can be distributed differently across these three aspects, but at any moment it remains finite. When we focus attention on one aspect, we necessarily have less for the others. A rider going through difficult terrain has to focus on navigating complexity, reducing their capacity for speed. An archer who wishes to shoot accurately will pause to control their breathing, trading off speed for precision. Just as the string can only stretch so far in one direction before pulling back from others, our attention can only extend so far toward one aspect of language before drawing away from the others.

Figure: Interrelationship between complexity, accuracy, and fluency

The relationships run in reverse. If you would like to be - or would like your students to be - more fluent, consider reducing the pressure for absolute accuracy. This may feel counterintuitive for teachers and students accustomed to valuing accuracy above all else. Grades often reflect this emphasis, as accuracy is easily measured. And, as English students improve, they're expected to face more complex linguistic challenges. But, as with obstacle courses, those added challenges slow us down. They may make us better climbers, but for increased running speed we need practice without obstacles. If you want to improve your language fluency, find ways to use the language without the constant worry about perfect grammar.

Another way is to reduce the pressure to be complex. Using familiar vocabulary and simpler grammatical structures allows us to focus on the flow, on strengthening fluency itself.

As we develop proficiency, we gradually automatize certain aspects of language use, requiring less conscious attention. This is like learning to stretch our perimeter; the total attention available increases through practice. But during the learning process itself, we often need to focus predominantly on one aspect. Figure shows how we might distribute attention when prioritizing fluency development.

Figure: Focus more on fluency and less on accuracy complexity.

anchor: Fluency and flow > What is fluency?
text:
Of course, the focus should not always and only be on fluency. But it should be on fluency often enough, certainly much more than has traditionally been the case.

And when it is, the internal experience of fluency should be almost a lack of any experience at all. There is no effort, no focusing of attention. Language production or comprehension simply happens the way a leaf floats downstream in a river, without planning, without decisions.

anchor: Fluency and flow > What is fluency? > Box: Check your understanding: the CAF triangle
text:
Consider your own language learning experience:

- When you focus intensely on getting grammar exactly right (accuracy), what happens to your speaking speed and smoothness (fluency)?

- When you try to use complex vocabulary or sentence structures (complexity), how does this affect your fluency?

- Can you think of a time when reducing pressure for perfection actually improved your communication?

This illustrates how our limited attention creates trade-offs between the three dimensions of language performance.
box_title: Check your understanding: the CAF triangle

anchor: Fluency and flow > What is fluency? > Fluency vs proficiency
text:
Forgive me for going on, but I often find that people confound fluency and proficiency, even once the distinction has been clearly set out. And this is understandable; the two are related concepts. Still, they are distinct. High linguistic proficiency makes fluency much easier over a broad range of situations, tasks, and topics, but someone with low proficiency may still be quite fluent in limited situations. Consider Table. (For an example of low proficiency and fluency, see https://youtu.be/48yr93hwvCk?t=21. For low proficiency with much higher fluency, see https://youtu.be/m0UGhSufSJk?t=45. And for high proficiency with low fluency, see this interview of Ted Kennedy: https://youtu.be/e5TkhNWPspM?t=76.)

Table: Comparison of Fluency and Proficiency

Fluency is characterized by flowing production or processing of language. This aspect of linguistic ability is specific to one skill at a time, such as speaking, reading, or listening, and is highly variable depending on the situation. For instance, a person might be more fluent in casual conversation but less so in a formal presentation. Fluency is marked by a natural ease in using language, where communication occurs with minimal hesitations or disruptions. This doesn't necessarily imply complete accuracy or complexity in language use. As expert language users, it's important to note that you and I may not always exhibit fluency, especially in unfamiliar contexts or when using specialized terminology.

On the other hand, proficiency encompasses a broader range of skills and knowledge. It includes a general ability in various aspects of language, from grammar and vocabulary to the ability to understand and produce complex language structures. Proficiency tends to be more stable or progressively improving over time. It is a measure of one's overall capacity in a language, encompassing both the depth and breadth of linguistic understanding. Anyone able to read this book in English is proficient, meaning you have a comprehensive grasp of English, including its rules, nuances, and complexities.

The relationship between fluency and proficiency is dynamic. While high proficiency can facilitate fluency, it doesn't automatically guarantee it. Conversely, a speaker might demonstrate fluency in certain contexts despite having lower overall proficiency.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Dimensions of fluency
text: Fluency, as I've hinted, is not a monolithic construct. It's a multi-faceted beast, with tentacles reaching into various domains of language use. When we talk about fluency, we're really talking about a constellation of fluencies, each with its own unique characteristics and challenges. Let's untangle this knot and look at each dimension in turn.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Dimensions of fluency > Speaking fluency
text:
The most obvious manifestation of fluency (or disfluency) is in speech. We've all experienced those magic moments when the words just flow, when our tongues seem to have a mind of their own, when we're in the zone. But we've also all had those other moments, the ones where we stumble, hesitate, grope for words, start and stop like a sputtering engine.

Fluent speech employs a smooth, rapid delivery with few pauses, hesitations, or false starts. It's the difference between a jazz musician effortlessly riffing and a novice haltingly plunking out a tune. Consider the following examples:

[]So then I said to him, I said, Look, if you're not happy with the arrangement, we can always renegotiate. But we need to do it soon, before the project deadline. And he just sort of nodded and said, Yeah, okay, let's talk tomorrow. So, I think it's going to work out.

[]So then I said to him, I said, um, Look, if you're not, uh, happy with the, the arrangement, we can always, you know, rene, renegotiate. But we need to do it, uh, soon, before the, um, project deadline. And he just sort of, uh, nodded and said, Yeah, okay, let's, let's talk tomorrow. So, I, I, I think it's, uh, it's going to work out.

The first example () flows smoothly, with few pauses or hesitations. The speaker seems to know exactly what they want to say and how to say it. In contrast, the second example () has a bunch of filled pauses (um, uh), repeated words and phrases, and false starts (rene-, renegotiate). The speaker seems to be grasping for words, struggling to formulate their thoughts in real time. Not that this is bad. In fact, it's quite normal. It's just less fluent than ().

But fluency isn't just about speed or smoothness. It's about automaticity, the ability to retrieve and articulate words and phrases without conscious effort. It's about proceduralization, the gradual transformation of declarative knowledge ("knowing that") into procedural skill ("knowing how").

Think about the first time you tried to drive a car (if you drive). Everything required conscious effort: checking the mirrors, signalling, shifting gears, monitoring your speed. But over time, these actions became automatic, proceduralized. You could do them without thinking, freeing up cognitive resources for higher-level tasks like navigating or conversing with passengers. If you're like me, you may have set out to drive one place and instead found yourself on your regular route to work, a route you've taken so many times that it has become automatic.

The same is true of speaking a language: as lower-level processes like word retrieval and articulation become more automatic, more fluent, you're able to devote more attention to the content and nuances of your message.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Dimensions of fluency > Speaking fluency
text: And our main cognitive limitation is that: attention. Linguists used to be very focused on trying to imagine grammars that use as little memory as possible on the assumption that our memory for individual words and expressions was quite limited. The thinking was why remember jump, jumped, jumps, jumping along with all the other regular verb forms when you can just remember jump and then produce the rest on the fly with rules? It seems more likely that memory retrieval may sometimes be a problem, but storage capacity is practically unlimited (Erman et al., 2000; Jackendoff et al., 2018).

anchor: Fluency and flow > Dimensions of fluency > Speaking fluency > Box: Fluency reflection: automaticity in action
text:
Think about activities you've automatized in your life:

- What language tasks feel automatic to you in your first language? (greeting people, ordering coffee, giving directions?)

- In a second language you're learning, which elements still require conscious effort?

- How might reducing the cognitive load of basic language functions help improve overall communication?

Understanding automaticity helps explain why fluency activities often focus on familiar, well-practiced language patterns.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Dimensions of fluency > Listening fluency
text:
But speaking is only half the conversation. The other half is listening, and it comes with its own fluency demands. Listening fluency is about processing language in real-time, about keeping up with the flow of speech as it comes at you. It's about parsing the stream of sound into meaningful chunks, and interpreting the message, all on the fly. For fluent listeners, this all happens effortlessly, automatically. They're able to follow the thread of conversation, to anticipate where it's going, to read between the lines.

Obviously, listening is somewhat different from speaking in that we control very little of what happens. The contents, form, volume, and speed of the text - I'll call it a text even though it's not written - are all dictated by the speaker. We're hardly even in control of our ability to understand it. If someone within earshot is speaking a language you know, you're generally going to understand what they're saying, whether you want to or not. Speakers can, in a sense, hijack our brains.

Because of the unique elements of speech, listening fluency is often reduced to language comprehension, though the two are not exactly the same. You've almost certainly listened to an English speaker with an unfamiliar accent. In such cases, your listening comprehension may drop significantly below your potential language comprehension. Similarly, there are people who have learned English entirely through print, who can read with some fluency, but who can't parse the speech stream. They're unfamiliar with how the language sounds.

If you have trouble picturing this, pull up a subtitled YouTube video in a language you don't understand. The subtitles should be a direct transcription, not a summary or a translation. Then, turn the subtitles off and count the words. That's all. It doesn't have to be a long segment. Even just a few seconds is fine. Just try to count the number of words, and when you're done, turn the subtitles back on and check. You'll probably find that you could not reliably separate the text into individual words. When we speak, in any language, we just run the words all together.

Because of this, most beginning students can benefit from listening while reading. In other words, they should have access to recordings or computer-generated text-to-speech output while they read. Or, in a classroom, a teacher can read to them as they follow along. This provides visual clues about word boundaries and sometimes helps word recognition.

The main problem, though, is vocabulary knowledge. You can't recognize a word you don't know. Even once someone is able to parse the speech stream reasonably well, they will still be unfamiliar with literally thousands of words and expressions.

The role of context in listening fluency is often overstated. Fluent listeners do make use of contextual cues, especially in understanding reference. They use their knowledge of the topic, the speaker's intentions, and the overall discourse to fill in gaps and make predictions about what will be said next. But comprehension is mostly driven by fluent word recognition for which guessing and inference is no substitute.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Dimensions of fluency > Reading fluency
text:
Then there's reading fluency. As discussed in, the Simple View of Reading (Gough et al., 1986) shows that fluent reading depends on both smooth word decoding and language comprehension (). But in the context of fluency development, what matters is how decoding automaticity frees up attention for comprehension: when decoding requires effort, it monopolizes the limited cognitive resources needed for following narrative threads and integrating ideas.

Because English has a somewhat opaque orthography, few students are able to fully transfer their existing decoding ability from another language, even if it has a Roman-alphabetic script. That said, most people who read in a Germanic or Romance language will have a strong head start in decoding English, and anyone who is literate in a Roman-alphabetic script will also be able to transfer some skills. Those who have little or no experience reading alphabetic scripts will necessarily have low reading fluency, even if they have good listening fluency in English.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Dimensions of fluency > Writing fluency
text:
Finally, there's writing fluency. In some ways, it's the inverse of reading. Instead of taking in and making sense of someone else's words, you're spinning your own out of the ether. But the core challenges are similar: translating thoughts into language, crafting phrases and sentences that capture your meaning, building a coherent structure.

A major difference between writing and speech, though, is that writing is often expected to be more thoughtful, developed, organized, and complex. All of those require attention, that most limited of cognitive resources, and typically reduce fluency.

For less fluent writers, crafting even a simple paragraph can be a start-stop affair, a matter of grinding out a few words or sentences, then pausing to reread, revise, rethink. They may struggle to find the right words, to vary their sentence structures, to maintain a consistent tone or point of view. The end result often lacks coherence and flow, reading more like a patchwork of ideas than a seamless narrative or argument.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Dimensions of fluency > Putting it all together
text:
These fluencies, though distinct, are deeply intertwined. Progress in one often fuels progress in the others. As your spoken vocabulary grows, so does your listening comprehension. As your reading fluency develops, so does your sense of what sounds right in your own writing.

And of course, the more you engage with a language across all these modalities, the more automatic and effortless it all becomes. Every conversation, every page read, every paragraph written, is another step on the path to overall fluency.

But the relationships are complex and sometimes counterintuitive. You can be a fluent speaker but a halting reader, a prodigy at writing but tongue-tied in conversation. During my time in Tokyo, I had practised a handful of Japanese phrases well enough to ask for directions with a semblance of fluency. The trouble was that the responses - rapid, detailed, idiomatic - far exceeded my listening fluency. I would nod and smile in feigned understanding, set off in the general direction indicated, and hope for the best. Speaking and listening are different skills, and fluency in one guarantees nothing about the other.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Measures of fluency > Speaking and listening
text:
A variety of tasks may be used to measure speaking fluency. Spontaneous speech tasks: Participants might be asked to describe a picture, tell a story, provide directions, or engage in a conversation based on a specific prompt. Researchers analyze the transcript afterward, looking at elements like words per minute, pauses, hesitations, and grammatical complexity.
Retelling tasks: Participants might listen to a short story or passage and then be asked to retell it in their own words. This measures their ability to process information and produce smooth, connected speech.
Timed reading tasks: Participants may be asked to read a passage aloud as quickly and accurately as possible. This provides insight into both reading fluency and speaking fluency.

The average speaking rate for native English speakers in the United States is around 150 words per minute (wpm) for general conversation. (This widely cited figure comes from the National Center for Voice and Speech; see https://tfcs.baruch.cuny.edu/speaking-rate/.) But this rate varies depending on the context and purpose of the speech. Presentations tend to be slower (100-150 wpm), while audiobooks are typically read at 150-160 wpm. Auctioneers and sports commentators can reach 250-400 wpm.

Speech rate is not a static measure and can be influenced by various factors such as nervousness, urgency, mental fatigue, the complexity of the words, the complexity of content, verbal pauses, and event-driven pauses. These factors can affect the overall speaking rate and vary among individuals based on their environment, upbringing, culture, and even geographical location. For example, people in London typically speak faster than those from Yorkshire. Additionally, if English is not the speaker's first language, they usually speak a bit slower.

Interestingly, speech rate is strongly correlated with vocabulary knowledge, suggesting that one way to increase speech rate is simply to learn a lot of useful vocabulary (See, ).

Along with the obvious implications for speaking, this has implications for listening. One study of recorded lecture comprehension in competent anglophones, which employed audio, slides, and video of the speaker, found no significant difference between the viewing speed (about 160 wpm) and, and only minor degradation at (Murphy et al., 2022).

anchor: Fluency and flow > Measures of fluency > Reading
text: Carver (1992) found that for proficient adult readers of English, the optimal reading speed is about 300 words per minute, with a roughly linear increase in average reading speed from about 120 words per minute in grade 2. When the adults are reading for learning, their speed slows to about 200 words per minute, and when reading very deliberately, for example when knowing they will be tested on the details of a passage, they commonly slow to rates of roughly 140 words per minute. For folks reading in a second language, it's typically much slower. Students studying in an English-medium university or high-school and able to score well on a reading comprehension test may read in the 80-120 words-per-minute range (Grabe, 2009). Japanese first-year university students may start reading simple English stories as slowly as 80 words per minute (McLean et al., 2017). Reading out loud is typically much slower than silent reading at about 170 words per minute, comparable to general conversation speeds.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Measures of fluency > Writing
text:
There is no lower bound on typical writing speeds. People who deem themselves to be engaged in writing, may produce not a single written word for long periods of time. This is because we often include planning and researching as part of our writing. And that's perfectly fair.

At the upper bound, some professional romance writers can produce 2,500 words per hour, or about 40 words per minute (Ha, 2017). But that's highly unusual and can only be sustained for an hour or two per day.

In a more typical case, Waes (2015) observed Master's students at the University of Antwerp who were studying foreign languages at the Common European Framework of Reference for Languages B2 level. They compared their first-language writing speed to that of the language they were studying. They were to write a short text about a recent experience, such as what they'd done on the weekend. Their first language speed was about 31.5 words per minute, or 34.5 if you count things they ended up deleting. The second language writing was about four words per minute slower. Overall, this is less than one quarter of typical speaking speeds.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Factors affecting fluency
text:
Overall, the factor most highly correlated with fluency across all skills is vocabulary knowledge. In reading, about 50% of the variance in fluency is accounted for by vocabulary knowledge (Grabe, 2010). As I've said, listening fluency is a bit hard to conceptualize, but vocabulary knowledge accounts for roughly 30-45% of the variance in listening comprehension (Zhang et al., 2022), which is roughly the same as for reading. In speaking, the relationship is more modest, with vocabulary knowledge accounting for around 15% of spoken fluency (Uchihara et al., 2019; Liu, 2020).

Writing is so variable that it's difficult to identify correlations. There are many bottlenecks to writing from the speed of your printing/writing/typing, to your level of distractedness. Remember that in a conversation, there's a good deal of social pressure to participate, while in writing, if you start daydreaming, you're typically the only one who can get the writing restarted. Obviously, proficiency is a limiting factor.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Factors affecting fluency > Cognitive processing and affective factors
text:
Cognitive processing and affective factors may have a stronger influence on L2 repair fluency than L1 repair patterns (Peltonen et al., 2024).

Repairs are generally more common in the L2 than in the L1, but individual preferences for repair types vary. Some people rely more heavily on false starts or repetitions, while others favor replacements or reformulations. For language teachers, this suggests the need for a nuanced understanding of repairs in student speech. Rather than viewing all repairs as disfluencies to be eliminated, teachers might consider how different repair types serve as coping mechanisms for students as they navigate the demands of L2 speech production. By acknowledging the role of anxiety and attentional control in repair behavior, teachers can create more supportive classroom environments that encourage students' fluency development.

At the same time, it's crucial to avoid overemphasizing repair reduction at the expense of fostering students' overall communicative competence and confidence.

anchor: Fluency and flow > Summary
text: The single strongest predictor of fluency across skills is vocabulary knowledge: roughly half the variance in reading fluency, a third of listening comprehension. That fact alone reframes how you allocate instructional time. And the CAF triangle gives you a way to read a student's hesitation: are they slow because they lack the words, or because they're investing attention in accuracy? The answer determines whether the right move is vocabulary building, lower-stakes practice, or something else entirely.

anchor: Information packaging
text:
Once

it was called moonlight. Foreign, now, incomprehensible,

it fills the air and falls upon the earth.Jan Zwicky, Far from Rome

anchor: Information packaging > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Explain the concept of information packaging and its role in effective communication

- Distinguish between given and new information in discourse contexts

- Identify topic and focus structures in English sentences

- Analyze cleft constructions and their information-highlighting functions

- Recognize fronting and postponement strategies for managing information flow

- Understand how active and passive constructions serve different discourse purposes

- Apply information packaging principles to improve clarity in student writing and speaking

- Recognize how inappropriate information packaging creates communication breakdowns

anchor: Information packaging > What is information packaging?
text:
When we communicate, it's not just about what we say, but also about how we structure and present that information. This is what linguists call information packaging.

Think of it like packing a suitcase. You have a variety of items you want to bring (the information), but how you arrange those items, what you put on top for easy access, what you tuck away at the bottom - that's all part of your packing strategy. And just as different packing styles can make it easier or harder to access what you need from your suitcase, different information packaging choices can make your message clearer or murkier for your audience.

In English, we have a toolbox full of linguistic strategies for information packaging. We can choose what to put in the subject of a clause, what to emphasize with intonation, what to keep together, and what to delay until the end. Each of these choices subtly shapes how our message will be understood.

Why does this matter for language learning and teaching? Because these packaging strategies aren't just stylistic frills; they're key to effective communication. Choosing the right packaging helps us highlight what's new or important, connect ideas to things already mentioned, and steer our listener's attention to the main point. Conversely, packaging things awkwardly can leave the audience confused, distracted, or missing the key ideas entirely.

So in the rest of this chapter, we'll unpack some of the main tools English offers for packaging information, and explore what they mean for the language classroom. We'll look at given vs new information, topic and focus, cleft sentences, fronting and postponing strategies, and the discourse functions of active and passive. Along the way, the goal is to develop a sharper eye and ear for how these information-packaging strategies work, so you can help your students make wiser choices in their own English communication.

anchor: Information packaging > What is information packaging? > Given vs new information
text:
One fundamental pragmatic aspect of information packaging is the interplay between given (or known) information and new information. Given information is what you can reasonably assume your listener is already aware of, based on the prior discourse or general knowledge. New information is what you're introducing for the first time, or highlighting as especially important or newsworthy.

We tend to structure our sentences so that given information comes earlier and new information comes later. This given-new ordering helps listeners process the message more easily, moving from what they're familiar with to what's being introduced. Consider ().

[]I bought a new car yesterday. It's a hybrid.

In the first sentence, a new car denotes new information; I'm telling you about it but I haven't mentioned it before. But I denotes old information. The very fact that you're hearing or reading those words means you know I'm addressing you. So I comes first and a new car comes later. If I said A new car was bought by me, flipping the order, that would just be weird.

In the second sentence, it refers back to this car, which is no longer new information, having now been established in the discourse by the previous sentence. The new information in the second sentence is the type of car, a hybrid. Again, I could say, a hybrid is the type of car, but again, it's a strange way to package the information.

We could even try going so far as to reverse the order of the sentences.

[?]It's a hybrid. I bought a new car yesterday.

Again, the result is very odd in (); it doesn't connect to any previously mentioned referent or anything obvious in the environment, so the pronoun feels abrupt and disconnected. The new information a new car comes awkwardly late.

So in general, people tend to instinctively structure their sentences and discourse in a given-to-new arc. Of course, this is a flexible principle, not an absolute rule. Speakers can intentionally depart from given-to-new order for special effects like dramatic emphasis. But most of the time, following this default makes the discourse feel more natural and easy to process. (Lambrecht (1994) expresses it like this: "In the unmarked case, a clause-initial subject will have a topic relation and a clause-final object a focus relation to the proposition.")

As an English teacher, attuning yourself and your students to this given-new flow may not be a big problem. It's the normal way to do things in any language, but sometimes thinking explicitly about it can help students structure larger pieces of discourses more coherently.

Recall the discussion of first-person subject dropping in English (see Section ). That's just a more extreme form of this. The subject I is so totally given, so much on the table that it doesn't even bear mentioning. But if we dropped the new information, then nobody would have any idea what we were saying.

anchor: Information packaging > What is information packaging? > Given vs new information
text: Ultimately, the given-new principle is a key foundation for the other information packaging strategies. Choosing what to present as given or new, and in what order, remains a core concern even as we turn to other tools like clefts and frontings. Keeping this foundation in mind will help make sense of the larger information packaging system.

anchor: Information packaging > What is information packaging? > Given vs new information > Box: Check your understanding: given-new flow
text:
Practice identifying given vs. new information:

- Sarah called me yesterday. She seemed upset about something.

- The concert was cancelled. Everyone received refund notifications.

For each pair: What's given information in the second sentence? What's new? How does this affect the naturalness of the information flow?

anchor: Information packaging > What is information packaging? > Topic and focus
text:
Closely related to the notion of given vs new information is the semantic distinction between topic and focus. The topic of a sentence is what the sentence is about, the starting point for the message. The focus is the most salient or newsworthy part of the message, the key piece of information the speaker wants to highlight.

In English, topics are often (but not always) given information, while focused elements tend to be new information. And just as given information usually comes before new, topics typically appear early in the sentence (often in subject position) while focused elements appear later. Consider ().

[]Q: What did Yoko buy at the store?
A: She bought a new laptop.

In the answer, Yoko is the topic: she's the one the sentence is about, and she's also given information carried over from the question. A new laptop is the focus, conveying the key new information requested by the question.

Now imagine the different context in ().

[]Q: Who bought a new laptop?
A: Yoko bought it.

Here, the new laptop is given in the question, while Yoko is the focused answer to who. This moves Yoko into the stressed, focal position at the beginning of the sentence. By doing so, it also inverts our usual given-new information order: Yoko is new and it is given.

We've already used the term focus for focused questions in Section. In focused questions, the (semantic) focus is the interrogative phrase. Sometimes this phrase is also the (syntactic) subject as it is in Who left, but usually it's not. When it's not, we focus it by moving it out of the position the answer would have been in in a declarative and putting it first, as in (b).

[]Who did Yoko bought a new laptop for Steve.[Declarative]

[]Who did Yoko buyght a new laptop for [gap]?[Interrogative]

Usually, the for PP has an object, as it does in for Steve. And we could ask he bought it for who? but we've focused the interrogative phrase by putting who at the front of the question/interrogative. Whether stuff appears at the front or the back, the very fact that it's not in its usual spot can focus it.

Of course, in speech, we can also put something into focus by stressing it, as we saw in Section. (We often represent this in writing with capital letters, italics, or other typographical cues.) This focus stress tells the listener where to direct their attention for the key information.

Apart from subject-auxiliary inversion, English has other syntactic strategies for marking focus, like clefts (see Section ), such as ().

[]It was Yoko who bought a new laptop.

[]It was for Steve that she bought it.

Clefts allow speakers to focus almost any element by making it a post-head complement of the VP, even elements that wouldn't normally appear in that position, such as for Steve in (b).

anchor: Information packaging > What is information packaging? > Topic and focus
text:
As for topics, English speakers have a few common ways of setting them up. One is simply to place the topic in subject position, as in The weather is lovely. Another is to use topic-announcing phrases like as for, regarding, speaking of, etc. (as in; mentioned in Section ).

[]Regarding the broken laptop, blame Steve.

This phrase explicitly marks the broken laptop as the topic around which the following sentence will revolve.

The topic and focus, in tandem with given-new ordering, form the backbone of information structure in English. By tuning in to these patterns, learners can understand why and when they might choose to use one of the information packaging constructions in the following sections.

anchor: Information packaging > What is information packaging? > Topic and focus > Box: Practice: topic-focus analysis
text:
Analyze the information structure in these question-answer pairs:

- Q: What did Maria do with the keys?
A: She left them on the kitchen table.

- Q: Who left the keys on the kitchen table?
A: Maria did.

Identify: What's the topic in each answer? What's the focus? How does the question determine the information structure of the answer?

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > The passive voice
text:
The passive voice is perhaps the most familiar of the information-packaging constructions. The active voice is the default, unmarked choice, (In linguistics, unmarked denotes the basic, default form of expression in a language, the version that requires no special grammatical modification.) the subject of the sentence often being an agent/actor. The passive voice allows the speaker to avoid any mention of that subject and instead topicalize and focus the patient or recipient. Consider ().

[]The company hired a new manager on Monday.[active]

[]Farah was hired on Monday.[passive]

In (a), the topic and focus is the company, which is the agent and the old information. The new manager and the fact of her employment is new information. In (b), the topic and focus shifts to Farah and the fact of her being hired. Farah is also the old information while the fact of her hiring is new. The company isn't mentioned, possibly because it's too obvious (it's an internal announcement) or because it's not important or perhaps isn't known. Compare that to ().

[]SpaceX hired a new manager on Monday.[active]

[]Farah was hired as a manager by SpaceX on Monday.[passive]

This time, in the passive, the topic is Farah, but the focus is arguably the remarkable position she landed. So the optional by phrase can be used to de-topicalize, but it can also be used for focus.

The structure of the passive construction in English most typically involves the use of the auxiliary verb be and the past participle of the lexical verb. The object of the active clause becomes the subject of the passive, while the subject of the active sentence is typically dropped. Alternatively, it can become the object in an optional by phrase.

This shift in focus can serve various discourse functions. One is to allow the topic of the passage to remain in the subject position, even if it's not the semantic agent of the action. Consider a paragraph like ().

[]XYZ Corporation has become a leader in the tech industry. Founded a little over a decade ago, the company quickly gained a reputation for innovation and quality. It was later awarded a prestigious industry prize for its software, and it was praised for its commitment to customer satisfaction.

Here, XYZ Corporation is the main topic of the paragraph, and the passive voice in the third and fourth sentences allows it to remain in the subject position throughout, even when it's the recipient of actions like being awarded a prize or being praised. This maintains a clear, consistent topic chain and preserves the old-new information structure.

The passive voice can also help us restructure a sentence to keep the head of the subject close to the verb. Consider (a) from Wikipedia where the two are right next to each other. Now compare the active counterpart (b) where they are separated by more than 20 words.

[][A convolutional neural network architecture]is formed by a stack of distinct layers that transform the input volume into an output volume (e.g. holding the class scores) through a differentiable function.]
[passive]

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > The passive voice
text:
[][A stack of distinct layers that transform the input volume into an output volume (e.g. holding the class scores) through a differentiable functionforms a convolutional neural network architecture.]
[active]

Of course, the passive voice can be overused or used inappropriately, but so can the active. You may have been told that you should avoid the passive, but no matter how many times you've heard such a thing, I think you'll agree that (a) is much better than (b).

The passive can also be used to intentionally de-emphasize or omit the agent, either because it's unknown or irrelevant, or because the speaker wants to avoid assigning explicit blame or responsibility:

[]Someone seems to have broken the window.[active]

[]The window seems to have been broken.[passive]

[]The government mishandled the crisis.[active]

[]The crisis was mishandled.[passive]

In (b), the agent is omitted entirely, shifting the focus away from the fact that someone did it, which seems fairly obvious, to the window itself. In (b), no mention is made of the government, perhaps to avoid a directly accusatory tone.

For English learners, understanding the discourse functions of the passive can help them make better choices in their own language production. Instead of following the blanket advice to always use active voice, they can learn to see the passive as a tool for managing information flow and emphasis.

As a teacher, you can help students develop this strategic awareness through targeted exercises and discussions. For example, you can present students with a paragraph (perhaps from a text they've recently read) in which all the passive clauses have been made active and ask them to passivize those where they think the passive would be better. In taking this up, you'd ask students for the reasons for their choices: to defocus the subject of the active, which is unimportant or unknown (e.g., ); to maintain a consistent topic (e.g., ); to avoid separating the head of the subject from the head verb (e.g., ), or for some other topic or focus purpose.

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > Cleft constructions
text: Cleft constructions allow the speaker to split a simple sentence into two parts, each with its own information structure. The two main types of clefts are it-clefts and pseudo-clefts.

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > Cleft constructions > It-clefts
text:
It-clefts have the following structure: it + be + focus + relative clause.

[]Yoko bought a new laptop.[non-cleft]

[]It was Yoko who bought a new laptop.[it-cleft]

[]It was a new laptop that Yoko bought.[it-cleft]

[]It was for Steve that Yoko bought a new laptop.[it-cleft]

In (b), Yoko is the focus; in (c), it's the new laptop; and in (d), it's who it was purchased for. The it-cleft structure allows the speaker to place anything in the focus position in the main clause, where it receives the most stress and prominence.

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > Cleft constructions > Pseudo-clefts
text:
Pseudo-clefts, also known as wh-clefts, have the following structure: relative clause + be + focus.

[]Jun will visit Paris next summer.[non-cleft]

[]What Jun will do next summer is visit Paris.[pseudo cleft]

[]Where Jun will go next summer is Paris.[pseudo cleft]

[]How it will go is unpredictable.[pseudo cleft]

In (b), the event of the visit is focused, while in (c), the location Paris is focused. And in (d), the unpredictability of the venture is focused. The pseudo-cleft allows the speaker to front the old information in the relative phrase, while placing the focus in the complement in the be VP.

Both it-clefts and pseudo-clefts serve to highlight or emphasize a particular element of the sentence. They're often used to contrast the focused element with some other possible alternative, as in ().

[]It was the red car, not the blue one, that she bought.

[]What I want is a vacation, not more work.

Adjuncts can be difficult to focus in their normal positions, but clefts allow them to be moved to the focus position.

[]We meet our friends tomorrow.[non-cleft]

[]It's tomorrow that we meet our friends.[it-cleft]

[]We're meeting at the park.[non-cleft]

[]Where we're meeting is at the park.[pseudo cleft]

While passives are common, especially in academic texts, clefts are less so. They're also mainly spoken constructions, with pseudo clefts being mostly limited to conversation (Biber et al., 1999).

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > Preposing and postposing > Preposing
text:
In a preposing construction, a constituent that would normally come later in the sentence is moved to the front, either because it is old information or to focus it. Focused interrogatives are one example of preposing (see Section ).

While it's possible to prepose an object or other complements in a VP, it's much more common with adjuncts or objects of prepositions. Examples are given in (-).

[]She took her own, but she left his.[default]

[]She took her own, but his she left.[preposed object]

[]I could open it with a hammer.[default]

[]With a hammer, I could open it.[preposed PP adjunct]

[]I'll go if you go.[default]

[]If you go, I'll go too.[preposed PP adjunct]

[]It worked because of all the practice.[default]

[]Because of all the practice, it worked.[preposed PP adjunct]

[]I haven't looked at the report yet.[default]

[]The report, I haven't looked at yet.[preposed object in PP]

[]We watched a movie last night.[default]

[]Last night, we watched a movie.[preposed NP adjunct]

Just as a fronted interrogative phrase triggers subject-auxiliary inversion or do support, sometimes, a fronted adjunct will do the same as in (-). This is particularly common with negative adjuncts.

[]I rarely wake up this early.[default]

[]Rarely do I wake up this early.[preposed AdvP adjunct]

[]They have never admitted it.[default]

[]Never have they admitted it.[preposed AdvP adjunct]

[]She not only got up, but she even left.[default]

[]Not only did she get up, but she even left.[preposed AdvP adjunct]

[]It passed so quickly that we missed it.[default]

[]So quickly did it pass that we missed it.[preposed AdvP adjunct]

[]You should not open it under any circumstances.[default]

[]Under no circumstances should you open it.[preposed PP adjunct]

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > Preposing and postposing > Postposing
text:
Postposing constructions place a constituent later than it would normally come. This is often done with a longer or heavy constituent to improve clarity and processing. It's also another way of shifting the focus. See the examples in (-).

[]The book that he'd been working on for years was finally published.
[default]

[]It was finally published, the book that he'd been working on for years.
[postposed subject]

[]The decision had been so difficult that I'd delayed it for months.
[default]

[]So difficult had been the decision that I'd delayed it for months.
[postposed subject]

[]You asked me to tell them, and I told them. [default]

[]You asked me to tell them, and tell them I did. [postposed subject]

[]He gave all the leftovers from the dinner to the dog. [default]

[]He gave to the dog all the leftovers from the dinner.
[postposed direct object]

[]The question of where we're staying could be raised. [default]

[]The question could be raised of where we're staying.
[postposed modifier]

[]Somebody who seemed to be in a hurry arrived with a package for you.
[default]

[]Somebody arrived with a package for you who seemed to be in a hurry.
[postposed modifier]

It's important to note that while preposing and postposing are possible in English, for the most part, they're marked structures and shouldn't be overused. Most sentences will follow the default word order, and deviations should be motivated by specific discourse needs.

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > Extraposed subject
text:
Subject extraposition packages information by moving a "heavy" subject to the end of the clause.
