It's particularly common with clausal subjects, those that contain a full clause, often introduced by that, whether, or if. The extraposed subject's original position is filled with the dummy pronoun it.

As with other information packaging constructions, extraposition can serve different discourse functions. One is simply to make sentences easier to process by moving long, complex subjects to the end. But it also helps maintain a natural flow of information from given to new, as in ().

[]John: I hope there are no surprises.

[]Minh: It worries me that Al hasn't replied yet.[extraposed]

[?]Minh: That Al hasn't replied yet worries me.[non-extraposed]

Minh's response in (b) exemplifies how extraposition helps maintain the given-to-new flow of information. The conversation has already established concern about possible surprises, so starting with it worries me connects to this given information before introducing the new information about Al. The non-extraposed version in (c) begins abruptly with new information, making it feel less connected to the ongoing discourse.

This reordering for processing and information flow is especially common with expressions of evaluation or stance, like appear, seem, be clear, be obvious, be important, and so on:

[]To finish this project by Friday seems impossible.[default]

[]It seems impossible to finish this project by Friday.[extraposed]

[]That everyone agreed was obvious.[default]

[]It was obvious that everyone agreed.[extraposed]

In fact, in many cases, the extraposed version sounds much more natural than keeping the clausal subject in its original position. The non-extraposed versions can feel awkward and overly formal. In other cases, such as (), extraposition is obligatory.

After reviewing the client's feedback...

[]...it seems that we need to revise our proposal.[extraposed]

[*]...that we need to revise our proposal seems.[non-extraposed]

Infinitival subjects are also commonly extraposed, especially when they're modified by additional phrases that make them heavier:

[]To work with such talented people is a pleasure.[default]

[]It is a pleasure to work with such talented people.[extraposed]

It's important to be aware that an extraposed subject is a type of complement; it's not the subject of the clause. In (), for instance, the one and only subject is it, even though it's semantically empty.

When student writing feels hard to read but you can't pinpoint a grammar problem, look for heavy subjects. A sentence like That many of the students who had enrolled in the advanced section did not complete the required coursework on time surprised the administration is grammatical but forces the reader to hold an enormous subject in memory before reaching the verb. Extraposing it (It surprised the administration that) solves the problem immediately. This is one of the clearest cases where information packaging explains a judgment that grammar alone cannot.

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > Right dislocation
text:
Right dislocation is an information packaging construction, mostly limited to spoken English, where we place a nominal expression at the end of the clause while leaving a pronoun in its normal position. It's especially common in conversation, where it helps clarify referents and manage information flow:

[]She's really smart, that new professor.

[]They're impossible to find, those books you mentioned.

The dislocated phrase at the end serves to clarify or elaborate on the pronoun earlier in the clause. This is particularly useful when the speaker realizes the listener might need help identifying the referent, or when the speaker wants to add emphasis.

Right dislocation differs from extraposition in important ways. With extraposition, the it is semantically empty; it's just holding the subject position until the real subject appears. But in right dislocation, the pronoun has full referential meaning, and the phrase at the end is providing additional information about that referent.

The construction is especially useful for adding commentary or evaluation while maintaining a smooth information flow:

[]He's always late, your brother.

[]It's beautiful, this time of year.

[]They're quite expensive, these organic vegetables.

In each case, the evaluation comes first, followed by clarification of what's being evaluated. This ordering lets speakers lead with their main point while ensuring their reference is clear.

Because right dislocation is largely a spoken construction, teachers assessing student speech should be careful not to treat it as redundancy or error. A student who says They've been really helpful, those online tutorials is demonstrating discourse competence, managing reference and evaluation in real time. The same pattern in formal writing, however, would be out of place: knowing the difference is part of what register awareness means.

Right dislocation can also help manage longer stretches of discourse by keeping the reference clear:

[]I couldn't believe it when I saw it in the store. It was enormous, that cake you were telling me about.

[]They've been giving me trouble all week, those new students in my afternoon class.

The passive section above showed how one construction connects to teaching practice. But the constructions in this chapter aren't isolated alternatives; they form a system. Each one packages the same content for a different discourse situation, and choosing a packaging means choosing what to treat as given, what to put in focus, and what to background. The following exercise makes this visible.

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > Right dislocation > Box: Exercise: one proposition, seven packages
text:
The sentences below all describe the same core event. For each one, identify (a) what's presented as given information, (b) what's in focus, and (c) what preceding context would make this the natural choice.

The principal cancelled the field trip on Friday.

The field trip was cancelled on Friday.

It was the principal who cancelled the field trip.

It was on Friday that the principal cancelled the field trip.

What the principal cancelled was the field trip.

On Friday, the principal cancelled the field trip.

She cancelled the field trip on Friday, the principal.

- Which versions present the principal as already under discussion? Which present the field trip as given?

- Versions (c) and (d) are both it-clefts. They don't just rearrange; they add an exhaustive contrast: it was X implies and not anyone/anything else. What alternative is each one ruling out?

- Version (g) is right dislocation, common in speech, odd in writing. What kind of conversation would produce it?

- Pick any two versions and imagine them in the same slot in a paragraph. How does each change what you expect the next sentence to be about?

anchor: Information packaging > Information packaging constructions > Right dislocation
text:
This exercise works in the classroom too. For intermediate learners, use three or four versions; for advanced learners, all seven. The key question (what context makes each version the natural choice?) develops the same skill at any level. Few of these constructions are frequent enough to be worth drilling as production skills; the opportunity cost of practising it-clefts is time not spent on higher-return areas like given-new flow and the passive (see Section ). But you don't need to teach clefts to recognize them when they make your materials harder than intended.

The biggest payoff is in how you select, adapt, and create materials. Whether you're evaluating a textbook passage, simplifying an authentic text, or reviewing something generated by an AI, you're making information-packaging decisions. Different genres tend to favour different packaging profiles: academic prose makes heavy use of extraposition and pseudo-clefts; conversation uses right dislocation and simple given-new chains; news writing uses passive for topic continuity (Biber et al., 1999). Materials whose packaging doesn't match the target genre give students the wrong model, and if you've worked with AI-generated text, you'll have noticed that a passage can be lexically academic but syntactically conversational. Keep in mind, too, that learners whose L1 uses morphological topic markers or discourse particles rather than word-order rearrangement may not notice the English packaging system at all. Without the analytical vocabulary from this chapter, these mismatches are hard to name and hard to fix. Chapter develops this further at the paragraph level, where packaging choices accumulate into coherence or its absence.

anchor: Information packaging > Summary
text: The reader's first job is to figure out, moment by moment, what can be treated as settled and what deserves the stress. When a sentence gets that bargain right, the new idea arrives exactly where attention is ready to receive it; when it gets it wrong, even a simple message feels oddly hard. The proposition may stay the same while the processing load shifts, so a teacher listening closely can hear why one version carries a reader forward and another makes the reader backtrack. Once you hear that difference in student writing, clear and awkward stop sounding like taste and start sounding like evidence.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion
text:
The mind makes us in millisecond myths,

moments that transform you into metaphor's whimBeth Goobie, You Are Story

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Distinguish between coherence (semantic/logical connections) and cohesion (linguistic connections) in discourse

- Analyze how topic chains create coherence across multiple sentences in texts

- Identify various cohesive devices including pronouns, lexical cohesion, and conjunctive relations

- Understand how inference and implicature contribute to text coherence

- Recognize abductive bridging as a mechanism for creating discourse connections

- Apply coherence and cohesion principles to improve student writing and reading comprehension

- Analyze authentic texts to identify patterns of coherence and cohesion

- Understand how what is left unsaid can contribute to textual coherence

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Introduction
text:
Consider the following excerpt from the short story Quantum convention by Eric Schlich:

[]Both my parents were high school teachers - my mother English, my father History.

[]They used to come home with stacks of student essays.

[]Sometimes they'd take sick days just to catch up on the grading.

This is a simple description of the narrator's parents and their travails. But it pulls you right into the story because of its structure.

In Chapter, we looked at how to topicalize various elements within the sentence, but a topic, having been established in one sentence, has to be unfurled across those that follow. It can neither simply be left to drop nor hoisted so often that the text becomes choppy or repetitive.

The first sentence (a) gives us both my parents. That both is then bisected into my mother and my father, and finally simplified to they in (b c). Pronouns like they create coherence by linking to and renewing previously invoked referents (Halliday et al., 1976; Brown et al., 1983).

Oddly enough, we can even create a sense of coherence in what is unsaid. Consider the ill-advised rewrite in ().

[]Both my parents had jobs as high school teachers.

[]My mother worked as a high school English teacher, and my father worked as a high school History teacher.

[]One part of their job was teaching high school students.

[]There were a lot of high school students and, high school students write essays as part of their studies.

[]There were a lot of essays produced.

[]The teaching and the writing happened at the school.

[]Part of my parents' jobs as high school teachers was grading essays that are produced by students at school, and jobs are supposed to be performed at the workplace.

Instead of this tediously detailed exposition suggesting all sorts of digressions, Schlich says only what we need to know. He strips (b) down to my mother English, my father History, a bare topic-comment construction that nevertheless recalls key elements, my parents and teachers, from the foregoing clause.

The connection between teachers and students is so obvious that it literally goes without saying. Including the details in (c) could only imply something less than straightforward. Likewise, the reason for bringing the essays home is left to us to infer, a process that requires the reader to draw on what has gone before. These mental passages back and forth among the ideas provide the warp and weft that allow the passage in () to cohere so tightly.

There is more to this than coherence. Consider the constructions used to manage past time reference. The italicized were in (a) uses the simple-past tense construction to establish the background. But establishing this foundation implies that a structure is to follow.

The second sentence (b) starts building by switching to the used to construction. Used to has a past habitual meaning, but in the discourse context, it frames the beginning of a story.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Introduction
text:
The third sentence (c) shifts to the past-tense modal would (contracted as 'd) combined with the take VP. This would again means habitual past action, similar to used to. But in the discourse, 'd is used to elaborate the details of the story.

Taken together, these verb constructions create a layered discourse structure that funnels the reader into the details of the parents' work life and challenges (Suh, 1992).

- Background (simple past)

- Story frame (used to)

- Story elaboration (would)

Such precision requires very particular structural choices, though not necessarily ones most speakers or writers could articulate. Discourse analysis like that above uncovers the subtle ways in which language is used to shape the structure and coherence of texts; it explores what the linguistic structures achieve in a text and how they do so.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Introduction > What is discourse?
text:
Discourse is another of those terms that has both an everyday meaning and a technical one. In everyday use, it refers to spoken or written communication, discussion, or debate, as in the discourse around climate change has become highly politicized. We might talk about public discourse, political discourse, or academic discourse, usually to highlight the ways language is being used in a particular domain or to achieve certain purposes. There's often a sense that discourse is not just casual talk, but language used in a more deliberate, purposeful, or even strategic way.

In linguistics, discourse has come to mean any stretch of language longer than a sentence, especially when considered in its full communicative and social context. In other words, it's conversations, speeches, stories, essays, articles, and other runs of language. Unlike isolated examples crafted solely to illustrate grammatical rules, discourse involves language actively used for communication, action, and interaction in the world.

So when we do discourse analysis, we're looking at language from observed, recorded, transcribed, or collected texts. These texts could be monologic (produced by one person) or dialogic (involving two people), or they could involve interactions among larger groups. We're interested in how meanings are constructed and negotiated in that text, how social relationships and identities are enacted, how power structures are reinforced or challenged, all through the minute details of the language used.

Discourse analysts ask questions like: How is the topic being introduced and developed? What roles and identities are the participants taking on? What assumptions or biases are being revealed? How are arguments being structured and evidence presented? What rhetorical strategies are being employed to persuade or manipulate? By carefully examining the linguistic choices made at every level (from the word to the clause to the larger text structure) discourse analysis can provide a rich, nuanced understanding of how language works in context.

As language teachers, we can use discourse as one more lens through which to reflect on English and to help our students to do so. After all, our learners' goal is not just to master isolated bits of grammar and vocabulary, but to communicate effectively. This is not to say that all aspects of English discourse are particular to English; the use of pronouns, for example, is common across languages, but the past-tense used to would structure of getting into a story may not have direct translations in other languages.

Students may benefit from knowing how to structure writing for various purposes, how to participate in a job interview, how to tell an engaging story, how to express disagreement politely, how to interpret the hidden meanings in an advertisement or political speech. This is discourse competence: the ability to understand and deal with text in a way that's not just grammatically correct, but socially appropriate and communicatively effective for a given purpose and context.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Introduction > What is discourse?
text: But more than the students, it may be us teachers who really need an understanding of discourse. It's easy to tell students how to form a given construction. But without an awareness of when and why it's deployed in texts (including conversations), we risk reducing language classes to the study of a set of mechanical, purposeless rules.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Coherence in discourse > What is coherence?
text:
Coherence is the property that distinguishes a well-formed text from a random collection of sentences. It's what makes a text hang together as a unified whole, allowing the reader to follow the flow of ideas and grasp the overall meaning.

But what exactly creates this sense of unity and flow? Is it the presence of explicit linking words like moreover, however, and therefore? Many writing textbooks would have you believe so. They often present these connectors as the magic glue that holds a text together.

While linking words can certainly be useful signposts, they are not the main drivers of coherence. In fact, an overreliance on them can make writing seem artificial and formulaic. The real key to coherence lies elsewhere: in the careful structuring of information and the strategic use of lexical cohesion, that is, the way words and phrases are repeated, varied, and related across the text (Halliday et al., 1976).

To understand how this works, let's consider a simple example that may not look so simple.

[]xGreenThe Renaissance marked xGreena profound transformation in European art and xPurpleculture.
This xPurplecultural xGreentransformation was particularly evident in the flourishing of visual arts.
xOrangeArtists of the xGreenera, such as xOrangeMichelangelo and Leonardo da Vinci, revolutionized artistic xPinktechniques.
xOrangeTheir innovative xPinkapproaches heralded a new xGreenera in the xPinkrepresentation of xPurplehuman experience.

The colours and formatting in () mark different lexical chains running through the passage, words that connect to each other in meaning, form, or association. Even without decoding every chain, you can see how the passage coheres: related words recur, vary, and cluster in ways that guide the reader through the argument. We'll return to this example once we have the vocabulary for what's going on.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Coherence in discourse > What is coherence? > Box: Quick analysis: lexical chains
text:
Practice identifying lexical connections in this short passage:

The storm approached rapidly. Dark clouds gathered on the horizon. Weather forecasters warned residents to prepare for severe conditions. The approaching tempest promised hours of heavy rain and strong winds.

Can you identify the chain of words related to the storm? How do these repetitions and variations create coherence? What would happen if we used it for every reference after the first mention?

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Coherence in discourse > What is coherence?
text:
While coherence is the overall sense that a text hangs together, cohesion refers to the specific linguistic tools writers and speakers use to create this effect. We can have one without the other, but they usually go together.

An example of a coherent paragraph with almost no cohesive devices is ().

A runner woke before sunrise. A gardener prepared compost behind a shed. Children discovered an old boat stranded near pebbles. A tailor examined patterns under faint light. Someone arranged cutlery in a distant kitchen. Nearby, a violinist practiced unfamiliar scores. Another figure observed silent machinery from a hidden alcove. In short, the world went on without him.

One employing cohesive devices but which is nevertheless incoherent is ().

The cat sat on the mat. The mat was blue, a primary colour, and rainbows include all the colours. They appear after rain and are the stuff of folklore. Cats don't like the rain, but they also commonly appear in folklore. Mats, however, don't, not even blue ones, but flying carpets are associated with Arabian folklore, and they're a kind of mat.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices
text:
Cohesion refers to the linguistic means by which coherence is achieved. In other words, it's the visible manifestation of the underlying semantic ties that hold a text together. There are several types of cohesive devices, each contributing in its own way to the overall unity and flow of a text.

The most basic way we create connections in text is through reference, using words that point to other elements in the discourse.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Reference, pronouns, and pro-forms
text:
Reference is a broad term that encompasses the use of words to point to other elements in a text or to the world outside the text. Pronouns are a common type of referential device, but they are not the only one.

It's important to distinguish between pronouns as a category of words and pro-forms as a type of meaning relation. Pro-forms are words or phrases that express the same content as another element in the text, with the meaning being recoverable from the context. They stand in for the other element, forming cohesion chains and reducing verbatim repetitions. This is the definition usually given for pronouns, and while it's typical (semantic) pronoun behaviour, it's not a good definition, as we discussed back in Section.

Most pronouns function as pro-forms, as in ().

[]Ethan is an excellent teacher. He always explains things clearly.

Here, the pronoun he refers to (stands in for) the noun Ethan, creating a cohesive link between the sentences. This act of referring back to something already mentioned is called anaphora, and the word or phrase it refers to is the antecedent.

But not all pronouns have antecedents. That is, they're not all pro-forms. Interrogative who, for instance, does not stand in for any other element; instead, it signals that the speaker is asking about the identity of a person, as in (; See Section ). (Don't confuse this with relative who, which does refer. See Section.)

[]Who is an excellent teacher?

Conversely, not every case of anaphora involves pronouns. Words from other lexical categories can also function as pro-forms, as did so does in ().

[]She told me to begin, and so I did so.

Reflexive pronouns (such as myself, yourself, himself, herself, itself, ourselves, yourselves, and themselves) play a crucial role in maintaining topicality within a clause, particularly when the subject of an action is also its object.

Consider the examples in (), each of which involves just one person.

[?]Brett hurt Brett. [clashing topical implications]

[?]Brett hurt him. [clashing topical implications]

[]Brett hurt himself. [maintains topicality]

In (a), the repetition of Brett in the object position conflicts with the assumption that topics need not be reintroduced within a clause, suggesting there are two Bretts, even though this interpretation has been ruled out. In (b), him avoids repetition but implies a different person, again clashing with the assumption that Brett remains the sole topic.

The reflexive pronoun himself resolves these issues, maintaining Brett as the clear topic throughout the clause without reintroducing the noun phrase:

- It preserves Brett as the consistent topic, even in the object position.

- It signals explicitly that the subject and object are the same, maintaining topical coherence.

- It respects the given status of the information without disrupting the flow.

This use of reflexive pronouns is particularly important with transitive verbs like hurt, which require an object. When the action reflects back on the subject, the reflexive pronoun allows us to maintain a single, clear topic throughout the clause.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Reference, pronouns, and pro-forms
text:
For many speakers, some reflexive pronouns (in particular myself and yourself) have acquired a secondary, socially driven function where their original discourse meaning (coreferentiality within a clause) is sidelined in favour of signalling formality or self-reference in a more distant, impersonal sense, as in (). This socially marked use can cause some confusion or even resistance among speakers who expect reflexives to serve only their coreferential, discourse-specific roles (Gilman1989, 1989).

Please, contact myself with any questions.

Reflexives also have the meaning of 'alone' or 'without the help of others', as in He did it himself. This has little to do with coherence and is another reminder that exclusively one-to-one form-meaning pairings are rare.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Definiteness
text:
Another important concept related to reference is definiteness. Noun phrases can be either definite or indefinite, depending on whether the speaker assumes the listener can identify the referent. One way you know your listener can identify the referent is if it's already been mentioned in the discussion. In other words, if an NP has an antecedent, it's going to be definite. For this reason, most pronouns are definite.

We often think of definite noun phrases as being those marked with the definite article the, and those marked by the indefinite article a/an or no article as being indefinite. And this is generally true, but expressing definiteness or indefiniteness is a more complex matter than that. First, there are a lot of other indefinite determinatives and a few other definite ones too (see Section ).

This interplay between definiteness and pronoun use is an important aspect of creating coherence in a text, at least in an English one. By using pronouns to refer back to already-mentioned entities, and by marking new entities as indefinite, writers can guide readers through the flow of information and help them track the main participants in the discourse. Keep in mind, though, that many languages create perfectly coherent texts without marking definiteness. It can be done, but in English, failing to mark definiteness is generally seen as unhelpful at best and often as ungrammatical.

It's worth noting that pronouns and pro-forms are often deictic, meaning they point to something, including something in the discourse. For example, the meanings of I and you change depending on who is speaking and who is being addressed. Similarly, the prepositions here and now point to the time and place where the speaker is, and last week, yesterday, this morning, tomorrow, and other related words all point to times relative to the speaker's point in time.

Most languages use pronouns and pro-forms in the ways described, so such use isn't anything students will be unfamiliar with, but the processing demands of dealing with a second language may be such that it's easier for students to simply repeat a given phrase verbatim instead of switching to a pronoun or another pro-form. In writing, they may fail to use a pronoun where they could or use an ambiguous one. And in listening or reading, it's not unusual to lose track of the antecedent of a given pronoun.

In summary, pronouns are a central part of the reference system in English, and as pro-forms, they work with definiteness and deixis to create coherence in texts. By attending to these various aspects of reference, writers (it's often impractical and of less urgency to address these topics in speech) can create texts that are both cohesive and coherent, guiding readers smoothly through the development of ideas.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Pronouns vs deictic determinatives
text:
This, along with that (and even which), are used without head noun to refer a preceding topic (and item, idea, situation), sentence, or paragraph. Many years ago, this was regularly criticized (the this in this sentence is an example), but today, such criticism is rare and usually unwarranted. Occasionally, the reference is improved by the addition of the head noun, but often its omission is the better choice.

The distinction between pronouns and deictic determinatives hinges significantly on their function within discourse coherence and attention management. Pronouns, such as it, primarily sustain focus on established referents, seamlessly maintaining coherence with minimal cognitive load. This characteristic is crucial in texts where continuity with previously introduced entities is paramount.

Deictic determinatives, notably this, typically shift, broaden, or narrow the discourse focus. They are often employed to highlight new propositions or aspects deriving from but not identical to prior text elements. This shift can introduce a subtle complexity, requiring readers to integrate or reinterpret elements within the discourse dynamically. Such usage not only enriches the narrative but also invites deeper engagement by challenging readers to form connections that are not immediately obvious.

As a result, a typical pattern is antecedent topic/idea/sentence/paragraph this it, as in ().

[]
Cloud computing offers flexibility and scalability that was once impossible. This has revolutionized how businesses store data and manage resources. It allows companies to focus on innovation instead of infrastructure.

You can see that this refers to the first sentence and topicalizes it. This specification is necessary because, from the first sentence on its own, we might assume that cloud computing generally is the topic. But this implies that it's the whole proposition about cloud computing, rather than cloud computing itself that is the topic. Once the topic has been established, then it maintains it.

The determinatives this and that are deictic (they have a pointing function) and this is what allows them to shift, focus, or broaden the topic. (The subordinator that is a different matter. It has no meaning at all. See Chapter.)

Here's another example.

[]What's Canada's comparative advantage in geopolitics and foreign policy, and how should it work?

[]What's Canada's comparative advantage in geopolitics and foreign policy, and how should it leverage that?

The focus interrogative topicalizes Canada's comparative advantage in geopolitics and foreign policy, and the most natural antecedent for a pronoun like it is the topic. That's exactly what we see in (a): it refers to the topic. In (b), though, the verb is leverage and usually agents, not policies leverage things. Also, we have a second anaphoric relationship in that. Putting these together, it picks out an agent (Canada), then that shifts the focus back to the previous topic.

While reference creates connections through pointing, lexical cohesion builds meaning through word choice itself.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Lexical cohesion
text:
Lexical cohesion is the use of word choice - repetition, variation, and semantic relationships - to create a network of meaning across a text. It is perhaps the most powerful form of cohesion, relying on the reader's understanding of the relationships between words and their ability to follow the threads of meaning woven through the text. Among these threads of lexical cohesion, we find:

- Repetition: Using the same word or phrase multiple times across a text. This can help maintain a clear focus, especially for key concepts.

- Variation: Using words that are morphologically or semantically related: categorial variations, synonyms, antonyms, and hyper/hyponyms (words in a super/subordinate relationship). This allows for variety, contrast, generalizing, and focussing, while still maintaining a sense of unity.

- Collocation: Using words that tend to co-occur frequently, creating a sense of naturalness and coherence.

Consider (; reproduced here as ) with the words coloured and italicized to illustrate these types of lexical cohesion more clearly:

[]xGreenThe Renaissance marked xGreena profound transformation in European art and xPurpleculture.
This xPurplecultural xGreentransformation was particularly evident in the flourishing of visual arts.
xOrangeArtists of the xGreenera, such as xOrangeMichelangelo and Leonardo da Vinci, revolutionized artistic xPinktechniques.
xOrangeTheir innovative xPinkapproaches heralded a new xGreenera in the xPinkrepresentation of xPurplehuman experience.

Verbatim repetition is seen in the dual appearances of xGreentransformation and xGreenera, respectively. Repetition with variation is even more abundant in this passage.

- art, arts, Artists, and artistic are all variations of the same root word, creating a strong morphological chain throughout the passage.

- xGreenThe Renaissance and xGreena profound transformation in the first sentence are semantically related, the Renaissance being a specific instance of a profound transformation.

- xPurpleculture, xPurplecultural, and xPurplehuman experience are semantically related.

- transformation, flourishing, revolutionized, innovative, and new all convey the idea of change and innovation, forming another lexical chain.

- xPinktechniques, xPinkapproaches, and xPinkrepresentation are related to the methods and practices of art.

- And, of course, xOrangeMichelangelo and xOrangeLeonardo da Vinci are specific xOrangeartists, to which xOrangetheir refers.

These semantic relationships allow the writer to avoid repetition while still maintaining clear connections between ideas.

Collocation is seen in phrases like profound transformation, visual arts, and human experience. These combinations create subtle but powerful links across a text, contributing to its cohesion in ways that might not be immediately obvious. (See, though, Section.)

mark and profound collocate with transformation, flourishing with arts, revolutionize with techniques, innovative with approaches, and herald with a new era. All are established word partnerships that speakers of English recognize, even if unconsciously. Each combination reinforces the text's theme of dramatic cultural change while creating natural-sounding prose.

Where much lexical cohesion is backwards looking, collocations suggest to us what's coming next. When you read The Renaissance marked a profound, you're primed for transformation. You may not always get exactly that, but it's often helpful in leading us to predict the next word.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Lexical cohesion
text:
It's important to note that these types of lexical cohesion often work together. For example, the combination of xGreenThe Renaissance and xGreenera is an example of repetition (of the concept of a time period) and variation (using a more general term in place of the specific name).

Sometimes the strongest connections in discourse come not from what we say, but from what we leave unsaid.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Ellipsis
text: Ellipsis is the omission of linguistic material that is recoverable from context. Unlike deletion for brevity, ellipsis creates cohesion by forcing readers or listeners to actively connect the elliptical structure to its antecedent. This mental work of recovery strengthens the coherence of the text.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Ellipsis > Types and patterns
text:
English licenses ellipsis in several syntactic environments, each with its own constraints:

Subject ellipsis occurs primarily in coordinate structures and informal registers:

[]She picked up the phone and she called her sister.

[]I Hope you're doing well. [informal written/spoken]

[]A: Where's Ahmad?
B: He Went home early.

While coordination freely allows subject ellipsis when the subjects are coreferential, conversational ellipsis follows more complex patterns. First-person subjects drop most readily (Think so, Don't know), while third-person dropping typically requires immediate prior mention.

VP ellipsis removes entire verb phrases after auxiliaries:

[]Maya can speak French, and Dmitri can speak French too.

[]A: Will you be attending?
B: I might be attending.

[]Someone should tell her, but I won't tell her.

The auxiliary carries tense and agreement information, making the full VP recoverable. This construction is particularly useful for avoiding repetition while maintaining grammatical parallelism.

Gapping removes the verb (and sometimes more) from coordinate structures:

[]John ordered coffee, and Mary ordered tea.

Unlike VP ellipsis, gapping requires parallelism between the clauses and typically occurs only in coordination.

Bare argument ellipsis reduces clauses to a single constituent:

[]A: Who can help with this?
B: Maybe Carlos can help with this?

[]They're serving pasta, they're serving probably lasagna.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Ellipsis > Discourse functions
text:
Ellipsis serves several discourse functions beyond mere economy:

Maintaining topicality: By omitting given information, ellipsis keeps focus on new information while reinforcing the active status of the topic. The very act of omission signals 'this element is so salient it needs no repetition'.

Managing participation: In conversation, elliptical responses show engagement with prior talk:

- [A] When does the library close?

- [B1] The library closes at eight. [sounds pedantic or uncooperative]

- [B2] At eight. [natural, cooperative response]

Creating cohesion across distance: Ellipsis can link elements across intervening material:

[]The committee proposed increasing fees - a move that upset many students and sparked protests - but the board ultimately didn't increase fees.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Ellipsis > Constraints and challenges
text:
Not all material can be elided. Ellipsis requires:

- Recoverability: The antecedent must be sufficiently salient

- Syntactic identity: The elided material must match its antecedent structurally (with some permitted mismatches)

- Appropriate context: Some ellipsis types are restricted to specific registers or constructions

These constraints explain certain errors:

[*]Mary can speak French, but I don't think John can speak French.

Here the ellipsis fails because can can't be recovered after John. We would need John can.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Ellipsis > Cross-linguistic variation
text:
Languages differ dramatically in their ellipsis patterns. Japanese and Korean regularly omit subjects and objects when recoverable from context (Kuno, 1973). Spanish and Italian allow more subject-dropping than English but require rich verbal morphology to identify the subject (JaeggliSafir1989, 1989). Mandarin permits both subject and object dropping but relies heavily on discourse context (Li et al., 1979).

English occupies a middle ground: more ellipsis than French or German, less than many East Asian languages. This creates predictable challenges:

- Japanese speakers may overuse ellipsis: *Yesterday bought book

- French speakers may underuse it: I think that it is possible

- Spanish speakers may drop subjects inappropriately: *Is raining

Use complete sentences
Discourse rarely gets much attention in teacher training, so it's not surprising that some common classroom directives sit awkwardly with how discourse actually works. Take the familiar instruction to use complete sentences.

The intention behind this directive is clear: teachers want students to produce grammatically well-formed output, especially at lower levels. But the directive can misfire, because in natural communication, complete sentences are often neither necessary nor appropriate. Consider a typical conversation:

- [A] Where are you headed?

- [B] The store. Need anything?

- [A] Just milk.

Each response here is perfectly coherent and communicatively effective, despite being incomplete by certain classroom standards. The discourse context provides all the necessary information for these fragments to be meaningful. In fact, responding with complete sentences would be terribly artificial and potentially even uncooperative:

- [A] Where are you headed?

- [B] I'm headed to the store. Do you need anything at the store?

- [A] Yes, I do need something at the store. I need milk at the store.

This disconnect between classroom expectations and real-world language use can be particularly problematic for language learners. When we insist on complete sentences in all contexts, we:

- Create artificial barriers to fluent communication

- Ignore the crucial role of discourse context in determining appropriate responses

- Miss opportunities to teach authentic language use

- Risk developing students who sound robotic or overly formal in casual conversation

The challenge is helping students recognize when ellipsis is appropriate (casual conversation, parallel structures) versus when fuller forms are expected (formal writing, initial introductions of topics). This requires attention to register, genre, and the specific communicative goals of each interaction.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Connectives
text:
Connectives, also known as linking words or transitional phrases, are used to signal logical relations between clauses and sentences. They're a collection of words and phrases from various lexical categories, each with its own unique properties and functions.

Coordinators, such as and, but, and or, are used to join words, phrases, or clauses of equal grammatical status. Despite what some may have heard, there's nothing wrong with starting a sentence with a coordinator. In fact, it can be an effective way to create a sense of continuity or to emphasize a contrast:

[]But let's not forget the importance of practice. And that's where the real challenge lies.

The key, as with other connectives, is not to overuse them in this position.

Prepositions such as because, if, though, and while, license subordinate clauses as complements and indicate their logical relationship to the main clause. They can signal cause and effect, conditionality, concession, simultaneity, or other relationships:

[]I enjoy learning languages because it allows me to connect with people from different cultures.

Here, because introduces a clause that gives the reason for the main assertion.

There are also various more or less set preposition phrases that function as connective adjuncts. Those such as for example, in addition, in contrast, on the other hand, as a result, and in conclusion, are invaluable, but also often overused.

Adverbs like however, moreover, therefore, and nevertheless are also common connectives, along with the plainer also and too. They can appear at the beginning of a clause or sentence, or they can be inserted within the clause, usually set off by commas:

[]Learning a new language can be challenging. However, it's also incredibly rewarding. Moreover, it opens up new opportunities for work, travel, and cultural exchange.

Here, however signals a contrast between the two ideas (challenging vs rewarding), while moreover signals the addition of further benefits.

It's worth noting that some adverbs, like however and therefore, are often used as connectives, while others, like quickly or loudly, typically modify verbs or adjectives. This highlights the functional diversity of the adverb category.

Used judiciously, connectives can help clarify the structure of an argument and guide the reader through the logic of a text. But they are not the primary source of coherence. A text that leans too heavily on connectives can feel choppy, formulaic, or just somewhat off. Unfortunately, many writing textbooks give the impression that they are the imprimatur of academic writing, and many teachers focus on them because they're rather easier to address - and assess - than clarity, voice, style, and having something interesting to say. But if used simply as a way to dress up weak writing in academic cloth, connectives will impress few readers and fool fewer still.

A prime example of this pedagogical approach is the FANBOYS mnemonic (for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so), which is presented in many composition textbooks as a complete and authoritative list of coordinating conjunctions. In fact, the FANBOYS list is neither internally coherent nor exhaustive, and its rigid application often relies on questionable punctuation rules (Reynolds, 2011).

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Connectives
text:
In summary, connectives are just one tool in the cohesion toolbox. They can be helpful in signalling logical relations, but they should be used in conjunction with, not as a replacement for, the other types of cohesive devices discussed in this section. By understanding the unique properties and functions of each type of connective, and by using them judiciously, writers can create texts that are both cohesive and engaging.

Beyond these basic connecting words, language also provides a rich set of expressions for managing the flow of discourse itself.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Discourse markers and pragmatic particles
text:
While pronouns and other referential devices create explicit connections in text, language also employs a set of words and phrases that manage the flow of discourse more broadly. These discourse markers (also called pragmatic particles or pragmatic markers) help speakers navigate the complex social and cognitive demands of communication (Schiffrin, 1987). Far from being mere verbal tics, they perform essential discourse work: managing turn-taking, signalling stance, repairing misunderstandings, checking comprehension, and marking relationships between utterances.

Consider this exchange:

- [A] Like, I was thinking about what you said yesterday?

- [B] Right, about the project deadline.

- [A] Yeah, well, I'm not sure we can finish by Friday.

- [B] I mean, we could ask for an extension.

- [A] You know, that's probably what we should do.

Each discourse marker here serves a distinct function. Initial like frames what follows as a thought in progress. Right acknowledges a shared understanding while transitioning to business. Yeah confirms receipt of information before well signals a shift to potentially problematic content. I mean introduces a clarification or suggestion. And you know invites agreement while marking the proposition as mutually accessible.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Discourse markers and pragmatic particles > Positional patterns and functions
text:
Discourse markers cluster strongly at utterance boundaries, particularly in initial position where they frame what follows:

- So: marks conclusions, summaries, or topic shifts: So what you're saying is
- Well: signals various transitions, often to dispreferred responses: Well, I'm not sure about that

- Now: introduces new topics or returns focus: Now, about those reports
- Look/Listen: demands attention, often preceding disagreement: Look, we need to be realistic

- Actually: corrects or contradicts expectations: Actually, that's not what happened

Final position markers check understanding or soften assertions:

- right?/yeah?: seek confirmation: We're meeting at three, right?

- though: qualifies preceding content: It's not a bad idea, though

- or whatever/or something: mark vagueness: We could get pizza or whatever

Medial markers often manage information flow or hedge commitment:

- like: marks approximation or exemplification: It was, like, really crowded

- I mean: self-repairs or clarifies: She's - I mean - she was my supervisor

- sort of/kind of: hedge precision: It's sort of blue-green

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Discourse markers and pragmatic particles > Prosodic sensitivity
text:
The meaning of discourse markers depends crucially on intonation. Consider well:

- Long falling tone: disagreement or bad news (We::ll, that's not quite right)

- Level tone: neutral transition (Well, let's see what happens)

- Rising tone: consideration or uncertainty (Well, maybe?)

Similarly, so varies by prosody:

- Emphatic stress: strong conclusion (SO you knew all along!)

- Unstressed: topic management (So anyway, about lunch)

- Lengthened: processing time (So:::, what should we do?)

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Discourse markers and pragmatic particles > Register and social meaning
text:
Discourse markers carry social meaning beyond their discourse functions. Some markers index informality (like, you know), while others suggest formality (indeed, moreover). Age and social group also matter: lowkey and highkey have emerged among younger speakers as stance markers, with lowkey meaning something like 'actually/honestly' in Lowkey this lasagna tastes awful.

The frequency and clustering of markers also carries social meaning. Heavy use of like may index youth or uncertainty, while sparse marker use can seem abrupt or unfriendly. The key is appropriateness to context rather than simple presence or absence.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Cohesive devices > Discourse markers and pragmatic particles > Cross-linguistic challenges
text:
Discourse markers present particular challenges for language learners because:

- Semantic elusiveness: Their meaning emerges from their discourse function rather than propositional content

- False friends: Similar forms across languages often have different functions (Japanese sou /soː/ vs English so)

- Prosodic integration: Correct form with wrong intonation changes the meaning

- Register sensitivity: Appropriate use varies dramatically by context

- Rapid change: New markers emerge and spread quickly through speech communities

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Abductive bridging and coherence
text:
The cohesive devices we've examined - pronouns, ellipsis, lexical chains - create explicit connections across texts. But much of what makes discourse coherent lies not in what's explicitly marked but in what readers infer. Consider this brief narrative:

Sarah walked into the restaurant. The waiter showed her to a table by the window.

Nothing explicitly links the waiter to the restaurant, but we effortlessly understand that this waiter works at this restaurant. This interpretive leap (called a bridging inference) illustrates how coherence emerges through active hypothesis construction rather than passive decoding.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Abductive bridging and coherence > The abductive engine
text:
Peirce, Charles Sanders

When we encounter the waiter with its definite article signalling identifiability, we face an interpretive puzzle: what makes this waiter identifiable when none has been mentioned? Following Peirce's notion of abduction (inference to the best explanation), we unconsciously generate hypotheses:

- Restaurants typically employ waiters

- This waiter is part of the restaurant scenario

- The definite article signals this expected relationship

The hypothesis that best explains the definite marking is that the waiter belongs to the restaurant frame. This abductive process happens automatically, drawing on our knowledge of typical situations (restaurants have waiters), linguistic conventions (definite articles mark identifiable referents), and discourse expectations (new information should connect to given information).

Bridging inferences can span various semantic relationships:

[]We entered the church. The altar was covered in flowers.
[part-whole]

[]I tried to call you yesterday. The line was busy.
[event-instrument]

[]John fell off his bike. The doctor said nothing was broken.

[cause-consequence]

[]The picnic was wonderful. The ants were the only problem.

[scenario-typical element]

In each case, coherence emerges not from explicit marking but from our ability to construct plausible connections. The definite article triggers a search for an antecedent; finding none explicitly mentioned, we abduce one from context.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Abductive bridging and coherence > The abductive engine > Box: Understanding bridging: try this
text:
Consider this sequence: Sarah decided to bake a cake. The oven wasn't working properly.

- Why does the oven feel natural despite never being mentioned before?

- What frame or scenario helps you make this connection?

- Try replacing the oven with the elephant—why does this break coherence?

This illustrates how our knowledge of typical situations creates invisible bridges between sentences.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Abductive bridging and coherence > Coherence without cohesion
text:
This abductive capacity explains why texts can cohere even with minimal cohesive marking. Consider:

Max fell. Blood. Ambulance. Hospital. Recovery.

Despite lacking pronouns, connectives, or even complete sentences, this telegraphic sequence coheres perfectly. We abduce causal and temporal links: falling causes injury (blood), injury requires medical attention (ambulance), ambulances transport to hospitals, hospitals enable recovery. The coherence emerges from our ability to construct a plausible narrative that connects these fragments.

This principle extends to more complex discourse. Academic writing in some disciplines favours what might seem like abrupt transitions:

Inflation rose 3% last quarter. The central bank meets Thursday.

No explicit connective links these sentences, but economists readily infer the connection: rising inflation typically triggers central bank action. The coherence relies on shared disciplinary knowledge that makes the bridge obvious to insiders.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Abductive bridging and coherence > Cross-linguistic variation in bridging
text:
Languages differ in how much they leave to inference. Japanese and Chinese texts often require more bridging than English texts, with readers expected to supply logical connections that English would explicitly provide (Hinds, 1987). Korean academic writing traditionally favoured a ki-sung-chon-kyul structure (introduction-development-turn-conclusion) that requires readers to infer the connection between the "turn" and the conclusion (Kaplan, 1966).

English occupies a middle position: more explicit than many East Asian languages but less so than German academic prose, which traditionally marks logical relationships meticulously. This variation creates predictable challenges:

- Japanese writers may produce English texts that seem disconnected to English readers

- German writers may produce English texts that seem over-connected

- English readers may struggle with texts requiring unfamiliar bridging patterns

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Weaving it all together
text:
Cohesion isn't just about linking words or following grammatical rules; it's about choosing expressions that resonate with each other and with the larger discourse. As we've seen throughout the chapter, cohesive devices work together to guide readers through the flow of ideas and create a sense of unity.

While languages differ in how they achieve cohesion, the basic principles we've discussed tend to hold across languages. Japanese and Korean, for instance, rely heavily on ellipsis, often omitting subjects and objects that would need to be expressed in English. Spanish and Italian also allow more ellipsis than English but make greater use of verb morphology to maintain reference. English, while perfectly comfortable with some ellipsis, tends to favour explicit pronouns and other pro-forms.

But despite these differences in specific devices, the underlying principles - managing information flow, maintaining reference, creating semantic links - are remarkably similar across languages. This means students often bring valuable intuitions about cohesion from their first language. They already know that texts need to hang together, that topics need to be maintained across sentences, and that information needs to be packaged in accessible ways. The challenge is learning the specific ways English handles these universal tasks.

As language teachers, particularly in the teaching of reading and writing, we can help students develop their command of English cohesive devices while drawing on their existing discourse competence. This might involve:

- Exploring how their first language achieves similar cohesive effects

- Analyzing the differences in when and how English uses pronouns vs ellipsis

- Practicing with lexical cohesion patterns typical of English

- Working with authentic texts to see how expert writers create coherence

One activity that can be particularly helpful is the text puzzle, which is created by separating each sentence and then having students use the cohesive devices to put them back in the (or a) logical order.

Another is to mark up the text to show lexical connections as in ().

The goal isn't just to master individual cohesive devices, but to develop an intuition for how they work together in English to create meaning. This means moving beyond isolated exercises to working with whole texts in meaningful contexts, helping students see how English-specific patterns serve universal discourse needs.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Summary
text:
Coherence is what the reader builds; cohesion is what the text provides to help. The distinction matters because a passage loaded with connectives can still be incoherent, and a passage with almost no explicit ties can cohere beautifully if the reader can bridge the gaps. When you're diagnosing a student's writing, that's the first question: is the problem missing cohesive ties, or missing logic? The fix for one is a but; the fix for the other is rethinking the argument.

Exercise: short-answer questions

- What is the difference between coherence and cohesion?

- Explain how pronouns contribute to cohesion in a text.

- Give an example of ellipsis and explain how it creates cohesion.

- Describe the concept of lexical cohesion and provide two examples of how it can be achieved.

- Why is understanding discourse important for language teachers?

- Explain the role of definiteness in creating coherence.

- Give an example of how variation can be used to create lexical cohesion.

- What are discourse markers and how do they differ from connectives?

- Why is it important to teach students about the appropriate use of discourse markers?

- Discuss the potential challenges that language learners might face with discourse markers.

Answer

- Coherence is the overall quality of a text that makes it understandable and logical, while cohesion refers to the specific linguistic features that connect sentences and ideas within a text.

- Pronouns contribute to cohesion by referring to previously mentioned NPs, creating a chain of reference, and avoiding unnecessary repetition. This helps to maintain topicality and guide the reader's understanding of the relationships between entities in the text.

- Example of ellipsis: Did you finish your homework? Yes, I did. In the response, the verb phrase finish my homework is omitted because it is understood from the context of the question. This avoids repetition.

- Lexical cohesion refers to the use of word choice to create connections between ideas in a text. Examples: a) Repetition: Using the same key words or phrases throughout a text to emphasize a particular concept. b) Synonymy: Using words with similar meanings to create variation while maintaining semantic links.

- Understanding discourse is important for language teachers because it helps them to teach language in a more communicative and context-aware manner. By focusing on how language is used in real-world situations, teachers can help students develop their discourse competence and communicate more effectively.

- Definiteness contributes to coherence by signalling whether a noun phrase refers to something already known to the listener or reader. Definite noun phrases typically refer to entities that have been previously introduced or are assumed to be shared knowledge, while indefinite noun phrases introduce new entities into the discourse.

- Example of variation: The ancient city was a marvel of engineering. Its intricate network of aqueducts and roads testified to the ingenuity of its builders. Here, the words marvel, intricate, and ingenuity are all variations on the concept of impressive skill and complexity, creating lexical cohesion without directly repeating the same words.

anchor: Coherence and cohesion > Summary
text:
- Discourse markers are expressions like well, you know, and like that function primarily at the discourse level to manage the flow of conversation, signal stance, and check comprehension. Connectives, on the other hand, typically signal logical relationships between clauses or sentences, such as cause and effect, contrast, or addition.

- They play a crucial role in natural and fluent communication.

- Challenges with discourse markers: a) Meaning: Their meaning is often context-dependent and difficult to define precisely. b) Variation: Their use can vary widely across dialects and registers. c) Transfer: Learners may overuse or misuse markers based on similar but not identical functions in their first language.

Discussion questions

- Analyze how reference, ellipsis, lexical cohesion, and connectives work together to create coherence in a specific text of your choice.

- Discuss the challenges of teaching discourse competence to language learners, and suggest some effective strategies for addressing these challenges in the classroom.

- Explain the role of discourse markers, focusing on their functions, positioning, and the potential pitfalls for language learners.

- Compare lexical cohesion in different genres, such as academic writing, fiction, and advertising. How do the choices of expressions reflect the purpose and audience of each genre?

- Discuss the concept of topicality and its importance for understanding how information is structured and presented in discourse. Illustrate your points with examples of how topicality is managed through various linguistic features.

anchor: Pragmatics
text: Go past the gate, you might have to move it. It's dummy locked and it will swing once you work the rust out of the hinge. Hasn't swung well for about 10 years. So you'll need to give it a good pull for encouragement.Conor Kerr, "Directions to the Culture Grounds"

anchor: Pragmatics > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Distinguish between semantic meaning and pragmatic meaning in communication

- Apply Grice's Cooperative Principle and conversational maxims to analyze implied meanings

- Identify different types of speech acts and their performative functions

- Understand politeness strategies and face-saving mechanisms in cross-cultural communication

- Recognize how context shapes interpretation of utterances in real-world language use

- Analyze conversational implicatures and how they arise from maxim violations

- Apply pragmatic principles to improve language teaching and intercultural communication

- Understand why pragmatic competence is essential for effective second language use

anchor: Pragmatics > Introduction
text:
Consider this exchange:

- [A] I'm on my way.

- [B] The door's open.

Nothing in the literal meaning of B's response addresses A's statement, but the exchange makes perfect sense. We can imagine a context in which A is likely going to B's house, and B is saying A can come right in without knocking. The study of how we make such inferences, how we derive meaning beyond what is literally said, is pragmatics.

Even a seemingly straightforward utterance like it's cold can mean radically different things depending on context. Imagine the following situations:

[][At home, near an open window] It's cold.[Request to close window]

[][Tasting soup] It's cold.[Complaint about food]

[][At the bus stop] It's cold.[Just making conversation]

The same three words can function as a request, a complaint, or an ice-breaker. The linguistic form remains constant, but the meaning shifts dramatically based on context. This is the domain of pragmatics: how context shapes meaning.

But pragmatics isn't just about context. When I say some students arrived late, you understand that not all students were late, even though some literally means 'at least one, possibly all'. Similarly, if you ask do you have the time? I understand that you are asking an open question ('What time is it?'), not a closed question ('Do you or do you not know what time it is?'). And when I respond three fifteen, you know I mean it's currently 3:15, not that the baseball score is 3 to 15 or that the number of students in the school is 315. These meanings arise not from the words themselves, but from shared assumptions about how we use language.

The study of these shared assumptions forms another key aspect of pragmatics. We expect speakers to be informative but not overly so, truthful, relevant, and clear. When they appear to violate these expectations, we search for additional meaning. If someone asks is Pat a good teacher? and receives the response Pat has neat handwriting, the apparent irrelevance triggers a search for implied meaning, in this case, that Pat is not a particularly good teacher.

So while semantics deals with the relationship between linguistic forms and their conventional meanings, pragmatics examines how context, shared knowledge, and principles of communication combine to create meaning in actual language use. For language teachers and learners, understanding pragmatics is crucial because so much of what we communicate goes beyond what we literally say.

anchor: Pragmatics > Introduction > Why pragmatics matters for language teaching
text:
Nation, Paul

Crucially for you, this may be the chapter where the learners' instincts are often better than the teachers'. After all, learners bring with them sophisticated pragmatic knowledge from their first language. They know that literal meanings aren't always intended meanings, that social relationships affect how we speak, and that context shapes interpretation. They've been making these kinds of pragmatic inferences their whole lives.

Recall the discussion of subject dropping in Section. When a student says Got it or Need help?, they're using perfectly standard English. But teachers, perhaps focused on an artificially formal register or misunderstanding what constitutes Standard English, may incorrectly mark these as errors. Similarly, the insistence on complete sentences (Section ) often reflects teachers' misconceptions about standard usage rather than any real grammatical principle. A response like On the table to the question Where's my phone? is entirely standard; in fact, answering with a complete sentence like Your phone is on the table could seem oddly formal or even uncooperative.

The issue isn't always that learners are breaking rules of Standard English, but rather that teachers sometimes operate under various misconceptions: conflating formality with accuracy, idealizing the written language, and failing to appreciate that more isn't always better. This may stem from their own education, where formal academic language was prioritized, or from textbooks that present an artificially rigid version of the language. It might reflect an over-reaction against genuine student errors, such as dropping heads, determiners, and other elements required in a particular context.

This highlights the need for a balanced approach to language development, encompassing Nation's Four Strands of language learning. Learners implicitly absorb pragmatic norms through Meaning-focused Input (listening and reading authentic interactions) and test their understanding through Meaning-focused Output (speaking and writing in context). Language-focused Learning (direct instruction on specific pragmatic features like politeness markers or speech acts) makes these often-subtle aspects explicit, while Fluency Development (practising known pragmatic routines until they become automatic) helps learners use them smoothly in real-time communication. Pragmatic competence, like vocabulary or grammar, develops most effectively when all four strands are nurtured rather than through exposure or isolated instruction alone.

Indeed, research has demonstrated that instruction in pragmatic features significantly improves learners' outcomes compared to mere exposure to the language (Jeon et al., 2006; Ren et al., 2023). For teachers, this means consciously incorporating pragmatic awareness into lesson planning rather than assuming students will simply absorb these conventions.

Keep this in mind as we explore the various aspects of English pragmatics in this chapter. Ask yourself whether you're insisting on more elaborate forms because they're more appropriate or just because they contain the structure you want students to practice. There's nothing wrong with the latter (explicit practice of forms is often necessary) but don't confuse it with teaching appropriate language use. And don't let it lead you to mark pragmatically appropriate responses as errors just because they don't showcase the target structure.
subsection_title: Why pragmatics matters for language teaching

anchor: Pragmatics > Cooperation and implicature
text:
Grice, Paul

Consider this exchange from Section:

- [Child] Can I go to the bathroom?

- [Adult] I don't know, can you?

This stock correction - you've probably heard it, and you may have used it yourself - depends on affecting to misunderstand the child's intended meaning. The child is making a request for permission, but the adult pretends to interpret can as a question about ability. The adult is being uncooperative, violating the normal expectations we have about how conversation works.

Here's a similarly uncooperative conversation:

- [A] Do you know where JB 110 is?

- [B] Yes.

Compare that to how most people would react:

- [A] Do you know where JB 110 is?

- [B] That's building J, there. And B is for basement.

B's response doesn't literally answer the yes/no question that was asked, but it's exactly what we expect. B understands that A is asking for directions, not conducting a survey of people's knowledge of campus buildings. This kind of inference, deriving unstated meaning from what is said, is called implicature.

Implicature generally works because we assume speakers are trying to be helpful. When someone says I hear you're a good cook, they're usually angling for an invitation. When a colleague mentions I didn't receive your email, they're asking you to send it again. When someone responds to How are you? with I've had better days, they're saying things aren't going well.

The philosopher Paul Grice formalized these expectations into what he called the cooperative principle: speakers try to make their contributions appropriate to the conversation. This principle breaks down into four basic maxims that we unconsciously follow and expect others to follow:

[]Be appropriately informative (not too little, not too much)

[]Be truthful

[]Be relevant

[]Be clear

Much of what we call implicature arises when these maxims appear to be violated. If I ask how was the movie? and you respond well, the popcorn was good, I understand you didn't enjoy the film. Your seemingly irrelevant response triggers a search for hidden meaning. Similarly, if I say some students passed the test, you understand that not all students passed; if everyone had passed, saying some would violate the maxim of being appropriately informative.

anchor: Pragmatics > Cooperation and implicature > Box: Practice: analyzing conversational implicatures
text:
Consider these exchanges and identify which maxim is violated and what is implicated:

- A: Did you do the homework?
B: I was really busy yesterday.

- A: How was your presentation?
B: Well, nobody fell asleep.

- A: Is Sarah coming to the party?
B: She said she might have other plans.

What does each response communicate beyond its literal meaning?

anchor: Pragmatics > Cooperation and implicature
text:
Grice didn't see the maxims as prescriptive rules about how we ought to speak. Rather, he meant them as descriptions of what speakers typically do and what listeners typically expect. When we deviate from these expectations, he observed, we usually do so deliberately, with the intention of creating additional meaning:

[][Asked How did you get here?]
I placed one foot in front of the other, repeatedly transferring my weight from the back foot to the front foot, while maintaining my balance and adjusting my trajectory as needed to avoid obstacles and ensure arrival at my intended destination.[too much information]

[][About a student's excuse]
Yes, and I'm the Queen of England.[not true]

[][Asked if they liked the gift]
Well, it's Thursday today.[not relevant]

In each case, the speaker is overtly breaching normal expectations about communication, but in a way that creates clear meaning. The absurdly detailed description of walking mocks the obvious question. The claim to be the Queen of England marks the student's excuse as equally incredible. And the complete non-sequitur about Thursday effectively communicates dislike of the gift without saying so directly.

These principles of cooperation operate in all languages, but the ways speakers flout them vary considerably. What counts as too much information in one culture might be appropriately thorough in another. What seems admirably direct in some contexts might seem unnecessarily blunt in others.

Even routine greetings may work differently: English speakers expect how are you? to receive only a brief, positive response. I recently heard of an interaction somebody had with a Russian in which the reply was Fine, like everyone in Canada.

Indonesian speakers use sudah mandi? ('have you showered yet?') as a similar kind of greeting that requires no literal answer about bathing (something I didn't at first understand when I was in Ambon). The extent to which speakers mark their non-cooperation varies too. English speakers often use elaborate irony or sarcasm - Oh, GREAT, that's EXACTLY what I needed right now - while speakers of other languages might prefer more subtle signals.

anchor: Pragmatics > Cooperation and implicature > Abduction as the engine of implicature
text:
The maxims help us recognize when additional meaning might be present, but they don't explain how we actually arrive at specific interpretations. When someone says the popcorn was good in response to a question about a movie, how do we leap from this apparent non-sequitur to the movie was bad? The answer lies in abduction, inference to the best explanation.

Consider again the exchange:

A: How was the movie?

B: Well, the popcorn was good.

The interpretive process isn't a matter of applying memorized rules. Instead, we unconsciously ask: what would make B's response sensible? We generate hypotheses:

- B misheard the question

- B has an unusual obsession with cinema snacks

- B is avoiding saying something negative about the film

Given the context - the hesitation marker well, the assumption that B is being cooperative, the social norm against unnecessary negativity - the third hypothesis best explains the utterance. This abductive leap from a surprising utterance to the best available explanation happens automatically and rapidly.

This same abductive capacity that helps us bridge gaps in written texts (Section ) operates in conversation, but with crucial differences. In reading, we typically bridge from given information to new information, inferring unstated connections between explicitly mentioned elements. In conversation, we bridge from literal meaning to speaker meaning, inferring unstated intentions behind utterances.

For language learners, this means implicature isn't fundamentally about learning new rules but about applying existing reasoning skills to new linguistic and cultural data. When a student hears I'm on my way followed by the door's open, they're not consulting a mental rulebook that says mentions of open doors following travel announcements indicate permission to enter. Instead, they're asking what would make this response relevant and helpful, and inferring that the speaker must be facilitating entry.

This abductive process explains why pragmatic competence can transfer surprisingly well across languages despite surface differences. The basic cognitive machinery (the ability to reason from puzzling communicative behaviour to likely intentions) is universal. What varies is the cultural knowledge that constrains which explanations count as best. In some contexts, nice weather today might be small talk; in others, it might be an indirect suggestion to go outside. The abductive process is the same; only the background assumptions differ.

Understanding implicature as abduction also clarifies where explicit instruction helps: when it provides learners with the language and cultural premises they need to generate appropriate hypotheses. Teaching that English speakers often use past-tense modals for politeness (could you... rather than can you...) isn't teaching a mechanical rule but enriching the hypothesis space learners can draw from when interpreting indirect speech.
subsubsection_title: Abduction as the engine of implicature

anchor: Pragmatics > Speech acts and performatives
text:
Austin, J. L.

Consider what happens when a judge says I sentence you to five years. These words don't just describe something - they do something. The act of sentencing is accomplished through the utterance itself. Or consider a priest saying I now pronounce you husband and wife. Again, the words themselves perform the action. These are clear examples of what J. L. Austin (1962) called performative utterances.

But speech accomplishes actions in less obvious ways too. When we say things like I promise to return it tomorrow or I apologize for being late, we're not just making statements - we're performing the acts of promising and apologizing. Even apparently simple statements like it's cold in here often perform actions, in this case, perhaps, requesting that someone close a window.

Austin's key insight was that all utterances perform actions, not just those with explicit performative verbs like promise, apologize, or sentence. When we say the bus leaves at six, we might be making a prediction, stating a fact, issuing a command, making a suggestion, or offering an excuse, depending on the context.

The same speech act can be realized by different syntactic constructions. This becomes particularly clear when we look at requests/directions:

[]Please close the window.[imperative directive]
