Both (a) and (b) are grammatical alternatives to (c). This is quite different from headed constructions like very young, where you can use the head alone (she's young) but not the dependent (*she's very). The coordination structure is shown in Figure.

Figure: Basic coordination with a marker: They brought books and games

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > Properties of coordination
text:
Three key properties distinguish coordination from other constructions:

There's no grammatical limit on the number of coordinates (except with but):

Nothing noble, sublime, profound, delicate, tasteful or even decent can find a place.

The coordinates must be functionally similar: they need to be able to serve the same function when used alone:

[]I'll be back next week or at the end of the month.[time adjuncts]

[*]We're leaving Rome and next week.[object vs adjunct]

An expanded coordinate (one with its coordinator) can never be moved to the front:

[]They attended but they aren't members.

[*]But they aren't members, they attended.

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > A note on sentence-initial coordinators
text: A prescriptive rule warns against beginning sentences with coordinators like and or but. This prohibition has no basis in English grammar or usage. Sentence-initial coordinators have been used by skilled writers throughout the history of English, including in this book; you'll find numerous examples in this and other chapters. While a sentence-initial coordinator can't join its sentence to the preceding one in a tight coordination (recall the fronting restriction in ()), it can serve as a discourse connector, signalling how the new sentence relates to what came before. The only genuine constraint is a stylistic one: like any device, sentence-initial coordinators can become monotonous if overused. But used judiciously, they are a valuable tool for managing discourse flow and signalling text relationships.

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > Types of coordination
text:
The simplest distinction is between symmetric coordination, where the order of coordinates doesn't matter, and asymmetric coordination, where it does:

[]I was hungry and tired.[symmetric]

[]I got up and had breakfast.[asymmetric]

In (a), reversing the coordinates doesn't change the meaning. But in (b), the order implies sequence: getting up happened before breakfast.

We also distinguish between distributive and joint coordination:

[]Kim and Pat are fine players.[distributive]

[]Kim and Pat are a good pair.[joint]

In (a), the property of being a fine player applies to Kim and Pat individually. But in (b), being a good pair can only apply to them together; neither alone could be a pair.

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > Marking coordination
text:
Coordination can be marked in several ways:

- With a coordinator: red and blue

- Without any marker: red, white, blue

- With repeated coordinators: red or white or blue

- With correlative markers: both red and blue

While coordinators typically express addition (and), alternatives (or), and contrast (but), they can carry additional meanings in asymmetric coordination:

[]Pay within a week and you'll get a discount.[conditional]

[]Pay within a week or you'll lose the discount.[negative conditional]

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > Marking coordination > Box: Pattern recognition: coordination vs subordination
text:
Apply the diagnostics to distinguish these structures:

- She thinks that he left vs She left and he stayed - Test: Can you substitute one clause for the whole structure?

- Although it rained, we went vs It rained but we went - Which shows dependency hierarchy vs equal status?

- *But we went, it rained vs Although it rained, we went - What does fronting reveal about structure?

These tests help distinguish hierarchical embedding from equal-status joining.

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > Marking coordination
text: With the introduction of coordinators, we have now encountered all the major lexical categories in English except for interjections, as well as the main syntactic functions they serve in clauses.

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > The structure of coordination
text:
When we coordinate elements, they must share the same syntactic function. This means that while you can coordinate complements with complements or modifiers with modifiers, you can't coordinate a complement with a modifier. Compare:

[]We listened to the teacher and the students.[complements]

[]We listened Monday and Tuesday.[modifiers]

[*]We listened to the teachers and Monday.[mixed functions]

The elements being coordinated (called coordinates) can come from any major phrase type: nouns, verbs, adjectives, prepositions, or larger phrases and clauses. What matters is that they're functioning in the same way in the larger structure.

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > Coordination with and without markers
text:
Coordination can occur with or without an explicit coordinator. When there's no coordinator, we call it asyndetic coordination:

[]She brought games, stories, songs.[asyndetic]

[]She brought games, stories, and songs.[syndetic]

In addition to simple coordination with a single coordinator, we can have correlative coordination, where markers appear with multiple coordinates:

[]Both tired and hungry

[]Either now or never

[]Neither simple nor easy

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > Layered coordination
text:
Sometimes coordinates themselves contain coordination, creating what we call layered coordination. Consider this example:

The story was short and sweet but complex and thought-provoking.

Here we have two main coordinates joined by but, but each of these coordinates contains its own internal coordination with and. This layering allows us to express complex relationships between ideas while maintaining clear structure. The layered structure is shown in Figure.

Figure: Layered coordination: short and sweet but complex and moving

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > Special types of coordination
text:
A particularly interesting type of coordination occurs in comparative structures:

[]He's as tall as or taller than his brother.

[]She writes more but understands less than before.

These structures often involve complex relationships between the coordinates and their shared elements. Their interpretation requires understanding both the coordination and the comparative relationship.

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Coordination > Special types of coordination > Box: Quick self-check: complex structures
text:
Test your ability to analyze layered structures:

- In I know that she left and he stayed, identify all subordinate and coordinate relationships

- Explain why Both young and talented, she succeeded uses correlative coordination

- Analyze the structure: He's taller than I thought but shorter than his brother

Understanding these complex patterns helps reveal how subordination and coordination work together to build meaning.

anchor: Subordination and Coordination > Summary
text: Subordination and coordination do the same job - connecting ideas - with opposite architectures. Subordination embeds; coordination joins. Good writing balances the two, and once you can tell which structure a student is reaching for and where it breaks, you can name the problem - too many layers, too much listing, a subordinator where a coordinator would do - instead of just sensing that something's off.

anchor: Relative constructions
text:
you wonder how your immune system works at all, how it could ever stay clear on what to fight and when
[0.5em]
and you know of course it sometimes loses track and gets it backwardRiver Halen, The Enemy

anchor: Relative constructions > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Understand the anaphoric relationship between relative clauses and their antecedents

- Identify and analyze gap positions in different types of relative constructions

- Distinguish between basic (that-marked, bare) and focused (articulated, infinitival, fused) relatives

- Recognize the syntactic restrictions on relative word selection based on gap function

- Analyze the difference between integrated (restrictive) and supplementary (non-restrictive) relatives

- Apply diagnostic tests to determine relative clause structure and interpretation

- Understand how relative clauses function as modifiers within noun phrase structure

- Appreciate the complexity of relative constructions beyond traditional prescriptive rules

anchor: Relative constructions > Introduction
text:
The following (paraphrased) question from Reddit/EnglishLearning (https://www.reddit.com/r/EnglishLearning/comments/1b03e2u/relative_pronouns_i_wouldnt_like_to_stay_in_a/) is a good illustration of the confusion that often swirls around relative constructions.

Can I use where instead of that in the relative clause in I wouldn't like to stay in a hotel that doesn't have air conditioning. It seems that, if where is used for places and a hotel is a place, then this should be possible. But it sounds ungrammatical.

Indeed it is ungrammatical, and although where certainly denotes places (along with situations, academic areas, etc.), we can't rely on semantics here. Once again, we need to look at the syntax.

A relative clause is relative because of an anaphoric relationship between it and the main clause. If we make the two independent, we get (a) along with (b) but not (c).

[]I wouldn't like to stay in a hotel.

[]It doesn't have air conditioning.

[*]There doesn't have air conditioning.

The relationship here is that between hotel in (a) and the pronoun it in (b). And the reason you can't use the preposition where in the relative clause is the same reason that you can't use there in the independent clause: because most clauses resist PP subjects. Instead we end up with ().

[]I wouldn't like to stay in a hotel that [it] doesn't have air conditioning.

This illustrates a key property of the relative clause: It does not actually appear. Instead there is a gap where the subject should be. To generalize, whatever anaphor would typically appear in an independent clause is replaced by a gap in its relative counterpart. The gap in () happens to be the subject, but in other relative clauses, it could be an adjunct, object, or other complement, as in (). This gapping behaviour should remind you of focused interrogatives, which leave a gap wherever the focused element would usually appear in a "normal" clause.

[]That's the kid who [gap] arrived yesterday.[subject]

[]The point which I raised [gap] was shot down.[object]

[]That's the place where I put it [gap].[complement]

[]That's the year when we won the cup [gap].[modifier]

anchor: Relative constructions > Syntactic types
text:
If you've heard anything at all about relative clauses, it's probably that restrictive relatives start with that and non-restrictive relatives start with which. Unfortunately, this factoid is rather unhelpful.

A clearer way to think about it is set out in Figure. English relative clauses come in two broad syntactic types: basic and focused. The basic ones can be subdivided into that-marked and bare relatives, while the focused types can be subdivided into articulated, infinitival, and fused relatives. Examples follow in ( and ).

Figure: The syntactic types of relative constructions.

basic

[]The places that we likeare nearby.[that-marked]

[]The places we likeare nearby.[bare]

focused

[]The places where we studyare nearby.[articulated]

[]They're places in which to study.[infinitival]

[]Where we gois nearby.[fused]

anchor: Relative constructions > Syntactic types > Basic types
text:
The basic types, that-marked and bare, use examples like (), with a range of possible gap positions.

[]That's the kid that [gap] arrived yesterday.[subject]

[]That's the one thatI bought [gap].[object]

[]That's the place thatI put it [gap].[complement]

[]That's the year thatI was born [gap].[adjunct]

When there's a gap in the subject position, as in (a), then that is obligatory. Otherwise, it's usually optional, as in (b-d). In other words, you have more or less free choice between the that-marked and bare types when the gap is in the object (b), other-complement (c), or adjunct (d) positions.

The that in that-marked relatives is the same subordinator you find in declarative subordinate clauses, such as ().

[]The idea thatI might be suitablewas nice.[content]

[]The idea thatI had [gap] was nice.[relative]

anchor: Relative constructions > Syntactic types > Basic types > Restrictions on the basic types
text:
The basic relatives have a more limited distribution than the focused types. Bare relatives can't appear if the gap is in the subject position:

[]The places that [gap] suit us are nearby.[that-marked]

[*]The places [gap] suit us are nearby.[bare]

If the focused type has a multi-word relative phrase, neither basic type is possible.

22pt

[]The places to which we go [gap] are nearby.[focused]

[*]The places thatto we go [gap] are nearby.[basic]

[]The friend whose bike we borrowed [gap] is nearby.[focused]

[*]The friend thatbike we borrowed [gap] is nearby.[basic]

Also, if the relative clause is in an NP with that as its head, the basic types don't work.

[]That which we like [gap] is nearby.[focused]

[*]That thatwe like [gap] is nearby.[basic]

Another tendency is that basic relatives are quite uncommon after coordinators.

[]I saw a car which I recognized [gap] but which I didn't expect.[focused]

[?]I saw a car that I recognized [gap] but that I didn't expect.
[basic]

Finally, if the relative clause is a supplement, rather than a modifier, the basic types are not possible. A supplemental relative clause is set off in writing with commas and in speech with its own intonation contour.

[]A jerk, which I am not [gap], would have left.[focused]

[*]A jerk, thatI am not [gap], would have left.[basic]

anchor: Relative constructions > Syntactic types > Focused types: articulated
text:
Articulated relatives are very common, especially in news and academic prose (Biber et al., 1999). In many ways they are similar to focused (main-clause) interrogatives. For one thing, focused relatives have a relative word, mirroring the interrogative words of focused interrogatives. These two sets of words overlap significantly. The most important of the relative words are who, whom, whose, which, where, and when, along with why and while, which are less common. Other minor members include whence along with whereby, wherein, whereupon, etc. For comparisons, see Table.

Table: Comparison of Interrogative and Relative Words

Examples of each of the relative words appear in ().

[]That's the guard who [gap] we gave it to.

[]That's the guard to whom we gave it [gap].

[]That's the jerk whose dog [gap] bit me.[dependent possessive]

[*]That's the jerk whose [gap] bit me.[independent possessive]

[]That's the one which I bought [gap].

[]That's the place where I put it [gap].

[]That's the year when I was born [gap].

[]That's the time while I was away [gap].

[]That's the reason why I did it [gap].

[*]That's the way how I did it [gap].

[*]That's the thing what I saw [gap].

[]That's what I saw [gap].

Semantically, the relative words are similar to the interrogatives, except that they refer. Relative while denotes durations of time, and why is strictly limited to reasons. And although interrogative whose is limited to denoting persons, relative whose may refer to non-persons as well, as () demonstrates.

[]I read it in a book title I no longer remember.[relative]

[*]Whose title do you no longer remember, the book's?[interrogative]

As with focused interrogatives in Section, focused relative clauses involve fronted phrases (from single words to longer phrases) that leave gaps in their original positions. In articulated relatives, this creates a three-way relationship between the antecedent, fronted phrase, and gap, as shown in. () should help you to visualize this situation.

The typical case, with the relative phrase consisting of the relative word alone, is (a). Those like (b-d), where the relative word is a dependent in the relative phrase, are less common.

The gaps may be in subject, object, complement, or adjunct positions, as in ().

2

[]I know the axGreenplace AxGreenwhere he works B[gap].

[]She hasn't yet met the bxGreenpeople CxGreenewhose house she's renting D[gap].

[]This is the cxGreenprofessor Eto xGreenwhom I sent it F[gap].

[]He set them a dxGreenproblem, Gthe anfswer to xGreenwhich they found H[gap] online.

anchor: Relative constructions > Syntactic types > Focused types: articulated > Box: Check your understanding: gap analysis
text:
Practice identifying gaps and their functions:

- In the student who failed the test, where is the gap and what function does it serve?

- Why is *the hotel where doesn't have AC ungrammatical? What would the gap position require?

- In the book whose author I met, trace the relationship between book, whose author, and the gap

Understanding gap positions clarifies why certain relative words work in specific constructions.

anchor: Relative constructions > Syntactic types > Other focused types: infinitival and fused > Infinitival
text:
There is a rather formal and relatively rare type of infinitival relative clause, which I will refer to as focused infinitival relatives. These are most commonly found as complements of prepositions such as with, in, and from. They are characterized by a relative phrase consisting of a preposition followed most commonly by which or whom. Consider the examples in.

[]Do you have a key which to open it [gap].

[]She is the right person in whom to put your trust [gap].

[]As a base from which to conduct raids [gap], it's perfect.

As these examples illustrate, the focused infinitival relative is an integrated relative (it's a modifier within the NP, not a supplementary relative). The relative phrase (e.g., with which, in whom) is fronted, just as in other focused relative types. After the fronted relative phrase comes an infinitival VP. Crucially, this VP does not contain an overt subject.

In fact, focused infinitival relatives are subject to two strict structural conditions:

The relative phrase must consist of a preposition followed by a wh-NP.

There can be no overt subject in the relative clause.

Condition (a) rules out examples like *the question which to answer, where the relative phrase is a bare wh-NP without a preposition. Condition (b) excludes *the person in whom for us to confide, where us is an overt subject.

Despite these restrictions, the gap in a focused infinitival relative clause can correspond to a variety of syntactic functions, just as in other relative clause types. In (a), for instance, the gap is an adjunct in clause structure. In (b), it's a complement in the put VP. And in (c), it's again an adjunct in clause structure.

Given their highly formal flavor and structural complexity, focused infinitival relatives are quite rare. They are unlikely to be a priority for most English language learners. Still, for learners aiming at a very high level of proficiency, especially in formal written registers, an awareness of this construction is part of a comprehensive understanding of English relative clauses.

For the language teacher, the main points to note about focused infinitival relatives are:

- They involve a fronted relative phrase consisting of a preposition + wh-NP.

- The relative clause has an infinitival VP with no overt subject.

- They are always integrated, never supplementary, relatives.

- They are restricted to very formal styles.

In teaching, focused infinitival relatives are probably best treated as a kind of linguistic curiosity, an example of the complex machinery of English relative clauses, but not a construction that learners need to actively master.
subsection_title: Other focused types: infinitival and fused

anchor: Relative constructions > Syntactic types > Other focused types: infinitival and fused > Fused
text:
The final type of relative clause construction we have to dig in to is the fused subtype of the focused relative. Like the infinitivals, they're really quite rare and rarely of concern to English-language teachers and learners.

Fused relatives differ from the relative clauses discussed above in that they don't contain a separate antecedent and relative phrase. Instead, these two components are fused into a single constituent. Consider the examples in ().

[][gap] said thatwas mistaken.

[]I've heard you said [gap].

[]colour you pick [gap] will work.

The bracketed portions in these examples are not simply relative clauses, but rather full NPs containing fused relatives. In each case, the italicized relative phrase (whoever, what, whichever colour) simultaneously functions as the antecedent and the relative phrase.

In (a), whoever is both the head of the NP (comparable to the person) and the relative phrase in the relative clause. Similarly, in (b), what serves as the head of the object NP and the relative phrase in the relative clause. And, in (c), the NP whichever colour is both the antecedent and the relative phrase.

The relative words used in fused relatives differ somewhat from those used in regular relative clauses. () shows those that appear in fused relatives in present-day Standard English. The productive fused-relative heads are:

what, whatever, whoever/whomever, whichever, where, wherever, when, whenever, how

The compound -ever words aren't just possible but common in fused relatives. What is also possible, but while is not.

A potential challenge to the teacher is distinguishing fused relatives from embedded interrogative clauses, which can have a similar surface appearance:

[]I'll read what you suggest [gap].[relative]

[]I wonder what you suggest [gap].[interrogative]

The distinction is not always immediately apparent, but there are tests that can help. For instance, only relative words include the compound -ever forms. Compare I'll read whatever you suggest and *I wonder whatever you suggest.

The reason teachers may need to be aware is so that they do not present a fused relative when they intend to discuss an interrogative. Students, though, should never be asked to distinguish between them, as long as there is no difficulty understanding and producing such examples.
subsection_title: Other focused types: infinitival and fused

anchor: Relative constructions > Difficulty and syntactic functions
text:
Not all kinds of relative clause are equally easy. This seems to be related to how frequently they appear (Reali et al., 2007), though a clause that is more difficult to process will likely be produced less and so it's hard to say which way the causality goes, or even whether the cause might lie elsewhere. Still, we can generally say that relative clauses with subject gaps are easier to process than those with object gaps (Lau et al., 2021), as shown in ().

[]The teacher who [gap] liked the studentsmarked the tests.
[subject]

[]The teacher who the students liked [gap] marked the tests.
[object]

This recalls the discussion of information packaging (Ch. ), in particular the section on passive constructions (Section ). There, we saw that the passive can be used to keep related items together. This could mean keeping the head noun of a subject NP close to the VP, or it could mean fronting a topic so that it is closer to a previous mention. When it comes to relative clauses, those with a subject gap will similarly minimize the distance between the gap and its antecedent.

But there's also the factor of whether or not there are multiple agents. For example, in (), someone could plausibly misunderstand the direction of liking. In (), though, there's only the kid who could be an arriver and one possible raiser, putter, or winner, which simplifies things.

In terms of which NP the relative clause appears in, it seems that a relative clause occurring at the end of the clause is easier to process than one in the middle. This means that a relative in the subject is going to be a bit harder than one in the object. This gives us the illustrative difficulty matrices in Tables and, with 1 being easiest and 4 being hardest, though these numbers are to be interpreted very roughly.

Table: Difficulty matrix for relative clauses with one agent

Table: Difficulty matrix for relative clauses with multiple agents

anchor: Relative constructions > Semantic restriction
text:
This brings us back to the oft-cited distinction we opened with between 'restrictive' and 'non-restrictive'. A relative clause syntactically integrated into the NP as a modifier very often is semantically restrictive. In other words, it tends to identify a subset of the whole set. For example, if there are four books, two of which cover grammar, I can ask, Can I have the books that/which cover grammar], splitting the whole set of books into those that cover grammar and those that don't. And the response might be ().

[]The books that/which [gap] cover grammar are there.[restrictive]

It's also true that supplementary relatives are semantically non-restrictive. But if the response were instead (), it would refer to all of the books, without restriction. The fact that they cover grammar is merely a supplementary fact.

[]The books, which [gap] cover grammar, are there.[non-restrictive]

Unfortunately, the common idea that restrictive relatives use that and non-restrictive which, misses the possibility of focused relatives as modifiers and ignores bare relatives and all the other relative words.

[]A decision everyone likes [gap] is hard to make.[restrictive]

[]A decision, which we need [gap], is lacking.[non-restrictive]

[]The kids who [gap] were abroad were successful.[restrictive]

[]The kids, who [gap] were aboard, were successful.
[non-restrictive]

Perhaps the most important point is that restrictive and non-restrictive are not clause types but rather descriptions of the semantic force clauses have in different functions: modifier, in the restrictive cases, and supplement, in the non-restrictive cases. The correct generalization is that integrated modifiers tend to be restrictive and supplementary modifiers are not, and only articulated focused relatives may be supplementary modifiers.

And this applies not just to relative clauses. Consider the difference between the (a) and (b) examples in ( and ).

[]The kids abroad were successful.[restrictive]

[]The kids, abroad, were successful.[non-restrictive]

[]The sleepy and full guestsleft, but other guests stayed.[restr]

[?]Sleepy and full, the guests left, but other guests stayed.
[non-restr]

In (a), the PP abroad is a modifier in the NP, and it has restrictive semantics, picking out the subset of kids who are abroad. The PP in (b), though, is a supplement; (b) means all the kids are abroad, and all were successful. Similarly, the sleepy and full modifier in (a) restricts the set of guests who went home to the sleepy and full subset, while the supplement in (b) declares all guests sleepy and full, making mention of 'other guests" confusing.

anchor: Relative constructions > Semantic restriction > Box: Pattern recognition: restrictive vs non-restrictive
text:
Practice distinguishing integration patterns:

- Students who work hard succeed vs Students, who work hard, succeed - Which restricts the student population?

- Why can't basic relatives be supplementary? Students, *that work hard, succeed

- In He left early, which surprised me, what is the antecedent of which?

These patterns reveal how syntactic integration correlates with semantic restriction versus supplementation.

anchor: Relative constructions > Relative clauses as supplements
text:
As I've mentioned, only the articulated focused relatives function as supplements, and, as supplements, they are set off by commas, dashes, or parentheses in writing and have their own intonation contour in speech.

[]
guy who likes books is com

A guy who likes books is coming.

[]
o likes Ma, isleav

Adam, who likes books, is coming.

Interestingly, when relatives function as supplements, the set of possible antecedents widens. Nouns are still possible, but so, now, are VPs, clauses, AdjPs, PPs, and even AdvPs. With these antecedents, the relative phrase is always which.

[]I like to make soups, which [gap] is good practice.[VP]

[]He ran, which [gap] caused problems.[Clause]

[]My poor grades, which [gap] is putting it kindly, worsened.[AdjP]

[]I left before 6, which [gap] is early.[PP]

[]She finished well, which [gap] is how she wanted to finish.[AdvP]

anchor: Relative constructions > Relative clauses in it-clefts
text:
As we saw in Section, it-clefts include a main clause followed by a relative clause. This can be a basic or articulated relative, and the head noun is stressed (indicated by " ), as in (), but such clefts are actually relatively uncommon.

[] It was my "friend thatwe met [gap].

[] It was my "friend who we met [gap].

An it-cleft like (), in which it has no referent, can easily be confused with a much more common, similar-looking plain old non-cleft construction with a relative, such as (), in which it refers to something like my lack of cash, or forgetting my password.

[]It was a problem thatI had not anticipated [gap].

[]It was a problem which I had not anticipated [gap].

Another potential confounder, which we saw in Section, and which, again, is much more common, is an extraposed subject like that in (). This is a declarative content clause, not a relative. You can tell because it doesn't have a gap. If you're not sure, try replacing the that with which, as in (b). If it's an extraposed subject, it won't work.

[]It was a problem that more people were now coming.

[*]It was a problem which more people were now coming.

anchor: Relative constructions > Pronoun retention, aka "resumptive pronouns"
text:
In Standard English, relative clauses contain a gap, but sometimes, the gap is filled with a pronoun linked anaphorically to the antecedent in the matrix clause. This pronoun retention, also known as a "resumptive pronoun", is rare but most common in informal spoken registers. In some languages though, notably Arabic, pronoun retention is standard and commonly used. As a result, speakers of these languages tend to retain pronouns in English relative clauses as well.

Consider the examples in, which illustrate this construction.

[*]This is the house that I painted it yesterday.

[*]I have a girlfriend who she's really into jazz.

[*]There's the books that I told you about them.

In Standard English, the pronouns in italics would be omitted, leaving a gap. But in the non-standard examples in (), they are retained, resuming the reference of the antecedents (house, girlfriend, books).

But even expert users of English sometimes retain pronouns in relative clauses, especially when the gap that would otherwise appear is deeply embedded or when the relative phrase would be more than just the relative word, as in (). The standard version is in (a), while (b) exhibits retention of genitive her.

[]I met the student whose essay the teacher said that she liked [gap].

[?]I met the student who the teacher said that she liked her essay.

The greater acceptability of resumptive pronouns in these contexts may be related to processing difficulty. As we saw in Section, relative clauses are harder to process when the gap is in a non-subject position and when there is more intervening material between the antecedent and the gap. The resumptive pronoun seems to alleviate some of this processing load by overtly signalling the anaphoric relationship and the function of the gap.

From a pedagogical perspective, it may be helpful for learners who retain pronouns to have the situation explained to them, though gaps may be a difficult concept to explain and to grasp. Still, in academic writing and other formal contexts, gaps are generally expected.

As a teaching tool, though, it may actually be useful to retain the written pronouns but put them in brackets or write them on the board with a different colour to indicate that they are merely placeholders.

anchor: Relative constructions > Recursive embedding
text:
One of the most interesting features of languages like English is recursion, the ability to embed structures inside structures of the same type, creating an expandable system from a finite set of rules. Relative clauses provide a prime example of this phenomenon.

Consider the examples in ().

[]This is the cat that [gap] killed the rat xGreenthat [gap] ate the malt xOrangethat [gap] lay in the house xPinkthat Jack built [gap] xPink4 xOrange3 xGreen2 1.

[]The book that I borrowed [gap] from the library xGreenthat [gap] is located in the city xPinkwhere I live [gap] xPink3 xGreen2 1 is overdue.

In each of these (slightly whimsical) examples, we have a relative clause that contains within it another relative clause, which may in turn contain yet another, and so on. In (a), for instance, we have four levels of embedding, each introduced by that. The subscript numbers indicate the level of embedding, with 1 being the topmost level.

Perhaps there is no grammatical upper limit on the number of relative clauses that can be nested in this way. Each relative clause is a modifier of the noun phrase that immediately contains it, and any noun phrase can be modified by a relative clause. So the rules of English grammar seem to allow for structures of arbitrary complexity.

But in practice, there are significant constraints on recursive embedding. The most obvious is the limit of human processing capacity. As the depth of embedding increases, the sentence becomes increasingly difficult to parse and understand. Even for native speakers, more than three or four levels of embedding can start to strain comprehension, especially in spoken language where the hearer doesn't have the luxury of backtracking.

Additionally, highly complex structures like those in (), while grammatical, are often stylistically dispreferred. In most communicative contexts, simpler structures are more effective. Recursive embedding is most commonly found in written, particularly literary, language, where the writer has more time to craft complex structures and the reader has more time to decipher them.

As a teacher, it's probably not necessary to actively teach or encourage deep embedding. But it's useful to be aware of the phenomenon, both to understand the power of the linguistic system and to help learners decipher complex relative clause structures when they encounter them. It can also be used as a kind of add-on game, where each subsequent participant adds another layer.

One practical tip is to encourage learners to break down complex sentences into smaller parts. For instance, (c) could be analyzed as follows:

- The book is overdue.

- I borrowed the book from the library.

- The library is located in the city.

- I live in the city.

By unpacking the nested structure in this way, the meaning should become clearer.

anchor: Relative constructions > Phrase structure
text: Having looked at the main types of relative clauses and their properties, I'll present some trees to explain their structure. As we've seen before, the x subscript (e.g., NPx) indicates an anaphoric relationship.

anchor: Relative constructions > Phrase structure > Structural types > Basic relatives
text:
The following trees show the two basic relative types: bare and that-marked. Bare clauses (Figure ) are not possible with a subject gap, but for the that-marked type, I've included one with an object gap and one with a subject gap (Figure ).

Figure: A tree diagram for a bare relative clause in He's the man we met.

Figure: Tree diagrams for that-marked relative clauses following He's the man with object and subject gaps

anchor: Relative constructions > Phrase structure > Structural types > Focused relatives
text:
The next set of tree diagrams shows the focused types. There are three articulated relatives: a pair with object and subject gaps (Figure ) and one with a complex relative phrase (Figure ). These are followed by a tree showing the structure of the infinitival subtype (Figure ) and one for the fused subtype (Figure ).

Figure: Tree diagrams for articulated relative clauses with object and subject gaps

Figure: A tree diagram for an articulated relative with a complex relative phrase.

Figure: A tree diagram for an infinitival relative.

Figure: A tree diagram for a fused relative.

The structure in Figure has a new function label: Head-Pre. This denotes the fusion of the head function of the NP and the pre function. In other words, it shows that whoever is both the head of the NP and simultaneously a pre in the relative clause. The fusion is also indicated by the two branches coming to the single word, first branch coming from the NP and the second from the relative clause.

anchor: Relative constructions > Phrase structure > Relative clauses as modifiers in noun phrase structure
text:
Most relative clauses function as modifiers in NP structure, appearing after the head noun, as in Figure. This is no different than any other post-head modifier.

Figure: A tree diagram for a relative clause as a modifier in NP structure.

A small difference here is that, because it's a bare relative, the GAPx is co-indexed not with the relative phrase, because there isn't one, but with the head noun in the NP, in this case, places.
subsection_title: Relative clauses as modifiers in noun phrase structure

anchor: Relative constructions > Phrase structure > Supplementary relative clauses
text:
As Figures and show, supplementary relative clauses are not integrated into the NP but rather attached as supplements to the main clause. They always involve an articulated relative clause.

Figure: A tree diagram for a relative clause as a supplement in NP structure.

Figure: A tree diagram for a relative clause as a supplement in clause structure.

anchor: Relative constructions > Phrase structure > Supplementary relative clauses > Box: Quick self-check: relative construction types
text:
Test your understanding of relative clause classification:

- Classify: the book that I read vs the book I read vs the book which I enjoyed

- Why can supplementary relatives use which but not that? The students, who/*that were tired, left

- In what you said surprised me, explain why this is a fused relative, not an interrogative

Systematic classification helps reveal the underlying structural principles governing relative constructions.

anchor: Relative constructions > Summary
text: Relative clauses are among the most complex structures students encounter, and the complexity is real - gaps, long-distance dependencies, processing load that increases with every layer of embedding. But the system is small. Two types (basic and focused), a handful of relative words, and one key variable (where the gap is). A student who struggles with the person who I gave it to is struggling with a specific, locatable structural demand, and you can now see exactly which one.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality
text:
Physiologically, they are so alike: four-chambered, cone and crescent shaped, but

the problem is night -

how daybreak transforms two identical stones into a motherland

and a daughter, depending on the snarl of grassland at their ankles.Alycia Pirmohamed, Avian Circulatory System

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Box: Learning objectives
text:
- Understand grammaticality as gradient acceptability rather than binary correctness

- Analyze the relationship between form-meaning pairings and community conventions

- Distinguish between grammaticality, communicative effectiveness, and social appropriateness

- Recognize how processing limitations affect grammatical judgments in real time

- Understand the role of speech communities in defining grammatical norms

- Analyze how language change affects perceptions of grammatical acceptability

- Appreciate the difference between descriptive and prescriptive approaches to grammar

- Apply multiple factors (syntax, semantics, pragmatics, social meaning) in grammatical evaluation

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Introduction
text:
Many people believe that grammaticality is like the rule that stops play in soccer when the ball exits the pitch. In other words, they believe that something is either in bounds or out. But if we're analogizing to the realm of soccer, then grammaticality is much more like the handball rule, with its nuanced application and interpretation.

At the core, both handball violations and grammatical errors involve a relationship between form and meaning. A handball isn't just about physical contact (form) but how that contact affects play (meaning). Similarly, grammaticality is at its core about form-meaning pairings. An interrogative clause typically has the meaning of a question, and the past tense usually means past time.

But form-meaning pairings aren't universal, and neither is the handball rule. If you want to be recognized as playing by FIFA's laws of the game, then your handball rule needs to be FIFA's. Similarly, if you want to be seen as speaking Standard English, then it's important that you follow its syntax and morphology.

But if you're playing American football or rugby, then different rules apply, just as they do in French and German. And if a local children's soccer league modifies the handball rule or a local community accepts and uses double modals (you might could do that), then the soccer players, league officials, and the might could users aren't failing to follow rules - they're following different but equally systematic ones. Might could is productive and grammatical for millions of Southern US speakers and flatly ungrammatical for everyone else, not because one group has the right rules, but because each community maintains its own conventions through use, correction, and transmission. What counts as grammatical at any given moment is what a community has entrenched, not what's logically necessary.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Form-meaning pairings: the foundation
text:
At its most basic, a grammatical construction is one with an accepted form-meaning pairing within a specific language community, dialect, register, or situation. These pairings involve both semantic components and socio-pragmatic aspects. For instance, Try it and you'll regret it isn't just a conditional statement; it often conveys a threat.

The meaning element of grammatical constructions extends beyond simple semantics. At the most basic level, a construction's meaning includes its truth-conditional semantics (what has to be true in the world for it to be true). But constructions can also encode pragmatic meaning (like social distance, as when did you want that is more formal than do you want that), social meaning (like a speaker's relationship to standard language norms, as when I ain't doing nothin' marks non-standardness in a way that I'm not doing anything doesn't), and discourse meaning (like managing turn-taking or expressing emotion). Some constructions, like interjections, may have little semantic content but rich pragmatic, social, or discourse meaning; consider how oh can mark surprise, manage turn-taking, or signal a change in the speaker's knowledge state, depending on its context and intonation.

And it's not the case that a given form must have a single meaning (see Section ) or that a meaning can only be conveyed by a unique form. Consider how the progressive construction can indicate ongoing action (she's working), future plans (we're moving next month), or habitual irritating behavior (he's always leaving dirty dishes). And future time can be expressed through the progressive (I'm leaving tomorrow), going to (it's going to rain), modal will (she will arrive at 6), or simple present (the train leaves at noon).

In fact, the relationship between form and meaning is even more flexible than these examples suggest.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Form-meaning pairings: the foundation > Meaning without grammar
text:
It's critical to realize that, although grammatical forms carry meaning, meaning can be conveyed and understood perfectly well without grammar. Though the following utterances employ neither syntax nor morphology (not even lexis in the case of speaker B in example (c)), there can be no mistaking their meaning.

[]Hungry. Please food. Help.

[]Coffee hot! Careful! No drink!

[]A: Where's the book?
B: (shrugs, raises eyebrows, and grunts unnnhunnh)

This raises an important distinction between grammaticality and communicative effectiveness. Grammaticality arises from and depends on form-meaning pairs (it needs meaning) but meaning is insufficient. What makes a construction grammatical is its acceptance within a specific community as a conventional way to express a meaning. And different communities make radically different choices, not just about what forms to use, but about which meanings must be grammatically encoded at all.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Form-meaning pairings: the foundation > Context and community
text:
Different language communities encode different distinctions as grammatically relevant. English requires marking progressive aspect in certain contexts (She works vs. She is working) while French does not. Using the simple present when progressive is required (*Look, he runs) or progressive when simple present is appropriate (*The sun is rising in the east every morning) violates English grammaticality. A French speaker, though, can use Elle travaille for both meanings, adding another construction if the distinction is important.

Similarly, Turkish mandates marking evidentiality (how the speaker knows something) in contexts where English leaves this information optional (Go, 2005). Consider:

Goran yemek yi-yor

Goran food eat-prog

'Goran is eating (I see him eating)'

Goran yemek yi-yor-muş

Goran food eat-prog-evid

'Goran is eating (I heard/inferred he's eating)'

Turkish grammar generally requires speakers to specify whether they directly witnessed an event or learned about it secondhand, a distinction that might seem oddly specific to English speakers. (You mean I have to indicate whether I actually saw Goran eating or just heard about it?!) But the mandatory nature of English progressive marking might seem equally puzzling to Turkish speakers (You're telling me I have to signal whether or not I'm considering the end of Goran's eating?!) A language's grammatical requirements may be arbitrary, but they're not unmotivated; they reflect what that speech community considers consistently worth marking. Just as Turkish's evidentiality marking reveals the cultural significance of information sources, English's progressive aspect shows that the community of Standard English speakers conceptualizes an action's temporal boundedness as relevant enough that we've grammaticalized its expression.

The notion of a community of speakers itself bears consideration. Such groups can split along innumerable geographical and social borders, such as those between region, age, profession, or even shared interests, each fostering its own linguistic norms and expectations.

For most of us, lift and use are strictly transitive verbs. Bodybuilders, though, use intransitive lift to mean 'lift weights', while drug users employ intransitive use to mean 'use drugs'. These specialized meanings emerge because the distinctions they encode are consistently relevant to these communities.

Similarly, bilingual communities may mix languages when certain distinctions from one remain communicatively important, handy, or just familiar. A Chinese-English bilingual family might say open the light, reflecting Chinese 開燈 (kāi dēng), or a Spanish-English family might use she's more tall than me, carrying over Spanish comparative patterns.

But these communities go beyond simple calques to develop more complex patterns of language mixing. Turkish-English bilinguals I know mix Turkish question marking within English syntax. For instance:

Come-di-n mi?

come-pst-2sg q

'Did you come?'

In this example, the Turkish question particle mi and second-person singular past-tense marker -din are combined with the English root come, adapting it to Turkish grammatical structure to mean 'did you come?' This type of mixing allows speakers to draw on familiar patterns from Turkish while using English vocabulary, creating a hybrid form that feels natural within their bilingual context.

Similarly, a Vietnamese student tells me that She eat rồi ('She has eaten already') is something Vietnamese-English bilinguals would say.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Form-meaning pairings: the foundation > Context and community
text:
And all of these are grammatical. They're not Standard English, but they don't purport to be. They're accepted form-meaning pairs within the respective speech communities. Of course, Standard English happens to be a very large, diverse, and powerful community (See Section ), whose shadow can be difficult to escape, especially when speakers of other dialects are bilingual with SE.

This interplay between general cognitive mechanisms and specific linguistic contexts reflects a broader pattern: domain-general biases often have domain-specific effects. While humans possess general cognitive preferences - like the bias toward simpler, more compressible patterns - these preferences manifest differently across domains. In language, they interact with linguistic representations and communicative pressures to create effects that appear highly specialized.

Consider how a general cognitive preference for simplicity plays out across different aspects of language. In phonology, it might lead to simpler syllable structures. In morphology, it pushes toward regular rather than irregular forms. In syntax, it favors consistent ordering patterns. Though the underlying bias is the same, its effects depend crucially on the specific structures involved. What counts as "simple" in each case depends on the representational system in question.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality
section_title: When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality
text: That doesn't mean that anything goes. Things fall apart - forms can fail to pair with any meaning, meanings can clash with the demands of other forms, and processing demands can exceed cognitive limits.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality > Form-meaning mismatches
section_title: When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality
text:
A construction becomes ungrammatical when the mapping between form and meaning breaks down in one of three fundamental ways:

- The grammatical form lacks any viable meaning interpretation

- The intended form-meaning pairing fails to align with conventional usage

- A non-standard form is used without pragmatic motivation

The first type covers meaningless forms. When a grammatical construction fails to map to any coherent meaning, we have the most severe type of ungrammaticality. Consider the utterance *Can the have running. This sequence of words follows no interpretable pattern in English syntax, making it impossible for speakers to construct any meaning from it. Unlike other types of ungrammaticality, such constructions are rejected not because they express meaning incorrectly, but because they fail to express any meaning at all.

The second type is meaning misalignment, where the form could potentially carry meaning, but that meaning clashes with either:

- The speaker's intended meaning

- Other meaning elements in the utterance

For example, when a French speaker says *I have 35 years (calqued from French J'ai 35 ans) to express their age, the utterance is ungrammatical not because it's syntactically malformed, but because its intended meaning ('I am 35 years old') fails to align with how English conventionally expresses this concept. Similarly, *I go there yesterday demonstrates a temporal contradiction between the present-time meaning of the verb tense and the past-time meaning of yesterday.

But the same string of words can become grammatical when pragmatically motivated:

[]So, I go there yesterday, and suddenly...

Here, the present tense serves a legitimate discourse function, the "historical present" for vivid narration, resolving what would otherwise be a meaning clash. This interpretation is facilitated by so = 'I'm about to tell you a story'.

A particularly interesting case of meaning misalignment occurs with question formation. Consider the sentence Kim believes the claim that Pat saw someone. When we try to ask for the identity of 'someone' by moving the word to the front, we get the ungrammatical *Who does Kim believe the claim that Pat saw? This illustrates how information-structural requirements can clash: the construction starting with who is a focus construction (see Section ). Part of its meaning is something like 'pay attention to the element in front'. But the the claim that... construction includes the meaning 'consider the content of this claim as a whole'. As Cuneo et al. (2022) argue, the resulting ungrammaticality arises from the clash between the discourse meaning of the syntactic structures.

The third type involves using non-standard forms without any clear communicative purpose. Consider *We sheared three sheeps. While English typically forms plurals with -s, sheep is conventionally invariant. The form sheeps could be grammatical in specific contexts:

- Distinguishing types of sheep:
We raise various sheeps, including Merino and Dorset.

- Referring to sports team members
The Sheffield Sheep won again - those Sheeps are unstoppable!

- In metalinguistic discussion
How many sheeps did you count in the text?

Without such pragmatic motivation, though, using sheeps represents a form-meaning mismatch: the speaker has departed from conventional form without any communicative justification.

These types demonstrate that grammaticality judgments must consider multiple levels of linguistic structure:

- Syntactic well-formedness

- Semantic coherence

- Pragmatic appropriateness

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality > Form-meaning mismatches
section_title: When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality
text:
- Information structural constraints

And so a complete account of grammaticality has to examine not just syntactic form, but the entire context of form-meaning mappings, including speakers' communicative intentions and the discourse environment.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality > Form-meaning mismatches > Box: Check your understanding: form-meaning analysis
section_title: When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality
text:
Apply the three-type framework to analyze ungrammaticality:

- *Can the have running: what makes this ungrammatical?

- *I have 35 years (for age) vs I have 35 years of experience: same form, different grammaticality judgments?

- So I go there yesterday...: how does pragmatic motivation rescue apparent ungrammaticality?

Understanding these distinctions clarifies how grammaticality involves multiple interacting factors beyond syntax.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality > Processing issues
section_title: When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality
text: Your sense of grammaticality is a detector, and like all detectors, it's noisy. It usually fires correctly; you know instantly that *Can the have running is wrong and that The dog chased the cat is fine. But it can misfire in both directions: judging grammatical constructions as ungrammatical (false negatives) and letting ungrammatical ones pass (false positives). These misfires work exactly like optical illusions: your eyes aren't broken when you see the Müller-Lyer lines as different lengths, and your grammaticality sense isn't broken when it misfires. Both are reliable systems with characteristic failure modes. For teachers, this matters: when you can't tell whether a student's sentence is grammatical, that's sometimes the detector being noisy, not you being ignorant.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality > Processing issues > False negatives
section_title: When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality
text:
A grammatical construction can be wrongly judged ungrammatical when:

- The structure is misperceived due to garden path effects (e.g., The old man the phones, meaning 'The elderly are responsible for handling the phones')

- Processing capabilities are exceeded by structural demands (e.g., The bread the baker the apprentice helped made is delicious)

Consider garden path sentences like The horse raced past the barn fell and The old man the phones. While syntactically well formed, such constructions often receive negative judgments because their initial parsing leads readers down an incorrect interpretive path. In The horse raced, readers typically interpret raced as a past-tense head verb. When they hit fell, the clause seems like a two-headed monstrosity. This happens because the horse raced past the barn is entirely plausible as a main clause and seems much likelier than for raced past the barn to be a modifier in the NP headed by horse (equivalent to The horse that was raced past the barn fell).

Similarly, in The old man the phones, readers initially interpret man as a noun rather than a verb because NPs like the old man are so much more common, making it difficult to realize that the subject is just the old. The strong initial activation of the simpler, more frequent interpretations can suppress recognition of the correct but less common readings, leading to perceived ungrammaticality.

A sentence like The bread the baker the apprentice helped made is delicious employs the usual form for centre-embedded relative clauses, but the burden of one inside another inside a third likely exceeds working memory capacity for most speakers.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality > Processing issues > False positives
section_title: When things go wrong: types of ungrammaticality
text:
Conversely, an ungrammatical construction can be wrongly judged grammatical when processing capabilities are exceeded by semantic demands. Consider:

[*]More people found Mr Bush's ads negative than they did Mr Kerry's.

This sentence appears to be a standard comparative construction. When you look carefully at it, though, you find that it doesn't actually mean anything: its structure entails something like comparing the number of people who had one perception to the degree of another perception. To see this more clearly, consider the grammatical alternatives:

[]More people found Mr Bush's ads negative than did Mr Kerry's.
[compares number of people]

[]People found Mr Bush's ads more negative than they did Mr Kerry's.
[compares negativity]

But the form up to than is likely enough to evoke the right meaning, and with that accomplished, we don't seem to notice that the complement in the than PP goes off the rails.

Such false positives aren't limited to comparative constructions. When multiple semantic features compete for our attention, even basic grammatical requirements like subject-verb agreement can be overlooked. Consider () from Corbett (2016).

[*]The patchwork of laws governing background checks, assault-weapons limits, and open-carry practices help explain why people continue to be wounded and killed.

Again, this strikes most people as fully grammatical on a first read, but the subject is singular (its head being patchwork), and so the verb should be helps, not help. There's just so much plurality going on that we're overloaded and we simply accept help as the correct choice.

In each case, the detector misfires, and knowing that it can misfire is what lets you step back and check the structure instead of trusting your first reaction.

These misfires sit on a continuum. A nonsense string triggers the detector instantly; a form-meaning mismatch in casual speech may not trigger it at all. The severity depends on how far the form departs from what the community has entrenched, which means it depends on the community, the register, and the moment in history.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change
text: Like the handball rule we started with, grammaticality shifts over time through use, reanalysis, and evolving needs. These changes don't happen randomly; they follow predictable paths driven by semantic, social, and structural pressures.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Motivations for change > Semantic opportunities
text:
Sometimes a metaphorical connection suggests itself as a handy way to express something, and speakers seize the opportunity. The French negative pas illustrates this perfectly. It started as the word for 'step', and speakers noticed that saying Je ne marche pas ('I don't walk a step') was a particularly vivid way to emphasize negation - after all, if you're not even taking one step, you're really not walking at all. This minimal unit of movement proved so useful for emphasizing negation that speakers extended it to other verbs: Je ne parle pas ('I don't speak a step'). Though the metaphor makes less literal sense here, the negative force was clear. Eventually, pas became so useful as a negative marker that it started taking over from ne entirely.

The development of going to in English shows a similar opportunistic adoption. When you're going somewhere to do something, your movement and your intention are bound together: I'm going to the store to buy milk. Speakers noticed this connection between movement and future action could be metaphorically extended. First it spread to cases where the movement was followed by another action, like I'm going to help you, and then to situations that didn't involve any movement: It's going to rain. What started as a handy metaphorical extension for expressing planned actions became fully grammaticalized as a general future marker.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Motivations for change > Structural pressures
text:
Sometimes grammar changes because the current system exceeds speakers' processing capacities. In Old English, word order was flexible because nouns inflected to show their functions in the sentence (as personal pronouns still do today). For instance, Se hund bit þone mann and þone mann bit se hund both meant 'The dog bites the man' because se hund marked the subject form and þone mann marked the object form, regardless of their position in the sentence.

When English lost these special endings, speakers had to find a new way to show who was doing what. The solution was to have the subject appear first, right before its verb. But this created a problem: making questions had long involved moving the verb around (Saw you him?). You couldn't keep subjects reliably before their verbs if you kept moving the verbs.

This wasn't just complicated - it had become unworkable. The new requirement to show who was doing what through word order clashed directly with the old system of moving verbs around to form questions and negatives. The language needed a solution that could satisfy both needs at once.

That solution emerged in the form of do as a stand-in that could move instead of the lexical verb. As Culicover (2008) shows, this innovation spread gradually and unevenly. By 1650-1700, do-support appeared in 92% of negative questions but only 46% of plain negatives. This pattern makes sense: questions required moving something to the front to be understood, while negation could be expressed through other means. The differential adoption rates suggest that speakers implemented the new construction most quickly where it was most needed.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Motivations for change > Social factors
text:
Sometimes grammar changes because of who uses a construction. As speakers shift from I'll write it tomorrow to I'm writing it tomorrow, they aren't solving any structural problem. Instead, certain groups, often younger or urban speakers, start using the construction to mark themselves as modern or sophisticated. If these speakers have social influence, others begin adopting the pattern too.

The opposite can also happen: constructions associated with lower status groups often become stigmatized and die out among those seeking higher status. Double negatives like I don't want nothing were once standard in English but became marked as uneducated. The same happened to ain't (e.g., I ain't tired), which was once used by all social classes but gradually became socially restricted as it acquired a stigma.

Social factors help explain why seemingly equivalent constructions end up with different fates. English could have solved its word order pressures in various ways, but do-support caught on in part because it first appeared in the writing of educated speakers. Whether a construction spreads or dies often has as much to do with who uses it as how well it works grammatically.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Domain-general biases in language change
text:
Recent research suggests that many changes in language arise from the interaction between domain-general cognitive biases and the specific properties of linguistic systems (Culbertson et al., 2016). Of particular importance is the bias toward simplicity, a general cognitive preference for patterns that can be represented more concisely. This domain-general bias can have highly specific effects on language when it interacts with linguistic representations and communicative pressures.

This simplicity bias helps explain several phenomena discussed earlier:

- A tendency toward regularization of irregular forms (as with English verbs moving from clomb and pled to climbed and pleaded)

- A preference for consistent head-before-dependent ordering across phrase types (e.g., in NPs knowledge that you said it, VPs know you said it, and PPs because you said it)

- A tendency for language to favour building new meanings by combining existing words/parts rather than creating entirely new words for each concept

In each case, the general cognitive preference for simplicity interacts with specific linguistic structures to shape how languages change over time.

Importantly, these biases don't need to be strong to have significant effects. Through cultural transmission (as language is learned and used across generations), even weak cognitive biases can be amplified to create strong patterns (Culbertson et al., 2016). This helps explain why languages show robust regularities despite the absence of hard constraints in our language faculty. A slight preference for regular forms in learning, for instance, can lead to near-complete regularization over generations of transmission.

This perspective helps unify our understanding of language change. Rather than positing separate explanations for different types of change, we can often trace them back to the interaction of domain-general biases with specific linguistic structures. The apparent diversity of linguistic patterns emerges not from a multitude of specialized constraints, but from how general cognitive preferences play out across different aspects of linguistic structure.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Processing and dependency distances
text: Processing limitations play an important role in grammatical evolution, particularly through dependency distance, the distance between syntactically related words (Gibson et al., 2013; Gibson, 2025). Long dependency distances place strain on working memory, which, over time, exerts pressure on languages to favour structures that minimize these distances.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Processing and dependency distances > Dependency distance and grammatical change
text:
Dependency distance measures how far apart syntactically connected words are within a sentence. For example:

The coach believes the team can win.

Here, the dependency distance between coach and believes is short, allowing for smooth processing. But what about a more complex sentence?

The coach who had been the most popular in years believed the team could win.

The dependency distance between coach and believed is longer, requiring more cognitive effort to track. Over time, languages tend to avoid or rephrase structures with such long dependencies, leading to shifts toward shorter, more manageable dependency distances. We saw this with the passive construction (See Section ) and again with ().

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Processing and dependency distances > Structural adjustments from processing pressures
text:
Several grammatical structures reflect a preference for reduced dependency distance:

- Right-branching structures: In languages like English, right-branching structures (where additional information follows the main clause, e.g., I met Rachel in early Spring last year, in Paris, where she was giving a presentation on trolleybus systems) ease processing by keeping dependencies short.

- Avoidance of long dependency chains: English and other languages often avoid structures with extended dependencies, favoring rephrasing that keeps related elements closer together.

- Emergence of auxiliary constructions: The development of do-support in English questions and negatives reduces dependency load by allowing lexical verbs to remain in place, simplifying the processing of complex sentences.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Processing and dependency distances > Impact on language evolution
text: Processing limitations, particularly related to dependency distance, influence grammatical change by favouring structures that align with human cognitive capacities. This pressure shapes grammatical systems over time, leading languages to adapt toward forms that facilitate efficient processing.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Usage-based theories of change
text: I've talked about established or conventional forms, but how do they become convention? It's mostly just exposure. Over time, high-frequency usage can lead to the entrenchment of certain forms, while less-used forms may fade or change.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Usage-based theories of change > Frequency and entrenchment
text: The frequency with which a structure is used strengthens its presence in the language. For example, the frequent use of certain verb-noun combinations, such as take a look or make a decision, reinforces these specific phrases and encourages speakers to treat them as conventional units. This process of entrenchment means that high-frequency patterns become more stable, while less frequently used forms are more susceptible to change or simplification.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Usage-based theories of change > Analogy and extension
text: High-frequency constructions often serve as models for other structures. For instance, the regularization of English past tense verbs, as seen with the analogy-driven shift from clomb to climbed, reflects speakers' tendency to extend well-established patterns to new words. Usage-based theories emphasize how analogy allows speakers to generalize patterns across the language, gradually reshaping grammatical structures over time.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Usage-based theories of change > Grammaticalization from repeated usage
text:
Usage-based theories explain grammaticalization, the process by which lexical items become grammatical markers, as resulting from patterns of frequent use. While the going to construction eventually shifted from expressing physical movement to marking future tense, this was a rare metaphorical extension that took hold within the grammar. New metaphors arise in language constantly, but only a few become entrenched enough to change grammar.

For a metaphor to grammaticalize, it must occur frequently in contexts that reinforce its reinterpretation. In the case of going to, speakers consistently used it in ways that emphasized intention or future action, rather than movement. Over time, this frequent usage allowed it to shed its literal meaning and take on a purely grammatical role. But most metaphorical expressions, like grasp the idea or kick the habit, remain lexical phrases because they don't occur in contexts that reinterpret them as grammatical markers.

This selective process shows how grammaticalization requires high-frequency use in specific communicative contexts, where meanings shift incrementally until the original lexical item becomes a stable grammatical marker.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Usage-based theories of change > Grammaticalization from repeated usage > Box: Pattern recognition: processing and change
text:
Analyze how cognitive factors influence grammatical patterns:

- Why does The horse raced past the barn fell create processing difficulty despite being grammatical?

- How do false positives like *More people found Bush's ads negative than they did Kerry's reveal processing limitations?

- What role does dependency distance play in the development of do-support in English questions?

Understanding these processing effects reveals how cognitive constraints shape grammatical evolution.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Evolution and change > Usage-based theories of change > Grammaticalization from repeated usage > Box: Quick self-check: grammaticality factors
text:
Test your understanding of multi-factor grammatical analysis:

- How can the same form (I go there yesterday) be both grammatical and ungrammatical depending on context?

- Why might We sheared three sheeps be acceptable in some contexts but not others?

- What makes Turkish-English come-di-mi grammatical within its speech community?

Systematic analysis reveals grammaticality as a community-based form-meaning convention rather than universal rule-following.

anchor: The Nature of Grammaticality > Summary
text: Grammaticality is what a community has settled on, and the settlement shifts. A form that was ungrammatical a generation ago may be standard now; a form that's standard in one classroom may be marked in the next. For teachers, the practical upshot is a three-way distinction: is this ungrammatical (no community accepts it), non-standard (some communities accept it but the target variety doesn't), or unfamiliar to me (a community accepts it and I just haven't encountered it)? That question replaces the red pen with a professional judgment.

anchor: Understanding language proficiency
text: If you've taught English for any length of time, you've probably been asked to assess someone's level or help a student reach a particular benchmark. Maybe you've wondered why one student who speaks fluently in class discussions can't pass a writing test, or why another who aces grammar exercises freezes up in conversation. These puzzles point to something fundamental: proficiency isn't the straightforward concept that scales and frameworks make it seem.

anchor: Understanding language proficiency > What is proficiency?
text:
When we talk about someone's English proficiency, what exactly are we measuring? The question seems simple enough; after all, we make these judgments constantly. We know when someone speaks English well or poorly. But dig a little deeper and the concept starts to fracture.

A new colleague contributes sophisticated ideas in meetings using academic vocabulary and complex structures. But at lunch, when conversation turns to weekend plans or TV shows, she seems lost. What's her proficiency level?

A taxi driver navigates the city expertly, chatting with passengers about routes, weather, and local news. He's never studied English formally and his grammar is noticeably inconsistent. But he's never had a passenger complain. How proficient is he?

These aren't trick questions; they're the reality of language proficiency. It's not a single skill that develops evenly. It's multidimensional, context-dependent, and deeply tied to what someone needs to do with language.

Most frameworks divide proficiency into skills: speaking, listening, reading, writing. But even speaking includes wildly different activities: giving presentations, telling jokes, comforting friends, negotiating conflicts. Each requires different resources. Then there's the fluency dimension we explored in Chapter, that sense of flow and ease that doesn't always correlate with accuracy or complexity. Someone might speak with an easy flow despite grammatical inconsistencies, while another produces perfect sentences but sounds stilted and unnatural.

And we haven't even touched on pragmatic competence, knowing not just what you can say but what you should say. The ability to make requests politely, refuse without offending, or read between the lines of indirect communication. These pragmatic skills often determine real-world success more than grammatical accuracy, but they're notoriously difficult to evaluate.

anchor: Understanding language proficiency > How frameworks see proficiency
text:
Proficiency frameworks represent a classic case of what James Scott (1998) calls seeing like a state, the need to make messy human realities legible for institutional purposes. Just as states create standardized maps that flatten complex territories into manageable abstractions, proficiency scales flatten the wild diversity of human communication into neat levels and descriptors.

This isn't necessarily bad. Institutions need ways to make decisions: who qualifies for university admission, who can practice medicine, who gets citizenship. Teachers need ways to group students and track progress. Learners themselves often want concrete goals and clear markers of achievement. The frameworks serve these legitimate needs.

But like Scott's simplified maps, proficiency scales capture some things while obscuring others. They make certain aspects of language visible and measurable while rendering others invisible or irrelevant.

anchor: Understanding language proficiency > How frameworks see proficiency > Canadian Language Benchmarks (CLB)
text:
The CLB emerged from a specific institutional need: helping adult immigrants integrate into Canadian society (Benchmarks, 2012). It divides proficiency into 12 benchmarks across four skills, grouped into three stages:

- Stage I (Benchmarks 1 -4): Basic language ability

- Stage II (Benchmarks 5 -8): Intermediate ability

- Stage III (Benchmarks 9 -12): Advanced ability

The CLB's task-based approach specifies concrete things learners can do (Benchmarks, 2012):

- At CLB 7 Speaking, learners may need to give instructions for technical and non-technical tasks, procedures, and processes.

- At CLB 7 Reading, learners may need to follow multistep instructional texts, including diagrams for assembling furniture or equipment.

This specificity makes the framework practical for teaching and assessment. But it also reveals what the framework values: workplace communication, consumer interactions, institutional engagement. The CLB maps the territory of successful integration as economic participation and system navigation.
